RhabdomyolysisEthanol, heroin, cocaine, statins, cyclosporineAcute necrosisAcute: CK >10,000, myoglobinuria, AKI risk; treat: aggressive IV hydration

Endocrine Myopathies

Thyroid: Hypothyroid myopathy (proximal weakness, CK elevated, Hoffman syndrome — stiffness/pseudomyotonia); hyperthyroid myopathy (proximal wasting, normal CK). Parathyroid: Hyperparathyroidism → proximal muscle weakness + atrophy. Adrenal: Cushing's/steroid → type 2B fiber atrophy; Addison's → fatigue/weakness. Acromegaly: Proximal weakness.

Other Acquired Myopathies

Sarcoid myopathy (nodular or chronic), amyloid myopathy (macroglossia, pseudohypertrophy), checkpoint inhibitor-associated myositis (treat with IV steroids/IVIG/PLEX; may overlap with MG/myocarditis).

❓ Board-Style Question — Myopathy

A 67-year-old man presents with 2 years of progressive difficulty rising from chairs and gripping objects. Exam shows asymmetric weakness of knee extension (R 3/5, L 4/5) and finger flexion (R 3/5, L 4+/5) with relative preservation of shoulder abduction and hip flexion. CK is 580 U/L. EMG shows mixed myopathic and neurogenic features. He was previously treated with prednisone 60 mg/day for 6 months without improvement. What is the most likely diagnosis?

Answer: Inclusion Body Myositis (IBM)

Classic features: (1) Age >50, male; (2) Asymmetric weakness of quadriceps (knee extension) and finger flexors (FDP) — the hallmark IBM pattern; (3) Moderately elevated CK; (4) Mixed myopathic + neurogenic EMG pattern (characteristic of IBM); (5) Refractory to immunosuppression (failed prednisone). Biopsy would show rimmed vacuoles, endomysial CD8+ T cell inflammation, and TDP-43+ inclusions. Anti-cN1A antibody may be positive (~33-51%). Key teaching: IBM is the most important acquired myopathy to NOT treat with chronic immunosuppression — focus on physiotherapy and supportive care. Avoid the common mistake of escalating immunotherapy for a "non-responding PM."

▼5.6 Other NMJ Disorders & Peripheral Neuropathy Overview

Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS)

FeatureDetails
PathogenesisAntibodies against P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels (VGCC) at presynaptic nerve terminal → ↓ ACh release
Association60% paraneoplastic (SCLC — small cell lung cancer); 40% autoimmune
ClinicalProximal weakness (LE > UE); fatigable but initial facilitation (improves with exercise then fatigues); areflexia that improves post-exercise; autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth #1, constipation, ED)
DiagnosisAnti-VGCC Ab (P/Q-type); RNS: low baseline CMAP with >100% increment at 20-50 Hz stimulation or post-exercise; ⚠️ Screen for SCLC (CT chest, PET-CT)
TreatmentTreat underlying cancer first; symptomatic: 3,4-diaminopyridine (3,4-DAP/amifampridine) — blocks K+ channels → prolongs presynaptic depolarization → ↑ Ca²+ entry → ↑ ACh release; pyridostigmine as adjunct; IVIG/PLEX for severe; immunosuppression for autoimmune LEMS

MG vs LEMS — Quick Comparison

FeatureMGLEMS
Antibody targetPostsynaptic (AChR/MuSK)Presynaptic (VGCC)
Weakness patternOcular/bulbar → generalizedProximal LE → UE; less ocular
ReflexesNormal or presentAbsent → improve post-exercise
AutonomicNot typicalProminent (dry mouth, constipation)
RNS patternDecrement at low frequencyLow CMAP baseline + increment >100% at high frequency
CancerThymoma (10-15%)SCLC (60%)

Botulism

🔑 Botulism — Descending Paralysis:
• Pathogenesis: Clostridium botulinum toxin blocks presynaptic ACh release (cleaves SNARE proteins)
• Forms: Foodborne (most common adult), wound, infant (honey ingestion), iatrogenic
• Clinical triad: (1) Descending symmetric flaccid paralysis (cranial nerves first → limbs → respiratory); (2) Autonomic dysfunction (mydriasis → fixed dilated pupils, dry mouth, constipation, urinary retention); (3) Alert mental status — NO sensory deficit
• Key distinction from GBS: Descending (vs ascending in GBS); pupils affected; no sensory changes; NCS: low CMAPs with increment on rapid RNS
• Treatment: Antitoxin (trivalent ABE or heptavalent) ASAP; supportive care + ICU; aminoglycosides/NMJ-blocking drugs CONTRAINDICATED

Congenital Myasthenic Syndromes (CMS)

Genetic (non-autoimmune) disorders of NMJ transmission. Onset: birth to childhood. Types: presynaptic (ChAT deficiency), synaptic (endplate AChE deficiency), postsynaptic (AChR subunit mutations — most common; slow-channel/fast-channel CMS; rapsyn deficiency; Dok-7 CMS). Treatment varies by subtype — AChEI effective for some; 3,4-DAP for presynaptic; quinidine/fluoxetine for slow-channel; salbutamol/ephedrine for Dok-7 CMS. ⚠️ AChEI may WORSEN slow-channel CMS and Dok-7.

Approach to Peripheral Neuropathy (Overview)

📋 Peripheral Neuropathy — Systematic Approach
  • Classification by anatomy: Mononeuropathy / mononeuritis multiplex / polyneuropathy / radiculopathy / plexopathy
  • Classification by fiber type: Motor-predominant (GBS, CIDP, MMN, CMT) / Sensory-predominant (diabetic, B12, paraneoplastic) / Autonomic (diabetic, amyloid, GBS)
  • Classification by pathology: Demyelinating (prolonged DL, slow CV, conduction block) vs Axonal (low amplitudes, preserved CV)
  • Time course: Acute (<4 wk: GBS, vasculitis, toxin) / Subacute (4 wk-2 mo) / Chronic (>2 mo: CIDP, CMT, metabolic, diabetic)

Hand Weakness Differential Approach

Unilateral Hand Weakness/WastingBilateral Hand Weakness/Wasting
Monomelic amyotrophy (Hirayama)Motor neuron disease (ALS)
C8-T1 radiculopathySyringomyelia (dissociated sensory loss)
Lower trunk plexopathyCervical spondylotic myelopathy
MMN with conduction blockPolio-like myelopathy
Ulnar/median neuropathy
🔑 Wasted Hand Approach:
• APB wasting only → median nerve (C8-T1, thenar)
• ADM/1st DI wasting → ulnar nerve (C8-T1, hypothenar/interossei)
• All three (APB + ADM + 1st DI) → multiple possibilities: spinal cord (anterior horn), MND, C7-T1 root/plexus, peripheral neuropathy, distal myopathy

Multifocal Motor Neuropathy (MMN)

💊 MMN — Treatable ALS Mimic
  • Clinical: Asymmetric, distal > proximal, upper limb predominant; pure motor; NO UMN signs
  • EDx: Motor conduction block at non-compressible sites (key finding!); normal sensory NCS
  • Antibody: Anti-GM1 IgM (50%)
  • Treatment: IVIG (first-line — high response rate); steroids are NOT effective and may worsen; PLEX not beneficial
▼5.7 Key References & Guidelines — Neuromuscular Disorders

📚 Guidelines

  • [G1] Thai MG Guideline — SNAC/Siriraj protocol (Kumutpongpanich T, SNAC 13th, 2025)
  • [G2] EAN/PNS Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of GBS (van Doorn et al, Eur J Neurol 2023;30:3646-3674)
  • [G3] EAN Guideline on ALS Management (Van Damme et al, Eur J Neurol 2024;31:e16264)
  • [G4] International consensus guidance on management of MG (Sanders et al, Neurology 2016)
  • [G5] EFNS/PNS CIDP Guidelines 2021

📊 Landmark Trials

  • [T1] MGTX Trial 2016 — Thymectomy + pred vs pred alone in non-thymomatous AChR+ gMG → better QMG, less steroids (Wolfe GI et al, NEJM 2016;375:511-522)
  • [T2] REGAIN 2017 — Eculizumab in refractory generalized AChR+ MG (Howard JF et al, Lancet Neurol 2017;16:976-986)
  • [T3] ADAPT 2021 — Efgartigimod (anti-FcRn) in generalized MG (Howard JF et al, Lancet Neurol 2021;20:526-536)
  • [T4] CHAMPION-MG 2022 — Ravulizumab in generalized MG (Vu T et al, NEJM 2022;386:1132-1142)
  • [T5] RAISE 2023 — Zilucoplan in generalized MG
  • [T6] MycarinG 2023 — Rozanolixizumab in AChR/MuSK MG
  • [T7] SID-GBS 2021 — Second IVIG dose in GBS: no benefit, more AEs
  • [T8] VALOR 2022 — Tofersen for SOD1-ALS (Miller TM et al, NEJM 2022;387:1099-1110)
  • [T9] CENTAUR 2020 — AMX0035 in ALS (Paganoni S et al, NEJM 2020;383:919-930)
  • [T10] ProDERM 2021 — IVIG in dermatomyositis

📖 Key Reviews

  • MG subgroups — Gilhus NE, NEJM 2016;375:2570-81
  • MG pathogenesis — Neurotherapeutics 2016;13:118-131
  • ALS Clinical Review — Masrori P, Van Damme P, Eur J Neurol 2020;27:1918-1929
  • ALS Nature Reviews Primer — Hardiman O et al, Nat Rev Dis Primers 2017;3:17085
  • ALS classification — Al-Chalabi A et al, Lancet Neurol 2016;390:2084-2098
  • IIM classification — Greenberg SA, Nat Rev Rheum 2018
  • Myositis antibodies — Meyer A et al, Joint Bone Spine 2018;85:23-33

6. Demyelinating Diseases & Neuroimmunology

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of CNS demyelinating and neuroimmune disorders. Coverage includes MS (McDonald 2017 → 2024 criteria with all 10 updates, complete DMT landscape, BTK inhibitors), NMOSD (IPND 2015/NEMOS 2023, AQP4-IgG astrocytopathy, targeted biologics — eculizumab/satralizumab/inebilizumab), MOGAD (2023 diagnostic criteria, clinical phenotypes), Autoimmune Encephalitis (Graus 2016, antibody-specific syndromes), ADEM, and comprehensive differential diagnosis.

6.1 Multiple Sclerosis (MS)

6.1.1 Epidemiology & Risk Factors

Multiple sclerosis is the most common chronic inflammatory demyelinating disease of the CNS, affecting approximately 2.8 million people worldwide (Atlas of MS, 3rd edition 2020). Prevalence follows a striking latitude gradient — increasing with distance from the equator — with the highest rates in Northern Europe, North America, and Australasia (>100/100,000). In Southeast Asia, including Thailand, prevalence is lower (~5–20/100,000) but rising with improved diagnostic awareness.

Key Epidemiological Features of MS
FeatureDetails
Peak onset age20–40 years (can occur at any age; pediatric MS ~3–5% of cases)
Sex ratioF:M ≈ 3:1 (increasing over time, suggesting environmental factors)
Genetic riskHLA-DRB1*15:01 strongest (OR ~3); >200 non-HLA susceptibility variants identified
EBVStrongest environmental risk factor; HR 32.4 for MS after EBV seroconversion (Bjornevik et al., Science 2022); virtually 100% EBV seropositive in MS
Vitamin DLow 25(OH)D associated with ↑ MS risk and relapse rate; explains latitude gradient partially
Smoking1.5–1.8× increased risk; accelerates progression; dose–response relationship
ObesityAdolescent obesity (BMI >30) doubles MS risk, especially in females
Other factorsNight-shift work, organic solvents, gut microbiome dysbiosis under investigation
Clinical Pearl — EBV & MS: The landmark longitudinal study of 10 million US military personnel (Bjornevik et al., Science 2022) demonstrated that EBV infection preceded MS onset in virtually all cases, with a 32-fold increased risk after seroconversion. Other herpesviruses showed no association. This supports the hypothesis that EBV is necessary but not sufficient for MS development — additional genetic (HLA) and environmental triggers are required.

6.1.2 Pathogenesis

MS pathogenesis involves two complementary mechanisms:

A. Relapsing Focal Inflammation (Acute Relapses)

  • Peripheral activation: Autoreactive CD4+ T-cells (Th1/Th17) are activated in the periphery, likely by molecular mimicry (EBV peptides cross-reacting with myelin antigens)
  • BBB transmigration: Activated T-cells cross the blood–brain barrier via adhesion molecules — VLA-4/VCAM-1 and LFA-1/ICAM-1 interactions (therapeutic target of natalizumab)
  • CNS immune cascade: Reactivated T-cells recruit macrophages, B-cells, and plasma cells → release pro-inflammatory cytokines (IFN-γ, IL-17, TNF-α) → demyelination and axonal injury
  • B-cell involvement: Intrathecal B-cells produce oligoclonal IgG bands (OCBs); B-cells serve as APCs and cytokine sources (target of anti-CD20 therapies)
  • Complement activation & antibody-mediated damage in active lesions

B. Smouldering / Compartmentalized Inflammation (Progression)

  • Microglial activation: Chronic microglia/macrophage activation at lesion edges → slowly expanding lesions (SELs) visible as paramagnetic rim lesions (PRLs) on susceptibility-weighted MRI
  • Meningeal inflammation: Tertiary lymphoid follicle-like structures in meninges (especially in SPMS) → subpial cortical demyelination
  • Oxidative stress: Mitochondrial dysfunction, iron accumulation, virtual hypoxia in chronically demyelinated axons → neurodegeneration independent of acute inflammation
  • Failure of remyelination: Oligodendrocyte precursor cells (OPCs) present but fail to differentiate → persistent naked axons vulnerable to degeneration
High-Yield: MS Pathogenesis Summary

Relapses = focal inflammatory demyelination (T-cell/B-cell driven, BBB breach, Gd-enhancing lesions on MRI)
Progression = smouldering neurodegeneration (microglia/iron-rim lesions, meningeal inflammation, brain atrophy) — occurs INDEPENDENT of relapses and begins early in the disease course. This dual-mechanism model explains why anti-inflammatory DMTs reduce relapses but have limited impact on progression in advanced disease.

6.1.3 Clinical Features & Phenotypes

MS Phenotype Classification (Lublin 2014 / Updated)

MS Clinical Phenotypes
PhenotypeFrequencyKey Features
Radiologically Isolated Syndrome (RIS)~5% of incidental MRI findingsIncidental MRI lesions meeting DIS criteria without clinical symptoms; ~30–50% convert to CIS/MS within 5–10 years. Now can be diagnosed as MS under McDonald 2024 if criteria met.
Clinically Isolated Syndrome (CIS)First presentationFirst clinical demyelinating event (ON, myelitis, brainstem syndrome); monophasic at presentation. Conversion to MS: ~60–80% if brain MRI abnormal, ~20% if normal.
Relapsing-Remitting MS (RRMS)~85% at onsetDiscrete relapses with full or partial recovery; no progression between relapses. Median age onset 30 years.
Secondary Progressive MS (SPMS)~50% of RRMS convert by 15–20 yearsGradual neurological decline independent of relapses; may have superimposed relapses (active SPMS) or not (non-active SPMS). Retrospective diagnosis — no single biomarker.
Primary Progressive MS (PPMS)10–15%Progressive accumulation from onset without initial relapses; equal sex ratio; older onset (~40–50 years); spinal cord predominant; fewer Gd-enhancing lesions.

Common Clinical Presentations

MS Clinical Syndromes
SyndromeClinical FeaturesNotes
Optic NeuritisUnilateral eye pain (worse with eye movement), subacute visual loss, RAPD, dyschromatopsia, central scotomaMost common initial presentation (~25%); typically recovers well
Transverse MyelitisAsymmetric/partial myelopathy, Lhermitte sign, band-like sensory level, UMN signs below level, bladder dysfunctionMS myelitis: short segment (<3 vertebral segments), peripheral/dorsal cord, partial cross-section (vs NMOSD LETM)
Brainstem/CerebellarINO (MLF syndrome), diplopia, vertigo, trigeminal neuralgia, facial palsy, ataxia, nystagmusINO in young patient highly suggestive of MS
SensoryNumbness, tingling, Lhermitte sign (electric sensation down spine on neck flexion), neuropathic painMost common symptom category overall
MotorSpastic paraparesis, monoparesis, UMN signsRarely pure motor at onset
CognitiveProcessing speed ↓, memory retrieval ↓, executive dysfunction; affects 40–65% of MS patientsScreen with SDMT (Symbol Digit Modalities Test) or BICAMS; can occur early
FatiguePresent in >80%; most disabling symptom reported by patientsCentral (CNS-driven) + peripheral components; multifactorial
Clinical Pearl — Uhthoff Phenomenon: Transient worsening of neurological symptoms with body temperature elevation (exercise, hot bath, fever). Caused by temperature-dependent conduction block in demyelinated axons. Pathognomonic for demyelination but NOT a relapse — symptoms resolve with cooling. Important to distinguish from true relapse for treatment decisions.

6.1.4 Diagnostic Criteria: McDonald 2017 → 2024

The McDonald criteria remain the gold standard for MS diagnosis, enabling earlier diagnosis by combining clinical, MRI, and laboratory evidence. The 2024 revision represents a paradigm shift with 10 major updates that improve sensitivity while maintaining specificity.

Fundamental Diagnostic Principle

High-Yield: MS Diagnosis Core Concept

MS = Dissemination in Space (DIS) + Dissemination in Time (DIT) − Other Diseases
Simplified: ≥2 attacks affecting ≥2 different CNS areas, with exclusion of mimics
McDonald criteria allow MRI and CSF to substitute for clinical evidence of DIS/DIT.

McDonald 2017 Framework (Baseline Reference)

McDonald 2017 Criteria — Clinical Scenarios
Clinical PresentationAdditional Data Needed for MS Diagnosis
≥2 attacks + objective evidence of ≥2 lesionsNone (DIS + DIT met clinically)
≥2 attacks + objective evidence of 1 lesionDIS: ≥1 T2 lesion in ≥2 of 4 MS-typical regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) OR await further attack
1 attack + objective evidence of ≥2 lesionsDIT: simultaneous Gd+ and Gd− lesions OR new T2/Gd+ on follow-up OR CSF OCBs
1 attack + objective evidence of 1 lesion (CIS)DIS + DIT as above
Insidious progression (suspected PPMS)≥1 year progression + 2 of 3: ≥1 periventricular/cortical/juxtacortical T2 lesion, ≥2 spinal cord T2 lesions, CSF OCBs

McDonald 2024: The 10 Key Updates

McDonald 2024 — Revision Committee (Lancet Neurol 2025): International panel revision incorporating advances in MRI technology, biomarkers, and understanding of MS across age groups and phenotypes.
McDonald 2024 — Ten Major Updates
#UpdateDetailsClinical Impact
1 Optic nerve as 5th DIS topography Optic nerve added to DIS locations: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord, optic nerve. Evidence: clinical ON, RAPD, VEP abnormality, or MRI optic nerve lesion. Patients with ON + 1 other lesion location now meet DIS (previously needed ≥2 of only 4 regions)
2 DIS requires ≥2 of 5 topographies Threshold unchanged at ≥2 regions but now from 5 (not 4) possible topographies Additional optic nerve pathway increases DIS sensitivity
3 DIT no longer always required If DIS met + CSF positive (OCBs or kFLC) → MS diagnosis without DIT. Rationale: CSF+ indicates chronic intrathecal inflammation serving as biological surrogate for DIT. Major change — enables diagnosis at first attack (CIS) with DIS + CSF+ without waiting for new MRI activity. Accelerates treatment initiation.
4 Central Vein Sign (CVS) — Select-6 CVS on T2*-weighted/FLAIR* MRI: ≥3 perivenular lesions among 6 selected lesions (Select-6 method) can substitute for DIS. Sensitivity 59–89%, specificity 75–98%. Distinguishes MS from mimics (migraine, NMOSD, vascular) where CVS prevalence is low (<40%)
5 Paramagnetic Rim Lesions (PRL) ≥1 PRL on susceptibility-weighted imaging → contributes to DIS and/or supports MS diagnosis. PRLs represent chronic active lesions with iron-laden microglia. Specificity ~98% for MS. Identifies smouldering inflammation; PRLs rare in NMOSD/MOGAD. Promising biomarker for progression.
6 Kappa Free Light Chain (kFLC) index kFLC index >6.1 accepted as alternative to OCBs for CSF+ determination. Quantitative, cheaper, faster, easier to standardize than isoelectric focusing for OCBs. CSF+ = OCBs (pattern 2/3) OR kFLC index >6.1. Improves access in labs without OCB capability.
7 RIS can now be MS Radiologically Isolated Syndrome meeting DIS + DIT or DIS + CSF+ can be diagnosed as MS even without clinical symptoms. Enables earlier treatment of high-risk RIS (previously had to wait for clinical event)
8 Unified pediatric/adult criteria Same criteria apply to all ages (previously pediatric MS required different approach, especially for ADEM distinction in children <11 years) Simplifies pediatric MS diagnosis; reduces diagnostic delay in children
9 Unified relapsing/progressive framework PPMS and relapsing MS now share a unified diagnostic framework. PPMS criteria: ≥1 year progression + DIS (≥2 of 5 topographies or CVS) + CSF+ or DIT. Recognizes MS as a single disease spectrum rather than distinct subtypes
10 Stricter criteria for age ≥50 For patients aged ≥50 at first presentation: mandatory CSF+ (OCBs or kFLC) regardless of MRI findings, due to higher prevalence of vascular/degenerative mimics. Reduces misdiagnosis in older adults where small vessel disease and other mimics are common
Clinical Pearl — DIT No Longer Required (Update #3): This is perhaps the most practice-changing update. Previously, a patient presenting with CIS and MRI showing DIS still needed either (a) simultaneous Gd+/Gd− lesions, (b) new lesions on follow-up MRI, or (c) CSF OCBs to demonstrate DIT. Under McDonald 2024, DIS + CSF+ = MS without needing DIT at all. This means a patient with a first clinical event, lesions in ≥2/5 topographies, and positive OCBs or kFLC >6.1 can be diagnosed and treated immediately.

McDonald 2017 vs 2024: Side-by-Side Comparison

Key Differences: McDonald 2017 → 2024
FeatureMcDonald 2017McDonald 2024
DIS topographies4 (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord)5 (+ optic nerve)
DIT requirementAlways required (clinical, MRI, or OCBs as surrogate)Not always required — DIS + CSF+ suffices
CSF+ definitionOCBs only (IgG, pattern 2/3)OCBs OR kFLC index >6.1
CVSNot includedSelect-6 method: ≥3/6 perivenular → supports DIS
PRLNot included≥1 PRL on SWI → contributes to DIS/diagnosis
RISCannot be diagnosed as MSCan be diagnosed as MS (DIS + DIT or DIS + CSF+)
Pediatric criteriaSeparate considerationsUnified with adult criteria
Progressive MSSeparate PPMS criteriaUnified framework with relapsing MS
Age ≥50Same criteria for all agesMandatory CSF+ for ≥50 years
Optic nerveNot a DIS region5th DIS topography (clinical, VEP, or MRI)

6.1.5 MRI Features in MS

MS MRI Characteristics
FeatureDescriptionSignificance
Periventricular lesionsOvoid, perpendicular to ventricles (Dawson fingers) on sagittal FLAIRMost specific topography for MS; reflect perivenular inflammation
Cortical/juxtacorticalLesions touching or within cortex; U-fiber lesionsDIR (double inversion recovery) most sensitive for cortical lesions
InfratentorialBrainstem (pons, medulla, midbrain), cerebellar pedunclesCommon in MS; help distinguish from vascular disease
Spinal cordShort-segment (<3 vertebral segments), dorsolateral/peripheral, partial cross-sectionDistinguish from NMOSD (LETM ≥3 segments, central cord)
Optic nerveT2/STIR hyperintensity, Gd enhancement in acute ON; typically unilateral, anterior segmentNew DIS region in McDonald 2024
Gadolinium enhancementOpen-ring enhancement pattern; homogeneous or incomplete ringActive inflammation; enhances for ~2–8 weeks. Open ring ≈ MS; closed ring → consider abscess/tumor
Central Vein Sign (CVS)Central venule within white matter lesion on T2*/FLAIR*~85% of MS lesions have CVS vs <40% in mimics. Select-6: ≥3/6 perivenular → supports MS
Paramagnetic Rim Lesion (PRL)Dark rim on phase/SWI; represents iron-laden microglia at lesion edgeChronic active lesion; specificity ~98% for MS; marker of smouldering inflammation. ≥1 PRL supports MS in McDonald 2024
T1 black holesHypointense on T1 (chronic lesions)Reflect severe tissue destruction, axonal loss; correlate with disability
Brain atrophyGlobal and regional volume loss; >0.4%/year pathologicalCorrelates with cognitive decline and progression; measured by BPF (brain parenchymal fraction)
High-Yield: MRI Clues — MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD

MS: Ovoid perpendicular periventricular (Dawson fingers), short-segment cord lesions, CVS+, PRL+
NMOSD: LETM (≥3 segments, central cord, H-sign on axial), area postrema lesion, non-specific brain lesions or normal, no Dawson fingers
MOGAD: Fluffy/ill-defined bilateral ON, ADEM-like large fluffy brain lesions, conus/lower cord LETM, cortical encephalitis (FLAIR cortical signal)

6.1.6 CSF Analysis & Biomarkers

CSF & Serum Biomarkers in MS
BiomarkerMethod/ThresholdInterpretation
Oligoclonal Bands (OCBs)Isoelectric focusing + immunofixation; Pattern 2 (CSF only) or Pattern 3 (CSF > serum)Positive in ~95% of MS; indicates intrathecal IgG synthesis. Rare in NMOSD (<20–25%), variable in MOGAD.
Kappa Free Light Chains (kFLC) indexkFLC index = (CSF kFLC / serum kFLC) ÷ (CSF albumin / serum albumin); >6.1 = positiveAlternative to OCBs in McDonald 2024. Quantitative, reproducible, lower cost. Sensitivity comparable to OCBs (~90–95%).
IgG index(CSF IgG / serum IgG) ÷ (CSF albumin / serum albumin); >0.7 positiveLess sensitive than OCBs (~70–80%); not included in McDonald criteria but supportive
MRZ reactionIntrathecal antibody response to Measles, Rubella, Varicella-Zoster; ≥2 positive = MRZR+Positive in ~67% MS; absent in NMOSD. Highly specific polyspecific intrathecal immune response.
Neurofilament Light Chain (NfL)Serum or CSF; no universal threshold (age-adjusted)Marker of axonal injury; elevated during relapses and in progressive MS. Useful for monitoring treatment response; lower = less neuroaxonal damage. Elevated in many neurological diseases (not MS-specific).
GFAPSerum or CSFMarker of astrocytic damage; elevated in NMOSD (astrocytopathy) >> MS. Rising interest as progression biomarker in MS. Markedly elevated in NMOSD attacks (reflects astrocyte destruction).
AQP4-IgGCell-based assay (CBA) preferred; sensitivity >80%, specificity >99%Must be negative to diagnose MS. If positive → NMOSD.
MOG-IgGLive CBA; IgG1 isotypeMust be negative to diagnose MS. If positive → MOGAD.
Clinical Pearl — When to Test AQP4/MOG Antibodies: Test for AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG in any patient with: (1) LETM (≥3 segments), (2) severe/bilateral/recurrent optic neuritis, (3) area postrema syndrome, (4) periependymal brain lesions, (5) atypical MS features (OCB−, poor recovery), (6) coexisting autoimmune disease (SLE, Sjögren's). Do NOT routinely test in typical MS with classic Dawson fingers and OCB+.

6.1.7 Disease-Modifying Therapies (DMTs) — Complete Landscape

Over 20 DMTs are now approved for MS, representing one of the richest therapeutic landscapes in neurology. The choice of DMT depends on disease activity, phenotype, patient factors (age, sex, comorbidities, family planning), JCV status, and treatment strategy (escalation vs induction).

Platform / Moderate-Efficacy DMTs

Moderate-Efficacy DMTs
DrugMechanismRoute/FrequencyARR ReductionKey Adverse Effects
Interferon β-1a (Avonex, Rebif)Immunomodulatory (↓Th17, ↑Treg, ↓BBB permeability)IM weekly (Avonex) / SC 3×/week (Rebif)~30%Flu-like symptoms, injection site reactions, hepatotoxicity, depression, lymphopenia
Interferon β-1b (Betaferon)Same as aboveSC every other day~30%Same as above; neutralizing antibodies may reduce efficacy
Peginterferon β-1a (Plegridy)Pegylated IFN-βSC every 2 weeks~36%Same class effects; less frequent dosing improves adherence
Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone)Shifts Th1→Th2; myelin decoySC daily or 3×/week (40mg)~30%Injection site reactions, lipoatrophy, immediate post-injection reaction (flushing, chest tightness — benign)
Teriflunomide (Aubagio)DHODH inhibitor → ↓ proliferating lymphocytesOral daily (14mg)~30–36%Hair thinning, diarrhea, hepatotoxicity, teratogenic (pregnancy category X — requires accelerated elimination with cholestyramine/charcoal before conception)
Dimethyl fumarate (DMF) (Tecfidera)Nrf2 activation → antioxidant + anti-inflammatory; ↓ NFκBOral BID (240mg)~44–53%Flushing, GI (nausea, diarrhea), lymphopenia (monitor ALC — risk of PML if severe), hepatotoxicity
Diroximel fumarate (Vumerity)Same as DMF (active metabolite monomethyl fumarate)Oral BIDSimilar to DMFBetter GI tolerability than DMF; same lymphopenia risk

High-Efficacy DMTs

High-Efficacy DMTs
DrugMechanismRoute/FrequencyARR ReductionKey Adverse Effects & Monitoring
Fingolimod (Gilenya)S1P receptor modulator → lymphocyte sequestration in lymph nodesOral daily (0.5mg)~54%First-dose bradycardia (6-hour monitoring), macular edema, ↑ infections (VZV/HSV — check VZV immunity), hepatotoxicity, skin cancers, rebound on discontinuation
Siponimod (Mayzent)Selective S1P1/S1P5 modulatorOral daily (2mg maintenance)SPMS: ↓ 3-month CDP by 21% (EXPAND trial)Similar to fingolimod; CYP2C9 genotype required (avoid *3/*3); approved for active SPMS
Ozanimod (Zeposia)Selective S1P1/S1P5Oral daily (0.92mg)~38–48%Similar class; less first-dose effect; dose titration over 7 days
Ponesimod (Ponvory)Selective S1P1Oral daily (20mg)Superior to teriflunomide (~30.5% relative reduction)Similar class; rapid lymphocyte recovery on cessation (~1 week)
Natalizumab (Tysabri)Anti-α4-integrin (VLA-4) → blocks lymphocyte migration across BBBIV q4wk or SC q4wk~68%PML risk — stratify by: JCV Ab status, JCV index, prior immunosuppression, duration >2 years. JCV Ab−: risk ~0.1/1000; JCV Ab+ index >1.5 + prior IS + >2yr: risk up to 11/1000. Extended interval dosing (EID, q6wk) may ↓ PML risk. Rebound on discontinuation.
Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus)Anti-CD20 (humanized) → B-cell depletionIV q6mo (initial: 300mg ×2 two weeks apart; then 600mg q6mo)~46–47% (OPERA I/II); PPMS: ↓ 12-wk CDP by 24% (ORATORIO)Infusion reactions, ↑ infections (screen HBV), hypogammaglobulinemia (long-term IgG monitoring), only DMT approved for PPMS
Ofatumumab (Kesimpta)Anti-CD20 (fully human) → B-cell depletionSC monthly (20mg; initial: day 0, 7, 14 then monthly)~50–58% (ASCLEPIOS I/II)Injection site reactions, similar infection/Ig monitoring as ocrelizumab; self-administered at home
Ublituximab (Briumvi)Anti-CD20 (glycoengineered) → enhanced ADCCIV q6mo (1-hour infusion after initial)~55–59% (ULTIMATE I/II)Similar to other anti-CD20s; shorter infusion time advantage
Alemtuzumab (Lemtrada)Anti-CD52 → pan-lymphocyte depletion (IRT)IV 5 days Y1, 3 days Y2 (±additional courses)~49–55% (CARE-MS I/II)Secondary autoimmunity: thyroid (40%), ITP (3%), anti-GBM disease (0.3%); monthly blood/urine monitoring for 4 years post-last dose. Infections (VZV prophylaxis). Reserved for highly active MS.
Cladribine (Mavenclad)Purine analogue (IRT) → selective lymphocyte depletion (preferential B-cell/T-cell reduction)Oral: 2 short courses in Y1 + Y2 (cumulative 3.5mg/kg); then treatment-free~55–58% (CLARITY)Lymphopenia (nadir ~2–3 months, recovery by ~9 months), herpes reactivation (consider prophylaxis if Grade 4), malignancy concern (theoretical, not confirmed in extensions)
MitoxantroneTopoisomerase II inhibitor → immunosuppressionIV q3mo (max lifetime dose 140 mg/m²)~67%Cardiotoxicity (cardiomyopathy — LVEF monitoring), therapy-related AML (~0.8%); rarely used now due to toxicity; legacy agent

Pipeline / Emerging Therapies

Emerging MS Therapies
AgentMechanismStatusNotes
TolebrutinibCNS-penetrant BTK inhibitor → targets microglia + B-cells in CNSPhase 3 (HERCULES, PERSEUS, GEMINI)First DMT potentially targeting smouldering inflammation; hepatotoxicity signal in trials
FenebrutinibNon-covalent BTK inhibitorPhase 3 (FENtrepid, FENhance)Highly selective; targets both relapsing and progressive MS
EvobrutinibBTK inhibitorPhase 3 (evolutionRMS)Did not meet primary endpoint in RRMS; development uncertain
FrexalimabAnti-CD40L → blocks T-cell co-stimulationPhase 3Near-complete suppression of MRI activity in Phase 2
Remyelination therapiesVarious: anti-LINGO-1 (opicinumab), clemastine, bexaroteneEarly phaseAiming to repair rather than prevent damage; unmet need in progressive MS
High-Yield: BTK Inhibitors — The Next Frontier

Bruton's tyrosine kinase (BTK) inhibitors are unique because they cross the BBB and can target both B-cells and microglia/macrophages within the CNS compartment. This makes them the first DMT class with potential to address smouldering/compartmentalized inflammation driving progression — a mechanism not reached by current anti-CD20 antibodies (which deplete peripheral B-cells but do not cross the BBB). If successful, BTKi could be transformative for progressive MS.

6.1.8 Treatment Strategy

Escalation vs Induction vs Early High-Efficacy

MS Treatment Strategies
StrategyApproachWhen to Consider
EscalationStart moderate-efficacy DMT (IFN-β, GA, DMF, teriflunomide) → switch to high-efficacy if breakthroughLow disease activity, risk-averse patient, older age, mild CIS
Early High-Efficacy (EHT)Start with high-efficacy DMT (natalizumab, anti-CD20, S1P modulators) from diagnosisYoung patient with active disease, ≥2 relapses/year, Gd+ lesions, high T2 lesion load, poor prognostic factors, spinal cord lesions. Increasing evidence favors EHT: DELIVER-MS, TREAT-MS trials show benefit.
Immune Reconstitution Therapy (IRT)Short-course treatment (alemtuzumab, cladribine) aiming for durable immune reset → treatment-free remissionHighly active MS, young patients wanting treatment-free periods, family planning (after washout)
AHSCTAutologous Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation — myeloablative/non-myeloablative conditioning → immune resetHighly active relapsing MS, young (<40–50), short disease duration (<10 years), inflammatory phenotype. MIST, BEAT-MS trials show superiority over DMTs. TRM ~0.3% in experienced centers.
Clinical Pearl — Treat-to-Target in MS: Modern MS management increasingly adopts a treat-to-target approach — the target being NEDA (No Evidence of Disease Activity): (1) no relapses, (2) no new/enlarging T2 lesions, (3) no Gd-enhancing lesions, (4) no brain atrophy progression (NEDA-4). While NEDA is aspirational and difficult to maintain long-term, it guides escalation decisions. Increasingly, serum NfL is used as a fifth dimension of monitoring ("NEDA-5") — persistently elevated NfL despite DMT suggests subclinical disease activity.

6.1.9 Acute Relapse Management

Acute MS Relapse Treatment
TreatmentRegimenNotes
IV Methylprednisolone (IVMP)1 g/day × 3–5 days (no taper usually needed)First-line for moderate-severe relapses. Shortens relapse duration but does not change final recovery. Check glucose, blood pressure. PPI cover if GI risk.
Oral high-dose steroidsOral methylprednisolone 500–1250 mg/day × 3–5 days OR bioequivalent prednisoneCOPOUSEP trial: oral methylprednisolone non-inferior to IV. Alternative when IV access difficult.
Plasma Exchange (PLEX)5–7 exchanges over 10–14 daysSecond-line for steroid-refractory severe relapses (especially myelitis, severe ON, brainstem). Start within 2–4 weeks of relapse onset for best response. ~40–50% of steroid-non-responders improve.
IVIG0.4 g/kg/day × 5 daysThird-line; less evidence than PLEX for steroid-refractory relapses. May be considered when PLEX unavailable or contraindicated.

Question: A 28-year-old woman presents with right eye pain and visual loss for 3 days. MRI brain shows a right optic nerve lesion with enhancement, plus 4 periventricular T2 lesions perpendicular to the ventricles and 1 cervical cord lesion. CSF shows positive OCBs. AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG are negative. Can she be diagnosed with MS by McDonald 2024?

Answer: Yes. By McDonald 2024:

  • DIS: Lesions in ≥2 of 5 topographies — optic nerve (clinical ON + MRI lesion), periventricular (4 lesions), spinal cord (cervical lesion) = 3/5 topographies → DIS met
  • DIT: Not strictly needed because DIS + CSF+ (OCBs positive) is sufficient for MS diagnosis under McDonald 2024 Update #3
  • Other diseases excluded: AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG negative, Dawson finger-like periventricular lesions typical for MS

Under McDonald 2017, she would also meet criteria (DIS in ≥2/4 regions + DIT via OCBs as surrogate). However, note the key 2024 improvement: the optic nerve now counts as a DIS region (Update #1), and DIT is no longer required when CSF+ (Update #3). She should be started on a DMT promptly — given young age and multi-regional disease, early high-efficacy therapy should be discussed.

6.1.10 Monitoring & Special Situations

Routine MS Monitoring

MS Follow-Up Monitoring Schedule
AssessmentFrequencyPurpose
Clinical visit + EDSSEvery 3–6 months (early), 6–12 months (stable)Detect relapses, progression, disability change
Brain MRI (with Gd)Baseline, 3–6 months after DMT start, then annuallyNew/enlarging T2 lesions, Gd+ activity → breakthrough disease
Spinal cord MRIBaseline, then if symptoms or surveillanceCord lesions correlate with disability; less routinely monitored than brain
OCT (retinal nerve fiber layer)Baseline, then annually or after ONSubclinical optic nerve axonal loss; RNFL thinning correlates with brain atrophy
Cognitive screening (SDMT)AnnuallyProcessing speed decline; ≥4-point drop clinically meaningful
Serum NfLEmerging — every 6–12 monthsSubclinical disease activity monitoring; guide treatment decisions
DMT-specific labsPer drug protocolCBC/LFTs (all), JCV Ab (natalizumab q6mo), lymphocyte count (fingolimod/DMF/cladribine), thyroid/CBC/urinalysis (alemtuzumab monthly ×4yr), immunoglobulins (anti-CD20 annually)

Pregnancy & MS

MS DMTs and Pregnancy
CategoryDMTsRecommendation
Contraindicated (teratogenic)Teriflunomide (Category X), fingolimod, siponimod, ozanimod, ponesimod, cladribine, mitoxantroneDiscontinue well before conception. Teriflunomide requires accelerated elimination (cholestyramine 8g TID × 11 days or activated charcoal 50g BID × 11 days) + serum level <0.02 mg/L confirmed ×2. S1P modulators: stop ≥2 months before; risk of rebound. Cladribine: ≥6 months washout.
Stop before conceptionDMF, diroximel fumarate, alemtuzumabStop DMF ≥1 month before conception. Alemtuzumab: wait ≥4 months after last infusion.
May continue / considerIFN-β, glatiramer acetateLow risk in pregnancy; glatiramer acetate generally considered safest. Can continue until positive pregnancy test or throughout if high relapse risk.
Special considerationNatalizumab, anti-CD20 (ocrelizumab, ofatumumab)Natalizumab: may continue through pregnancy in highly active MS (risk of neonatal hematological effects in 3rd trimester vs severe rebound). Anti-CD20: stop ≥6 months before conception (B-cell repopulation); exposed pregnancies generally show good outcomes. Plan last infusion timing strategically.
Clinical Pearl — Pregnancy & MS Course: Relapse rate decreases during pregnancy (especially 3rd trimester — natural immunotolerance) but increases in the first 3–6 months postpartum (return to 70% of pre-pregnancy rate or higher). Exclusive breastfeeding may be protective. Strategy: in highly active MS, consider "bridging" with natalizumab during pregnancy or timing last anti-CD20 dose ~6 months before planned conception so B-cells repopulate during pregnancy with residual immunosuppressive effect. Resume DMT promptly postpartum, especially in active disease — breastfeeding is compatible with many DMTs (GA, IFN-β, and increasingly anti-CD20 considered acceptable).

6.2 Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD)

6.2.1 Epidemiology & Risk Factors

NMOSD Epidemiology
FeatureDetails
Prevalence~1.54/100,000 (varies by ethnicity — higher in non-White populations: African, Asian, Latin American)
Incidence~0.4–4.4/million/year
Peak onset age~40 years (later than MS); range 18–70; rare in children
Sex ratioF:M ≈ 9:1 (one of the strongest female predominance in autoimmune disease)
Genetic riskHLA-DRB1*03:01 strongest association; non-HLA variants near complement genes
EthnicityHigher prevalence in non-White populations; in Asia, NMO/NMOSD was previously classified as "opticospinal MS"
Autoimmune associationsSLE, Sjögren syndrome, myasthenia gravis, autoimmune thyroid disease — screen for coexisting autoimmunity

6.2.2 Pathogenesis

NMOSD is an autoimmune astrocytopathy — fundamentally different from MS in mechanism. The target is the astrocyte, not the oligodendrocyte.

Pathogenic Cascade

  1. Peripheral B-cell activation: Autoreactive B-cells (likely triggered by molecular mimicry — AQP4 shares homology with Clostridium perfringens ABC-TP protein) differentiate into plasmablasts producing AQP4-IgG
  2. AQP4-IgG crosses BBB: IgG1 subclass; crosses disrupted or permeable BBB (especially at circumventricular organs — area postrema, which lacks BBB)
  3. Binding to AQP4: AQP4-IgG binds aquaporin-4 (AQP4) water channels on astrocyte foot processes surrounding blood vessels → AQP4 internalization
  4. Complement activation: AQP4-IgG activates classical complement cascade → C3b deposition → membrane attack complex (MAC) C5b-9 formation → astrocyte lysis (complement-dependent cytotoxicity, CDC)
  5. Antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity (ADCC): AQP4-IgG also recruits NK cells and macrophages → ADCC pathway → additional astrocyte killing
  6. Secondary demyelination: Astrocyte destruction → loss of trophic support to oligodendrocytes → secondary demyelination and axonal injury (NOT primary demyelination as in MS)
  7. Inflammatory infiltrate: Neutrophils and eosinophils prominent (vs macrophages/T-cells in MS) → tissue necrosis, cavitation

Key Cytokine: IL-6

IL-6 is the central cytokine in NMOSD pathogenesis:

  • Promotes B-cell differentiation → plasmablast → AQP4-IgG production
  • Increases BBB permeability
  • Drives inflammation via JAK-STAT pathway
  • CSF IL-6 levels correlate with disease activity
  • Therapeutic target: satralizumab (anti-IL-6R)
High-Yield: MS vs NMOSD Pathogenesis — Fundamental Difference

MS: T-cell/B-cell → primary demyelination (oligodendrocyte injury) → OCBs in CSF → macrophage-predominant infiltrate
NMOSD: AQP4-IgG → primary astrocytopathy (astrocyte destruction via complement/ADCC) → secondary demyelination → neutrophil/eosinophil-predominant infiltrate → tissue necrosis

This explains why: (1) NMOSD attacks are more severe/destructive with poor recovery, (2) MS DMTs (IFN-β, natalizumab, fingolimod) are ineffective and may worsen NMOSD, (3) complement inhibition (eculizumab) works in NMOSD but not MS.

6.2.3 Diagnostic Criteria: IPND 2015 (Wingerchuk)

International Panel for NMO Diagnosis (IPND) 2015 — Wingerchuk et al., Neurology 2015: Unified criteria replacing the 2006 NMO criteria. Introduced the spectrum concept (NMOSD) and provided separate criteria for AQP4-IgG-positive and AQP4-IgG-negative patients.

Six Core Clinical Characteristics

NMOSD Core Clinical Characteristics (IPND 2015)
#Core CharacteristicFrequencyKey Features
1 Optic neuritis (ON) 45–60% Severe, bilateral (simultaneous or sequential), poor recovery (vs MS ON: unilateral, good recovery). Posterior/chiasmal involvement more common than MS (anterior). RAPD, pain, severe visual loss (often <20/200).
2 Acute myelitis ~85% (most common) LETM (longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis, ≥3 vertebral segments). Central cord involvement, complete transection pattern. Severe motor/sensory deficits, bladder dysfunction. May cause painful tonic spasms.
3 Area postrema syndrome (APS) 15–40% Intractable nausea, vomiting, and/or hiccups lasting days–weeks. May be the presenting symptom of NMOSD in ~7–15%. Area postrema (dorsal medulla at floor of 4th ventricle) is a circumventricular organ — rich in AQP4, lacks BBB. Highly specific for NMOSD.
4 Acute brainstem syndrome Variable Includes: dorsal medulla lesion (area postrema), periependymal brainstem lesion, intractable hiccups/nausea, INO, facial palsy, hearing loss, trigeminal neuralgia. Not explained by area postrema alone.
5 Acute diencephalic syndrome ~3.4% Narcolepsy/hypersomnia, SIADH, hypothermia, obesity, endocrine dysfunction from hypothalamic/thalamic lesion. Rare but characteristic.
6 Symptomatic cerebral syndrome Rare Large confluent/tumefactive hemispheric white matter lesions (ADEM-like or periependymal). Corpus callosum involvement (Marburg-like pattern). More common in children with NMOSD.

IPND 2015 Diagnostic Criteria

NMOSD Diagnostic Criteria — IPND 2015
AQP4-IgG StatusCriteria
AQP4-IgG POSITIVE
  1. At least 1 core clinical characteristic
  2. Positive AQP4-IgG (cell-based assay preferred)
  3. Exclusion of alternative diagnoses
AQP4-IgG NEGATIVE or Unknown
  1. At least 2 core clinical characteristics from ≥2 different attacks AND all of:
    • At least 1 must be ON, myelitis with LETM, or APS
    • Dissemination in space (≥2 different core characteristics)
    • Additional MRI requirements met for each characteristic
  2. Negative AQP4-IgG or testing unavailable
  3. Exclusion of alternative diagnoses
Clinical Pearl — AQP4-IgG Testing: Cell-based assay (CBA) is mandatory — sensitivity >80%, specificity >99%. Older indirect immunofluorescence or ELISA methods have lower sensitivity (~60–70%) and may miss low-titer positives. Test during acute attack for highest sensitivity (titers fluctuate with immunotherapy). IgG isotype only (not IgM/IgA). Retest if initially negative in high clinical suspicion — seroconversion can occur. Always use live CBA or fixed CBA with M23 AQP4 isoform.

6.2.4 NEMOS 2023 Diagnostic Recommendations

NEMOS (Neuromyelitis Optica Study Group) 2023 — J Neurol: Practical diagnostic recommendations based on IPND 2015, with structured approach (Box 1–5) for clinical application.
NEMOS 2023 Diagnostic Framework
BoxContentKey Points
Box 1Core clinical characteristicsSame 6 as IPND 2015 (ON, myelitis, APS, brainstem, diencephalic, cerebral) with detailed MRI criteria for each
Box 2MRI findings for each core characteristicON: long segment (>½ optic nerve), chiasmal involvement; Myelitis: LETM ≥3 segments, central cord, H-sign axial; APS: dorsal medulla/area postrema lesion; Brain: periependymal pattern
Box 3Differential diagnosesMS, MOGAD, sarcoidosis, CNS lymphoma, SLE cerebritis, Sjögren's CNS, infections (TB, syphilis, HIV), spinal AVF, B12 deficiency, paraneoplastic
Box 4Red flags against NMOSDProgressive course without attacks, Dawson finger lesions, short cord lesions, cortical lesions (→ think MS/MOGAD), CSF OCBs positive (→ MS), male sex (less against but weaker), onset <12 years
Box 5MOGAD considerationAlways test MOG-IgG when AQP4-IgG negative; MOGAD has different phenotype and prognosis

6.2.5 Investigations

NMOSD Investigations
InvestigationFindings in NMOSDSignificance
AQP4-IgG (CBA)Positive in ~75–80% of NMOSD patientsHighly specific (>99%); diagnostic cornerstone. CBA mandatory (ELISA/IIF insufficient).
MOG-IgG (CBA)Negative (by definition in AQP4+ NMOSD)Must test to exclude MOGAD if AQP4-IgG negative. Double-positive (AQP4+ MOG+) is exceedingly rare and should raise concern for false positive.
CSF cell countPleocytosis (often >50 WBC/μL) with neutrophil predominance during acute attacksHelps distinguish from MS (lymphocytic, usually <50 cells). Eosinophils may also be present.
CSF OCBsAbsent in 75–80%Major differentiator from MS (OCBs positive in ~95% of MS). If OCBs positive → strongly reconsider MS diagnosis.
CSF proteinOften elevated; elevated QAlb (albumin quotient) reflecting BBB disruptionHigher protein than typical MS
Serum GFAPMarkedly elevated during attacks (reflects astrocyte destruction)Higher in NMOSD than MS; emerging biomarker for attack severity and monitoring
Serum NfLElevated during attacksMarker of axonal injury; correlates with attack severity and disability
MRZ reactionAbsentPositive in ~67% MS, absent in NMOSD — useful differentiator when available
MRI spineLETM ≥3 vertebral segments; central cord (H-sign on axial T2); often thoracic; may show ring/patchy enhancement, cavitation in severe casesLETM is hallmark; but short myelitis can occur in relapses after treatment. Acute: may show cord swelling + Gd enhancement.
MRI brainNormal or non-specific; periependymal lesions around 3rd/4th ventricle; area postrema lesion; hypothalamic lesion; hemispheric ADEM-like. No Dawson fingers.~60% have brain MRI abnormalities but pattern differs from MS. Periependymal = "cloud-like" enhancement.
MRI optic nervePosterior/chiasmal involvement; long segment (>½ nerve length); bilateral; may show "bright-spot" lesion on T1+GdLonger segment and posterior location vs MS (anterior, shorter). Orbital fat-suppressed T2/STIR essential.
OCTSevere RNFL thinning after ON (often <50 μm); GCL lossGreater damage per ON episode than MS; reflects more destructive pathology. Poor visual outcome correlates with thin RNFL.
High-Yield: CSF Clues — MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD

MS: OCBs positive (~95%), lymphocytic mild pleocytosis (<50), normal-mildly elevated protein, MRZ reaction positive (~67%)
NMOSD: OCBs negative (~75–80%), neutrophilic pleocytosis (can be >50), elevated protein/QAlb, GFAP markedly elevated, MRZ negative
MOGAD: OCBs usually negative, lymphocytic/mixed pleocytosis, protein elevated, GFAP mildly elevated (less than NMOSD)

6.2.6 Treatment

A. Acute Attack Treatment

NMOSD Acute Attack Management
LineTreatmentRegimenNotes
1st lineIV Methylprednisolone1 g/day × 3–7 days (often 5 days; can extend to 7 if severe)Start ASAP after attack onset. Follow with oral taper (prednisone 1 mg/kg tapering over weeks) more commonly in NMOSD than MS. Longer courses often needed.
2nd line (early)Plasma Exchange (PLEX)5–7 exchanges over 10–14 daysUse early — some experts advocate PLEX concurrently with IVMP for severe attacks (myelitis with paraplegia, severe bilateral ON). Earlier initiation → better outcome. Removes pathogenic AQP4-IgG and complement factors.
AlternativeIVIG0.4 g/kg/day × 5 daysThird-line; when PLEX unavailable or contraindicated. May modulate complement. Less evidence than PLEX.
Clinical Pearl — PLEX Early in NMOSD: Unlike MS where PLEX is reserved for steroid-refractory cases, in NMOSD experts increasingly advocate early/concurrent PLEX with IVMP for severe attacks (LETM with paraplegia, bilateral ON). The rationale: NMOSD is antibody/complement-mediated, so directly removing the pathogenic antibody is mechanistically logical. Delay in PLEX initiation correlates with poorer recovery. Some centers start PLEX within 48–72 hours of attack onset alongside IVMP.

B. Preventive (Maintenance) Therapy

Critical principle: ALL patients with AQP4-IgG-positive NMOSD should receive long-term preventive therapy after a first attack. Relapses are typically severe and cumulative disability is stepwise (each attack adds permanent deficit). Unlike MS, there is no significant progression between attacks — disability is attack-related.

Standard Preventive DMTs
Standard NMOSD Preventive Therapies
DrugMechanismDoseMonitoringNotes
Azathioprine (AZA) Purine synthesis inhibitor → ↓ T/B lymphocytes 2–3 mg/kg/day CBC q1–3mo, LFTs, TPMT/NUDT15 genotype before starting Widely used, affordable. Onset 3–6 months; bridge with oral prednisone. TPMT/NUDT15 deficiency → severe myelosuppression.
Mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) IMPDH inhibitor → selective B/T-cell antiproliferative 2000–3000 mg/day (divided BID) CBC q1–3mo, LFTs, GI symptoms Effective alternative to AZA; may have faster onset. Teratogenic — contraception mandatory. GI side effects common.
Rituximab (RTX) Anti-CD20 → B-cell depletion Induction: 1000 mg × 2 (days 0, 14) or 375 mg/m² × 4 weekly; Maintenance: 1000 mg q6mo or CD19/CD20 monitoring-guided redosing CD19/CD20 count, immunoglobulins, HBV screening, infection surveillance Highly effective; most commonly used worldwide. Some advocate redosing based on CD19/CD27+ memory B-cell repopulation rather than fixed interval. Infusion reactions (premedicate). Long-term: hypogammaglobulinemia risk.
Targeted Biologic Therapies (Newer)
NMOSD Targeted Biologics with Trial Data
DrugMechanismRegimenPivotal TrialKey ResultsPractical Notes
Eculizumab (Soliris) Anti-C5 complement → blocks MAC (C5b-9) formation 900 mg IV q2wk × 4 doses → 1200 mg IV q2wk PREVENT (NEJM 2019) Relapse rate: 3% (eculizumab) vs 43% (placebo) over 48 weeks (HR 0.06). 97% relapse-free in treatment group. Extremely effective but extremely costly (~$500K/year). Requires meningococcal vaccination (MenACWY + MenB) ≥2 weeks before start — risk of meningococcal sepsis. Biweekly IV infusions. Can be used on background immunosuppression.
Ravulizumab (Ultomiris) Long-acting anti-C5 (engineered eculizumab) Weight-based IV loading → maintenance q8wk (vs q2wk for eculizumab) CHAMPION-NMOSD Pending full publication; designed to show non-inferiority to eculizumab with less frequent dosing Advantage: q8wk dosing vs q2wk; same meningococcal vaccination requirement
Satralizumab (Enspryng) Anti-IL-6 receptor (recycling antibody) 120 mg SC at weeks 0, 2, 4 → then q4wk SAkuraSky (NEJM 2019) & SAkuraStar (Lancet Neurol 2020) SAkuraSky (add-on): relapse rate 20% vs 43% (HR 0.38) in AQP4+. SAkuraStar (monotherapy): 22% vs 57% (HR 0.26) in AQP4+. Most benefit in AQP4+ patients; less clear benefit in AQP4−. Self-administered SC injection q4wk. Generally well-tolerated. Especially effective in AQP4+. Unique recycling antibody technology — longer half-life. Can be monotherapy or add-on.
Inebilizumab (Uplizna) Anti-CD19 → broader B-cell depletion than anti-CD20 (CD19 expressed on plasmablasts and some plasma cells that CD20 misses) 300 mg IV day 0, day 15 → then 300 mg IV q6mo N-MOmentum (Lancet 2019) Relapse rate: 12% vs 39% (HR 0.272) over 28 weeks. 87.6% remained attack-free (vs 56.6% placebo). Also reduced disability worsening. Advantage over rituximab: targets CD19 (broader B-lineage depletion including plasmablasts). Q6mo IV infusion. Infusion reactions; infection monitoring; HBV screening.
High-Yield: NMOSD Biologic Mechanisms — Three Distinct Pathways

The three FDA-approved biologics for NMOSD each target a different pathogenic mechanism:
1. Complement inhibition: Eculizumab/ravulizumab → blocks C5 → prevents MAC formation → stops complement-mediated astrocyte lysis
2. IL-6 blockade: Satralizumab → blocks IL-6 receptor → reduces plasmablast differentiation and AQP4-IgG production, ↓ BBB permeability
3. B-cell depletion: Inebilizumab → depletes CD19+ B-cells (broader than CD20: includes plasmablasts) → reduces AQP4-IgG production

All three showed dramatic efficacy in AQP4+ NMOSD. Choice depends on: access/cost, patient preference (SC vs IV, frequency), comorbidities, and whether monotherapy vs add-on is desired.

C. Drugs to AVOID in NMOSD

High-Yield: MS DMTs That WORSEN NMOSD

The following MS DMTs are contraindicated or potentially harmful in NMOSD:

  • Interferon-β: Increases relapse rate in NMOSD
  • Natalizumab: May worsen NMOSD (case reports of severe relapses)
  • Fingolimod: Reports of NMOSD exacerbation; does not prevent relapses in NMOSD
  • Alemtuzumab: Case reports of worsening; mechanism unclear
  • Dimethyl fumarate: Insufficient data; not recommended

This underscores the critical importance of distinguishing NMOSD from MS before starting treatment. Always check AQP4-IgG (and MOG-IgG) before initiating MS-specific DMTs in atypical presentations.

6.2.7 Prognosis & Disease Course

NMOSD Prognosis
FeatureDetails
Course~85–90% relapsing; ~5–10% monophasic. Progressive course is NOT a feature of NMOSD (red flag for MS).
Attack severityMore severe than MS; poor/incomplete recovery. Stepwise disability accumulation with each relapse.
Visual outcomeFunctional blindness (≤20/200) in ≥1 eye in ~50% by 5–10 years without treatment
Motor outcomeWheelchair dependence in ~30% by 5 years without treatment (due to myelitis attacks)
Mortality5-year mortality ~5–10% (improved with modern biologics); respiratory failure from cervical myelitis or area postrema-related complications
Predictors of poor outcomeOlder age at onset, African descent, many early relapses, poor recovery from first attack, high AQP4-IgG titer, coexisting autoimmune disease
With modern treatmentDramatically improved — PREVENT trial showed 97% relapse-free on eculizumab; early aggressive prevention critical

Question: A 45-year-old woman presents with intractable nausea, vomiting, and hiccups for 2 weeks. MRI brain shows a dorsal medullary lesion. She is found to be AQP4-IgG positive. What is the diagnosis and management?

Answer:

Diagnosis: NMOSD — Area Postrema Syndrome (Core Characteristic #3). She meets IPND 2015 criteria for AQP4-IgG positive NMOSD: 1 core clinical characteristic (APS) + positive AQP4-IgG + excludes other causes.

Acute management:

  • IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 5 days
  • Consider PLEX if refractory to steroids

Preventive therapy (start early):

  • Options: rituximab, satralizumab, eculizumab, inebilizumab, or azathioprine/MMF (depending on access and resources)
  • Even after a "mild" first attack like APS, prevention is critical because subsequent attacks (myelitis, ON) are typically more severe

Key point: APS (intractable nausea/vomiting/hiccups) is often the first and mildest attack in NMOSD. It is frequently misdiagnosed as GI disease, causing diagnostic delay of months to years. The dorsal medullary MRI lesion in a middle-aged woman with intractable vomiting should always trigger AQP4-IgG testing.

6.3 MOG Antibody-Associated Disease (MOGAD)

6.3.1 Overview & Epidemiology

MOGAD is a distinct inflammatory demyelinating disease of the CNS, separate from both MS and NMOSD. It is defined by seropositivity for MOG-IgG (myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies) detected by cell-based assay. MOGAD was only recognized as a distinct entity in the 2020s, previously overlapping with "seronegative NMOSD" or ADEM.

MOGAD Epidemiology
FeatureDetails
Prevalence~1.3–3.4/100,000 (comparable to NMOSD)
Age distributionBimodal — pediatric peak (ADEM phenotype, median ~5–7 years) and adult peak (ON phenotype, median ~30–40 years)
Sex ratioSlight male predominance in children (ADEM); slight female predominance in adults — overall less skewed than NMOSD (F:M ~1.2–2:1)
Course~50% monophasic (especially pediatric ADEM); ~50% relapsing (especially adult ON). More favorable prognosis overall than NMOSD.

6.3.2 Pathogenesis

MOG is a glycoprotein expressed exclusively on the outermost surface of the myelin sheath produced by oligodendrocytes in the CNS. Unlike AQP4 (astrocyte), MOG is on the myelin/oligodendrocyte surface — making MOGAD a true primary demyelinating disease.

MOGAD vs NMOSD vs MS Pathogenesis Comparison
FeatureMOGADNMOSDMS
TargetMOG on oligodendrocyte/myelin surfaceAQP4 on astrocyte foot processesMyelin/oligodendrocyte (T-cell mediated)
Primary pathologyDemyelination (oligodendrocyte injury)Astrocytopathy (astrocyte destruction)Demyelination + neurodegeneration
AntibodyMOG-IgG (IgG1)AQP4-IgG (IgG1)None specific (OCBs = polyclonal IgG)
Complement roleLimited complement deposition; less complement-mediated damage than NMOSDMajor: classical pathway → MAC → astrocyte lysisVariable complement involvement
PathologyPerivenous demyelination with relative axonal preservation; granulocytic infiltrateAstrocyte loss, secondary demyelination, necrosis, neutrophil/eosinophil infiltratePerivenous and confluent demyelination, axonal injury, microglia activation
RemyelinationGood remyelination potential (better than MS/NMOSD)Poor (astrocyte loss disrupts myelin repair)Variable; fails in progressive MS
Clinical Pearl — MOGAD Has Better Remyelination: Unlike MS and NMOSD, MOGAD pathology shows perivenous demyelination with relative axonal preservation and robust remyelination capacity. This explains the generally better recovery from individual attacks compared to NMOSD (where astrocyte necrosis causes irreversible damage) and may explain why many pediatric ADEM patients recover completely.

6.3.3 Diagnostic Criteria (2023 International Panel)

MOGAD Diagnostic Criteria — Banwell et al., Lancet Neurol 2023: First international consensus diagnostic criteria for MOGAD, requiring MOG-IgG positivity + compatible clinical phenotype + exclusion of alternatives.
MOGAD 2023 Diagnostic Criteria
CriterionRequirements
1. Core clinical presentation At least one of:
  • Optic neuritis
  • Myelitis
  • ADEM (acute disseminated encephalomyelitis)
  • Cerebral monofocal or polyfocal deficits
  • Brainstem/cerebellar presentation
  • Cerebral cortical encephalitis (often with seizures)
2. MOG-IgG positive Cell-based assay (CBA) mandatory — live CBA preferred; fixed CBA with full-length human MOG acceptable. IgG1 isotype only. Low-positive titers require compatible clinical + MRI features for diagnosis (false positives more common at low titers). Serum preferred; CSF MOG-IgG can support diagnosis if serum negative.
3. Exclusion of alternatives Rule out MS, NMOSD (AQP4+), infection, sarcoidosis, CNS lymphoma, CLIPPERS, etc.
Clinical Pearl — MOG-IgG Testing Caveats: (1) Low-positive titers have higher false-positive rates — if titer is borderline and clinical phenotype is atypical, retest in 6–12 weeks. (2) Titers may become negative over time, especially in monophasic MOGAD — this does NOT exclude the diagnosis. (3) MOG-IgG should be tested by CBA only — ELISA and older assays have unacceptably high false-positive rates. (4) Double-positive (MOG-IgG + AQP4-IgG) is extremely rare (<1%) and should prompt retesting — usually one is false positive.

6.3.4 Clinical Phenotypes

MOGAD Clinical Phenotypes by Age
PhenotypeAge GroupFrequencyKey Features
ADEM (Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis) Children (<12 years) Most common pediatric MOGAD phenotype (~50–70%) Encephalopathy + multifocal neurological deficits + large fluffy bilateral brain lesions. Often post-infectious/post-vaccination. MOG-IgG positive in ~40–60% of pediatric ADEM. Usually monophasic with good recovery.
Optic Neuritis Adults (most common adult phenotype) ~50–70% of adult MOGAD Bilateral ON more common than MS (simultaneous or sequential). Anterior segment; disc swelling/papillitis prominent (vs retrobulbar in MS). Generally good visual recovery but prone to relapse. Long segment ON on MRI.
Myelitis Both ~25–30% LETM similar to NMOSD (≥3 segments) but often involves conus medullaris/lower cord (vs thoracic in NMOSD). Central cord. May have "H-sign" on axial. Generally better motor recovery than NMOSD myelitis.
Cortical Encephalitis Both (more adults) ~5–15% Unique to MOGAD — seizures, headache, fever, cortical FLAIR signal abnormality (unilateral or bilateral). May mimic HSV encephalitis or autoimmune encephalitis. CSF pleocytosis. EEG: focal slowing/epileptiform.
Brainstem syndrome Both ~10–15% Diplopia, ataxia, cranial neuropathies. Periependymal brainstem lesions. May overlap with area postrema involvement.
NMOSD phenotype Adults Variable Meets IPND 2015 criteria for AQP4-negative NMOSD; important to test MOG-IgG in all AQP4-negative NMOSD patients

6.3.5 MRI Patterns in MOGAD

MOGAD MRI Characteristics
RegionPatternDistinction from MS/NMOSD
BrainLarge, fluffy, ill-defined bilateral white matter lesions (ADEM-like); deep gray matter involvement (thalami, basal ganglia) in children; cortical FLAIR signal (cortical encephalitis)More ADEM-like than MS; no Dawson fingers; no PRL; cortical encephalitis unique to MOGAD
Optic nerveLong segment involvement, anterior predominant, bilateral, perineural enhancement ("optic nerve sheath" sign), optic disc swelling on fundoscopyMS: retrobulbar, unilateral, shorter; NMOSD: posterior/chiasmal, long; MOGAD: anterior/long, bilateral, perineural
Spinal cordLETM (≥3 segments), central cord, conus medullaris involvement characteristic; may show "H-sign"NMOSD: thoracic LETM, upper cord; MOGAD: lower cord/conus; MS: short segment, dorsolateral
LeptomeningealLeptomeningeal enhancement in some casesNot typical of MS or NMOSD

6.3.6 Treatment

A. Acute Attack Treatment

MOGAD Acute Treatment
LineTreatmentNotes
1st lineIV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days → oral taper (prednisone, slow taper over 3–6 months)Responds well to steroids (generally better than MS/NMOSD). Slow taper critical — rapid steroid withdrawal associated with early relapse in MOGAD.
2nd lineIVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 daysEffective in steroid-refractory cases; some advocate as first-line adjunct in severe attacks
3rd linePLEX 5–7 exchangesLess evidence than in NMOSD; may be considered for severe myelitis
Clinical Pearl — Steroid-Dependent Relapses in MOGAD: A hallmark of MOGAD is steroid dependence — patients relapse when oral steroids are tapered too quickly. Unlike MS (where steroid tapers are brief), MOGAD often requires slow oral prednisone taper over 3–6 months after acute attack. If relapse occurs during taper, this strongly suggests MOGAD (rather than MS) and should prompt MOG-IgG testing and consideration of maintenance immunotherapy.

B. Preventive (Maintenance) Therapy

Not all MOGAD patients need long-term prevention — ~50% have monophasic disease. Prevention is recommended for relapsing MOGAD (≥2 attacks) or severe first attack with poor recovery.

MOGAD Preventive Therapies
DrugMechanismDoseEvidence LevelNotes
IVIG (maintenance)Immunomodulation, anti-idiotype antibodies0.4 g/kg monthly or 1–2 g/kg q1–3 monthsRetrospective series; most consistent efficacy dataConsidered first-line prevention for relapsing MOGAD by many experts. Well-tolerated. Cost may be limiting.
AzathioprinePurine synthesis inhibitor2–3 mg/kg/dayRetrospectiveTraditional immunosuppressant; onset 3–6 months; bridge with oral prednisone
Mycophenolate mofetilIMPDH inhibitor2000–3000 mg/dayRetrospectiveAlternative to AZA; teratogenic
RituximabAnti-CD20Standard NMOSD dosingRetrospective; less effective in MOGAD than NMOSDBreakthrough relapses reported on rituximab in MOGAD — MOG-IgG may be produced by CD20-negative long-lived plasma cells. Consider if IVIG fails or unavailable.
TocilizumabAnti-IL-6R8 mg/kg IV q4wk or 162 mg SC weeklyCase seriesEmerging option for refractory MOGAD
Low-dose oral prednisoloneBroad immunosuppression5–10 mg/dayRetrospectiveMay be used as bridge or adjunct; concern for long-term steroid side effects
High-Yield: Why Rituximab May Fail in MOGAD

Unlike NMOSD (where rituximab is highly effective), MOGAD shows higher breakthrough rates on rituximab. The proposed explanation: MOG-IgG may be partly produced by long-lived plasma cells and plasmablasts that are CD20-negative (rituximab depletes CD20+ B-cells but spares CD20− plasma cells). This contrasts with AQP4-IgG in NMOSD, which appears more dependent on CD20+ B-cell renewal. This is why IVIG is often preferred for MOGAD prevention — it modulates the immune response through multiple mechanisms rather than relying on B-cell depletion alone.

6.3.7 Prognosis

MOGAD Prognosis
FeatureDetails
Overall prognosisBetter than NMOSD for individual attacks (better recovery); comparable or milder than MS overall
Monophasic vs relapsing~50% monophasic (especially pediatric ADEM); ~50% relapsing (especially adult ON/myelitis). Persistent MOG-IgG seropositivity predicts relapsing course.
Visual outcomesBetter than NMOSD; most patients recover useful vision after ON (≥20/40 in majority). However, recurrent ON can cause cumulative damage.
Motor outcomesBetter recovery from myelitis than NMOSD; ambulation usually preserved
Cognitive outcomesADEM phenotype: usually full cognitive recovery in children. Cortical encephalitis: may have residual epilepsy.
MOG-IgG seroconversionPatients who seroconvert to negative have lower relapse risk; those who remain persistently seropositive are more likely to relapse
MortalityRare; lower than NMOSD. Deaths reported with fulminant ADEM or severe brainstem involvement.

Question: A 7-year-old boy presents with fever, encephalopathy, and multifocal neurological deficits 2 weeks after a viral URTI. MRI brain shows large bilateral fluffy white matter lesions with deep gray matter involvement. MOG-IgG is positive. AQP4-IgG is negative. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis. What is the diagnosis, and how does MOG-IgG positivity change management?

Answer:

Diagnosis: MOGAD presenting as ADEM. This is the most common MOGAD phenotype in children — post-infectious encephalopathy with large fluffy brain lesions + MOG-IgG positive.

Acute treatment:

  • IVMP 30 mg/kg/day (max 1g) × 3–5 days → slow oral steroid taper over 3–6 months (critical — rapid withdrawal risks relapse)
  • IVIG 2 g/kg over 2–5 days if steroid-refractory

How MOG-IgG changes management:

  • Prognosis: MOG+ ADEM generally has good recovery (~80–90% near-complete). But ~20–30% may have recurrent attacks (multiphasic ADEM or relapsing MOGAD).
  • Steroid taper: Slow taper is essential — steroid-dependent relapses are characteristic of MOGAD.
  • Follow-up: Monitor for relapse; recheck MOG-IgG at 6–12 months — seroconversion to negative suggests lower relapse risk.
  • NOT MS: Despite multifocal brain lesions, this is NOT pediatric MS — MOG-IgG positivity excludes MS. Do not start MS DMTs (IFN-β, natalizumab, etc.).
  • If relapsing: Consider maintenance IVIG or AZA/MMF for prevention.

6.4 Autoimmune Encephalitis (AE)

6.4.1 Overview & Classification

Autoimmune encephalitis encompasses a group of inflammatory brain diseases mediated by antibodies against neuronal cell-surface or synaptic proteins. Recognition has dramatically increased since the discovery of anti-NMDAR encephalitis (Dalmau 2007). AE is now considered as common as infectious encephalitis and is a treatable cause of rapidly progressive encephalopathy.

Classification by Antibody Target

Autoimmune Encephalitis Classification
CategoryTarget LocationMechanismExamplesPrognosis
Cell-surface / Synaptic antibodies Extracellular domain of neuronal surface proteins Antibody-mediated: receptor internalization, complement activation, or blocking of receptor function → potentially reversible with immunotherapy Anti-NMDAR, anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-GABABR, anti-AMPAR, anti-DPPX, anti-IgLON5 Generally good if treated early; often reversible
Intracellular / Classical paraneoplastic antibodies Intracellular antigens (onconeural) T-cell mediated: cytotoxic T-cells → neuronal death → often irreversible. Antibodies are biomarkers, not directly pathogenic. Anti-Hu (ANNA-1), anti-Yo (PCA-1), anti-Ri (ANNA-2), anti-CV2/CRMP5, anti-amphiphysin, anti-Ma2 Generally poor; limited response to immunotherapy; tumor treatment is priority
High-Yield: Cell-Surface vs Intracellular Antibodies

Cell-surface antibodies (NMDAR, LGI1, CASPR2, GABABR): directly pathogenic, cause functional disruption (receptor internalization/blocking), damage is potentially reversible → responds to immunotherapy.
Intracellular antibodies (Hu, Yo, Ri, CV2): markers of T-cell cytotoxicity, not directly pathogenic themselves, damage is structural/neuronal death → irreversible → priority is tumor removal, immunotherapy has limited CNS benefit.

This distinction has critical treatment implications: aggressive immunotherapy is warranted and effective for cell-surface antibodies but has limited benefit for intracellular antibodies (where the focus should be cancer treatment).

6.4.2 Diagnostic Criteria (Graus 2016)

Graus et al., Lancet Neurol 2016: Clinical approach to diagnosis of autoimmune encephalitis. Provides criteria for possible AE (clinical), probable AE (antibody-specific), and definite AE.

Possible Autoimmune Encephalitis (Clinical Criteria)

All three must be met:

  1. Subacute onset (rapid progression in <3 months) of working memory deficits, altered mental status, or psychiatric symptoms
  2. At least one of:
    • New focal CNS findings
    • Seizures not explained by prior epilepsy
    • CSF pleocytosis (WBC >5/μL)
    • MRI features suggestive of encephalitis (T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes or multifocal involving gray/white matter)
  3. Reasonable exclusion of alternative causes (infection, metabolic, neoplastic, other autoimmune)
Clinical Pearl — "Possible AE" Triggers Workup: Meeting "possible AE" criteria should immediately trigger a comprehensive antibody panel (serum + CSF), tumor screening, and consideration of empiric immunotherapy while awaiting results. Do NOT wait for antibody results before starting treatment in clinically suspicious cases — delays worsen outcomes, especially in anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

6.4.3 Antibody-Specific Syndromes

Anti-NMDA Receptor Encephalitis

Anti-NMDAR Encephalitis
FeatureDetails
EpidemiologyMost common AE; predominantly young women (F:M ~4:1); median age ~21 years; also in children and males
Tumor associationOvarian teratoma in ~40–50% of females aged 12–45 (contains neural tissue expressing NMDAR). Less common in children (<5%) and males (<5%). Always screen with pelvic MRI/US.
Clinical stages
  1. Prodromal (1–2 weeks): fever, headache, viral-like illness
  2. Psychiatric: anxiety, agitation, psychosis, hallucinations, bizarre behavior, insomnia — often misdiagnosed as primary psychiatric illness
  3. Neurological: seizures, memory loss, decreased consciousness, dyskinesias (orofacial), autonomic instability (tachy/bradycardia, hypoventilation, hyperthermia)
  4. Hypoventilation/catatonia: may require ICU/ventilation — central hypoventilation is characteristic and dangerous
  5. Recovery: slow (months), reverse order of symptom development
MRINormal in ~50%! When abnormal: non-specific T2/FLAIR in cortical, subcortical, or medial temporal regions. May see transient meningeal enhancement.
EEGExtreme delta brush (rhythmic delta with superimposed beta, present in ~30%) — relatively specific pattern. Also: diffuse slowing, epileptiform discharges.
CSFLymphocytic pleocytosis (~80%), elevated protein (~30%), CSF NMDAR-Ab more sensitive than serum (always test CSF). OCBs may develop.
Prognosis~80% good outcome (mRS 0–2) with immunotherapy + tumor removal. Recovery slow (months to >1 year). ~12% relapse. Mortality ~5%.

Anti-LGI1 Encephalitis (Leucine-rich Glioma-Inactivated 1)

Anti-LGI1 Encephalitis
FeatureDetails
EpidemiologyOlder adults (median ~60 years); M:F ~2:1 (male predominance unlike most AE)
Tumor associationRare (<10%); thymoma in some cases
Clinical triad
  1. Faciobrachial dystonic seizures (FBDS): brief (<3 seconds), frequent (up to 100/day), unilateral face + ipsilateral arm tonic posturing. Pathognomonic for LGI1. Often precedes encephalitis — recognizing FBDS early can prevent progression.
  2. Limbic encephalitis: memory loss, confusion, temporal lobe seizures, psychiatric symptoms
  3. Hyponatremia: present in ~60% due to SIADH (LGI1 expressed in hypothalamus/kidney)
MRIT2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes (uni- or bilateral); basal ganglia involvement with FBDS. Late: mesial temporal sclerosis/hippocampal atrophy.
EEGTemporal epileptiform discharges; FBDS may not have clear EEG correlate (subcortical origin)
CSFOften normal or minimal pleocytosis; serum Ab usually more sensitive than CSF for LGI1
Treatment responseGood response to immunotherapy; FBDS respond dramatically to corticosteroids (often within days). AEDs alone typically ineffective for FBDS.
PrognosisGenerally good with treatment; residual memory deficit common; late hippocampal atrophy → epilepsy

Anti-CASPR2 Encephalitis (Contactin-Associated Protein-like 2)

Anti-CASPR2 Encephalitis
FeatureDetails
EpidemiologyOlder males (median ~60 years); M:F ~9:1
Tumor associationThymoma in ~20–30%
Clinical features
  • Morvan syndrome: peripheral nerve hyperexcitability (neuromyotonia, cramps, fasciculations) + limbic encephalitis (insomnia, confusion, hallucinations) + autonomic dysfunction (hyperhidrosis, tachycardia)
  • Limbic encephalitis (similar to LGI1 but less common hyponatremia)
  • Neuromyotonia / Isaac syndrome: peripheral nerve hyperexcitability alone (cramps, stiffness, myokymia)
  • Neuropathic pain, cerebellar ataxia possible
CSFVariable; may be normal. Serum Ab usually sufficient.
PrognosisVariable; tumor removal essential if thymoma present. Generally responds to immunotherapy.

Other Antibody-Specific Syndromes

Other Autoimmune Encephalitis Antibodies
AntibodyKey SyndromeTumor AssociationDistinguishing Features
Anti-GABABRLimbic encephalitis with prominent seizuresSCLC (~50%)Seizures often the presenting and dominant feature; temporal lobe predominant. Responds to immunotherapy + tumor treatment.
Anti-AMPARLimbic encephalitis; psychiatric symptoms prominentLung, breast, thymoma (~65%)Relapsing course common; high tumor association. Test both GluA1 and GluA2 subunits.
Anti-DPPXEncephalitis + GI dysmotility (severe diarrhea, weight loss precede neurological symptoms)B-cell neoplasms (rare)Prodromal severe diarrhea → encephalopathy, hyperekplexia, myoclonus, cognitive decline
Anti-IgLON5Sleep disorder (parasomnia, OSA, stridor) + progressive supranuclear palsy-like syndrome + bulbar dysfunctionNoneUnique: tauopathy on pathology (resembles neurodegenerative disease). HLA-DRB1*10:01 strongly associated. Chronic course. Poor response to immunotherapy in advanced disease.
Anti-GAD65Stiff-person syndrome, cerebellar ataxia, limbic encephalitis, epilepsyRare (thymoma); strong association with type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroidIntracellular target but high titers (>2000 IU/mL) associated with neurological syndromes. Low titers are non-specific (common in T1DM). Response to immunotherapy: variable (GAD65 is intracellular → T-cell mediated component).

Classical Paraneoplastic Antibodies (Intracellular)

Classical Paraneoplastic (Intracellular) Antibodies
AntibodySyndromeCancerPrognosis
Anti-Hu (ANNA-1)Sensory neuropathy, limbic encephalitis, brainstem encephalitis, autonomic neuropathySCLC (most common)Poor; limited immunotherapy response; cancer treatment priority
Anti-Yo (PCA-1)Cerebellar degeneration — subacute progressive ataxiaOvarian, breastPoor; Purkinje cell destruction irreversible
Anti-Ri (ANNA-2)Opsoclonus-myoclonus, brainstem encephalitisBreast, SCLCVariable; some improvement with tumor treatment
Anti-CV2/CRMP5Mixed: neuropathy, chorea, cerebellar ataxia, optic neuritis, uveitisSCLC, thymomaVariable
Anti-amphiphysinStiff-person syndrome, myelopathyBreast, SCLCVariable
Anti-Ma2 (Ta)Limbic/diencephalic encephalitis: hypersomnia, vertical gaze palsy, hypokinesisTesticular germ cell tumor (young men)Better with orchiectomy + immunotherapy
Clinical Pearl — Tumor Screening in AE: Always perform comprehensive tumor screening based on antibody type: (1) Anti-NMDAR → pelvic MRI/US (ovarian teratoma); CT chest/abdomen/pelvis. (2) Anti-LGI1 → CT chest (thymoma, rare). (3) Anti-CASPR2 → CT chest (thymoma ~20%). (4) Anti-GABABR → CT chest (SCLC ~50%). (5) Anti-Hu → CT chest (SCLC). (6) Anti-Yo → CT/MRI pelvis + mammography (ovarian/breast). (7) Anti-Ma2 in young men → testicular US. If initial screen negative, repeat at 6 months and consider whole-body PET-CT — tumor may be occult.

6.4.4 Treatment Algorithm

AE Treatment — Stepwise Approach
LineTreatmentDetails
First-line immunotherapy
  1. IV Methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days
  2. IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days
  3. PLEX 5–7 exchanges
Combinations used: IVMP + IVIG or IVMP + PLEX 		Start as soon as "possible AE" criteria met — do NOT wait for antibody results. PLEX particularly effective for cell-surface antibody syndromes. Assess response at 2–4 weeks.
Second-line immunotherapy
(if insufficient first-line response by 2–4 weeks)
  1. Rituximab 375 mg/m² × 4 weekly or 1000 mg × 2 (days 0, 14)
  2. Cyclophosphamide 750 mg/m² monthly × 6
 Rituximab preferred for anti-NMDAR and other cell-surface Ab syndromes. Cyclophosphamide for severe/refractory cases. Do not delay second-line if first-line fails — outcomes correlate with treatment intensity and speed.
Tumor removal Remove underlying tumor as soon as identified Essential for anti-NMDAR (teratoma removal → faster recovery), anti-GABABR/AMPAR/Hu/Yo (cancer treatment paramount). Tumor removal alone may be insufficient — combine with immunotherapy.
Maintenance immunotherapy
(prevent relapse)
  • MMF 1000–3000 mg/day
  • Azathioprine 2–3 mg/kg/day
  • Rituximab q6mo (for relapsing anti-NMDAR)
  • Chronic IVIG monthly (selected cases)
 Duration: ≥2 years minimum; some require indefinite. Monitor antibody titers — declining titers correlate with clinical improvement. Relapse rates: ~12% for anti-NMDAR, higher for AMPAR.
Emerging therapies
  • Bortezomib (proteasome inhibitor → plasma cell depletion)
  • Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R)
  • Low-dose IL-2 (Treg expansion)
 For refractory cases resistant to rituximab. Bortezomib targets antibody-secreting plasma cells not depleted by rituximab. Case series show benefit in refractory anti-NMDAR encephalitis.

Question: A 22-year-old woman develops new-onset psychosis, agitation, and seizures over 2 weeks. She was seen in psychiatry initially and started on antipsychotics without improvement. She now develops orofacial dyskinesias and decreased consciousness. What is the most likely diagnosis, and what immediate workup and treatment are needed?

Answer:

Most likely diagnosis: Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitis. The clinical progression — psychiatric symptoms → seizures → movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias) → decreased consciousness — is the classic staged presentation. Young woman + psychiatric → neurological = anti-NMDAR until proven otherwise.

Immediate workup:
