  • CSF: Anti-NMDAR antibodies (CSF more sensitive than serum); cell count, protein, glucose, OCBs, viral PCR (exclude HSV)
  • Serum: Anti-NMDAR antibodies, comprehensive AE panel
  • MRI brain: May be normal in ~50%; look for temporal/cortical FLAIR signal
  • EEG: Diffuse slowing; look for extreme delta brush; exclude status epilepticus
  • Pelvic MRI/US: Screen for ovarian teratoma
  • CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Tumor screening

Immediate treatment (do NOT wait for antibody results):

  • First-line: IVMP 1 g × 5 days + IVIG 0.4 g/kg × 5 days (start concurrently)
  • ICU admission: Monitor for autonomic instability, hypoventilation (central), status epilepticus
  • If teratoma found: Urgent surgical removal
  • If no/poor response by 2–4 weeks: Escalate to rituximab ± cyclophosphamide
  • Stop antipsychotics — neuroleptic sensitivity common; may worsen dyskinesias

6.5 Acute Disseminated Encephalomyelitis (ADEM)

6.5.1 Overview

ADEM is a monophasic inflammatory demyelinating disorder of the CNS, predominantly affecting children (peak age 5–8 years). It is characterized by encephalopathy (mandatory diagnostic criterion) plus multifocal neurological deficits, typically occurring 1–4 weeks after a viral infection or vaccination.

ADEM Key Features
FeatureDetails
DefinitionFirst episode of CNS demyelination with encephalopathy (altered consciousness or behavioral change beyond fever-related); multifocal neurological deficits; polyfocal MRI lesions
AgePrimarily children (median ~5–8 years); rare in adults (when occurs, tends to be more severe)
TriggerPost-infectious (~70–80%): upper respiratory, GI viral illness; post-vaccination (rare, ~5%). Latency 1–4 weeks.
MOG-IgGPositive in ~40–60% of pediatric ADEM — now classified as MOGAD if MOG-IgG+
Clinical featuresEncephalopathy (required) + multifocal deficits: ataxia, hemiparesis, cranial neuropathies, optic neuritis, myelitis, seizures. Fever, headache, meningism common.
MRILarge (>1–2 cm), bilateral, fluffy, poorly-demarcated T2/FLAIR lesions in white matter ± deep gray matter (thalami, basal ganglia). No periventricular Dawson fingers. May involve brainstem, cerebellum, spinal cord.
CSFLymphocytic pleocytosis (~50–80%), elevated protein; OCBs usually absent (if present, consider MS)
CourseMonophasic by definition. If relapse occurs → consider multiphasic ADEM (MDEM), MOGAD, or MS.

6.5.2 ADEM Treatment & Prognosis

ADEM Treatment
TreatmentRegimenNotes
IVMP20–30 mg/kg/day (max 1 g) × 3–5 days → oral prednisone taper over 4–6 weeksFirst-line. Slow oral taper to prevent relapse (especially if MOG-IgG+).
IVIG2 g/kg over 2–5 daysSecond-line or adjunct if steroid-refractory
PLEX5–7 exchangesFor severe steroid- and IVIG-refractory cases
ADEM Prognosis
OutcomeDetails
RecoveryGood — ~60–80% full recovery; ~20–30% mild residual deficits (behavioral, cognitive, motor)
Mortality~1–5% (fulminant cases: acute hemorrhagic leukoencephalitis / Hurst disease)
RelapseIf new event >3 months after initial: reconsider as MS, MOGAD, or MDEM
Distinction from MSADEM: encephalopathy + large fluffy lesions + OCB− + monophasic; MS: no encephalopathy + Dawson fingers + OCB+ + relapsing. In children with ADEM, if >2 non-ADEM events → likely MS.

6.6 Differential Diagnosis: MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD

6.6.1 Master Comparison Table

Comprehensive Comparison: MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD
FeatureMSNMOSD (AQP4+)MOGAD
TargetMyelin/oligodendrocyte (T/B-cell mediated)AQP4 on astrocyte foot processesMOG on oligodendrocyte surface
AntibodyNone specific (OCBs = polyclonal)AQP4-IgGMOG-IgG
Peak age20–40 years~40 yearsBimodal: children (~5–7) + adults (~30–40)
Sex ratio (F:M)3:19:1~1–2:1
EthnicityNorthern European predominantNon-White (African, Asian, Latin American)All ethnicities
Clinical Features
Optic neuritisUnilateral, retrobulbar, mild–moderate, good recoverySevere, bilateral, chiasmal/posterior, poor recoveryBilateral, anterior (papillitis), long segment, good recovery
MyelitisShort segment (<3 vertebral segments), partial, asymmetric, dorsolateralLETM ≥3 segments, complete, central cord, thoracicLETM, central, conus/lower cord, good recovery
Area postremaNot typicalCharacteristic (intractable N/V/hiccups)Rare
Brain involvementDawson fingers, periventricular, juxtacorticalPeriependymal, diencephalic, non-specific or normalADEM-like fluffy, cortical encephalitis
SeizuresUncommonRareCommon (cortical encephalitis phenotype)
EncephalopathyNot typicalRareCommon (ADEM, cortical encephalitis)
Attack severityMild–moderateSevere, poor recoveryModerate, good recovery
MRI Features
Brain lesionsOvoid perpendicular periventricular (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial, small well-definedNon-specific, periependymal (3rd/4th ventricle), area postrema, large hemispheric, or normalLarge fluffy bilateral (ADEM-like), deep gray matter, cortical FLAIR signal
Spinal cordShort segment, dorsolateral, peripheralLETM, central (H-sign), thoracicLETM, central, conus medullaris
Optic nerve MRIShort, anterior, unilateralLong, posterior/chiasmalLong, anterior, bilateral, perineural enhancement
CVS~85% lesions CVS+Low CVS (<40%)Low CVS
PRLPresent (chronic active)AbsentAbsent
CSF & Lab
OCBsPositive ~95%Negative ~75–80%Usually negative
PleocytosisMild lymphocytic (<50)Can be >50; neutrophil predominanceMild–moderate; lymphocytic/mixed
GFAPMildly elevatedMarkedly elevated (astrocyte destruction)Mildly elevated
MRZ reactionPositive ~67%NegativeNegative
Disease Course & Treatment
CourseRelapsing → progressive; continuous diseaseRelapsing (~90%); stepwise disability; NO progression between attacks~50% monophasic, ~50% relapsing
PreventionMS DMTs (IFN-β, anti-CD20, S1P modulators, etc.)AZA, MMF, RTX, eculizumab, satralizumab, inebilizumab. MS DMTs harmful!IVIG, AZA, MMF, ± RTX (less effective)
PrognosisVariable; 50% SPMS by 15–20 yearsPoor per attack; cumulative disability; mortality ~5–10% at 5 years without treatmentBetter overall; good recovery per attack
High-Yield: Quick Diagnostic Clues

Think MS: Young woman + Dawson fingers + short cord lesions + OCBs+ + CVS+ + PRL+
Think NMOSD: Middle-aged woman + LETM + severe bilateral ON + area postrema (intractable vomiting) + AQP4-IgG+ + OCBs−
Think MOGAD: Child with ADEM or adult with bilateral ON + papillitis + conus myelitis + MOG-IgG+ + good recovery + steroid-dependent relapses

6.6.2 Other CNS Inflammatory / Demyelinating Differentials

Important Differential Diagnoses
ConditionKey Distinguishing Features
NeurosarcoidosisCranial neuropathies (especially VII), hypothalamic/pituitary involvement, leptomeningeal enhancement, hilar lymphadenopathy, elevated ACE, non-caseating granulomas
CNS vasculitis (PACNS)Headache, stroke-like episodes, multifocal infarcts, vessel beading on angiography, brain biopsy may be needed
Behçet diseaseOral/genital ulcers, uveitis, brainstem/diencephalic lesions, CSF neutrophilic pleocytosis, pathergy test
Susac syndromeTriad: encephalopathy + branch retinal artery occlusions (BRAO) + sensorineural hearing loss. Corpus callosum "snowball" lesions. Young women.
CNS lymphomaHomogeneously enhancing periventricular mass; restricted diffusion; older age or immunocompromised; CSF cytology/flow cytometry
NeurosyphilisPositive treponemal tests; CSF VDRL; meningitis, tabes dorsalis, general paresis; consider in all unexplained CNS inflammation
Neuroborreliosis (Lyme)Cranial neuropathies (VII bilateral), radiculitis, lymphocytic meningitis; endemic area exposure; Borrelia serology + CSF index
CADASILMigraine with aura, subcortical infarcts, anterior temporal pole + external capsule white matter lesions, family history, NOTCH3 mutation
LeukodystrophiesSymmetrical white matter changes; progressive course; metabolic markers (very long-chain fatty acids for adrenoleukodystrophy, arylsulfatase A for metachromatic)
CLIPPERSChronic lymphocytic inflammation with pontine perivascular enhancement — "peppering" punctate Gd-enhancing lesions in pons/brainstem; steroid-responsive

Section 6 — Key References
TopicReference

7. CNS Infections

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of CNS infections relevant to Internal Medicine and Neurology practice. Coverage includes acute bacterial meningitis (Thai-specific empirical regimens, ESCMID/IDSA guidelines, organism-specific therapy, adjunctive dexamethasone — Cochrane 2015), viral CNS infections (HSV encephalitis, VZV, CMV, JE, rabies, HIV-associated), tuberculous meningitis (Thwaites trial, BIS guidelines, BMRC staging), fungal CNS infections (cryptococcal meningitis — AMBITION trial/WHO 2022, aspergillosis, mucormycosis), parasitic infections (cerebral toxoplasmosis, NCC, eosinophilic meningitis from Angiostrongylus/Gnathostoma, cerebral malaria), immunocompromised host approach, and neuroimaging patterns.

7.1 Bacterial Meningitis

7.1.1 Overview & Epidemiology

Acute bacterial meningitis (ABM) is a medical emergency with an incidence of 0.9–80 per 100,000/year globally and an overall mortality of ~54%, with neurological complications in ~25% of survivors (JAMA 2022). Prompt recognition and treatment within 60 minutes of arrival is critical (ESCMID 2016).

Thai Epidemiology (Ramathibodi data, Aimbudlop et al.): Among Thai adults with meningitis: C. neoformans 37%, Bacteria 31.5%, M. tuberculosis 27%, Virus 4.5%. HIV-positive patients account for ~30% of all meningitis cases. Thammasat data (Neurology Asia 2020): bacterial 61%, viral 15%, TB 12%, crypto 13%.

Common Pathogens by Age & Risk Group

Age / Risk GroupCommon PathogensEmpirical Therapy (ESCMID/Lancet 2021)
<1 monthS. agalactiae, E. coli, ListeriaAmoxicillin/ampicillin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin; or ampicillin + aminoglycoside
1–23 monthsS. agalactiae, S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidisVancomycin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin (cefotaxime or ceftriaxone)
2–50 yearsS. pneumoniae, N. meningitidisVancomycin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin
>50 yearsS. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, L. monocytogenes, aerobic GNRVancomycin + ampicillin + 3rd-gen cephalosporin
ImmunocompromisedS. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, L. monocytogenes, S. aureus, Salmonella spp., PseudomonasVancomycin + ampicillin + cefepime or meropenem
Thai-Specific Empirical Regimen (Siriraj data):
• Age <50y: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h (covers N. meningitidis, S. pneumoniae, S. suis)
• Age >50y / pregnancy / alcoholism / steroids: Add Ampicillin 2g IV q4h (covers S. agalactiae, L. monocytogenes, GNR)
• No vancomycin needed empirically in Thailand — drug-resistant S. pneumoniae rate <1% (NARST data 2022: vancomycin resistance 0%). Siriraj organism distribution: S. agalactiae > S. suis > S. pneumoniae (differs from Western patterns where S. pneumoniae predominates at ~72%).
Meningeal Dose Antibiotics
DrugMeningeal Dose
Vancomycin30–60 mg/kg/day (target trough 15–20 µg/mL)
Ceftriaxone4 g/day (2g q12h)
Cefotaxime8–12 g/day
Cefepime6 g/day
Ampicillin12 g/day (2g q4h)
Penicillin G24 million units/day
Meropenem6 g/day (2g q8h)

7.1.2 Clinical Presentation & Diagnosis

Classic Triad & Sensitivity

The classic triad of fever, neck stiffness, and altered mental status is present in only 41% of adults with ABM (JAMA 2022). However, >90% will have at least 2 of the 4 symptoms: fever, headache, decreased consciousness, or stiff neck (Seminars in Neurology 2019). Only ~30% of elderly/immunocompromised patients exhibit neck stiffness.

Clinical SignSensitivitySpecificityPPVNPV
Neck stiffness31%71%41%61%
Kernig’s sign11%95%60%60%
Brudzinski’s sign9%95%50%62%

Combined data from Thomas (n=297), Uchihara (n=54), Waghdhare (n=190) — Brouwer et al. Lancet 2012;380:1684–92

CT Brain Before LP — Indications

Indications for CT Before LP (IDSA 2004 / NEJM 2001)
  1. Age >60 years
  2. Immunocompromised state
  3. Known CNS lesion
  4. Seizure within 1 week
  5. Abnormal level of consciousness
  6. Focal neurologic findings
  7. Papilledema / clinical suspicion of elevated ICP

ESCMID 2016 (stricter, 4 indications): GCS <10, focal neurological deficit, new-onset seizure, severe immunocompromise. Of note, 97% of patients without these indications have a normal CT (Hasbun et al. NEJM 2001).

Clinical Pearl — Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT: Start empirical antibiotics + dexamethasone BEFORE CT/LP if imaging is needed. Delay of ATB >6 hours is associated with increased mortality from 6% to 45% and increased neurological sequelae from 10% to 70% (JAMA 2022). ESCMID 2016: initiate within 60 minutes of arrival. If no improvement within 48 hours, brain imaging is indicated to detect hydrocephalus, infarction, empyema, or abscess.

7.1.3 CSF Analysis & Interpretation

CSF ParameterNormalBacterialViralTBFungalParasiticCarcinomatous
Opening Pressure (cmH2O)10–20HighNormal–highHighVery highUsually highNormal–high
ColourClearCloudyClearClear–yellowishClear–cloudyClearClear
Cells/mm³<5100–50,0005–500100–50020–500VariableVariable
DifferentialMonoPMNMonoLymph (N early)LymphEos >10%Mixed/Mono
CSF Glucose / PG ratioNormal (60%)Very lowNormal<45 mg/dL (80%)Normal–lowLowLow
Protein (mg/dL)<45>100050–100100–500020–500>60100–500
CSF Interpretation Pitfalls

Traumatic LP correction: Subtract 1 WBC per 500–1000 RBCs; subtract protein 1 mg/dL per 1000 RBCs.

Partially treated bacterial meningitis: Normal glucose in 70%, lymphocyte-predominant in 15%, normal protein in 10%, but normal cell count in 0%. CSF sterilization occurs within 2–4 hours of parenteral antibiotics.

Eosinophilic meningitis (>10% eos): Think parasites (Angiostrongylus cantonensis, Gnathostoma), TB, Coccidioides, lymphoma.

Microbiological Diagnosis

TestSensitivityNotes
Gram stain25–90%Depends on organism load; S. pneumoniae highest Sn
CSF culture60–90%Drops to 20–30% with prior ATB; sterilization 2–4h
CSF PCR (multiplex)89% D1–3; 70% D4–6; 33% D7–10Less affected by pretreatment
CSF lactateSn 93%, Sp 96%Best discriminator for bacterial vs viral; >3.5 mmol/L suggests bacterial
Procalcitonin (serum)Sn 90%, Sp 98%Useful to differentiate bacterial from aseptic; >0.5 ng/mL

7.1.4 Organism-Specific Bacterial Meningitis

Streptococcus pneumoniae

S. pneumoniae is the most common cause of bacterial meningitis globally (~72% of adult cases). It is a common nasopharyngeal colonizer in 5–10% of healthy adults and 20–40% of children. Routes of CNS entry include hematogenous spread (50% associated with pneumonia), direct extension from otitis/sinusitis/mastoiditis, and post-traumatic CSF leak.

Risk factors: basilar skull fracture with CSF leak, hyposplenism, hypogammaglobulinemia, multiple myeloma, glucocorticoid use, DM, CKD, alcoholism, malnutrition, chronic liver disease.

Cerebrovascular complications are particularly common: arterial stroke in up to 30%, cerebral venous thrombosis in up to 9%, intracerebral hemorrhage in up to 9% of patients.

Mortality rate: 10–37% with neurological sequelae up to 24.7%. Therapy should NOT be withdrawn early in comatose patients with preserved brainstem reflexes (a quarter eventually recover fully).

Vaccination: All survivors should receive PCV13 or PCV15, followed by PPSV23 or PCV20, due to high recurrence risk.

Treatment: Penicillin-susceptible: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone × 10–14 days. Penicillin-resistant (MIC ≥0.12): Ceftriaxone. Ceftriaxone-resistant (MIC ≥2): Vancomycin + Ceftriaxone ± Rifampicin.

Thai Context (NARST 2022, 68 hospitals): In Thailand, S. pneumoniae vancomycin resistance = 0%. Levofloxacin resistance 0–1.5%. Penicillin G non-susceptibility (oxacillin screen) ~38%. Therefore, ceftriaxone alone without vancomycin is appropriate for empirical therapy in Thailand. The ESCMID guideline also states that in areas with very low pneumococcal cephalosporin resistance, ceftriaxone alone is considered appropriate.

Neisseria meningitidis

Gram-negative diplococcus; nasopharyngeal carrier in healthy individuals. Causes 11% of adult meningitis (age >16y). Endemic in Africa, India, and developing areas. Risk factors: crowded living, chronic illness, asplenia, complement deficiency, corticosteroid use.

Distinctive features: Petechial/purpuric rash in 63–80% (Sp 83–92% for meningococcal disease). Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome (bilateral adrenal hemorrhage) in fulminant meningococcemia.

Mortality: Meningitis ~3–10%; meningococcemia ~30%.

Treatment: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone × 7 days.

Chemoprophylaxis (close contacts): Rifampin 600mg PO q12h × 2 days, OR Ciprofloxacin 500–750mg PO single dose, OR Ceftriaxone 250mg IM single dose.

Listeria monocytogenes

Small gram-positive bacillus; causes ~5% of bacterial meningitis in adults >16y. Environmental/food-borne (raw meat, vegetables, milk — isolated from 70% of these products). Mortality 15–35%.

Groups at risk: Pregnant women, infants, elderly (>60y), alcoholism, immunosuppressed, chronic liver/renal disease, DM, iron-overload conditions.

CNS manifestations:

  • Meningoencephalitis (hematogenous route, immunocompromised host)
  • Rhombencephalitis (brainstem encephalitis, oral route, immunocompetent host) — 1–24% of Listeria CNS infections
  • Brain abscess — ~10% of Listeria CNS infections; treat with Ampicillin ≥6 weeks ± surgical intervention

Clinical pearl: May present as “aseptic meningitis” (CSF lymphocyte-predominant). Listeria is intrinsically resistant to cephalosporins — always add Ampicillin in at-risk populations.

Treatment: Ampicillin ± Gentamicin × ≥21 days. Stop dexamethasone if Listeria confirmed (JAMA 2022).

Streptococcus suis

Zoonotic pathogen from pigs with highest prevalence in Asia (especially Thailand, Vietnam, China). Risk factor: exposure to pigs/pork (~61% of cases). Main clinical syndrome: subacute meningitis (68%), followed by sepsis, arthritis, endocarditis, endophthalmitis.

Male predominant (82%); mortality rate 2.9%. Major sequela: hearing loss (53%) and vestibular dysfunction.

Treatment: Penicillin G or Ceftriaxone × 14–21 days. Dexamethasone reduces severe hearing loss (PLOS NTD 2015).

Thai Pearl — S. suis: Think of S. suis in any Thai patient with meningitis + hearing loss + history of eating raw pork products (“larb moo” / “lab dib”). S. suis is the second most common bacterial meningitis organism at Siriraj Hospital.

Streptococcus agalactiae (Group B Streptococcus)

Most common cause of bacterial meningitis at Siriraj Hospital. Classically neonatal, but increasingly recognized in adults >50y, DM, pregnancy, immunosuppression.

Treatment: Penicillin G ± Gentamicin × 14–21 days.

Duration of Antibiotic Therapy by Organism

OrganismDuration (days)Preferred Regimen
N. meningitidis7Penicillin G / Ceftriaxone
H. influenzae7–10Ampicillin / Ceftriaxone
S. pneumoniae10–14Penicillin G (PenS) / Ceftriaxone (PenR) / Vanco+Ceftriaxone (CeftriR)
S. suis14–21Penicillin G / Ceftriaxone
S. agalactiae (GBS)14–21Penicillin G ± Gentamicin
L. monocytogenes≥21Ampicillin ± Gentamicin
Enterobacterales / GNR≥21Ceftriaxone / Meropenem
StaphylococcusVariable (usually ≥14)Nafcillin (MSSA) / Vancomycin (MRSA)

7.1.5 Adjunctive Dexamethasone

Dexamethasone in Bacterial Meningitis

Dose: Dexamethasone 10 mg IV q6h × 4 days (0.15 mg/kg q6h). Must be given before or with first antibiotic dose (not after).

Evidence (Cochrane 2015, 25 RCTs, 4121 patients):

  • Hearing loss: RR 0.67 (13.8% vs 19.0%)
  • Neurological sequelae: RR 0.83 (17.9% vs 21.6%)
  • Mortality: RR 0.90
  • Benefits primarily in high-income countries and S. pneumoniae meningitis (mortality 29.9% vs 36.0%)
  • No beneficial effect in low-income countries
  • S. suis: dexamethasone decreases severe hearing loss

Stop dexamethasone if: Listeria monocytogenes is confirmed.

In the Netherlands, pneumococcal meningitis in-hospital mortality decreased from 31% to 17% between 1998–2002 and 2006–2018, possibly attributable to corticosteroid use (JAMA 2022).

7.1.6 Antibiotic BBB Penetration Pharmacology

Key principles for CNS antibiotic selection: bactericidal, small molecular weight, lipophilic, low protein binding, low efflux transporter affinity.

Penetration CategoryAntibioticsClinical Relevance
Good CNS penetration (above MIC)Fluoroquinolones, TMP-SMX, Metronidazole, Chloramphenicol, LinezolidReliable CNS levels; useful for targeted therapy
Adequate with inflamed meninges (close to MIC)Penicillins, Cephalosporins, MeropenemMeningeal doses needed; mainstay of empirical Rx
Poor CNS penetration (below MIC / toxic)Aminoglycosides, Colistin, Vancomycin (variable)May need intrathecal administration
Limited experience (below MIC)β-lactam/β-lactamase inhibitor combinationsAvoid as sole CNS-directed therapy

7.1.7 Postoperative & Healthcare-Associated Meningitis

Types: post-craniotomy, VP/VA shunt infection, external ventricular drain (EVD) infection. Of these cases, 60–75% are aseptic (inflammatory reaction to blood products/tumor debris).

Risk factors: posterior fossa surgery, pediatric patients, longer operative time, lack of antibiotic prophylaxis, CSF leakage.

Key discriminator: CSF lactate >4 mmol/L best differentiates bacterial from aseptic postoperative meningitis (Sn 88–97%, Sp 78–98%).

Common organisms: S. aureus (including MRSA), coagulase-negative Staphylococci, Cutibacterium acnes, GNR including Pseudomonas, Acinetobacter.

Empirical treatment: Vancomycin + anti-pseudomonal β-lactam (cefepime, meropenem, or ceftazidime) ± intrathecal/intraventricular vancomycin or aminoglycoside for refractory cases. Hardware removal when feasible.

7.1.8 Brain Abscess

Brain abscess is a focal suppurative infection within the brain parenchyma, typically located at the gray-white matter junction. Three mechanisms: (1) contiguous spread from sinusitis/otitis/mastoiditis/dental (~40–50%), (2) hematogenous seeding from distant focus (~30%), (3) post-neurosurgical/traumatic (~10–20%).

Microbiology

SourceCommon Organisms
Sinusitis / otitis / dentalStreptococci (viridans, anginosus group), anaerobes (Bacteroides, Fusobacterium, Prevotella)
Hematogenous (lung, endocarditis)Staphylococci, Streptococci, polymicrobial
Post-surgical / traumaS. aureus, Enterobacterales, Pseudomonas
ImmunocompromisedNocardia, Aspergillus, Toxoplasma, Listeria, Mycobacteria

Clinical Features & Diagnosis

Headache (70%), focal neurological deficits (50%), fever (50%), seizures (25–35%), nausea/vomiting, papilledema. Classic triad of headache + fever + focal deficit in only ~20%.

Imaging: CT: hypodense lesion with ring enhancement post-contrast. MRI: ring-enhancing lesion at gray-white junction; DWI restriction within abscess cavity is characteristic (differentiates from tumor). Hemorrhage possible.

LP is contraindicated in most cases of brain abscess due to risk of herniation.

Treatment

Medical therapy: Empirical: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h ± Vancomycin (if post-surgical or MRSA risk). Duration: 6–8 weeks IV, often followed by oral step-down.

Surgical intervention: Stereotactic aspiration (preferred for deep/eloquent lesions) or excision (for superficial/encapsulated abscesses). Surgery indicated for: abscess >2.5 cm, significant mass effect, posterior fossa location, failure to improve with antibiotics, need for microbiological diagnosis.

Corticosteroids: Only for significant mass effect/impending herniation; may impair capsule formation and reduce antibiotic penetration. Use for shortest duration possible.

Q: A 55-year-old Thai man presents with fever, headache, and hearing loss. He reports eating raw pork 2 weeks ago. CSF shows WBC 500 (85% PMN), glucose 20 mg/dL, protein 250 mg/dL. What is the most likely organism and optimal treatment?
Most likely organism: Streptococcus suis — subacute meningitis with hearing loss in a Thai patient with raw pork exposure is classic.
Treatment: Ceftriaxone 2g IV q12h × 14–21 days + Dexamethasone 10mg IV q6h × 4 days (reduces hearing loss). Penicillin G is an alternative.
Key points: S. suis is the #2 cause of bacterial meningitis in Thailand. Hearing loss is the most common sequela (53%). Vestibular dysfunction is also common.
Q: A 70-year-old immunocompetent woman presents with acute bacterial meningitis. CSF Gram stain shows gram-positive bacilli. Which antibiotic is essential and why should cephalosporins alone not be used?
Answer: Gram-positive bacilli in CSF suggest Listeria monocytogenes. Ampicillin is essential because Listeria is intrinsically resistant to all cephalosporins. The mechanism is lack of affinity for Listeria PBP3. Add Gentamicin for synergy. Duration ≥21 days. Stop dexamethasone if Listeria is confirmed. Risk factors include age >50, pregnancy, immunosuppression, and chronic liver/renal disease.

7.2 Viral CNS Infections

7.2.1 Viral (Aseptic) Meningitis

Viral meningitis is the most common cause of aseptic meningitis. Most common agents: Enteroviruses (echovirus, coxsackievirus — ~85% of cases), HSV-2, HIV, arboviruses, mumps. CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (5–500 cells), normal glucose, mildly elevated protein (50–100 mg/dL). Generally self-limiting with full recovery.

Mollaret’s Meningitis: Recurrent benign lymphocytic meningitis caused by HSV-2. Episodes of meningitis resolving spontaneously within 2–5 days, with complete recovery between episodes. Diagnosis: CSF HSV-2 PCR positive during episodes. Prophylactic valacyclovir may reduce recurrence frequency.
Important: Acyclovir is NOT Beneficial for Viral Meningitis

Unlike HSV encephalitis (where acyclovir is life-saving), acyclovir has no proven benefit in viral meningitis even when caused by HSV-2. Treatment is supportive. Do not confuse viral meningitis with meningoencephalitis.

Aseptic Meningitis — Differential by CSF Predominant Cell Type

CSF PredominanceDifferential Diagnosis
NeutrophilicPartially treated bacterial meningitis, parameningeal infection (abscess, empyema), early viral (first 24h may be PMN-predominant)
LymphocyticViral (enterovirus, HSV-2, HIV), rickettsial, early chronic meningitis (TB, fungal), drug-induced, autoimmune (SLE, sarcoidosis, Behçet), CNS lymphoma/carcinomatous
Eosinophilic (>10%)Parasitic (Angiostrongylus cantonensis, Gnathostoma spinigerum), TB, Coccidioides, drug reactions, CNS lymphoma

7.2.2 Herpes Simplex Encephalitis (HSE)

HSV-1 encephalitis is the most common cause of sporadic fatal encephalitis worldwide. Mortality untreated: 70%; with acyclovir: 15–20%. Occurs via reactivation of latent virus in trigeminal ganglion or primary infection reaching the temporal lobe via olfactory/trigeminal nerves.

Clinical Features

Acute onset of fever, headache, behavioral changes, confusion, aphasia (dominant temporal lobe), seizures (focal > generalized), focal neurological deficits. Temporal lobe predilection is characteristic. Olfactory hallucinations may occur.

Diagnosis

InvestigationFindings
CSFLymphocytic pleocytosis (10–500), elevated protein, normal glucose, RBCs may be present (hemorrhagic necrosis); CSF HSV-1 PCR is the gold standard (Sn 98%, Sp 94%)
MRI BrainT2/FLAIR hyperintensity in medial temporal lobes (often bilateral but asymmetric), insular cortex, inferior frontal lobe; DWI restriction; hemorrhagic changes
CT BrainMay be normal early; later shows low-density temporal lobe lesions ± hemorrhage
EEGPeriodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over temporal regions; temporal sharp waves
HSE Treatment Protocol

Empirical treatment must NOT be delayed for test results.

Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h × 14–21 days

  • Start immediately on clinical suspicion (fever + encephalopathy + temporal lobe findings)
  • 14 days minimum; extend to 21 days if immunocompromised or severe disease
  • Repeat CSF HSV PCR at day 14 before stopping — if still positive, continue treatment
  • Monitor renal function (crystalline nephropathy risk); adequate hydration essential
  • No role for adjunctive corticosteroids (unlike bacterial meningitis)

Prognosis: Even with treatment, significant morbidity: memory impairment (anterograde > retrograde), behavioral changes, aphasia, epilepsy. Younger age and early treatment predict better outcomes.

Clinical Pearl — HSV-2 Encephalitis: HSV-2 encephalitis occurs primarily in neonates (birth canal transmission) and immunocompromised adults. In adults, HSV-2 more commonly causes meningitis (benign, recurrent) rather than encephalitis.

7.2.3 Varicella-Zoster Virus (VZV) CNS Infections

VZV can cause multiple CNS syndromes beyond typical dermatomal zoster:

SyndromeFeaturesDiagnosis
VZV VasculopathyStroke (large vessel = granulomatous arteritis; small vessel = multifocal leukoencephalopathy). May occur weeks-months after zoster. Can occur without rash (“zoster sine herpete”)CSF VZV PCR and/or anti-VZV IgG in CSF
VZV MeningitisLymphocytic meningitis; self-limitedCSF VZV PCR
VZV EncephalitisDiffuse encephalitis, more common in immunocompromisedCSF VZV PCR + MRI
VZV MyelitisLongitudinally extensive transverse myelitis; consider in differential with NMOSDCSF VZV PCR; spinal MRI
Ramsay Hunt SyndromeVZV reactivation in geniculate ganglion: ear pain, vesicles in ear canal, ipsilateral facial nerve palsy ± CN VIII involvement (hearing loss, vertigo)Clinical; vesicular rash in ear

Treatment: Acyclovir 10–15 mg/kg IV q8h × 10–14 days (higher dose than HSV in some protocols). Ramsay Hunt: Valacyclovir 1g PO TID + corticosteroids.

7.2.4 Cytomegalovirus (CMV) CNS Infections

CMV CNS disease occurs almost exclusively in severely immunocompromised patients (HIV with CD4 <50, transplant recipients).

Syndromes: Ventriculoencephalitis (periventricular enhancement on MRI), polyradiculomyelitis (ascending weakness mimicking GBS, CSF PMN-predominant), retinitis.

Diagnosis: CSF CMV PCR. MRI: periventricular enhancement (ventriculitis).

Treatment: Ganciclovir 5 mg/kg IV q12h + Foscarnet 90 mg/kg IV q12h (combination for CNS disease). Duration: induction ≥3 weeks, then maintenance until immune reconstitution.

7.2.5 Japanese Encephalitis (JE)

Flavivirus transmitted by Culex mosquitoes; most important cause of viral encephalitis in Asia. Endemic in Thailand (especially rainy season, rural/agricultural areas). Incubation 5–15 days.

Clinical features: Acute encephalitis with high fever, altered consciousness, seizures, parkinsonian features (tremor, rigidity, masked facies), dystonia, upper motor neuron signs. Thalamic involvement is characteristic.

Diagnosis: Serum/CSF JE IgM (ELISA). MRI: bilateral thalamic T2 hyperintensity (highly characteristic), substantia nigra, basal ganglia.

Treatment: Supportive only; no specific antiviral. Mortality 20–30%; 30–50% of survivors have significant neurological sequelae (cognitive impairment, movement disorders, epilepsy).

Prevention: Vaccination (SA 14-14-2 live attenuated, or IXIARO inactivated). Included in Thai EPI schedule.

7.2.6 Rabies Encephalitis

Rhabdovirus (Lyssavirus) transmitted via bite of rabid animal (dogs most common in Thailand). Incubation: 1–3 months (range: days to years). Once symptoms appear, virtually 100% fatal.

Two clinical forms:

FormFrequencyFeatures
Furious (encephalitic)~80%Hydrophobia, aerophobia, agitation, fluctuating consciousness, autonomic dysfunction, inspiratory spasms triggered by air/water on face
Paralytic (dumb)~20%Ascending flaccid paralysis (may mimic GBS), less hydrophobia, longer course

Diagnosis: Nuchal skin biopsy (immunofluorescence for rabies antigen), saliva RT-PCR, serum/CSF rabies antibodies. Brain autopsy: Negri bodies (pathognomonic eosinophilic inclusions in hippocampal neurons).

Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP):

  • Wound care: Thorough washing with soap & water + povidone-iodine
  • Rabies immunoglobulin (RIG): 20 IU/kg (human) or 40 IU/kg (equine), infiltrated around wound + remainder IM at site distant from vaccine
  • Rabies vaccine: Thai Red Cross intradermal regimen (2-2-2-0-2): 0.1mL ID × 2 sites on days 0, 3, 7, 28. Or WHO Essen IM regimen: 1.0mL IM days 0, 3, 7, 14, 28
Thai Context: Thailand has an excellent post-exposure prophylaxis program. The Thai Red Cross developed the intradermal (ID) regimen which is WHO-endorsed and widely used in resource-limited settings. Despite this, Thailand still reports 5–10 rabies deaths annually, primarily from delayed PEP. Dog bite is the most common exposure.

7.2.7 HIV-Associated Neurological Disorders

Neurological manifestations occur in 40–70% of HIV-infected patients and may be the presenting feature. The differential depends heavily on the CD4 count.

HIV-Associated Opportunistic CNS Infections by CD4 Count

CD4 CountOpportunistic CNS Infections
<500TB meningitis, herpes zoster, bacterial meningitis
<200PCP, chronic HSV, HIV-associated dementia (HAD)
<100Cerebral toxoplasmosis, Cryptococcal meningitis, CMV encephalitis, M. avium complex (MAC), PML (JC virus), cryptosporidiosis
<50CMV retinitis/encephalitis, MAC (disseminated), Toxoplasma

Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML)

Potentially fatal demyelinating disease caused by reactivation of JC virus in immunosuppressed individuals: AIDS (CD4 <100), transplant recipients, natalizumab therapy (MS treatment). No specific treatment — restore immunity (initiate/optimize ART, reduce immunosuppression).

Clinical features: Subacute progressive neurological deficits: hemiparesis, hemianopia, diplopia, cognitive decline, gait ataxia, seizures. No fever (unlike infections).

Diagnosis: MRI: multifocal, asymmetric white matter T2/FLAIR hyperintensities (no mass effect, no enhancement typically). CSF JC virus PCR (Sn 72–92%). Brain biopsy if PCR negative but clinical suspicion high.

Prognosis: Without immune reconstitution, uniformly fatal within months. With ART-induced immune reconstitution, ~50% survival; however, risk of PML-IRIS (paradoxical worsening with inflammation).

Q: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute onset fever, confusion, word-finding difficulty, and seizures over 3 days. MRI shows T2 hyperintensity in the left medial temporal lobe and insular cortex with DWI restriction. What is the diagnosis and what is the most critical next step?
Diagnosis: HSV-1 encephalitis (HSE) — acute febrile encephalopathy with temporal lobe predilection, aphasia, and seizures is classic.
Most critical next step: Start Acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h immediately while obtaining CSF for HSV PCR. Do NOT delay treatment for diagnostic testing. LP: expect lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, ± RBCs. CSF HSV-1 PCR confirms diagnosis (may be negative in first 72h — repeat if initially negative). Duration: 14–21 days. EEG may show PLEDs. Untreated mortality is 70%.
Q: An HIV-positive patient (CD4 = 45) presents with progressive left-sided weakness and visual field cut over 3 weeks without fever. MRI shows multifocal non-enhancing white matter lesions. What is the most likely diagnosis?
Diagnosis: Progressive Multifocal Leukoencephalopathy (PML) due to JC virus reactivation.
Key features: Subacute progressive focal deficits without fever, CD4 <100, non-enhancing asymmetric white matter lesions on MRI (no mass effect). Confirm with CSF JC virus PCR. No specific antiviral treatment — initiate ART to restore immunity. Brain biopsy if PCR negative. Beware PML-IRIS (paradoxical worsening after starting ART, with new contrast enhancement).

7.3 Tuberculous Meningitis (TBM)

7.3.1 Overview & Epidemiology

TBM is the most severe form of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, with mortality of 20–50% and significant neurological morbidity in survivors. It represents ~1% of all TB cases but carries the highest case-fatality rate. TBM accounts for 27% of meningitis in Thai adults (Ramathibodi data).

Delayed treatment is strongly associated with death and neurological sequelae. Empirical therapy should be given unless an alternative diagnosis is confirmed.

Spectrum of Neurotuberculosis

ManifestationDescription
TBMMeningitis, basal arachnoiditis, spinal meningitis
TuberculomaIntracranial (parenchymal or extraparenchymal) or spinal mass lesion
TB abscessRare; larger cavitary lesion, often multiloculated
TB encephalopathyWith or without meningitis; diffuse cerebral edema
Spinal TBSecondary to skeletal TB (Pott’s disease) or meningeal TB

7.3.2 Clinical Presentation

TBM typically presents as subacute meningitis evolving over 1–4 weeks (unlike acute bacterial meningitis).

Symptom/SignFrequency
Fever~90%
Meningism (headache, neck stiffness)~88%
Focal neurological deficit~52%
Altered consciousness33–72%
Cranial nerve palsy (especially CN VI, III)20–40%
Seizures~20%
BMRC (British Medical Research Council) Staging
StageDefinitionPrognosis
Stage IGCS 15, no focal deficitsGood outcome if treated early
Stage IIGCS 11–14, or any focal deficitIntermediate
Stage IIIGCS ≤10 (coma)Mortality >50%, severe disability in survivors

7.3.3 Diagnosis

CSF Findings

CSF ParameterTypical Findings in TBM
Opening pressureElevated (>25 cmH2O) in ~50%
WBC100–500 cells/mm³, lymphocyte predominant (may be neutrophilic in early <10 days)
Protein100–500 mg/dL (can reach >1000 in spinal block)
Glucose<45 mg/dL in ~80% (CSF:serum ratio <0.5)
ADA>40 IU/L (Sn ~79%); less specific in HIV
Diagnostic Pitfalls in TBM:
• Elderly patients may have acellular CSF despite active TBM
• Early TBM (<10 days) may show neutrophil predominance mimicking bacterial meningitis
• HIV co-infection alters CSF profile: lower WBC, lower protein, ADA less reliable
• Normal CSF does not exclude TBM in early disease

Microbiological Diagnosis

TestSensitivityNotes
AFB smear (Ziehl-Neelsen)5–30%Low yield; improved by large-volume CSF (10–20 mL) and repeated sampling
CSF culture (MGIT/LJ)30–50%Gold standard but takes 2–8 weeks; do NOT wait for results before treating
CSF TB PCR (GeneXpert MTB/RIF)56–95% (pooled ~60–70%)WHO-endorsed; rapid (~2h); also detects rifampicin resistance; lower Sn in CSF than sputum
GeneXpert Ultra70–95%Improved sensitivity over Xpert MTB/RIF; WHO recommends as initial test (conditional, 2017)
ADA (>10 IU/L)Sn 79%, Sp 91%Useful adjunct; threshold varies by study

Neuroimaging in TBM

MRI findings: Basal meningeal enhancement (basal cisterns), hydrocephalus (communicating or obstructive), tuberculomas (ring-enhancing lesions), infarcts (especially basal ganglia — due to vasculitis of perforating arteries). CT: basal cistern enhancement, hydrocephalus, tuberculomas.

Lancet Consensus Diagnostic Criteria for TBM (Marais et al. 2010)

Scoring system incorporating clinical (max 6), CSF (max 4), cerebral imaging (max 6), and evidence of TB elsewhere (max 4). Total score ≥12 = definite, 10–11 = probable, 6–9 = possible. This scoring aids clinical decision-making when microbiological confirmation is pending or unavailable.

7.3.4 Treatment of TBM

Anti-TB Regimen for CNS Tuberculosis (BIS 2009 / WHO)
DrugDoseDurationCSF Penetration
Isoniazid (H)300 mg/day (5 mg/kg)10–12 monthsExcellent — penetrates CSF freely; potent bactericidal
Rifampicin (R)450 mg (<50 kg) / 600 mg (>50 kg)10–12 monthsModerate — penetrates less well; high mortality from rifampicin-resistant TBM
Pyrazinamide (Z)1.5 g (<50 kg) / 2.0 g (>50 kg)2 monthsExcellent — well-absorbed orally, high CSF concentration
Ethambutol (E)15 mg/kg2 monthsPoor; no controlled trial data for CNS TB. Caution: optic neuropathy

Total treatment duration: at least 10–12 months. Can prolong until lesion subsides (especially tuberculomas).

Alternatives for the 4th drug: Streptomycin (caution: pregnancy, renal impairment, resistance common), Levofloxacin (limited data for prolonged therapy; caution in pregnancy/breastfeeding).

Adjunctive Corticosteroids in TBM

DrugDoseDuration
Dexamethasone0.3–0.4 mg/kg/day (dose depends on BMRC stage)4 weeks, then gradual taper over 2–4 weeks
PrednisoloneEquivalent dose (alternative)4 weeks + taper
Landmark Trial: Thwaites et al. NEJM 2004

Thwaites Trial: RCT of adjunctive dexamethasone in Vietnamese adults with TBM (n=545). Dexamethasone significantly reduced mortality (RR 0.69; 9-month mortality 31.8% vs 41.3%) across all BMRC stages. Effect most pronounced in Stage II/III. However, did NOT reduce disability among survivors. Now standard of care worldwide.

Clinical Pearl — Paradoxical Reaction: 10–30% of TBM patients develop paradoxical worsening (new/enlarging tuberculomas, increasing meningeal enhancement) during appropriate treatment due to immune reconstitution. Management: continue anti-TB, add/increase corticosteroids. More common with HIV co-infection (TBM-IRIS).

7.3.5 TBM in HIV-Positive Patients

All patients with suspected CNS TB should be tested for HIV. The treatment regimen is the same as for HIV-negative patients, including corticosteroids.

ART Timing in CNS TB + HIV (BIS 2009)
CD4 CountRecommended ART Timing
>200 cells/µLDefer ART until end of TB treatment; start if CD4 falls <200 during treatment
100–200 cells/µLStart ART after ~2 months of anti-TB treatment
<100 cells/µLStart ART within first 2 weeks of anti-TB treatment

Key considerations:

  • Drug interactions: Rifampicin is a potent CYP3A4 inducer; use Efavirenz (not boosted PIs) or Dolutegravir 50mg BID (double dose) with rifampicin
  • IRIS risk: especially when CD4 <50; can be life-threatening in TBM (worsening meningitis, hydrocephalus, new tuberculomas)
  • Overlapping toxicities: hepatotoxicity (isoniazid + ART), peripheral neuropathy (isoniazid + stavudine/didanosine)

7.3.6 Complications & Management

Hydrocephalus

Communicating hydrocephalus is the most common complication of TBM, occurring in 60–80% of cases. Caused by basal cistern exudates blocking CSF absorption at arachnoid granulations.

Management: Serial therapeutic LP (if communicating), EVD for acute obstructive hydrocephalus, VP shunt for chronic/persistent hydrocephalus. Acetazolamide/furosemide have limited role.

Vasculitis & Stroke

TBM vasculitis affects perforating arteries at the base of the brain (lenticulostriate, thalamoperforating), causing basal ganglia and thalamic infarcts in 30–40% of TBM cases. Large vessel (MCA, ACA) involvement is less common but more devastating.

Management: Anti-TB therapy + corticosteroids. Aspirin 150 mg/day has shown mortality benefit in one RCT (Misra et al. Neurology 2010; n=118; mortality 14.6% vs 26.7% for Stage II/III).

Cranial Nerve Palsies

CN VI (most common), CN III, and CN VII affected due to basal meningeal inflammation. Usually improve with anti-TB treatment. Vision loss can occur from optochiasmatic arachnoiditis (requires urgent treatment).

Q: A 35-year-old Thai man presents with 2-week history of headache, low-grade fever, and progressive confusion. CSF shows WBC 250 (80% lymphocytes), protein 280 mg/dL, glucose 28 mg/dL (serum 110), ADA 52. CT shows basal cistern enhancement and hydrocephalus. What is the diagnosis and management plan?
Diagnosis: Tuberculous meningitis (BMRC Stage II — GCS ~12 with altered consciousness).
Diagnostic workup: CSF for AFB smear, GeneXpert MTB/RIF (or Ultra), culture; HIV test; CXR (pulmonary TB in ~50%).
Treatment (do NOT wait for microbiological confirmation):
1. Anti-TB: HRZE intensive phase (2 months) → HR continuation (total 10–12 months)
2. Dexamethasone 0.3–0.4 mg/kg/day × 4 weeks, then taper over 2–4 weeks
3. Hydrocephalus management: serial therapeutic LP (if communicating); EVD/VP shunt if progressive
4. If HIV+: test CD4 and plan ART timing accordingly
5. Pyridoxine 50 mg/day (prevent isoniazid neuropathy)
Prognosis: Stage II mortality ~20–30% with treatment; better if started early.

7.4 Fungal CNS Infections

7.4.1 Cryptococcal Meningitis

Cryptococcus neoformans is a budding yeast with polysaccharide capsule, the most common cause of meningitis in HIV-positive patients and the most common cause of meningitis overall at Ramathibodi Hospital (37% of all meningitis). Acquisition: inhalation of spores from pigeon droppings and soil. Hematogenous dissemination to CNS with predilection for meninges.

Clinical Features

S&S: Subacute-chronic headache (most common), increased ICP, altered consciousness, focal deficits. Meningism may be absent in immunocompromised. Can present as meningitis or cryptococcoma (mass lesion). Fever in ~70%. Papilledema in ~30%.

Important: Elevated ICP is a major cause of morbidity/mortality. Opening pressure >25 cmH2O in >60% of HIV-associated cases.

Diagnosis

TestPerformanceNotes
CSF CrAg (Cryptococcal Antigen)Sn >99%, Sp >99%Best initial test; lateral flow assay (LFA) rapid point-of-care. Titer correlates with fungal burden
Serum CrAgSn >99% (in HIV)Can be used for screening; if serum CrAg+ → LP mandatory
India Ink stainSn 50–80%Encapsulated budding yeast; less sensitive than CrAg
CSF Fungal cultureSn 95–100%Gold standard for species identification; growth in 2–7 days on SDA
CSF profile—Mildly elevated WBC (lymph predominance), mildly elevated protein, normal/decreased glucose. May be near-normal in AIDS (paucicellular)
Clinical Pearl: In severely immunocompromised HIV patients, CSF may appear almost normal despite massive fungal burden. Always check CrAg even with normal CSF parameters. Serum CrAg screening in HIV patients with CD4 <100 before starting ART can prevent cryptococcal meningitis (preemptive fluconazole if CrAg+).

Treatment: WHO 2022 Guidelines

WHO 2022 Treatment of Cryptococcal Meningitis in HIV
PhaseOptionRegimen
Induction
(Week 1–2)		 1. Preferred Single-dose Liposomal Amphotericin B 10 mg/kg (Day 1) + Flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day ÷4 × 14 days + Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 14 days
2. If L-AmB unavailableAmphotericin B deoxycholate 1 mg/kg/day + Flucytosine × 1 week; then Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 1 week
3. If no AmB availableFluconazole 1200 mg/day + Flucytosine × 2 weeks
4. If no flucytosineL-AmB 3–4 mg/kg/day + Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 2 weeks
5. If neither L-AmB nor flucytosineAmB deoxycholate 1 mg/kg/day + Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 2 weeks
Consolidation
(Weeks 8–10)		Fluconazole 800 mg/day
Maintenance
(Onwards)		Fluconazole 200 mg/day — continue until CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART, VL suppressed, and ≥1 year of antifungal therapy
Landmark Trial: AMBITION (NEJM 2022)

AMBITION Trial: Open-label RCT in 5 African countries (n=844). Single high-dose liposomal AmB 10 mg/kg + flucytosine + fluconazole was non-inferior to 7-day AmB deoxycholate-based regimen for 10-week all-cause mortality (24.8% vs 28.7%). Significantly fewer adverse events (less nephrotoxicity, anemia, hypokalemia). Now the WHO-preferred regimen.

ICP Management

Elevated ICP is the major killer in cryptococcal meningitis. Aggressive ICP management is critical.

  • Therapeutic LP: Daily LP to normalize pressure if OP >25 cmH2O; remove 20–30 mL CSF per session until OP <20. Repeat daily until stabilized.
  • Lumbar drain or VP shunt: For refractory elevated ICP
  • Mannitol/acetazolamide: NOT recommended (ineffective, may be harmful)
  • Corticosteroids: NOT recommended for ICP management in cryptococcal meningitis (Beardsley et al. NEJM 2016: dexamethasone increased mortality)
ART Timing in Cryptococcal Meningitis: Delay ART for at least 4–6 weeks after starting antifungal therapy to avoid IRIS. The COAT Trial (Boulware, NEJM 2014) showed early ART (<2 weeks) increased mortality (45% vs 30% at 26 weeks) compared to deferred ART (5 weeks).

7.4.2 CNS Aspergillosis

Aspergillus spp. (septate mold) causes invasive CNS disease in severely immunocompromised patients: neutropenic (post-chemotherapy, HSCT), solid organ transplant, high-dose corticosteroids. Mechanism: hematogenous dissemination from pulmonary or direct extension from sinonasal aspergillosis.

S&S: Sinusitis, endophthalmitis, headache, seizure, cranial neuropathies, focal neurological deficits. CNS aspergillosis carries extremely high mortality (>90%).

Imaging: Multiple ring-enhancing or non-enhancing lesions, often hemorrhagic. Aspergillus has tropism for blood vessels → hemorrhagic infarction is characteristic. May see sino-orbital invasion with skull base erosion.

Diagnosis: Brain biopsy (septate hyphae with 45° branching); CSF galactomannan (variable sensitivity); culture.

Treatment: Voriconazole 6 mg/kg IV q12h ×2 doses, then 4 mg/kg IV q12h (drug of choice). Alternatives: Isavuconazole, Posaconazole, Liposomal Amphotericin B. Surgical debridement when possible. Restore immunity (G-CSF, reduce immunosuppression).

7.4.3 Mucormycosis (Mucorales)

Non-septate (pauciseptate) mold — Mucor, Rhizopus spp. Risk factors: uncontrolled DM (especially DKA), neutropenia, transplant, deferoxamine use, COVID-19-associated mucormycosis (CAM). Angioinvasive with rapid tissue necrosis.

Rhino-orbito-cerebral form: Most common CNS presentation. Starts in paranasal sinuses → invades orbit → cavernous sinus → brain. S&S: sinusitis, periorbital/facial pain, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, black necrotic eschar on palate/nasal mucosa, cranial neuropathies, cavernous sinus thrombosis.

Diagnosis: Biopsy showing broad, ribbon-like, pauciseptate hyphae with irregular (90°) branching. Culture on SDA. MRI: sino-orbital soft tissue enhancement, perineural invasion, cavernous sinus involvement.

Treatment:

  • Surgical debridement — ESSENTIAL, aggressive, often disfiguring but life-saving
  • Liposomal Amphotericin B 5–10 mg/kg/day (first-line). Duration: weeks-months
  • Step-down: Posaconazole or Isavuconazole
  • Note: Mucorales are resistant to voriconazole (unlike Aspergillus)
  • Restore immunity; correct DKA, reverse neutropenia

7.4.4 Other Fungal CNS Infections

Dimorphic Fungi

OrganismGeographic DistributionCNS ManifestationTreatment
Talaromyces (Penicillium) marneffeiSoutheast Asia (Thailand, Vietnam) — HIV/AIDS, anti-IFN-γ AbMeningitis, brain abscess (rare CNS involvement, ~5%)L-AmB induction → Itraconazole maintenance
Histoplasma capsulatumAmericas (Ohio/Mississippi river valleys), AfricaChronic meningitis, mass lesionsL-AmB × 4–6 weeks → Itraconazole × ≥1 year
Coccidioides immitisSouthwestern US, Mexico, Central/South AmericaBasilar meningitis (CSF eosinophils), hydrocephalusFluconazole (lifelong in meningitis) — one of few fungal meningitides where azole alone is primary

Candida CNS Infections

CNS candidiasis occurs in: neonates, neurosurgical patients (post-operative, shunts), disseminated candidiasis. Manifests as meningitis, brain abscess, or ventriculitis.

Treatment: Liposomal Amphotericin B ± Flucytosine (induction) → Fluconazole (step-down). Remove infected hardware.

Thai Context — Fungal Classification Approach: In Southeast Asia, key CNS-relevant fungi include:
• Yeasts: Cryptococcus (most important), Candida
• Dimorphic fungi: Talaromyces marneffei (endemic in northern Thailand, especially Chiang Rai), Histoplasma
• Molds — Septate: Aspergillus, Fusarium, Pseudallescheria boydii
• Molds — Non-septate: Mucor, Rhizopus (Mucorales)
Q: An HIV-positive patient (CD4 = 35) presents with 10-day headache and confusion. CSF: OP 38 cmH2O, WBC 15 (100% lymph), protein 60 mg/dL, glucose 38 mg/dL. CSF India Ink shows encapsulated budding yeast. CrAg titer 1:2048. What is the treatment plan?
Diagnosis: Cryptococcal meningitis with elevated ICP (OP 38).
Treatment plan:
1. Induction (preferred WHO 2022): Single-dose Liposomal AmB 10 mg/kg (Day 1) + Flucytosine 100 mg/kg/day ÷4 × 14 days + Fluconazole 1200 mg/day × 14 days
2. ICP management (CRITICAL): Daily therapeutic LP, removing 20–30 mL CSF until OP <20 cmH2O. This is the most important mortality-reducing intervention.
3. Consolidation: Fluconazole 800 mg/day × 8 weeks
4. Maintenance: Fluconazole 200 mg/day (until CD4 >200 for 6+ months on ART)
5. Delay ART for 4–6 weeks (COAT trial: early ART increases mortality)
6. Do NOT give corticosteroids (Beardsley NEJM 2016: increased mortality)

7.5 Cerebral Toxoplasmosis & CNS Parasites

7.5.1 Cerebral Toxoplasmosis

Toxoplasma gondii is an obligate intracellular protozoan. Transmission: ingestion of oocysts from cat feces or undercooked meat containing tissue cysts. In immunocompetent hosts, infection is usually asymptomatic. Reactivation of tissue cysts in immunosuppressed persons (especially AIDS with CD4 <100) causes toxoplasmic encephalitis — the most common cause of intracerebral mass lesions in AIDS.

Clinical Features

Headache, seizure, altered consciousness, focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis). Subacute onset over days to weeks. Fever in ~50%.

Diagnosis

Usually presumptive: clinical syndrome + positive Toxoplasma IgG + characteristic imaging in an HIV patient with CD4 <100.

Imaging: CT/MRI shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions with surrounding edema, typically at corticomedullary junction and basal ganglia. Classic “eccentric target sign” (eccentric enhancing nodule within ring).

If no clinical improvement after empirical therapy × 10–14 days → brain biopsy to exclude CNS lymphoma.

Treatment

SettingRegimenDuration / Comments
Compromised host (AIDS/transplant) — Primary regimen Pyrimethamine 200mg load, then 50–75mg/day + Sulfadiazine 4–6g/day + Leucovorin 10–25mg/day Treat 6 weeks (acute), then secondary suppression
Alternative regimen TMP-SMX (co-trimoxazole) 5mg/kg TMP component q12h 6 weeks; WHO preferred in resource-limited settings
Alternative regimen Pyrimethamine + Clindamycin 600mg q6h If sulfa allergy
Secondary prophylaxis TMP-SMX 1 DS tab daily (or pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine) Continue until CD4 >200 for ≥6 months on ART
Competent host Usually no treatment needed Treat active chorioretinitis (3 weeks), pregnancy (4 weeks or through pregnancy), congenital (at least 1 year)

7.5.2 Neurocysticercosis (NCC)

Caused by the larval form (cysticercus) of Taenia solium (pork tapeworm). Most common parasitic CNS infection worldwide and leading cause of acquired epilepsy in endemic areas. Transmission: fecal-oral ingestion of T. solium eggs (NOT from eating pork; that causes intestinal taeniasis).

Clinical Manifestations

Depends on cyst location, number, and host inflammatory response:

StageImagingSymptoms
Vesicular (viable cyst)Cystic lesion with scolex, no edema/enhancementUsually asymptomatic
Colloidal vesicular (degenerating)Ring enhancement, perilesional edemaSeizures (most common presentation), headache
Granular nodularNodular enhancement, decreasing edemaSeizures, resolving symptoms
CalcifiedCalcified nodule, no enhancementMay still cause seizures (perilesional edema flares)

Special forms:

  • Subarachnoid NCC: Racemose cysts in basal cisterns; causes chronic meningitis, hydrocephalus, vasculitis, cranial neuropathies. Worst prognosis.
  • Intraventricular NCC: Cyst in ventricle; acute obstructive hydrocephalus (emergency). Requires endoscopic removal.
  • Spinal NCC: Rare; cord compression.

Diagnosis

Del Brutto Criteria (2017): Absolute criteria: histologic demonstration or cystic lesion with scolex on imaging. Major criteria: enhancing lesions, multilobulated subarachnoid cysts, typical parenchymal calcifications. Minor/epidemiologic criteria: positive serology (EITB/ELISA), endemic area, suggestive clinical features.

Serology: EITB (enzyme-linked immunoelectrotransfer blot) is best (Sn 98% for >2 cysts; lower for single cyst). ELISA less specific.

Treatment

NCC Treatment Algorithm

1–2 viable parenchymal cysts: Albendazole 15 mg/kg/day (max 1200mg) × 10–14 days + corticosteroids (dexamethasone or prednisolone started 1–2 days before and continued during). Add anti-epileptic drugs for seizures.

>2 viable parenchymal cysts: Albendazole + Praziquantel 50 mg/kg/day × 10–14 days (combination is superior per Garcia et al. Lancet ID 2014).

Subarachnoid/racemose NCC: Prolonged albendazole (months) + corticosteroids; often requires VP shunt for hydrocephalus. Methotrexate may be needed as steroid-sparing agent.

Intraventricular NCC: Endoscopic removal (treatment of choice); albendazole pre/post-operatively. EVD for acute hydrocephalus.

Calcified NCC only: No antiparasitic therapy; manage seizures (AEDs ± short-course steroids for perilesional edema).

Do NOT give antiparasitic therapy for: Cysticercotic encephalitis (massive cyst burden with diffuse edema — steroids only), calcified-only disease.

7.5.3 Eosinophilic Meningitis (Thai-Relevant Parasites)

Angiostrongylus cantonensis

Rat lungworm; transmission via ingestion of raw snails, slugs, or contaminated produce. Most common cause of eosinophilic meningitis worldwide, highly relevant in Thailand and Southeast Asia.

Clinical features: Headache (severe), meningism, cranial nerve palsies (especially CN VII), paresthesias; self-limited over 2–4 weeks in most cases.

CSF: Eosinophils >10% (often >50%), elevated protein, normal glucose, very high opening pressure. Larvae rarely found in CSF.

Treatment: Supportive (analgesics, serial therapeutic LP for headache relief). Corticosteroids (prednisolone 60 mg/day × 2 weeks) reduce headache duration. Avoid anthelmintics in acute phase (may worsen inflammation from dying larvae). Albendazole controversial.

Gnathostoma spinigerum

Acquired from eating raw freshwater fish (“som fak,” “pla ra” in Thai cuisine). Highly relevant in Thailand.

CNS manifestation: Eosinophilic meningoencephalitis/myelitis (worm migrates through CNS). Can cause subarachnoid hemorrhage, radiculomyelitis, cranial nerve palsies. More severe than Angiostrongylus — can be fatal.

CSF: Bloody or xanthochromic (pathognomonic of CNS gnathostomiasis), eosinophilic.

Treatment: Albendazole 400mg BID × 21 days + corticosteroids. Surgical removal if worm accessible.

Thai Pearl — Eosinophilic Meningitis DDx: In Thailand, when CSF shows eosinophils >10%:
• Angiostrongylus cantonensis (snails/slugs) — most common, self-limited
• Gnathostoma spinigerum (raw fish) — bloody CSF, more severe
• Toxocara (dog/cat roundworms) — rare CNS
• Coccidioides — not endemic in Thailand
• Drug reaction, TB, lymphoma — non-parasitic causes
Ask about dietary history: raw snail/slug → Angiostrongylus; raw freshwater fish → Gnathostoma.

7.5.4 Cerebral Malaria

Caused by Plasmodium falciparum; defined as unarousable coma (GCS ≤11 in adults, BCS ≤2 in children) in the setting of P. falciparum parasitemia with no other identifiable cause. Mechanism: sequestration of parasitized RBCs in cerebral microvasculature.

Clinical features: Diffuse encephalopathy, seizures (especially in children), retinopathy (pathognomonic: retinal whitening, vessel changes, hemorrhages). Mortality 15–25% with treatment.

Treatment: IV Artesunate 2.4 mg/kg at 0, 12, 24h, then daily (WHO first-line; superior to quinine based on SEAQUAMAT and AQUAMAT trials). Complete course with ACT orally once able. Supportive: seizure management, ICP management, transfusion if severe anemia.

7.6 CNS Infections in the Immunocompromised Host

7.6.1 Framework: Immune Defect → Pathogen Prediction

Understanding which arm of immunity is compromised predicts the likely CNS pathogen:

Immune DefectMechanismTypical CNS Pathogens
B-cell / Antibody deficiency
(Rituximab, splenectomy, CVID, hypogammaglobulinemia)		 Loss of opsonization, complement activation S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Staphylococci; Enteroviruses (chronic meningoencephalitis in agammaglobulinemia)
T-cell / Cellular immunity deficiency
(HIV/AIDS, transplant, CNI, ATG, high-dose steroids, natalizumab)		 Loss of intracellular pathogen control Intracellular pathogens: Cryptococcus, Toxoplasma, TB/NTM, Listeria, CMV, VZV, JC virus (PML), Aspergillus, Candida, Pneumocystis, Salmonella
Complement deficiency
(Eculizumab, terminal complement)		 Loss of MAC formation Neisseria meningitidis (up to 2000-fold increased risk)
Neutrophil deficiency / dysfunction
(Post-chemotherapy, AML, HSCT pre-engraftment)		 Loss of phagocytosis Staphylococci, Gram-negatives, Aspergillus, Candida, Mucorales
Immunosuppressive Drugs & Their Immune Targets
CategoryDrugPrimary Immune Effect
Loss of T-cell function Basiliximab (anti-IL2R)T & B cell depletion
ATG (anti-thymocyte globulin)T cell depletion
Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine)Inhibit T cell proliferation
Mycophenolate / AzathioprineInhibit T & B cell proliferation
High-dose glucocorticoidsBroad immunosuppression, T > B
Loss of B-cell / antibody function Rituximab (anti-CD20)B cell depletion
Splenectomy / aspleniaLoss of opsonophagocytic function
PlasmapheresisRemoves all antibodies
Complement inhibition EculizumabTerminal complement (C5) inhibition

Level of immune suppression: IV Cyclophosphamide > Mycophenolate = Azathioprine. All suppress T cells > B cells.

7.6.2 Anti-IFN-γ Autoantibody Syndrome

Emerging entity highly relevant in Southeast Asia (Thailand, Taiwan). Patients develop neutralizing autoantibodies against IFN-γ, causing acquired immunodeficiency resembling HIV/AIDS but without HIV infection.

Typical infections: Disseminated NTM (M. abscessus, MAC), Talaromyces marneffei, Salmonella, Cryptococcus, Burkholderia pseudomallei, VZV.

CNS involvement: Meningitis (NTM, Crypto), brain abscess, pachymeningitis.

Treatment: Prolonged antimicrobial therapy + rituximab (to deplete B cells producing anti-IFN-γ Ab) + cyclophosphamide in refractory cases.

7.6.3 Post-Transplant CNS Infections Timeline

PeriodCommon CNS Infections
<1 monthNosocomial bacteria (S. aureus, GNR), Aspergillus (neutropenic HSCT), HSV reactivation
1–6 monthsCMV, Aspergillus, Toxoplasma, Listeria, Nocardia, PML (JC virus), TB
>6 monthsCryptococcus, PML, community-acquired infections, VZV

7.7 Neuroimaging in CNS Infections

Imaging Classification of CNS Infections

LocationFocalDiffuse
Extra-axialEpidural abscess, subdural empyemaPachymeningitis, leptomeningitis
Intra-axialBrain abscess (gray-white junction), tuberculomaEncephalitis (temporal/basal for HSV; diffuse for others)

Key Imaging Patterns by Pathogen

Imaging PatternPathogen / Diagnosis
Medial temporal lobe T2 hyperintensityHSV-1 encephalitis
Bilateral thalamic T2 hyperintensityJapanese encephalitis
Basal meningeal enhancement + hydrocephalusTBM
Multiple ring-enhancing lesions + edema (at gray-white junction)Cerebral toxoplasmosis (AIDS), brain abscess, metastases
Ring-enhancing lesion with DWI restrictionBrain abscess (pyogenic)
Asymmetric non-enhancing white matter lesions (no mass effect)PML (JC virus)
Periventricular enhancementCMV ventriculitis
Cystic lesion with scolexNeurocysticercosis
Hemorrhagic lesion + ring enhancement + sino-orbital invasionMucormycosis or Aspergillosis
Imaging Pearl: CT is useful for rapid emergency assessment (hydrocephalus, mass effect, hemorrhage). MRI is superior for detailed characterization of CNS infections (meningeal enhancement, parenchymal lesions, DWI for abscess). Always compare imaging findings with clinical context and immune status.

7.8 References & Guidelines

Key Guidelines

  1. ESCMID Guideline on Acute Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Microbiol Infect 2016;22:S37–S62.
  2. IDSA Practice Guidelines for Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004;39:1267–84.
  3. WHO Guidelines on Cryptococcal Disease in HIV-infected Adults, Adolescents and Children. WHO 2022.
  4. Thwaites G et al. British Infection Society Guidelines for TB of the CNS. J Infect 2009;59:167–87.

Landmark Trials & Key Studies

  1. Thwaites Trial: Thwaites GE et al. Dexamethasone for TBM in adults & adolescents. NEJM 2004;351:1741–51.
  2. AMBITION Trial: Jarvis JN et al. Single-dose L-AmB for Cryptococcal Meningitis. NEJM 2022;386:1109–20.
  3. COAT Trial: Boulware DR et al. Timing of ART after Cryptococcal Meningitis. NEJM 2014;370:2487–98.
  4. Beardsley Trial: Beardsley J et al. Adjunctive Dexamethasone in Cryptococcal Meningitis. NEJM 2016;374:542–54.
  5. de Gans J, van de Beek D. Dexamethasone in Adults with Bacterial Meningitis. NEJM 2002;347:1549–56.
  6. Cochrane Review: Corticosteroids for Bacterial Meningitis. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2015;9:CD004405.
  7. Brouwer MC et al. Community-acquired Bacterial Meningitis. Lancet 2012;380:1684–92.
  8. van de Beek D et al. Bacterial Meningitis. Lancet 2021;398:1171–83.

  11. Garcia HH et al. Combined Albendazole + Praziquantel for NCC. Lancet Infect Dis 2014;14:687–95.

  14. Ngan NT et al. Treatment of Cryptococcal Meningitis. Drugs 2022.

Thai Data & Local Sources

  1. Aimbudlop et al. Etiology of meningitis in Thai adults. J Infect Chemother (Ramathibodi data).
  2. Thammasat Meningitis Series. Neurology Asia 2020.
  3. NARST (National Antimicrobial Resistance Surveillance Thailand). 2022 Annual Report, 68 hospitals.
  4. Pakpoom Phoompoung. CNS Infection in Immunocompetent Host. Siriraj Hospital Lecture Series.
  5. Thananya Wongsinin. Treatment of Neurological Infection in Immunocompromised Setting. CNMI, Ramathibodi/Mahidol University.
  6. Abhinbhen W. Saraya. Treatment of Neurological Infection in Community Setting. King Chulalongkorn Memorial Hospital, Thai Red Cross.
  7. Siriththin Chansirikarnjana. Imaging in Infectious Disease. NIT Radiology.

8. Dementia & Cognitive Disorders

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of dementia and cognitive disorders. Coverage includes the neurodegenerative proteinopathy framework (molecular mechanisms, protein misfolding, prion-like propagation), Alzheimer’s disease (amyloidopathy, tauopathy, neuroinflammation, genetics — Goldman score & 17-gene panel, epigenetics), the NIA-AA 2024 revised biomarker framework (ATX(N)IVS with T1/T2 split), plasma biomarker revolution (p-tau217), FDG-PET signature patterns, diagnostic pathway algorithms, disease-modifying anti-amyloid therapy (lecanemab CLARITY-AD, donanemab TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2, ARIA monitoring), symptomatic pharmacotherapy, DLB (McKeith 2017, αSyn-SAA), FTD spectrum (bvFTD, PPA variants, C9orf72/GRN/MAPT genetics, FTD-ALS continuum, PSP & CBS), vascular cognitive impairment, NPH, reversible dementias, and rapidly progressive dementia including prion diseases.

8.1 Neurodegenerative Proteinopathy — Molecular Framework

8.1.1 The Proteinopathy Paradigm

Modern understanding of neurodegenerative diseases has shifted from purely clinical-anatomical classification to a molecular proteinopathy framework. All major neurodegenerative dementias share a common pathogenic cascade: protein misfolding → oligomer formation → fibrillar aggregation → prion-like propagation → selective neuronal vulnerability → network degeneration.

Clinicopathological Spectrum of Neurodegenerative Proteinopathy
Misfolded ProteinNormal FunctionDisease SpectrumKey Pathology
Aβ (Amyloid-beta)APP: synapse formation, neuroplasticity, iron exportAlzheimer’s disease, CAAExtracellular amyloid plaques (diffuse & neuritic)
Tau (3R/4R)Microtubule stabilization, axonal transportAD (secondary), PSP, CBD, Pick disease, FTLD-tau, AGD, CTENeurofibrillary tangles (NFT), neuropil threads
α-SynucleinSynaptic vesicular transport, SNARE complexPD, DLB, MSA, PDDLewy bodies, Lewy neurites, glial cytoplasmic inclusions (MSA)
TDP-43RNA metabolism, splicing, transportFTLD-TDP, ALS, FTD-MND, LATECytoplasmic inclusions with nuclear clearing
FUSRNA metabolism, DNA repairFTLD-FUS, ALS-FUSCytoplasmic/intranuclear inclusions
PrPSc (Prion)PrPC: copper homeostasis, cell signalingCJD, vCJD, FFI, GSS, KuruSpongiform change, PrP deposits

8.1.2 Molecular Mechanisms of Protein Misfolding

The pathogenic cascade involves failure of protein quality control (PQC) systems:

PQC MechanismFunctionFailure Consequence
Molecular chaperones (HSP70, HSP90)Assist correct folding, prevent aggregationAccumulation of misfolded monomers
Ubiquitin-proteasome system (UPS)Degrade short-lived/misfolded proteinsUbiquitinated protein aggregates
Autophagy-lysosome pathway (ALP)Clear aggregated proteins, damaged organellesFailure to clear oligomers/fibrils

Prion-like propagation: Misfolded proteins act as “seeds” that template the misfolding of normal proteins. Cell-to-cell spread follows anatomically connected neural networks, explaining the stereotypical regional progression of each disease (e.g., Braak staging in AD and PD).

Clinical Pearl — Why Proteinopathy Matters for Clinicians: Understanding the molecular substrate guides: (1) biomarker selection (Aβ, p-tau, αSyn-SAA, NfL), (2) therapeutic targets (anti-amyloid antibodies, tau aggregation inhibitors, α-synuclein immunotherapy), (3) recognition of mixed pathology (up to 50% of dementia cases have ≥2 coexisting proteinopathies at autopsy), and (4) genetic counseling (protein-specific gene panels).

8.1.3 Neuroinflammation in Neurodegeneration

Neuroinflammation is now recognized as a central driver (not just bystander) of neurodegeneration, particularly in AD.

Microglia: The Double-Edged Sword

PhaseMicroglial PhenotypeEffect
Early/ProtectiveHomeostatic → phagocyticAβ clearance, debris removal, synapse pruning
Chronic/PathologicalPro-inflammatory (DAM/MGnD)Sustained cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α), complement activation, synaptic stripping

The NLRP3 Inflammasome — Positive Feedback Loop

Aβ oligomers activate microglial NF-κB pathway → NLRP3 inflammasome assembly → Caspase-1 activation → IL-1β/IL-18 release. Critically, released ASC specks (adaptor protein aggregates) serve as binding cores for further Aβ aggregation, creating a self-amplifying cycle. This links innate immunity directly to amyloid pathology (Venegas et al. Nature 2017).

Microglia–Astrocyte Crosstalk

Activated microglia secrete IL-1α, C1q, TNF-α → convert resting astrocytes to A1 neurotoxic astrocytes that lose normal supportive functions and actively kill neurons and oligodendrocytes (Liddelow et al. Nature 2017). GFAP (glial fibrillary acidic protein) elevation in CSF/plasma reflects this astrocyte activation and is now incorporated in the NIA-AA 2024 biomarker framework as an inflammation/astrocytic biomarker.

Pathogenesis Cascade: From Risk Factors to Dementia

Risk factors (genetics [APOEε4, TREM2], aging, DM, hypertension, obesity, infections, gut dysbiosis, air pollution) → Systemic inflammation → BBB disruption (pericyte loss, tight junction dysfunction) → Aβ accumulation + Tau hyperphosphorylation + Neuroinflammation (microglia/astrocyte activation) → Synaptic loss → Neuronal death → Network failure → Cognitive decline → Dementia

8.1.4 Genetics of Dementia

Heritability & Genetic Architecture

Gene/LocusProteinMechanismDiseaseInheritance
APP (chr 21)Amyloid precursor protein↑β-secretase cleavage → ↑Aβ42Early-onset AD (<65y)AD (autosomal dominant)
PSEN1 (chr 14)Presenilin-1 (γ-secretase)↑γ-secretase → ↑Aβ42/40 ratioEarly-onset AD (most common genetic cause, onset 30–50y)AD
PSEN2 (chr 1)Presenilin-2 (γ-secretase)↑γ-secretase → ↑Aβ42Early-onset AD (rarer, later onset than PSEN1)AD
APOE (chr 19)Apolipoprotein Eε4: ↓Aβ clearance, ↑aggregation; ε2: protectiveLate-onset AD (strongest genetic risk factor)Risk modifier
MAPT (chr 17)TauAltered 3R/4R tau ratio, ↑aggregationFTLD-tau, PSP, CBDAD
GRN (chr 17)ProgranulinHaploinsufficiency → lysosomal dysfunctionFTLD-TDP (FTD)AD
C9orf72 (chr 9)C9orf72 proteinGGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansionFTD, ALS, FTD-ALS (most common genetic cause)AD
TREM2Microglial receptorImpaired microglial Aβ phagocytosisAD (risk factor, OR ~2–4)Risk modifier
PRNP (chr 20)Prion proteinMisfolding susceptibility; octapeptide repeat insertionsGenetic CJD, FFI, GSSAD
NOTCH3 (chr 19)Notch3 receptorSmall vessel vasculopathyCADASILAD
Modified Goldman Score — When to Offer Genetic Testing
Goldman ScoreFamily History PatternRecommendation
1≥3 affected family members across ≥2 generations (autosomal dominant pattern)Strongly recommend genetic testing & counseling
2≥3 affected but in 1 generation onlyConsider testing
31 other affected family memberMay consider, especially if early-onset
3.5Family history unknown/limitedCase-by-case
4No family history of dementiaGenerally do NOT test (unless early-onset <60y or atypical phenotype)

Suggested algorithm: AD onset <60y OR strong FHx → targeted dementia gene panel (17 genes: APP, CHMP2B, CSF1R, FUS, GRN, ITM2B, MAPT, NOTCH3, PRNP, PSEN1, PSEN2, SERPINI1, SQSTM1, TARDBP, TREM2, TYROBP, VCP) + C9orf72 repeat expansion PCR. Suspected HD → HTT CAG repeat. Suspected prion → PRNP sequencing.

Genetic Heterogeneity: Pleiotropy & Phenocopies

Pleiotropy: A single gene can produce multiple phenotypes (e.g., C9orf72 expansion → bvFTD, ALS, FTD-ALS, PPA, psychosis, or parkinsonism). Phenocopy: Different genes can produce identical clinical syndromes (e.g., bvFTD phenotype from MAPT, GRN, C9orf72, or TARDBP mutations). This underscores why broad gene panels are preferred over single-gene testing.

Epigenetics in Dementia

Epigenetic modifications mediate gene–environment interactions in neurodegeneration:

  • DNA methylation: Hypermethylation of neuroprotective genes; altered in AD hippocampus
  • Histone modifications: Deacetylation → chromatin compaction → reduced synaptic gene expression
  • Non-coding RNAs: miR-132 (downregulated in AD; regulates tau splicing), miR-146a (neuroinflammation modulator)
  • Environmental modifiers: Heavy metals (lead, mercury), air pollution (PM2.5), pesticides, nutritional factors (folate, B12), physical activity, sleep quality

8.1.5 Alzheimer’s Disease — Comprehensive Review

Epidemiology

AD accounts for 60–70% of all dementia worldwide. Prevalence doubles every 5 years after age 65. Thai prevalence: ~3.3% in age ≥60 (community-based studies). Women have 1.5–2× higher risk than men (longevity, hormonal, APOEε4 interaction).

APP Processing & Amyloidopathy

Amyloid Precursor Protein (APP) Processing Pathways
PathwayEnzymeProductsEffect
Non-amyloidogenic (physiologic)α-secretase (ADAM10) then γ-secretasesAPPα + p3 fragmentNeuroprotective: neurotrophic, anti-apoptotic
Amyloidogenic (pathologic)β-secretase (BACE1) then γ-secretasesAPPβ + Aβ40/Aβ42Neurotoxic: Aβ42 is more hydrophobic, aggregation-prone → oligomers → fibrils → plaques

Genetic mutations shift balance toward amyloidogenic pathway:

  • APP mutations: ↑β-secretase cleavage efficiency
  • PSEN1/PSEN2 mutations: alter γ-secretase → ↑Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio
  • ADAM10 mutations: ↓α-secretase → less sAPPα, more substrate for β-secretase
  • APOEε4: ↓Aβ clearance (impaired microglial phagocytosis, reduced perivascular drainage)

Tauopathy in AD

Tau is a secondary proteinopathy in AD (triggered by amyloid), unlike primary tauopathies (PSP, CBD, Pick). The pathogenic sequence:

  1. Aβ oligomers activate kinases (GSK-3β, CDK5) & inhibit phosphatases (PP2A)
  2. Hyperphosphorylated tau detaches from microtubules → microtubule collapse → impaired axonal transport
  3. Free p-tau aggregates into paired helical filaments (PHF) → neurofibrillary tangles (NFT)
  4. NFT-bearing neurons undergo apoptosis
  5. Released tau seeds propagate in prion-like fashion along connected neural circuits
Braak Staging of Tau Pathology in AD
Braak StageRegionClinical Correlation
I–II (Transentorhinal)Entorhinal cortex, hippocampusPreclinical (no symptoms or subjective decline)
III–IV (Limbic)Hippocampus, amygdala, medial temporal lobeMCI, early AD (episodic memory impairment)
V–VI (Neocortical)Widespread neocortex (temporal, frontal, parietal)Moderate-severe AD (multi-domain cognitive impairment)

Key insight: Tau burden (not amyloid plaque load) correlates most closely with cognitive decline and neurodegeneration. This is why T2 biomarkers (tau PET, MTBR-tau243) are best for disease staging and prognosis.

Primary vs. Secondary Tauopathies

FeaturePrimary TauopathySecondary Tauopathy (AD)
ExamplesPSP, CBD, Pick disease, FTLD-MAPT, AGD, CTEAlzheimer’s disease, CAA
Tau isoformUsually 3R or 4R predominantMixed 3R+4R
Amyloid pathologyAbsentPresent (Aβ drives tau)
Tau PET patternVaries (frontal/subcortical in PSP; asymmetric frontoparietal in CBD)Temporoparietal, posterior cingulate/precuneus
Response to anti-amyloidNone expectedMay slow tau spread indirectly

8.1.6 Clinical Features of AD

Cognitive Continuum

The AD clinical trajectory spans a continuum over 15–25 years from initial molecular changes to severe dementia:

StageDefinitionKey FeaturesApproximate Duration
Preclinical ADBiomarker-positive (A+T+), cognitively normalNo subjective or objective cognitive impairment; only detectable by biomarkers10–20 years before symptoms
Subjective Cognitive Decline (SCD)Self-perceived decline, normal on testingPatients notice “memory isn’t what it used to be”; objective tests normalVariable
MCI due to ADObjective cognitive impairment, preserved ADLEpisodic memory impairment (amnestic MCI); CDR 0.5; conversion to dementia ~10–15%/year2–5 years
Mild AD dementiaCognitive impairment affecting ADLRecent memory loss, word-finding difficulty, spatial disorientation, impaired IADL2–4 years
Moderate ADIncreasing dependencyBPSD prominent (agitation, wandering, psychosis), impaired BADL, apraxia2–3 years
Severe ADComplete dependencyMinimal verbal output, incontinence, dysphagia, immobility, total care needs2–3 years

AD Subtypes & Atypical Presentations

SubtypeAge of OnsetPredominant DeficitNeuroimaging Pattern
Typical (amnestic) ADUsually >65yEpisodic memory (hippocampal)Medial temporal atrophy, temporoparietal hypometabolism
Posterior Cortical Atrophy (PCA)50–65yVisuospatial/visuoperceptual (Balint, simultanagnosia)Occipitoparietal atrophy & hypometabolism
Logopenic PPA (lvPPA)55–70yWord-finding, sentence repetition impairedLeft temporoparietal junction atrophy
Frontal/Behavioral variant AD50–65yExecutive dysfunction, behavioral changes (mimics bvFTD)Frontal atrophy/hypometabolism
Corticobasal syndromeVariableAsymmetric parkinsonism, apraxia, alien limbAsymmetric frontoparietal atrophy
Clinical Pearl — Early-Onset AD (<65y): Accounts for ~5–10% of all AD. More likely to have atypical presentations (PCA, lvPPA, frontal variant), faster progression, stronger genetic contribution (check APP, PSEN1, PSEN2), and more prominent non-memory deficits. Biomarker confirmation is particularly important in this group to distinguish from non-AD dementias. APOEε4 homozygosity may present as early-onset AD with typical amnestic pattern.

8.1.7 Cognitive Assessment Tools

ToolDomains TestedCut-off (Thai)Strengths / Limitations
MMSEOrientation, registration, attention, recall, language, construction≤23/30 (adjusted for education)Widely used; ceiling effect in MCI; poor for frontal/executive dysfunction
MoCAExecutive, visuospatial, naming, memory, attention, language, abstraction, orientation≤25/30 (add 1 point if education ≤12y)Better sensitivity for MCI; tests executive function; Thai version validated (MoCA-Thai)
CDR (Clinical Dementia Rating)Memory, orientation, judgment, community affairs, home/hobbies, personal care0 = normal; 0.5 = MCI; 1–3 = mild–severe dementiaGold standard for staging; requires informant interview; used in clinical trials (CDR-SB)
ADAS-CogMemory, language, praxis, orientationHigher score = worse (0–70)Standard outcome measure in AD drug trials
Thai Mental State Examination (TMSE)Similar to MMSE, adapted for Thai language/culture≤23/30Validated for Thai population; widely used in community screening
Clinical Pearl — BPSD Assessment: Behavioral and Psychological Symptoms of Dementia affect >80% of AD patients at some stage. Use NPI (Neuropsychiatric Inventory) for systematic assessment: delusions, hallucinations, agitation, depression, anxiety, apathy, disinhibition, irritability, aberrant motor behavior, sleep disturbance, appetite change. NPI score guides pharmacological intervention decisions.
Q: A 58-year-old man presents with 2-year progressive difficulty with reading, judging distances, and dressing. He gets lost in familiar places. His memory for recent events is relatively preserved. MMSE 22/30 (loses points on construction, attention, orientation). MRI shows bilateral occipitoparietal atrophy. Amyloid PET is positive. What is the diagnosis and what biomarker framework category does this fall under?
Diagnosis: Posterior Cortical Atrophy (PCA) — an atypical presentation of Alzheimer’s disease.
Key features: Progressive visuospatial/visuoperceptual deficits (reading difficulty = visual variant, judging distances = visuospatial, dressing difficulty = dressing apraxia), relatively preserved episodic memory, occipitoparietal atrophy, amyloid-positive.
NIA-AA 2024 biomarker category:
• A+ (amyloid positive on PET)
• T1 would need p-tau217/181 measurement (likely positive given clinical AD)
• T2 (Tau PET) would show occipitoparietal tau deposition pattern (different from typical temporoparietal AD pattern)
