• Classification: AD with PCA phenotype. This is biologically AD regardless of atypical clinical presentation.
Management: AChEI trial (donepezil); occupational therapy for visual aids; driving cessation; genetic testing if age <60; consider referral for anti-amyloid therapy clinical trial or commercial treatment (lecanemab) if early-stage.

8.2 AD Biomarkers — NIA-AA 2024 Revised Framework & Diagnostic Approach

8.2.1 Evolution of the Biomarker Paradigm

The understanding of AD has shifted from a clinicopathological model (diagnosis based on symptoms, confirmed at autopsy) to a biological model (diagnosis based on in vivo biomarkers identifying molecular pathology regardless of symptoms). This paradigm shift was formalized in the NIA-AA 2018 Research Framework (Jack CR Jr et al. Alzheimers Dement 2018) and further refined in the NIA-AA 2024 Revised Criteria (Jack CR Jr et al. Nat Med 2024).

Proposed AD timeline (Thientunyakit T et al. J Med Biol Eng 2022):

PhaseDuration (approx.)Key Events
Biochemical phase~10 years before symptomsProteopathic stress, Aβ clearance failure, initial tau phosphorylation, astrocyte activation (GFAP↑)
Cellular phase~20 years before symptomsChronic neuroinflammation, synaptic loss, circuitry imbalance, neuronal death, brain atrophy
Clinical phaseOnset of symptomsMemory/cognitive impairment, functional decline, behavioral changes

8.2.2 NIA-AA 2024 Revised Biomarker Framework: ATX(N)IVS

NIA-AA 2024 Revised Biomarker Categories (Jack CR Jr et al. Nat Med 2024)
CategoryDesignationWhat It MeasuresBiomarkers
Core 1
(Diagnostic)		A — Aβ proteinopathyAmyloid plaque burdenCSF Aβ42 (or Aβ42/40 ratio), Amyloid PET, Plasma Aβ42/40
T1 — Phosphorylated/secreted AD tauEarly soluble tau response to amyloid (reaction to Aβ plaques)p-tau217 (best performer), p-tau181, p-tau231 (CSF or plasma)
Core 2
(Staging)		T2 — AD tau aggregate proteinopathyPhysical tau tangles (NFT burden); tracks with neurodegeneration & symptomsTau PET (flortaucipir, MK-6240), MTBR-tau243 (CSF), p-tau205
Non-specificN — Neurodegeneration/injuryNeuronal/axonal damage (not specific to AD)NfL (CSF/plasma), anatomic MRI (hippocampal volume, cortical thickness), FDG-PET
I — Inflammation/immune activationAstrocyte & microglial activationGFAP (plasma/CSF), sTREM2 (CSF), YKL-40
Non-AD copathologyV — Vascular brain injuryCerebrovascular disease contributionMRI: WMH, lacunes, microbleeds, infarcts; CT: ischemic changes
S — α-SynucleinLewy body co-pathologyαSyn-SAA (CSF/skin; seed amplification assay)
Key Distinction: T1 vs. T2 — Why It Matters
FeatureT1 (Soluble Tau Response)T2 (Tau Aggregates)
What it reflectsNeuronal reaction to Aβ plaques; tau is phosphorylated and secretedPhysical NFT burden; tau is aggregated in neurons
TimingAbnormal concurrently with amyloid PET (early)Abnormal later, as tangles spread beyond MTL
CorrelationCorrelates with amyloid burdenCorrelates with neurodegeneration & cognitive decline
Clinical useDiagnosis (confirms AD biology)Staging & prognosis (predicts progression)
Key biomarkersp-tau217, p-tau181, p-tau231Tau PET, MTBR-tau243, p-tau205

8.2.3 Biomarker Testing Modalities

PET Imaging

PET TracerTargetKey Features
Amyloid PET (Florbetapir, Florbetaben, Flutemetamol, 11C-PiB)Fibrillar Aβ plaquesVisual read (positive/negative) + quantitative (Centiloid scale; CL >25 = amyloid positive). Validated against autopsy. High sensitivity & specificity (>90%). False positive: CAA, normal aging (low levels)
Tau PET (Flortaucipir [18F-AV-1451], 18F-MK-6240, 18F-PI-2620)Tau NFT (primarily 3R+4R in AD)Staging tool (Braak-like distribution). Tracks with cognition better than amyloid PET. Off-target binding in choroid plexus, basal ganglia (flortaucipir). MK-6240 has less off-target
FDG-PETCerebral glucose metabolismPattern-specific: AD (posterior cingulate/precuneus/temporoparietal hypometabolism); useful for differential diagnosis of dementia subtypes

CSF Biomarkers

BiomarkerDirection in ADPerformanceNotes
CSF Aβ42↓ (sequestered in plaques)Sn/Sp >90%Use Aβ42/Aβ40 ratio for better accuracy (~50% fold difference normal vs abnormal)
CSF p-tau181↑Sn/Sp ~85–90%Current clinical standard for T1
CSF p-tau217↑Sn/Sp >95%Superior to p-tau181; not yet widely clinically available
CSF t-tau↑Sn ~80%, Sp ~70%Non-specific neurodegeneration marker; less useful than p-tau for AD diagnosis
CSF NfL↑Non-specificElevated in AD, FTD, ALS, MS, stroke; useful for monitoring neurodegeneration rate
CSF MTBR-tau243↑EmergingDirectly reflects tau aggregation (T2); correlates with tau PET; research stage

Plasma Biomarkers — The Breakthrough

Plasma Biomarkers: Toward Blood-Based AD Diagnosis
Plasma BiomarkerPerformanceClinical Readiness
Plasma p-tau217AUC 0.92–0.96 for Aβ+ discrimination; diagnostic accuracy equivalent to CSF biomarkersMost advanced; FDA breakthrough device (PrecivityAD2, Lumipulse); the “90% Rule” — standalone Core 1 diagnostic when clearly abnormal
Plasma p-tau181AUC 0.85–0.92Available clinically (Quanterix Simoa); good but inferior to p-tau217
Plasma p-tau231AUC 0.80–0.88; changes earliestResearch; may detect preclinical AD before p-tau181
Plasma Aβ42/40AUC 0.80–0.87Moderate performance alone; better in combination (hybrid ratios: p-tau217/Aβ42)
Plasma GFAPElevated from preclinical stageResearch/emerging; reflects astrocyte activation; increases early in AD continuum
Plasma NfLNon-specific neurodegenerationAvailable clinically; useful for monitoring, not diagnosis

The “90% Rule” for plasma p-tau217: When plasma p-tau217 falls in clearly abnormal or clearly normal ranges (avoiding the intermediate/grey zone), it achieves ≥90% concordance with amyloid PET and CSF, sufficient for standalone Core 1 biological diagnosis of AD without confirmatory PET or LP. This enables scalable screening in primary care and low-resource settings.

8.2.4 Chronobiological Biomarker Sequence

Biomarkers become abnormal in a predictable temporal sequence (Bjorkli C et al. Front Aging Neurosci 2020):

  1. Astrocyte dysfunction (GFAP↑) — earliest detectable change
  2. CSF Aβ42↓ (amyloid sequestration in plaques)
  3. Amyloid PET+
  4. Microglial activation (sTREM2↑, inflammation PET)
  5. p-tau↑ (T1 biomarkers: p-tau217, p-tau181)
  6. FDG-PET hypometabolism
  7. Hippocampal volume loss (MRI)
  8. CSF t-tau↑, NfL↑
  9. MRI structural atrophy (cortical thinning)
  10. Cognitive decline (MoCA/MMSE drop)
  11. Clinical functional impairment (ADL decline)

8.2.5 FDG-PET Metabolic Patterns in Neurodegenerative Dementia

FDG-PET Signature Patterns (Thientunyakit T, Muangpaisan W, Minoshima S. Semin Nucl Med 2025)
DiseaseFDG-PET Hypometabolism PatternKey Distinguishing Feature
Typical ADPosterior cingulate/precuneus + temporoparietal cortexBilateral, symmetric; earliest in posterior cingulate
lvPPA (logopenic)Left lateral temporal + inferior parietalAsymmetric left-sided (AD pathology)
PCAMedial & lateral occipital + posterior parietalOccipital involvement distinguishes from typical AD
bvFTDFrontal (medial & lateral) + anterior temporal + caudateFrontal-predominant; caudate involvement
svPPA (semantic)Left anterior temporal poleAsymmetric anterior temporal (often left > right)
nfvPPA (nonfluent)Left inferior frontal (Broca’s area) + insula + premotorPerisylvian left-sided
DLBTemporoparietal + occipitalOccipital hypometabolism distinguishes from AD; “cingulate island sign” (preserved posterior cingulate)
PSPMidbrain + medial frontal + caudate + thalamusSubcortical + midline frontal
CBDAsymmetric frontoparietal (contralateral to affected limb)Marked asymmetry
Huntington’s diseaseCaudate/putamen (early: may see hypermetabolism → late: hypometabolism)Striatal-predominant

8.2.6 Diagnostic Pathway Algorithm

Based on Chêtelat G et al. Lancet Neurol 2020:

Three-Pathway Diagnostic Algorithm for Neurodegenerative Dementia
PathwayClinical SuspicionRecommended First-Line BiomarkerRationale
Pathway 1AD suspected (amnestic presentation, typical age)Amyloid PET or CSF Aβ/p-tau (or plasma p-tau217 if available)Confirm or exclude AD biology
Pathway 2Non-AD suspected (behavioral, language, or atypical presentations)FDG-PETPattern-based differential diagnosis (FTD vs AD vs DLB vs PSP/CBD)
Pathway 3Cognitive + movement disorder overlapDaT-SPECT or MIBG scintigraphyConfirm presynaptic dopaminergic deficit (DLB/PD) or cardiac sympathetic denervation

Practical approach in Thailand: Structural MRI (atrophy pattern) + cognitive testing → If AD suspected: plasma p-tau217 (when available) or CSF Aβ42/p-tau181 → If non-AD suspected: FDG-PET → If unclear: amyloid PET. For movement disorder + dementia: DaT-SPECT (available at major centers: Siriraj, Ramathibodi, Chulalongkorn).

NIA-AA vs IWG Diagnostic Criteria Comparison (Ossenkoppele R et al. Nat Med 2022)
Biomarker ProfileNIA-AA ClassificationIWG Classification5-Year Progression to MCI
A−T−Not at riskNot at risk8.9%
A+T−Preclinical AD pathological changeAt-risk (not preclinical AD)3.3%
A+TMTL+Preclinical ADPreclinical AD49.0%
A+TNEO-T+Preclinical ADPreclinical AD, highest risk53.3%

Key takeaway: A+T− individuals (amyloid positive but tau negative) have lower short-term risk of progression than A−T−. It is the combination of A+ with medial temporal or neocortical tau (T2+) that signals high progression risk. This has major implications for clinical trial enrollment and treatment decisions.

8.2.7 Disease-Modifying Therapy: Anti-Amyloid Monoclonal Antibodies

Anti-Amyloid Immunotherapy Landscape (2023–2025)
DrugTargetTrialKey ResultsFDA Status
Lecanemab (Leqembi®)Aβ protofibrils (soluble aggregates)CLARITY-AD (NEJM 2023)
n=1,795; early AD (MCI/mild); 18 months		CDR-SB: −27% decline vs placebo (0.45 points; p<0.001). Amyloid PET: mean −59 CL (amyloid clearance). Brain volume loss greater with treatment (likely pseudo-atrophy from amyloid removal).Full FDA approval (July 2023). IV infusion 10 mg/kg q2w
Donanemab (Kisunla™)Aβ N-truncated pyroglutamate (deposited plaques)TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 (JAMA 2023)
n=1,736; early symptomatic AD; 76 weeks		CDR-SB: −29% in low/medium tau (−36% slowing). 47% reached amyloid negativity by 6 months. Benefit more pronounced in low/medium tau (T2) subgroup. Time to next stage of disease: −39%.FDA approved (July 2024). IV infusion; unique: stop dosing when amyloid cleared
Aducanumab (Aduhelm™)Aβ aggregated formsEMERGE/ENGAGEControversial; inconsistent results between two Phase 3 trials. Amyloid clearance confirmed. Withdrew from market 2024.FDA accelerated approval 2021; withdrawn 2024

ARIA: Amyloid-Related Imaging Abnormalities

ARIA Monitoring Protocol
ARIA TypeMechanismFrequencyMonitoringManagement
ARIA-E (Edema/effusion)Perivascular inflammation during amyloid clearanceLecanemab: 12.6%; Donanemab: 24.0%MRI at baseline, prior to 5th, 7th, 14th infusion (lecanemab); per protocol for donanemabMost asymptomatic (75%). If symptomatic: hold treatment, monitor MRI, resume when resolved. Severe/symptomatic: discontinue
ARIA-H (Hemorrhage)Microhemorrhages/superficial siderosisLecanemab: 17.3% (micro); Donanemab: 19.7%Same MRI schedule as ARIA-E<4 new microbleeds: may continue. ≥4 or superficial siderosis: hold/discontinue

Risk factors for ARIA: APOEε4 carriers (especially homozygotes: 32–36% ARIA-E rate), CAA, anticoagulant use, baseline microbleeds (>4). APOE genotyping is recommended before initiating anti-amyloid therapy for risk stratification and informed consent.

Eligibility Criteria for Anti-Amyloid Therapy (Practical):
• Include: Early symptomatic AD (MCI or mild dementia due to AD), confirmed amyloid-positive (PET or CSF/plasma biomarkers), CDR 0.5–1
• Exclude or high-risk: Moderate-severe dementia (CDR ≥2), >4 microbleeds or any superficial siderosis on MRI, anticoagulation, APOEε4 homozygotes (relative contraindication — discuss risk), significant CAA
• Thai context: Currently limited availability; lecanemab approved by Thai FDA (under special access); cost is a major barrier (~$26,500/year in US, pricing varies in Thailand); amyloid PET access mainly at Siriraj (PET center), Chulalongkorn, Ramathibodi

8.2.8 Symptomatic Pharmacotherapy

Cognitive Enhancers in AD
Drug ClassDrugDoseIndicationKey Side Effects
AChEI
(Acetylcholinesterase Inhibitors)		Donepezil5 mg/day × 4 weeks → 10 mg/day (max 23 mg)Mild–severe AD; also DLBGI (nausea, diarrhea), bradycardia, vivid dreams, muscle cramps
RivastigminePatch: 4.6 mg/24h → 9.5 → 13.3 mg/24hMild–moderate AD; PDD (only AChEI with PDD indication)GI (less with patch), application site reactions
Galantamine8 mg/day → 16 → 24 mg/day (ER)Mild–moderate ADGI, allosteric nicotinic receptor modulation (dual mechanism)
NMDA antagonistMemantine5 mg/day → titrate to 20 mg/dayModerate–severe AD (can combine with AChEI)Dizziness, headache, confusion (rare)

Combination therapy (donepezil + memantine): The DOMINO trial (Howard et al. NEJM 2012) demonstrated that continuing donepezil and adding memantine in moderate-severe AD provided better cognitive and functional outcomes than either drug alone or placebo.

BPSD Pharmacotherapy

SymptomFirst-LineSecond-LineImportant Cautions
Agitation/AggressionNon-pharmacological first; Brexpiprazole (FDA-approved 2023 for AD agitation)Low-dose risperidone (0.5–1 mg), quetiapine (25–100 mg)FDA black box: antipsychotics increase mortality in elderly with dementia (~1.6–1.7×). Use lowest dose, shortest duration
DepressionSSRIs (Sertraline 50–100 mg, Escitalopram 5–10 mg)Mirtazapine (if insomnia/appetite loss)Avoid TCAs (anticholinergic burden worsens cognition)
PsychosisPimavanserin (selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist; FDA-approved for PD psychosis, studied in AD)Quetiapine 25–50 mg, risperidone 0.25–0.5 mgAvoid in DLB/PDD (neuroleptic sensitivity — see Section 8.3)
InsomniaSleep hygiene; trazodone 25–50 mg; Suvorexant 10 mg (orexin antagonist)Melatonin 2–5 mgAvoid benzodiazepines (falls, paradoxical agitation, cognitive worsening)
ApathyOptimize AChEI; Methylphenidate 5–20 mg/day (ADMET 2, Lancet Neurol 2021)Structured activitiesMonitor for agitation, cardiovascular effects
Thai Context — Practical Prescribing: In Thailand, donepezil and memantine are on the NLEM (National List of Essential Medicines) and covered by government health insurance (UC, SSS, CSMBS). Rivastigmine patch and galantamine require special authorization. Brexpiprazole and pimavanserin are not yet available in Thailand. For BPSD, quetiapine and risperidone are most commonly used; always document rationale for antipsychotic use in elderly with dementia.
Q: A 72-year-old woman with 3-year progressive memory loss is diagnosed with mild AD (CDR 1, MMSE 21). Plasma p-tau217 is elevated. MRI shows bilateral hippocampal atrophy with 2 microbleeds. She is APOE ε3/ε4 heterozygous. Her family asks about lecanemab. What is your assessment and management plan?
Assessment:
• Diagnosis: Mild AD, biologically confirmed (A+ by plasma p-tau217, clinical syndrome consistent)
• Anti-amyloid therapy candidacy: Early-stage AD (CDR 1) — within treatment window. APOE ε3/ε4 heterozygote has intermediate ARIA risk (~15–20% ARIA-E). 2 microbleeds is below the >4 threshold.
Management plan:
1. Start donepezil 5 mg/day → titrate to 10 mg at 4 weeks (standard symptomatic treatment)
2. Lecanemab consideration: Patient is eligible based on: early AD, biomarker-confirmed, <4 microbleeds. However, discuss:
 • Expected benefit: ~27% slowing of cognitive decline over 18 months
 • ARIA risk: ~15% for ARIA-E as ε3/ε4 carrier, mostly asymptomatic but requires regular MRI monitoring
 • Practical: IV infusion every 2 weeks; cost considerations; availability in Thailand
3. Baseline MRI with ARIA protocol (FLAIR, GRE/SWI for microbleed quantification)
4. Non-pharmacological: Cognitive stimulation, physical exercise (150 min/week), Mediterranean diet, social engagement, caregiver education
5. Address modifiable risk factors: Optimize blood pressure, diabetes control, hearing, depression
6. Advance care planning discussion while patient still has capacity

8.3 Dementia with Lewy Bodies (DLB)

8.3.1 Overview & Pathology

DLB is the second most common neurodegenerative dementia (~15–25% of autopsy-confirmed dementias), caused by cortical and limbic α-synuclein aggregation (Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites). Often co-occurs with AD pathology (Aβ plaques in ~50%; mixed DLB/AD). Median survival ~5–8 years from diagnosis.

α-Synuclein spreading pattern: Brainstem (locus coeruleus, dorsal motor nucleus of vagus) → midbrain (substantia nigra) → limbic (amygdala, cingulate) → neocortex. This parallels the Braak PD staging but with earlier/more prominent cortical involvement in DLB.

DLB vs. PDD: The One-Year Rule

FeatureDLBPDD
Temporal relationshipDementia onset within 1 year of or before parkinsonismDementia develops >1 year after established PD motor symptoms
PathologyIdentical (α-synuclein cortical Lewy bodies)Identical
SpectrumDLB and PDD are considered a spectrum of Lewy body dementia. The one-year rule is a practical clinical convention but both represent the same disease biology.

8.3.2 Diagnostic Criteria: McKeith 2017 Consensus (4th Report)

McKeith 2017 Diagnostic Criteria for DLB (Neurology 2017)

Essential feature: Progressive cognitive decline sufficient to interfere with normal social/occupational function. Prominent early memory impairment may not necessarily occur; deficits in attention, executive function, and visuospatial ability are typically prominent early.

Core Clinical Features (need ≥2 for probable, 1 for possible)

Core FeatureDescriptionFrequency
Fluctuating cognitionPronounced variations in attention and alertness; episodes of staring, disorganized speech, “zoning out”. Distinguish from delirium~60–80%
Recurrent visual hallucinationsTypically well-formed, detailed (people, animals). Often early in disease. Patient may have partial insight~60–80%
REM sleep behavior disorder (RBD)Dream enactment behavior (kicking, punching during sleep); confirmed by polysomnography. May precede cognitive decline by years/decades~75–80%
Spontaneous parkinsonismBradykinesia, rigidity, resting tremor (less common than in PD). Gait disturbance. Not drug-induced~70–85%

Indicative Biomarkers (count as Core feature equivalents)

BiomarkerFindingNotes
DaT-SPECT / DaT-PETReduced dopamine transporter uptake in basal gangliaSn 78%, Sp 90% (vs AD). Does NOT distinguish DLB from PD/PDD/MSA/PSP
123I-MIBG myocardial scintigraphyReduced cardiac uptake (H/M ratio <1.6)Cardiac sympathetic denervation; Sn 69%, Sp 87%. Normal in AD, PSP, MSA
Polysomnography (PSG)REM sleep without atonia (RWA)Confirms RBD; highly specific for synucleinopathy

Supportive Features

Severe neuroleptic sensitivity, postural instability, repeated falls, syncope, autonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, urinary incontinence, constipation), hypersomnia, hyposmia, hallucinations in other modalities, systematized delusions (Capgras syndrome), depression, anxiety, apathy.

Diagnostic formula:

  • Probable DLB: ≥2 core features OR 1 core + ≥1 indicative biomarker
  • Possible DLB: 1 core feature alone OR ≥1 indicative biomarker alone

8.3.3 α-Synuclein Biomarkers — The SAA Revolution

α-Synuclein Seed Amplification Assay (αSyn-SAA)

The αSyn-SAA (also called RT-QuIC for α-synuclein) detects misfolded α-synuclein seeds in CSF or skin biopsies by amplifying trace amounts through cyclic fragmentation and regrowth, analogous to prion RT-QuIC.

TestSensitivitySpecificityNotes
CSF αSyn-SAA86–96% (DLB), 87–96% (PD)95–100% (vs AD, controls)Now incorporated in NIA-AA 2024 framework as “S” biomarker. Detects Lewy body copathology in AD patients
Skin biopsy αSyn-SAA~89–96%>95%Less invasive alternative to CSF; detects α-synuclein in dermal autonomic nerve fibers

Clinical significance: αSyn-SAA transforms DLB/PD diagnosis from probabilistic (clinical criteria) to biological (molecular confirmation). Positive αSyn-SAA in a dementia patient with one core DLB feature virtually confirms Lewy body pathology. Also detects preclinical synucleinopathy in isolated RBD patients (prodromal DLB/PD).

Neuroimaging in DLB

ModalityFinding in DLBDistinguishing from AD
Structural MRIRelative preservation of medial temporal lobe/hippocampiAD: prominent hippocampal atrophy. DLB: less MTL atrophy despite similar dementia severity
FDG-PETTemporoparietal + occipital hypometabolism; “Cingulate island sign” (preserved posterior cingulate metabolism)AD: posterior cingulate hypometabolism (no island sign); AD spares occipital cortex
Amyloid PETPositive in ~50% (AD copathology)Does NOT distinguish DLB from AD (both can be positive)
DaT-SPECTReduced striatal dopamine transporter uptakeNormal in AD (unless basal ganglia vascular disease)
EEGPosterior dominant rhythm slowing; prominent posterior slow-wave activitySupportive but not diagnostic

8.3.4 Treatment of DLB

Pharmacotherapy in DLB
Symptom DomainTreatmentEvidence/Notes
Cognitive declineDonepezil 5–10 mg/day (first-line)
Rivastigmine patch (alternative)		AChEI more effective in DLB than AD; significant cholinergic deficit. Rivastigmine has best evidence for visual hallucinations
Visual hallucinationsOptimize AChEI first;
Pimavanserin (if available)		If distressing despite AChEI: low-dose quetiapine (12.5–50 mg). AVOID typical antipsychotics and high-potency atypicals
ParkinsonismLevodopa (cautious, low dose: start 50 mg TID)Less effective than in PD; may worsen hallucinations/psychosis. Avoid dopamine agonists (worsen psychosis)
REM sleep behavior disorderMelatonin 3–12 mg hs (first-line)
Clonazepam 0.25–0.5 mg hs (if needed)		Melatonin preferred (fewer side effects, no fall risk). Clonazepam: risk of falls, sedation in elderly
DepressionSSRIs (sertraline, escitalopram)Avoid TCAs (anticholinergic)
Autonomic dysfunctionOrthostatic hypotension: fludrocortisone, midodrine, droxidopa; Constipation: PEG, lubiprostoneNon-pharmacological first (compression stockings, salt, fluid)
CRITICAL: Neuroleptic Sensitivity in DLB

Up to 50% of DLB patients develop severe, potentially fatal neuroleptic sensitivity reactions:

  • Severe exacerbation of parkinsonism (rigidity, immobility, falls)
  • Impaired consciousness, delirium
  • Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS) — even with atypical antipsychotics
  • Irreversible deterioration and death reported

Rules for antipsychotic use in DLB:

  • NEVER use typical antipsychotics (haloperidol, chlorpromazine) or high-potency atypicals (olanzapine, risperidone >0.5 mg) in DLB
  • If absolutely required: quetiapine (lowest D2 affinity) at lowest dose (12.5–25 mg), with close monitoring
  • Clozapine at ultra-low doses (6.25–25 mg) is the safest option but requires WBC monitoring (agranulocytosis risk)
  • Pimavanserin (5-HT2A inverse agonist, no D2 activity): theoretically ideal, FDA-approved for PD psychosis, data emerging in DLB
  • Always try optimizing AChEI first before considering any antipsychotic
Clinical Pearl — RBD as a Prodromal Biomarker: Isolated RBD (iRBD) is the strongest clinical predictor of future synucleinopathy. The NAPS study (Postuma et al. Brain 2019) showed that >90% of iRBD patients phenoconvert to PD, DLB, or MSA within 15 years (median conversion ~8 years). αSyn-SAA is positive in ~90% of iRBD patients, identifying them as having “prodromal Lewy body disease.” This has implications for future neuroprotective trial enrollment.
Q: A 74-year-old man presents with 1-year history of fluctuating alertness, recurrent formed visual hallucinations (seeing children in his room), and mild bradykinesia/rigidity. His wife reports he has been acting out violent dreams (kicking, shouting) for the past 5 years. MMSE 22/30, MoCA 18/30 (poor visuospatial and attention). MRI shows mild generalized atrophy with relative hippocampal preservation. What is the diagnosis, and how would you manage the hallucinations if they become distressing?
Diagnosis: Probable DLB (McKeith 2017).
• Core features present (3/4): fluctuating cognition, recurrent visual hallucinations, RBD (5-year history of dream enactment — predates cognitive decline, consistent with prodromal synucleinopathy), parkinsonism (bradykinesia + rigidity).
• Supportive: visuospatial/attention deficits on MoCA, relative hippocampal preservation on MRI.
• Recommended biomarker confirmation: DaT-SPECT (expect reduced uptake) or CSF αSyn-SAA (expect positive).
Management of hallucinations:
1. First: Start donepezil 5 mg → 10 mg (AChEI is first-line for both cognition AND hallucinations in DLB)
2. If hallucinations persist and are distressing:
 • Quetiapine 12.5–25 mg at bedtime (lowest effective dose)
 • NEVER use haloperidol, olanzapine, or risperidone >0.5 mg (neuroleptic sensitivity risk)
 • Consider pimavanserin if available (no D2 activity)
3. For RBD: melatonin 6–12 mg at bedtime (first-line); bed safety measures (padding, floor mattress)
4. For parkinsonism: low-dose levodopa (50/12.5 mg TID) if functionally limiting, but may worsen hallucinations — balance carefully

8.4 Frontotemporal Dementia (FTD) Spectrum

8.4.1 Overview

FTD is the second most common cause of early-onset dementia (<65 years), accounting for ~10–20% of all degenerative dementias and up to 50% in those aged 45–65. Mean onset: 50–60 years. Characterized by progressive frontal and/or anterior temporal lobe degeneration with prominent behavioral, executive, and/or language dysfunction. Memory is relatively preserved early (unlike AD). Median survival ~6–8 years from symptom onset.

FTD Terminology & Classification

LevelClassificationExamples
Clinical syndromeBehavioral/language phenotypebvFTD, svPPA, nfvPPA, lvPPA, FTD-MND
Pathological substrateFTLD + protein typeFTLD-tau (Pick, PSP, CBD), FTLD-TDP (Type A–E), FTLD-FUS
Genetic causeSpecific gene mutationC9orf72, GRN, MAPT, TARDBP, VCP, CHMP2B

8.4.2 Behavioral Variant FTD (bvFTD)

Most common FTD subtype (~60% of FTD). Characterized by progressive personality change, behavioral dysregulation, and executive dysfunction.

FTDC Diagnostic Criteria for bvFTD (Rascovsky et al. Brain 2011)

Possible bvFTD: ≥3 of the following 6 features (progressive deterioration):

#FeatureExamples
1Early behavioral disinhibitionSocially inappropriate behavior, loss of manners/decorum, impulsive/reckless acts
2Early apathy or inertiaLoss of motivation, interest, initiative; not due to depression
3Early loss of sympathy/empathyDiminished response to others’ needs/feelings, reduced social interest
4Early perseverative/compulsive/ritualistic behaviorStereotyped movements, compulsive/ritualistic behaviors, hoarding
5Hyperorality & dietary changesAltered food preferences (sweet craving), binge eating, oral exploration, increased alcohol/smoking
6Neuropsychological profileExecutive/generation deficits with relative sparing of episodic memory and visuospatial function

Probable bvFTD: All of possible criteria + functional decline + imaging consistent (frontal/anterior temporal atrophy or hypometabolism).

Definite bvFTD: Histopathological confirmation or known pathogenic mutation.

Clinical Pearl — bvFTD Mimics and Phenocopies: Up to 30% of patients meeting possible bvFTD criteria may not have neurodegenerative disease (“phenocopy syndrome”). These patients show behavioral symptoms but lack progressive atrophy on serial MRI and have normal FDG-PET. Causes: late-onset psychiatric disorders (bipolar, OCD), brain injury, ASD. Tip: if MRI and FDG-PET are both normal after 2+ years, reconsider diagnosis. Serial neuropsychological testing showing stability rather than decline also suggests phenocopy.

8.4.3 Primary Progressive Aphasia (PPA)

PPA presents with insidious, progressive language impairment as the most prominent early feature (at least 2 years before other cognitive domains affected). Three canonical variants:

PPA Variants: Differential Diagnosis (Gorno-Tempini et al. Neurology 2011)
FeatureSemantic (svPPA)Nonfluent/Agrammatic (nfvPPA)Logopenic (lvPPA)
Core deficitLoss of word/object meaning (semantic memory)Effortful, halting speech with grammar errors (agrammatism) and/or apraxia of speechWord-finding difficulty with impaired sentence repetition
FluencyFluent (empty speech, circumlocutory)NonfluentFluent but with frequent pauses
NamingSeverely impaired (confrontation naming)Relatively preservedImpaired
RepetitionPreserved (for single words)May be impaired (for complex sentences)Impaired (especially sentences/phrases)
ComprehensionImpaired for single wordsPreserved for single words; impaired for complex grammarPreserved for single words
Reading/WritingSurface dyslexia/dysgraphia (regularization errors)Agrammatic writingPhonological errors
Atrophy patternLeft anterior temporal pole (asymmetric, often “knife-edge”)Left inferior frontal (Broca’s) + insula + premotorLeft temporoparietal junction (posterior perisylvian)
FDG-PETLeft anterior temporalLeft perisylvian frontal/insularLeft temporoparietal
Usual pathologyFTLD-TDP (Type C) — ~80%FTLD-tau (4R; PSP or CBD) — ~60%AD pathology — ~65% (not FTLD!)
Key Insight — lvPPA is Usually AD: Unlike svPPA and nfvPPA (which are FTD/FTLD), logopenic PPA is most commonly caused by Alzheimer’s disease pathology. Therefore, lvPPA patients are often amyloid PET positive and may respond to AChEI and potentially anti-amyloid therapy. Always check amyloid biomarkers in lvPPA.

8.4.4 FTD–ALS Continuum

FTD and ALS (amyotrophic lateral sclerosis) exist on a clinicopathological spectrum linked by TDP-43 proteinopathy:

PhenotypeFeaturesPathologyGenetics
Pure ALSUMN + LMN signs, no cognitive/behavioral featuresTDP-43 (Type B mostly)SOD1, FUS, TARDBP, C9orf72
ALS-FTD (ALS with cognitive/behavioral impairment)ALS + executive dysfunction/behavioral change (may meet bvFTD criteria)TDP-43 (Type B)C9orf72 (most common), TARDBP
FTD-MNDbvFTD as primary presentation + MND features (fasciculations, UMN signs, EMG denervation)FTLD-TDP (Type B)C9orf72
Pure FTD (bvFTD)Behavioral/cognitive onlyFTLD-tau or FTLD-TDPMAPT, GRN, C9orf72
Clinical Pearl — Screen for MND in bvFTD: Up to 15% of bvFTD patients develop MND features during their disease course. Always examine for UMN/LMN signs (fasciculations, hyperreflexia, Babinski, wasting) at every visit. EMG/NCS if suspected. FTD-MND has significantly worse prognosis (median survival 2–3 years) than pure bvFTD. C9orf72 expansion is the most common genetic cause of the FTD-ALS spectrum.

8.4.5 Genetics of FTD

FTD has the highest heritability of any neurodegenerative dementia (~40% have positive family history; ~10–20% have autosomal dominant mutations).

Major FTD Genes
GeneChromosomePathologyClinical FeaturesUnique Characteristics
C9orf729p21FTLD-TDP (Type A or B) + dipeptide repeat proteinsbvFTD, ALS, FTD-ALS, PPA, parkinsonism, psychosis (hallucinations, delusions common)Most common genetic cause of both FTD and ALS; GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion (>30 repeats pathogenic); PCR-based repeat expansion testing required (not detected by gene panels/sequencing alone)
GRN17q21FTLD-TDP (Type A)Asymmetric cortical atrophy; may mimic CBS, PPA (nfvPPA or mixed), or AD-like presentationHaploinsufficiency mechanism; plasma progranulin levels can be used as screening biomarker (↓50%); variable phenotype even within families
MAPT17q21FTLD-tau (3R, 4R, or mixed)bvFTD (classic), parkinsonism (PSP-like or CBS-like), semantic dementiaTau-positive inclusions; more predictable phenotype than C9orf72/GRN; responds to anti-tau therapy targets
TARDBP1p36FTLD-TDPALS predominant, some FTDEncodes TDP-43 itself; rare FTD cause
VCP9p13FTLD-TDP (Type D)FTD + inclusion body myopathy + Paget’s disease of bone (IBMPFD)Multi-system proteinopathy

8.4.6 PSP & CBS: The 4R Tauopathies

Progressive Supranuclear Palsy (PSP)

FeatureDetails
Core phenotype (PSP-Richardson)Vertical supranuclear gaze palsy (initially downgaze > upgaze), postural instability with early falls (backward), axial rigidity > limb, bradykinesia, frontal cognitive impairment, pseudobulbar affect
Other PSP phenotypesPSP-P (parkinsonism predominant, mimics PD); PSP-F (frontal/bvFTD-like); PSP-CBS (corticobasal syndrome); PSP-SL (speech/language: nfvPPA); PSP-PGF (pure gait freezing)
Pathology4R tau: globose NFT in subcortical nuclei, tufted astrocytes (pathognomonic), midbrain atrophy (“hummingbird sign”/“morning glory sign”)
ImagingMRI: midbrain atrophy (AP diameter <14 mm), “hummingbird sign” sagittal, “morning glory sign” axial, superior cerebellar peduncle atrophy. FDG-PET: frontal + midbrain + caudate + thalamic hypometabolism
TreatmentNo disease-modifying therapy. Levodopa trial (usually poor response, <30% improve). Amantadine for speech. PT for falls prevention. Anti-tau trials ongoing (semorinemab, bepranemab)

Corticobasal Syndrome (CBS) / Corticobasal Degeneration (CBD)

FeatureDetails
Clinical featuresMarkedly asymmetric parkinsonism (rigidity, dystonia) + cortical signs: limb apraxia, cortical sensory loss, alien limb phenomenon, myoclonus. Progressive; usually one side affected first
Pathology4R tau: ballooned/achromatic neurons, astrocytic plaques (pathognomonic), corticobasal inclusions. BUT: CBS phenotype can be caused by AD, PSP, TDP-43 — only ~50% have true CBD pathology
ImagingMRI: asymmetric frontoparietal atrophy (contralateral to affected limbs). FDG-PET: asymmetric frontoparietal hypometabolism
Important noteCBS ≠ CBD. CBS is a clinical syndrome; CBD is a pathological diagnosis. Always consider AD as underlying pathology in CBS (check amyloid biomarkers)

8.4.7 FTD Treatment & Management

SymptomApproachNotes
Behavioral symptoms (disinhibition, agitation, compulsions)SSRIs (trazodone 50–300 mg; sertraline 50–200 mg) — most evidence. Environmental modificationAChEI NOT recommended in FTD (may worsen behavior; no cholinergic deficit). Boxer et al. Lancet Neurol 2023: trazodone may help NPI scores
ApathyStructured routines, activity scheduling. Stimulants (methylphenidate) limited evidenceMost treatment-resistant symptom
Language (PPA)Speech-language therapy. Communication aids (picture boards, apps)No pharmacological treatment proven effective
Parkinsonism (PSP, CBS)Levodopa trial (low response rate). Botulinum toxin for focal dystoniaDopamine agonists generally ineffective and may cause psychosis
Genetic counselingOffer to all FTD patients, especially onset <60y, positive FHx, or atypical featuresPre-symptomatic testing available for C9orf72, GRN, MAPT. Follow Huntington protocol for disclosure
Clinical Pearl — AChEI in FTD: Unlike AD and DLB, FTD does NOT have a prominent cholinergic deficit. AChEI should be avoided as they may paradoxically worsen behavioral symptoms in bvFTD. If there is diagnostic uncertainty between AD and FTD, obtain amyloid biomarkers before starting AChEI. The exception: lvPPA (usually AD pathology) may benefit from AChEI.
Q: A 55-year-old woman presents with 2-year progressive personality change. Her husband reports she has become disinhibited (making inappropriate sexual comments to strangers), apathetic at home, compulsively eats sweets (gained 15 kg), and has lost empathy for her children. She scores 26/30 on MMSE (loses points on attention) but 18/30 on MoCA (poor clock draw, poor abstraction, poor verbal fluency). MRI shows bilateral frontal and anterior temporal atrophy. Her mother died at age 60 from ALS. What is the most likely diagnosis, what genetic test is most important, and what pharmacotherapy would you recommend?
Diagnosis: Probable bvFTD (Rascovsky 2011 criteria).
• ≥3 core features: (1) behavioral disinhibition, (2) apathy, (3) loss of empathy, (4) hyperorality/dietary changes, (5) executive dysfunction (neuropsychological profile: poor on MoCA executive tasks, relatively preserved MMSE).
• Functional decline + imaging support (frontal/anterior temporal atrophy).
Most important genetic test: C9orf72 GGGGCC hexanucleotide repeat expansion testing (PCR-based, NOT standard gene panel/sequencing).
• Rationale: family history of ALS in mother; C9orf72 is the #1 genetic cause of both FTD and ALS and the FTD-ALS continuum. Autosomal dominant inheritance.
• Also examine for MND signs (fasciculations, hyperreflexia, wasting) at every visit given family history.
Pharmacotherapy:
1. Trazodone 50 mg → titrate to 150–300 mg/day (for disinhibition, compulsive behaviors)
2. Sertraline 50–100 mg/day (alternative SSRI for behavioral symptoms)
3. Do NOT start AChEI (may worsen behavioral symptoms in bvFTD)
4. Genetic counseling for patient and family members
5. Caregiver support and education (high burden disease)

8.5 Vascular Cognitive Impairment & Vascular Dementia

8.5.1 Classification

Vascular cognitive impairment (VCI) encompasses all cognitive disorders attributable to cerebrovascular disease, from mild VCI to vascular dementia (VaD). VaD is the second most common cause of dementia overall (after AD), and mixed dementia (AD + VCI) is extremely common, especially in the elderly.

SubtypeMechanismClinical FeaturesImaging
Multi-infarct dementiaMultiple cortical/subcortical infarctsStepwise decline; focal neurological signs; temporal relation to stroke eventsMultiple territorial infarcts
Strategic single-infarct dementiaInfarct in critical locationAcute cognitive decline after single stroke (thalamic, PCA territory, angular gyrus, caudate, hippocampal)Single infarct in strategic location
Subcortical ischemic VaD (Binswanger’s)Small vessel disease (SVD) → lacunes + white matter diseaseExecutive dysfunction, processing speed ↓, apathy, gait disturbance, urinary symptoms. Most common form of VaDExtensive WMH (Fazekas 3), lacunes, microbleeds, perivascular spaces
CADASILNOTCH3 mutation → hereditary SVDMigraine with aura (onset ~30s), recurrent strokes (onset ~40–50s), mood disturbance, subcortical dementiaAnterior temporal pole + external capsule WMH (pathognomonic); microbleeds
Post-hemorrhagic VCIICH, SAH, or chronic subduralCognitive impairment following hemorrhage; strategic location-dependentEvidence of prior hemorrhage
CAA-related cognitive impairmentCerebral amyloid angiopathyLobar microbleeds, cortical superficial siderosis, episodic cognitive events (TFNEs)Posterior-predominant lobar microbleeds/siderosis (Boston criteria v2.0)

8.5.2 Diagnostic Criteria (VASCOG 2014)

Major VCI (VaD): (1) Cognitive impairment in ≥1 domain sufficient to impair ADL, (2) Evidence of cerebrovascular disease on neuroimaging, (3) Temporal relationship (onset within 3 months of stroke OR stepwise/fluctuating course), (4) No other cause more likely.

Mild VCI: Same criteria but cognitive impairment does not impair ADL independence.

Clinical Pearl — SVD Burden Assessment on MRI: The total SVD score (Staals et al. 2014) assigns 1 point each for: (1) ≥1 lacune, (2) Fazekas periventricular WMH score 3, (3) ≥1 cerebral microbleed, (4) moderate-severe perivascular spaces in basal ganglia. Total score 0–4; score ≥3 associated with significantly increased stroke recurrence, cognitive decline, and mortality. This is a practical, MRI-based risk stratification tool.

8.5.3 VaD Treatment

  • Secondary stroke prevention: Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin, clopidogrel), anticoagulation (if AF), statin, antihypertensives (target BP <130/80 in established VCI)
  • Risk factor control: Diabetes (HbA1c <7%), smoking cessation, weight management, physical activity
  • Cognitive enhancement: AChEI (donepezil, galantamine) have modest benefit in VaD and mixed dementia (Donepezil 308 Study, Lancet 2004). Memantine may help. Thai NLEM covers donepezil for VaD
  • Depression: Common in VCI (~30%); SSRIs first-line

8.6 Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH)

8.6.1 Overview

NPH is a potentially reversible cause of dementia, characterized by the classic triad (Hakim-Adams syndrome): gait disturbance, dementia, urinary incontinence. Important because VP shunt placement can significantly improve symptoms when correctly identified. Prevalence: ~2–6% of all dementias.

8.6.2 Clinical Features

NPH Classic Triad (“Wet, Wacky, Wobbly”)
FeatureDescriptionOrder of Onset & Response to Shunt
Gait disturbanceMagnetic gait (feet stuck to floor), wide-based, shuffling, short steps, difficulty turning. Gait apraxia. Falls.Usually FIRST to appear; BEST response to shunting (~80% improve)
DementiaSubcortical pattern: executive dysfunction, psychomotor slowing, impaired attention. Memory recall impaired but recognition relatively preserved (≠ AD where encoding fails)Second; moderate response (~50–70% improve)
Urinary incontinenceInitially urinary urgency/frequency, then frank incontinenceThird; variable response

Note: Complete triad present in only ~50–60%. Isolated gait disturbance may be the only early sign. Always consider NPH in elderly patients with unexplained gait difficulty + ventriculomegaly.

8.6.3 Diagnosis

InvestigationFindingsNotes
CT/MRI brainVentriculomegaly (Evans’ index >0.3) disproportionate to sulcal atrophy; DESH (disproportionately enlarged subarachnoid-space hydrocephalus); callosal angle <90°DESH pattern: tight high-convexity sulci + widened Sylvian fissures + ventriculomegaly
CSF tap test (large-volume LP)Remove 30–50 mL CSF; assess gait at 1h, 24h, 72hPositive: ≥10% improvement in gait speed or steps. PPV for shunt response ~90%; but NPV only ~50% (false negatives common)
Extended lumbar drainage (ELD)Continuous CSF drainage (~150–200 mL/day) for 3–5 daysHigher sensitivity than tap test; more predictive of shunt response. Used when tap test equivocal
Opening pressureNormal or high-normal (≤18 cmH2O; some allow up to 24)“Normal pressure” — but pressure is “inappropriately normal” for the degree of ventriculomegaly (Wischke-Laplace law)

8.6.4 Treatment

VP (ventriculoperitoneal) shunt is the definitive treatment. Programmable valve preferred (allows pressure adjustment). LP shunt is an alternative. Endoscopic third ventriculostomy (ETV) has limited evidence in iNPH.

Predictors of good shunt response: Gait as predominant symptom, short symptom duration (<6 months), positive tap test, DESH pattern on MRI, known cause (sSNPH — post-SAH, post-meningitis).

Complications: Subdural hematoma/hygroma (10–15%), infection (3–5%), over-drainage (low-pressure headache), shunt malfunction.

Clinical Pearl — NPH vs. Other Gait Disorders: The gait of NPH (“magnetic”/gait apraxia) must be distinguished from: (1) Parkinsonism (PD, PSP — more rigidity/bradykinesia, response to levodopa), (2) Subcortical VaD (similar gait but vascular risk factors, WMH on MRI without DESH pattern), (3) Cervical spondylotic myelopathy (spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski). Key differentiator: DESH pattern on MRI + positive CSF tap test.

8.7 Reversible Dementias & Rapidly Progressive Dementia (RPD)

8.7.1 Reversible Causes of Dementia

Approximately 5–15% of dementias have a potentially reversible component. Every new dementia evaluation should screen for these “dementia mimics.”

Reversible Dementia — Mandatory Workup
CategoryCauseScreening TestTreatment
MetabolicHypothyroidismTSH, free T4Levothyroxine
Vitamin B12 deficiencySerum B12, methylmalonic acid, homocysteineB12 replacement (IM or high-dose oral)
Hepatic encephalopathyLFTs, ammoniaLactulose, rifaximin
Uremic encephalopathyBUN, creatinineDialysis
Hypo/hypernatremia, hypercalcemiaElectrolytes, calciumCorrection
InfectiousNeurosyphilisRPR/VDRL, FTA-ABS; CSF VDRL if positiveIV penicillin G × 14 days
HIV-associated neurocognitive disorderHIV testART
Chronic CNS infection (TB, fungal)CSF analysisSpecific antimicrobial
StructuralNPHCT/MRI brainVP shunt
Chronic subdural hematomaCT/MRI brainSurgical evacuation
Brain tumor (especially frontal meningioma)MRI brain with contrastSurgery ± adjuvant therapy
PsychiatricDepression (pseudodementia)PHQ-9, geriatric depression scaleAntidepressants, psychotherapy
ToxicMedications (anticholinergics, benzodiazepines, opioids)Medication reviewDiscontinue/substitute offending agent
Alcohol-related cognitive impairment / Wernicke-KorsakoffHistory, thiamine levelThiamine replacement; abstinence
AutoimmuneAutoimmune encephalitis (anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-NMDAR)Serum/CSF autoimmune encephalitis panelImmunotherapy (steroids, IVIg, PLEX, rituximab)

8.7.2 Rapidly Progressive Dementia (RPD)

RPD is defined as dementia developing within weeks to months (usually <1–2 years from onset to severe impairment). It represents a neurological emergency requiring urgent evaluation.

Differential Diagnosis of RPD — “VITAMINS” Mnemonic
CategoryCausesKey Features
V — VascularMulti-infarct, cerebral vasculitis (PACNS), cerebral venous thrombosisStepwise decline, focal signs, stroke risk factors
I — InfectiousCJD (prion), PML, HIV, Whipple diseaseMyoclonus + rapid dementia → think prion
T — Toxic/metabolicWernicke, hepatic/uremic, heavy metals, bismuthHistory of exposure, systemic signs
A — AutoimmuneAutoimmune encephalitis, Hashimoto encephalopathy, neurosarcoidosis, CNS lupusSubacute onset, seizures, psychiatric features, CSF pleocytosis
M — Metastatic/NeoplasticCarcinomatous meningitis, paraneoplastic (anti-Hu, anti-CV2), CNS lymphoma, gliomatosis cerebriWeight loss, known malignancy, CSF cytology
I — IatrogenicMedication-induced (chemotherapy, immunosuppressants), post-radiationTemporal relationship to treatment
N — NeurodegenerativeCJD, rapidly progressive AD (“rpAD”), DLB, FTDrpAD is a diagnosis of exclusion after prion workup
S — Systemic/SeizuresNon-convulsive status epilepticus, systemic illness (sepsis, HLH)EEG essential; may be treatable cause

8.7.3 Prion Diseases / Creutzfeldt-Jakob Disease (CJD)

CJD is the prototypical prion disease — a transmissible spongiform encephalopathy caused by conformational change of normal cellular prion protein (PrPC) to pathological PrPSc.

Types of CJD
TypeFrequencyOnsetFeatures
Sporadic CJD (sCJD)~85%Mean ~65yRapidly progressive dementia + myoclonus + ataxia. Median survival 5 months. Akinetic mutism as end-stage
Genetic CJD~10–15%VariablePRNP mutations (E200K most common); may have longer course. Includes FFI (D178N + 129M) and GSS (P102L)
Variant CJD (vCJD)RareYoung (median 28y)BSE-related; psychiatric onset, painful sensory symptoms, later dementia + movement disorder. “Pulvinar sign” on MRI
Iatrogenic CJDVery rareVariableDura mater grafts, growth hormone, corneal transplants, contaminated neurosurgical instruments

Diagnosis of sCJD

TestFindingPerformance
MRI DWI/FLAIRCortical ribboning (high signal in cortex) + caudate/putamen signalSn 92–96%, Sp 93–94%. Best initial diagnostic test. DWI more sensitive than FLAIR
CSF RT-QuICPrion seed amplification assay (positive)Sn 92–95%, Sp ~100%. Now the gold standard antemortem test. Included in CDC 2018 diagnostic criteria
CSF 14-3-3 proteinElevatedSn ~85%, Sp ~65%. Non-specific (elevated in stroke, encephalitis). Largely supplanted by RT-QuIC
CSF t-tauMarkedly elevated (>1,150 pg/mL)Sn ~85%, Sp ~85%. Better than 14-3-3. Very high t-tau (>10,000) strongly suggests CJD
EEGPeriodic sharp wave complexes (PSWC) at ~1 HzSn 64%, Sp 91%. Late finding; may be absent in early disease. Seen in ~60% of sCJD
Brain biopsy/autopsySpongiform change, PrPSc immunohistochemistryDefinitive diagnosis. Rarely done antemortem (RT-QuIC has reduced need)
Clinical Pearl — RPD Workup Protocol: For any patient with subacute cognitive decline over weeks-months, the initial workup should include: (1) MRI brain with DWI (rule out CJD, stroke, mass), (2) EEG (rule out NCSE, CJD periodic complexes), (3) CSF: cell count, protein, glucose, cytology, 14-3-3/RT-QuIC, autoimmune encephalitis panel, (4) Blood: CBC, CMP, TSH, B12, HIV, RPR, autoimmune encephalitis panel, paraneoplastic antibodies, (5) CT chest/abdomen/pelvis if paraneoplastic suspected. Treatable causes (autoimmune, infectious, metabolic) must be identified urgently.
Q: A 67-year-old woman presents with 2-month rapidly progressive cognitive decline, myoclonus, visual disturbance, and gait ataxia. MRI DWI shows cortical ribboning in bilateral parietal and occipital cortex with caudate hyperintensity. EEG shows generalized periodic sharp wave complexes. CSF 14-3-3 is positive and RT-QuIC is positive. What is the diagnosis and prognosis?
Diagnosis: Probable sporadic CJD (meets CDC 2018 criteria).
• Rapidly progressive dementia + myoclonus + ataxia + visual disturbance (Heidenhain variant with occipital involvement)
• MRI: cortical ribboning + caudate signal on DWI (highly characteristic)
• EEG: periodic sharp wave complexes
• CSF RT-QuIC positive (Sn 92–95%, Sp ~100%) — essentially confirms prion disease antemortem
Prognosis: Devastating. Median survival for sCJD is ~5 months (90% die within 1 year). No disease-modifying treatment exists.
Management:
1. Palliative care discussion and goals of care planning (immediate priority)
2. Symptomatic: clonazepam for myoclonus (0.5–2 mg), sodium valproate alternative
3. Infection control precautions for surgical instruments (prion-specific decontamination); standard precautions sufficient for routine care
4. PRNP sequencing to exclude genetic form (important for family counseling)
5. Report to national CJD surveillance (Thai Department of Disease Control)
6. Family support and counseling

8.8 References & Key Guidelines

Key References & Guidelines

Biomarkers & Diagnostic Frameworks

  • Jack CR Jr et al. NIA-AA Research Framework: Toward a biological definition of Alzheimer’s disease. Alzheimers Dement 2018;14(4):535–562.
  • Jack CR Jr et al. Revised criteria for diagnosis and staging of Alzheimer’s disease: Alzheimer’s Association workgroup. Nat Med 2024;30(4):999–1016. [NIA-AA 2024 Revised Criteria]
  • Thientunyakit T et al. Molecular neuroimaging in Alzheimer’s disease. J Med Biol Eng 2022;42:301–310.
  • Bjorkli C et al. Biomarkers of Alzheimer’s disease: a chronobiological approach. Front Aging Neurosci 2020;12:471.
  • Ossenkoppele R et al. Amyloid and tau PET-positive cognitively unimpaired individuals are at high risk for future cognitive decline. Nat Med 2022;28(11):2381–2387.
  • Chêtelat G et al. Amyloid-PET and 18F-FDG-PET in the diagnostic investigation of Alzheimer’s disease and other dementias. Lancet Neurol 2020;19(11):951–962.
  • Thientunyakit T, Muangpaisan W, Minoshima S. FDG-PET patterns in neurodegenerative dementia. Semin Nucl Med 2025 (in press).

Clinical Trials & Landmark Studies

  • van Dyck CH et al. Lecanemab in early Alzheimer’s disease. N Engl J Med 2023;388(1):9–21. [CLARITY-AD]
  • Sims JR et al. Donanemab in early symptomatic Alzheimer disease: the TRAILBLAZER-ALZ 2 randomized clinical trial. JAMA 2023;330(6):512–527.
  • Howard R et al. Donepezil and memantine for moderate-to-severe Alzheimer’s disease. N Engl J Med 2012;366:893–903. [DOMINO]
  • Rosenberg PB et al. Safety and efficacy of methylphenidate for apathy in Alzheimer’s disease: a randomized, placebo-controlled trial (ADMET 2). Lancet Neurol 2021;20(10):816–825.
  • Venegas C et al. Microglia-derived ASC specks cross-seed amyloid-β in Alzheimer’s disease. Nature 2017;552:355–361.
  • Liddelow SA et al. Neurotoxic reactive astrocytes are induced by activated microglia. Nature 2017;541:481–487.
  • Postuma RB et al. Risk and predictors of dementia and parkinsonism in idiopathic REM sleep behaviour disorder: a multicentre study. Brain 2019;142(3):744–759. [NAPS]

Diagnostic Criteria & Consensus

  • McKeith IG et al. Diagnosis and management of dementia with Lewy bodies: fourth consensus report. Neurology 2017;89:88–100.
  • Rascovsky K et al. Sensitivity of revised diagnostic criteria for the behavioural variant of frontotemporal dementia. Brain 2011;134(Pt 9):2456–2477. [FTDC Criteria]
  • Gorno-Tempini ML et al. Classification of primary progressive aphasia and its variants. Neurology 2011;76(11):1006–1014.
  • Höglinger GU et al. Clinical diagnosis of progressive supranuclear palsy: the movement disorder society criteria. Mov Disord 2017;32(6):853–864. [MDS-PSP Criteria]
  • Sachdev P et al. Diagnostic criteria for vascular cognitive disorders: a VASCOG statement. Alzheimer Dis Assoc Disord 2014;28(3):206–218.
  • Zerr I et al. Updated clinical diagnostic criteria for sporadic Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Brain 2009;132(Pt 10):2659–2668.

Thai Context & Regional Data

  • Muangpaisan W et al. Dementia prevalence in Thailand: a systematic review. Thai Journal of Neurology (various years).
  • Thai Dementia Guidelines (Royal College of Physicians of Thailand / Neurological Society of Thailand) — 2020 update.
  • NLEM (National List of Essential Medicines): Donepezil, memantine covered; rivastigmine patch requires special authorization.
  • Witsarut Nanthasi. World of Dementia (Siriraj Neurology Lecture Series). Faculty of Medicine Siriraj Hospital, Mahidol University, 2026.
  • Muangpaisan W. Biomarkers in Dementia: The Evolution to Precision. SiACG Conference, Siriraj Hospital, 2026.

9. Sleep Disorders

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of sleep disorders relevant to neurological practice. Coverage includes obstructive sleep apnea (PALM endotyping, PSG interpretation, CPAP/hypoglossal nerve stimulation/SURMOUNT-OSA pharmacotherapy, cardiovascular consequences, glymphatic system), narcolepsy (orexin pathophysiology, ICSD-3 criteria, MSLT protocol, complete pharmacotherapy including sodium oxybate/pitolisant/solriamfetol), REM sleep behavior disorder (as the strongest prodromal biomarker of synucleinopathy — iRBD phenoconversion data, αSyn-SAA, drug-induced RBD), NREM parasomnias (differential with SHE/nocturnal seizures), chronic insomnia (CBT-I components, DORA/Z-drug/melatonin agonist pharmacotherapy), circadian rhythm disorders (DSWPD, shift work, jet lag, Non-24), restless legs syndrome (IRLSSG criteria, iron pathway, augmentation management, α2δ ligand-first approach), sleep-related epilepsy (SHE), and sleep disorders in neurological disease.

9.1 Obstructive Sleep Apnea (OSA) — Comprehensive Review

9.1.1 Epidemiology & Pathophysiology

OSA is the most common sleep-related breathing disorder, affecting ~15–30% of men and ~10–15% of women (higher with obesity epidemic). Prevalence in Thailand: ~11.4% in urban adults (Bangkok Metropolitan Sleep Study). Markedly underdiagnosed — up to 80% of moderate-severe OSA cases remain unrecognized.

Pathophysiology: The Multi-Level Model

Endotype (Pathophysiological Trait)MechanismClinical Implication
Anatomical compromise (Vpassive)Small/collapsible pharyngeal airway: retrognathia, macroglossia, tonsillar hypertrophy, obesity (fat deposition in parapharyngeal space), craniofacial narrowingMost important factor (~70% of patients). Targeted by CPAP, MAD, surgery, weight loss
High loop gainOversensitive ventilatory control system: small ↑CO2 → exaggerated ventilatory response → overshoot → undershoot → cyclical collapseExplains central/mixed apnea patterns; may benefit from O2, acetazolamide, adaptive servo-ventilation (ASV) in some cases
Low arousal thresholdEasily aroused from sleep by minor respiratory events → prevents compensatory neuromuscular response → fragmented sleep~30–50% of OSA patients; may benefit from sedatives (low-dose trazodone, eszopiclone) to reduce arousals — counterintuitive but evidence-supported
Poor pharyngeal muscle responsivenessInadequate genioglossus/dilator muscle compensation during sleepTarget for hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire); desipramine, atomoxetine + oxybutynin combination (PALM trial concept)
Clinical Pearl — OSA Endotyping: The PALM model (Pcrit, Arousal threshold, Loop gain, Muscle responsiveness) explains why not all OSA patients respond equally to CPAP. Future precision medicine in OSA aims to match therapy to the dominant endotype: anatomical → CPAP/surgery; high loop gain → O2/acetazolamide; low arousal threshold → sedative; poor muscle → hypoglossal nerve stimulation. The Wellman et al. AJRCCM 2011 framework established this endotyping paradigm.

9.1.2 Diagnosis

ICSD-3 Diagnostic Criteria for OSA

Criterion A (symptomatic): Presence of ≥1 symptom (EDS, non-restorative sleep, fatigue, insomnia, snoring, witnessed apneas, nocturnal gasping/choking) AND PSG/HSAT showing AHI ≥5/h (predominantly obstructive events).

Criterion B (asymptomatic): AHI ≥15/h regardless of symptoms (associated with cardiovascular risk even without symptoms).

SeverityAHIAssociated Risk
Mild5–14/hEDS, cognitive impairment, mild cardiovascular risk elevation
Moderate15–29/hSignificant cardiovascular risk; hypertension; arrhythmia risk
Severe≥30/hHigh cardiovascular risk; resistant HTN; atrial fibrillation; stroke; sudden cardiac death

Screening Tools

ToolComponentsInterpretation
STOP-BANGSnoring; Tired; Observed apnea; Pressure (HTN); BMI >35; Age >50; Neck >40 cm; Gender (male)≥3: high risk; ≥5: very high risk for moderate-severe OSA. Sn 90%, Sp 36% at ≥3 (high sensitivity, low specificity)
Epworth Sleepiness Scale (ESS)8 situational sleepiness questions (0–24)>10: excessive daytime sleepiness; >16: severe. Subjective; poor correlation with AHI
Berlin Questionnaire3 categories: snoring, daytime fatigue, BMI/HTN≥2 positive categories: high risk

Polysomnography (PSG) vs. Home Sleep Apnea Test (HSAT)

FeatureIn-Lab PSG (Level 1)HSAT (Level 3)
ChannelsEEG, EOG, EMG, ECG, airflow (nasal pressure + thermistor), respiratory effort (chest/abdomen belts), SpO2, body position, videoAirflow, respiratory effort, SpO2 ± body position (no EEG)
Scoring metricAHI (events per hour of sleep)REI (Respiratory Event Index: events per hour of recording time) — may underestimate severity
IndicationGold standard; required for: central sleep apnea suspicion, narcolepsy/parasomnia (MSLT), complex patients (CHF, COPD, neuromuscular, opioid use)Appropriate for high pre-test probability of moderate-severe OSA in patients without significant comorbidities
LimitationCost, waiting times, first-night effectUnderestimates AHI; cannot detect central events reliably; misses REM-related OSA if low REM time
Key PSG Event Definitions (AASM 2024 Scoring Manual)
EventDefinition
Obstructive Apnea≥90% reduction in airflow ≥10 seconds with continued respiratory effort
Central Apnea≥90% reduction in airflow ≥10 seconds without respiratory effort
Mixed ApneaBegins as central, ends with obstructive effort
Hypopnea (Recommended)≥30% reduction in airflow ≥10 seconds + ≥3% O2 desaturation OR arousal
Hypopnea (Acceptable)≥30% reduction in airflow ≥10 seconds + ≥4% O2 desaturation
RERARespiratory Effort-Related Arousal: increasing effort leading to arousal, not meeting apnea/hypopnea criteria
RDIAHI + RERAs (used for Upper Airway Resistance Syndrome: RDI ≥5 but AHI <5)

9.1.3 Cardiovascular & Systemic Consequences of OSA

SystemConsequenceMechanism
CardiovascularResistant hypertension (most common CV consequence; 50–80% of resistant HTN have OSA), atrial fibrillation (×2–4 risk), heart failure, coronary artery disease, aortic dissectionIntermittent hypoxia → sympathetic activation, oxidative stress, endothelial dysfunction, systemic inflammation
CerebrovascularStroke risk ×2–3; worse stroke outcomes; cognitive impairmentNocturnal BP surges, AF, paradoxical embolism (PFO + apnea-related right-to-left shunt)
MetabolicInsulin resistance, T2DM (×1.5–2 risk), metabolic syndromeIntermittent hypoxia → sympathetic activation → cortisol ↑, adipokine dysregulation
PulmonaryPulmonary hypertension (10–20% of severe OSA), obesity hypoventilation syndrome (OHS) overlapChronic intermittent hypoxia → pulmonary vasoconstriction → vascular remodeling
NeuropsychiatricEDS, impaired vigilance, ×2.5 MVA risk, depression, cognitive decline, ↑ AD biomarker abnormalitiesSleep fragmentation, intermittent hypoxia → hippocampal damage, glymphatic clearance impairment
Clinical Pearl — OSA and Glymphatic System: The glymphatic system (brain’s waste clearance pathway) is most active during deep sleep. OSA causes sleep fragmentation and intermittent hypoxia, impairing glymphatic Aβ clearance. Multiple studies show OSA is associated with earlier amyloid deposition and accelerated cognitive decline. CPAP treatment may partially reverse this — a bridge between sleep medicine and the dementia biomarker framework (Section 8). This is relevant for your neurology and stroke/dementia practice.

9.1.4 Treatment

OSA Treatment Algorithm
TreatmentIndicationDetails
CPAPFirst-line for all moderate-severe OSA; mild OSA with symptomsGoal: AHI <5/h on therapy. Adherence target: ≥4h/night ≥70% nights (CMS definition). Auto-CPAP (APAP) preferred for most (auto-adjusting 4–20 cmH2O). Fixed CPAP if central apneas emerge on auto. Heated humidification reduces nasal dryness. Mask fitting crucial (nasal > full-face for adherence unless mouth breathing).
BiPAPCPAP intolerance (high pressure needs), OHS, COPD-OSA overlap, neuromuscularIPAP/EPAP differential; BiPAP-ST for OHS/hypoventilation (backup rate). Average volume-assured pressure support (AVAPS) for OHS.
Mandibular Advancement Device (MAD)Mild-moderate OSA; CPAP intolerance; patient preferenceCustom-fitted by sleep dentist preferred over OTC. AHI reduction ~50%. Better adherence than CPAP but less efficacy for severe OSA.
Hypoglossal Nerve Stimulation (Inspire)Moderate-severe OSA (AHI 15–65); CPAP intolerant; BMI <40 (updated to <40); no complete concentric collapse on DISESTAR Trial (Strollo et al. NEJM 2014): 68% AHI reduction at 12 months. Implanted device stimulates CN XII (genioglossus) synchronized with respiration. FDA approved 2014. Not yet available in Thailand but expanding in Asia-Pacific.
Positional therapySupine-predominant OSA (AHI supine ≥2× non-supine)Positional devices (NightBalance, tennis ball technique). Effective for ~25% of OSA patients with positional component.
Weight lossAll OSA patients with BMI ≥2510% weight loss → ~26% AHI reduction. Bariatric surgery: 75% AHI reduction but rarely cures OSA. SURMOUNT-OSA (Malhotra et al. NEJM 2024): tirzepatide 10–15 mg weekly → AHI reduction of ~50–60% in obese OSA patients (with or without CPAP); weight loss ~18–20%.
Pharmacotherapy (emerging)Adjunct or specific endotype targetingPALM combination: atomoxetine + oxybutynin (NRI + antimuscarinic): ↓AHI by ~50% in selected patients (targets muscle responsiveness endotype). Acetazolamide for high loop gain. Sulthiame (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor): Phase 3 positive 2024.

Surgical Options

SurgeryMechanismIndication
UPPP (Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty)Removes redundant oropharyngeal tissueRetropalatal obstruction on DISE; ~50% success rate (Sher criteria); less predictable than CPAP
Tonsillectomy ± adenoidectomyRemoves tonsillar obstructionFirst-line in pediatric OSA; adults with tonsillar hypertrophy (Grade 3–4)
Maxillomandibular advancement (MMA)Advances maxilla + mandible 10–12 mm → enlarges retrolingual/retropalatal airwaySevere OSA with craniofacial restriction; most effective surgery (~85–90% success); significant morbidity
TracheostomyBypasses upper airway entirelyLife-threatening OSA refractory to all other treatments; last resort (100% effective for OSA by definition)
Clinical Pearl — OSA in Stroke Patients: OSA prevalence in acute stroke is 50–75%. Sleep-disordered breathing worsens stroke outcomes (larger infarct volume, worse functional recovery, higher recurrence risk). Screen ALL stroke patients for OSA (STOP-BANG, then HSAT/PSG when stable). CPAP within 1–2 weeks post-stroke improves neurological recovery and reduces recurrent vascular events. The SAVE trial (McEvoy et al. NEJM 2016) showed modest benefit of CPAP for secondary cardiovascular prevention, but adherence was suboptimal (mean 3.3 h/night) — better adherence may yield greater benefit.
Q: A 52-year-old obese man (BMI 38) presents with excessive daytime sleepiness (ESS 16/24), witnessed apneas, morning headaches, and resistant hypertension on 3 antihypertensives. STOP-BANG score is 7. HSAT shows REI 42/h with nadir SpO2 74%. He is prescribed auto-CPAP but returns after 2 months reporting he cannot tolerate the mask. What are the next steps?
Assessment: Severe OSA (REI 42/h), significant hypoxemia, resistant HTN (likely OSA-driven), high cardiovascular risk. CPAP intolerance is common (~30–50% struggle with adherence).
Step-by-step approach:
1. Troubleshoot CPAP adherence first:
 • Mask interface: try different types (nasal pillows, nasal mask, full-face); professional mask fitting
 • Pressure comfort: ensure auto-CPAP range is appropriate; try EPR/pressure relief; ramp settings
 • Humidification: heated humidifier for nasal congestion/dryness
 • Behavioral: desensitization protocol, CBT for CPAP adherence
 • Download CPAP data: check actual usage hours, leak rates, residual AHI
2. If CPAP truly intolerable after optimization:
 • Hypoglossal nerve stimulation (Inspire): candidate if AHI 15–65, BMI now <40 (updated criteria), CPAP intolerant, no concentric collapse on DISE
 • MAD (mandibular advancement device): reasonable but may not adequately treat severe OSA (AHI 42)
3. Weight loss (critical):
 • GLP-1/GIP receptor agonist (tirzepatide or semaglutide): dual benefit — weight loss + direct AHI reduction (SURMOUNT-OSA data)
 • Consider bariatric surgery referral given BMI 38 + comorbidities
4. Address resistant hypertension: Treat OSA to improve BP control; add spironolactone (PATHWAY-2); renal denervation if truly resistant
5. Driving safety: Counsel about drowsy driving risk with untreated severe OSA and ESS 16

9.2 Narcolepsy — Comprehensive Review

9.2.1 Pathophysiology & Classification

Narcolepsy is a chronic disorder of central hypersomnolence caused by impaired orexin (hypocretin) signaling. Prevalence: ~25–50/100,000. Two types with fundamentally different biology:

Narcolepsy Type 1 vs. Type 2
FeatureNarcolepsy Type 1 (NT1)Narcolepsy Type 2 (NT2)
PathophysiologyAutoimmune destruction of orexin-producing neurons in lateral hypothalamus (>90% loss). T-cell mediated (CD8+ cytotoxic T cells targeting orexin neurons)Unknown; partial orexin loss? Orexin neurons intact but dysfunctional? ~30% may convert to NT1 over time
CSF orexin≤110 pg/mL (or <1/3 of mean normal) — diagnosticNormal (>110 pg/mL) or intermediate
CataplexyPresent (pathognomonic for NT1)Absent
HLA associationHLA-DQB1*06:02 in >90–98% (but also present in 12–38% of general population; necessary but NOT sufficient)HLA-DQB1*06:02 in ~40–50%
MSLTMean sleep latency ≤8 min + ≥2 SOREMPsMean sleep latency ≤8 min + ≥2 SOREMPs (same criteria; diagnosis of exclusion)
ComorbiditiesObesity (~30%), sleep paralysis (~50%), hypnagogic hallucinations (~60%), disrupted nocturnal sleepSimilar but generally less severe

Orexin/Hypocretin System

The orexin system is a master arousal/wake-promoting center:

ComponentDetails
Orexin neurons~70,000 neurons in lateral hypothalamus; produce orexin-A (hypocretin-1) and orexin-B (hypocretin-2)
ProjectionsWidespread: locus coeruleus (NE), dorsal raphe (5-HT), TMN (histamine), VTA (DA), basal forebrain (ACh), LDT/PPT — stabilizes the wake-sleep flip-flop switch
FunctionStabilize wakefulness; prevent inappropriate transitions to REM/NREM; regulate reward/motivation, feeding, autonomic function
Loss in NT1>90% orexin neuron loss → unstable state boundaries → intrusion of REM phenomena into wakefulness (cataplexy, sleep paralysis, hallucinations, SOREMPs)
Clinical Pearl — Autoimmune Triggers: NT1 onset frequently follows environmental triggers in genetically susceptible (HLA-DQB1*06:02+) individuals. Documented triggers include: H1N1 influenza infection (2009 pandemic → NT1 incidence spike), Pandemrix vaccine (AS03-adjuvanted H1N1 vaccine, especially in Scandinavian children — OR ~6–9), Streptococcal infection (molecular mimicry with orexin), and possibly SARS-CoV-2 (case reports). Typical latency: weeks to months after trigger. This makes NT1 one of the best-characterized autoimmune neurological diseases from an etiological standpoint.

9.2.2 Clinical Features

The Narcolepsy Pentad (NT1)

SymptomDescriptionFrequency in NT1
Excessive Daytime Sleepiness (EDS)Irresistible sleep attacks; brief refreshing naps; worst in monotonous situations. Most disabling symptom100% (required for diagnosis)
CataplexySudden bilateral loss of muscle tone triggered by positive emotions (laughter, surprise, excitement). Consciousness preserved. Duration: seconds to minutes. Can be partial (jaw drop, head nod, knee buckling) or complete (collapse)~70–80% of NT1. Pathognomonic
Sleep paralysisInability to move during sleep-wake transitions (seconds to minutes). Frightening but benign~50%
Hypnagogic/hypnopompic hallucinationsVivid visual/auditory hallucinations at sleep onset (hypnagogic) or awakening (hypnopompic)~60%
Disrupted nocturnal sleep (DNS)Frequent awakenings; fragmented sleep despite EDS. Paradoxical: sleepy during day, cannot maintain sleep at night~50–70%
Clinical Pearl — Cataplexy Mimics: Not all “drop attacks” are cataplexy. Key differential: (1) Syncope — loss of consciousness (absent in cataplexy); (2) Epileptic atonic seizures — EEG abnormality, not emotion-triggered; (3) Gelastic seizures — laughter-induced (hypothalamic hamartoma); (4) Psychogenic/functional — variable triggers, no consistent pattern. True cataplexy: preserved consciousness, emotion trigger (especially laughter), brief (<2 min), bilateral. Facial cataplexy (jaw drop, tongue protrusion) is highly specific for NT1 in children.

9.2.3 Diagnostic Workup

ICSD-3 Revised Diagnostic Criteria for Narcolepsy
CriterionNT1NT2
A (required)Daily periods of irrepressible need to sleep or daytime lapses into sleep for ≥3 months
B (NT1)≥1 of: (1) Cataplexy + MSLT positive; (2) CSF orexin-A ≤110 pg/mL—
B (NT2)—MSLT: mean sleep latency ≤8 min + ≥2 SOREMPs. CSF orexin-A >110 pg/mL or not measured. No cataplexy
ExclusionsOSA adequately treated (AHI <5), insufficient sleep excluded (sleep diary/actigraphy ≥7 days, ≥7h sleep opportunity), medication effects excluded (REM-suppressing drugs stopped ≥14 days or 5× half-life)

MSLT Protocol & Interpretation

AspectDetails
Protocol5 nap opportunities at 2-hour intervals beginning 1.5–3 hours after waking. Each nap: 20 minutes (or 15 min after sleep onset). Prior night PSG required (≥6h sleep). Stop REM-suppressants ≥14 days prior. Actigraphy/sleep diary ≥7 days showing adequate sleep (≥7h)
Sleep latencyMean across 5 naps. ≤8 minutes = pathological sleepiness
SOREMPSleep Onset REM Period: REM within 15 minutes of sleep onset. ≥2 SOREMPs required. A SOREMP on prior-night PSG (within 15 min of sleep onset) can count as one of the two
Positive MSLTMean SL ≤8 min + ≥2 SOREMPs → consistent with narcolepsy. Sensitivity ~90–95% for NT1; ~70–80% for NT2. Specificity issues: false positives in OSA, insufficient sleep, circadian disorders, depression
Clinical Pearl — Common MSLT Pitfalls: (1) Insufficient pre-MSLT sleep (<6h) → false positive SOREMPs. Always review prior-night PSG sleep time. (2) REM-suppressing drugs not adequately washed out (SSRIs, SNRIs, TCAs) → rebound SOREMPs after cessation = false positive; suppressed SOREMPs if still on drug = false negative. (3) Untreated OSA can cause short sleep latency on MSLT → treat OSA first, then repeat MSLT. (4) Circadian misalignment (shift workers, delayed sleep phase) can produce SOREMPs during habitual sleep time.

9.2.4 Pharmacotherapy

Narcolepsy Pharmacotherapy — Complete Guide
DrugTargetMechanismDoseKey Notes
Sodium oxybate (Xyrem)EDS + cataplexy + DNSGABAB agonist; consolidates deep sleep (SWS); mechanism for cataplexy control unclear2.25 g at bedtime + repeat 2.5–4h later (split dosing); titrate to 4.5 g ×2/night (max 9 g/night)Gold standard for NT1 (addresses all symptoms). REMS program. Avoid alcohol. Monitor respiratory depression. Sodium load concern (hypertension, HF) → low-sodium formulation (calcium/magnesium/potassium oxybate = Xywav). Takes 2–3 months for full cataplexy effect
Low-sodium oxybate (Xywav)EDS + cataplexy + DNSSame as Xyrem but 92% less sodiumSame dosing as XyremFDA 2020; preferred in patients with cardiovascular risk; also approved for NT2 and idiopathic hypersomnia (IH)
Once-nightly oxybate (FT218/Lumryz)EDS + cataplexyExtended-release sodium oxybate; single nightly dose4.5 g single dose at bedtime; titrate to 6–9 gFDA 2023. Eliminates need for mid-sleep second dose. Improved adherence. REMS program
ModafinilEDS (first-line wake-promoter)Weak DAT inhibitor; promotes wakefulness via DA/NE/histamine pathways100–200 mg morning; may add 100–200 mg noon (max 400 mg/day)Non-scheduled in some countries. Induces CYP3A4 → reduces oral contraceptive efficacy. SJS/TEN risk (rare). No effect on cataplexy
ArmodafinilEDSR-enantiomer of modafinil; longer half-life150–250 mg morningMore sustained wakefulness; same CYP3A4 induction
Pitolisant (Wakix)EDS + cataplexyH3 receptor inverse agonist → ↑histamine release from TMN; also ↑NE, DA, ACh4.45 mg → 8.9 mg → 17.8 mg → max 35.6 mg morningFDA 2019 (EDS); 2020 (cataplexy). Non-scheduled. Novel mechanism. QTc prolongation risk — avoid with QT-prolonging drugs. First-in-class. May be combined with modafinil
Solriamfetol (Sunosi)EDSDual DNRI (dopamine/norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor)75 mg → 150 mg morning (max 150 mg)FDA 2019. Rapid onset. Scheduled (IV). BP/HR monitoring. Also approved for EDS in OSA (on CPAP with residual sleepiness)
Methylphenidate / AmphetaminesEDSDAT/NET inhibitor (methylphenidate); DA/NE releaser (amphetamine)Methylphenidate: 10–60 mg/day; Dextroamphetamine: 5–60 mg/dayTraditional stimulants. Effective but higher abuse potential (Schedule II). Reserve for refractory cases or where modafinil unavailable (used in Thailand)
VenlafaxineCataplexy (off-label)SNRI → REM suppression via NE/5-HT75–225 mg/dayWidely used for cataplexy. NEVER stop abruptly → rebound cataplexy crisis (status cataplecticus). Taper over 2+ weeks
Clomipramine / TCAsCataplexyPotent REM suppression (NE/5-HT reuptake)Clomipramine 10–75 mg/dayMost potent anti-cataplectic; limited by anticholinergic side effects. Abrupt withdrawal → rebound cataplexy

Treatment Algorithm

Practical Treatment Approach

NT1 with cataplexy:

  1. Sodium oxybate / low-sodium oxybate (addresses EDS + cataplexy + disrupted nocturnal sleep) — if available and tolerated, best single agent
  2. Add modafinil/armodafinil in the morning if residual EDS on oxybate
  3. Alternative for cataplexy: pitolisant (addresses both EDS + cataplexy) or venlafaxine/clomipramine (cataplexy only)
  4. Refractory EDS: solriamfetol, methylphenidate, or amphetamine add-on

NT2 / EDS without cataplexy:

  1. Modafinil/armodafinil first-line
  2. Pitolisant or solriamfetol if inadequate
  3. Low-sodium oxybate (Xywav) — FDA approved for NT2 and IH

Thai context: Modafinil is available in Thailand (imported, special authorization). Sodium oxybate, pitolisant, solriamfetol are NOT available in Thailand. Methylphenidate is available (narcotic license required). Venlafaxine is widely available for cataplexy treatment.

Clinical Pearl — Narcolepsy in Children: Childhood-onset NT1 often presents differently: (1) cataplexy may manifest as “cataplectic facies” (perioral muscle hypotonia, tongue protrusion, eyelid drooping) rather than classic collapse; (2) rapid weight gain at disease onset (orexin regulates appetite/metabolism); (3) precocious puberty reported; (4) behavioral/attention problems may lead to ADHD misdiagnosis. CSF orexin measurement is particularly valuable in children where MSLT may be unreliable.
Q: A 28-year-old woman presents with 6 months of irresistible daytime sleepiness with daily “sleep attacks” and episodes of jaw drop and knee buckling triggered by laughter. She has vivid hallucinations when falling asleep. MSLT shows mean sleep latency 4.2 minutes with 4 SOREMPs. HLA-DQB1*06:02 is positive. What is the diagnosis and optimal treatment plan?
Diagnosis: Narcolepsy Type 1 (NT1)
• EDS ≥3 months — criterion A met
• Cataplexy present (jaw drop + knee buckling triggered by laughter = partial cataplexy) + MSLT positive (SL 4.2 min ≤8 + 4 SOREMPs ≥2) — criterion B met
• HLA-DQB1*06:02 positive supports diagnosis (98% of NT1) but is not required for diagnosis
• CSF orexin measurement would confirm (≤110 pg/mL expected) but not required when cataplexy + MSLT positive
Optimal treatment:
1. Sodium oxybate (Xyrem) or low-sodium oxybate (Xywav) — gold standard addressing EDS + cataplexy + disrupted nocturnal sleep. Start 2.25 g × 2 nightly, titrate over 4–8 weeks. Expect cataplexy control in 2–3 months
2. Add modafinil 200 mg morning for residual daytime sleepiness (often needed in combination)
3. If oxybate unavailable (e.g., Thailand):
 • Modafinil 200–400 mg/day for EDS
 • Venlafaxine 75–150 mg/day for cataplexy (warn: never stop abruptly → status cataplecticus)
4. Non-pharmacological: Scheduled naps (15–20 min, 1–2×/day); sleep hygiene; driving safety counseling; work/school accommodations
5. Contraception counseling: Modafinil reduces OCP efficacy (CYP3A4 induction) → use barrier methods or IUD

9.3 REM Sleep Behavior Disorder — The Prodromal Synucleinopathy

9.3.1 Pathophysiology & Definition

RBD is characterized by loss of normal REM atonia with resultant dream-enactment behavior. During normal REM sleep, subcortical circuits (sublaterodorsal nucleus/subcoeruleus → spinal motor neurons via ventromedial medulla) produce active muscle atonia. In RBD, α-synuclein deposition disrupts these brainstem circuits, allowing motor activity during REM.

ICSD-3 Diagnostic Criteria for RBD
CriterionDetails
ARepeated episodes of vocalization and/or complex motor behaviors during sleep
BBehaviors documented by PSG to occur during REM sleep OR presumed to be REM-related based on clinical history of dream enactment
CPSG demonstrates REM sleep without atonia (RSWA) on chin or limb EMG
DNot better explained by another sleep disorder, mental disorder, medication, or substance

Key PSG finding: RSWA — sustained (tonic) or intermittent (phasic) elevation of chin EMG or excessive phasic activity in limb EMG during REM sleep. Quantitative thresholds: tonic chin EMG >30% of REM epochs AND/OR phasic activity >15% of 3-second mini-epochs.

9.3.2 Isolated RBD (iRBD): The Strongest Prodromal Biomarker of Synucleinopathy

iRBD Phenoconversion Data
StudyCohortConversion RateKey Findings
Postuma et al. Brain 2019 (NAPS cohort)1,280 iRBD patients; multicenter6.3%/year; 73.5% at 12 yearsConversion to: PD (43%), DLB (25%), MSA (5%), unspecified parkinsonism/dementia (remainder). Median time to conversion ~8 years
Iranzo et al. Lancet Neurol 2014174 iRBD; 7.7-year follow-up90.9% at 14 yearsLongest follow-up; virtually all convert eventually

Phenoconversion predictors:

  • Abnormal DaT-SPECT (reduced striatal uptake) — predicts conversion to PD/DLB within 3–5 years
  • αSyn-SAA positive (CSF or skin biopsy) — ~90% of iRBD patients; confirms underlying synucleinopathy
  • Olfactory dysfunction (UPSIT score <22) — strong predictor
  • Cognitive impairment (MoCA decline, especially visuospatial/attention) — predicts conversion to DLB rather than PD
  • Color vision impairment, constipation, orthostatic hypotension, depression
Clinical Pearl — iRBD in Clinical Practice: Every patient diagnosed with iRBD should be counseled about the high lifetime risk of developing PD, DLB, or MSA. However, delivery requires sensitivity — no neuroprotective therapy is yet available outside clinical trials. Recommended approach: (1) Annual neurological examination (motor, cognitive, autonomic screening), (2) Baseline and serial DaT-SPECT/αSyn-SAA if available, (3) Discuss enrollment in neuroprotective clinical trials (PASADENA (prasinezumab), PADOVA targeting prodromal PD), (4) Manage RBD symptoms for safety and bed-partner quality of life.

Drug-Induced RBD

Drug ClassMechanismManagement
SSRIs / SNRIs (fluoxetine, venlafaxine, most common culprits)Serotonergic enhancement of REM atonia loss; increase phasic EMG activity in REMIf possible, switch to bupropion or mirtazapine (less RBD risk). If must continue: treat symptomatically with melatonin
TCAsSimilar to SSRIs; NE/5-HT reuptakeDose reduction or switch
Beta-blockers (bisoprolol, atenolol)Melatonin suppression; REM disruptionSwitch to non-lipophilic agent or add melatonin
