Important distinction: Drug-induced RBD may unmask subclinical synucleinopathy rather than being purely pharmacogenic. SSRI-associated RBD patients have higher rates of DaT-SPECT abnormalities and eventual phenoconversion than the general population — monitor these patients longitudinally.

9.3.3 RBD Treatment

TreatmentEvidenceNotes
Melatonin 3–12 mg at bedtimeFirst-line; restores REM atonia by unclear mechanism (possibly restores circadian REM regulation). Sn of response ~50–70%Safest option; no fall risk, cognitive impairment, or tolerance. Start 3 mg, titrate up to 12 mg. Sustained-release may be better. Take 30–60 min before bed
Clonazepam 0.25–2 mg at bedtimeEffective in ~80–90% (reduces motor behaviors); does NOT restore REM atonia but suppresses phasic activityRisks in elderly: falls, sedation, cognitive impairment, respiratory depression (avoid in OSA). Reserve for melatonin failures or severe/injurious RBD
Bed safety measuresEssential for ALL patientsPad floor around bed; remove sharp/breakable objects from bedside; sleep in sleeping bag (restricts movement); separate beds if bed partner at risk; low bed or mattress on floor

9.4 NREM Parasomnias & Other Sleep Disorders

9.4.1 NREM Parasomnias (Disorders of Arousal)

NREM parasomnias arise from incomplete arousal from deep sleep (N3/SWS), typically in the first third of the night. They share common pathophysiology: a dissociated brain state with simultaneous wake-like motor behavior and sleep-like consciousness.

ParasomniaAgeFeaturesDurationDistinguishing Points
Confusional ArousalsChildren > adultsConfused, disoriented behavior upon arousal; may sit in bed, mumble, appear awake but not responsiveMinutesNo ambulation; no autonomic activation
Sleepwalking (Somnambulism)Peak 8–12y; persists in ~4% adultsWalking/complex behavior during sleep; eyes open, blank stare; amnesia for event; can eat, cook, driveMinutes to >30 minAmbulation present; injury risk (falls, leaving house). No dream recall (unlike RBD)
Sleep TerrorsPeak 5–7ySudden arousal with intense fear, screaming, autonomic activation (tachycardia, sweating, mydriasis); inconsolable1–5 minIntense autonomic activation; amnesia; more dramatic than confusional arousals
Sleep-Related Eating Disorder (SRED)Adults > childrenRecurrent episodes of involuntary eating during partial arousals; unusual food choices; amnesticVariableAssociated with sleepwalking, zolpidem use; weight gain; injury (cooking while asleep)
NREM Parasomnia vs. RBD vs. Nocturnal Seizures: Critical Differential
FeatureNREM ParasomniaRBDNocturnal Frontal Lobe Epilepsy (NFLE/SHE)
Timing in nightFirst third (N3 predominant)Last third (REM predominant)Any time; often within 30 min of sleep onset; multiple per night
AgeChildren/young adults>50 yearsAny; onset in childhood/adolescence
EyesOpen, glassyClosed (dreaming)Open or closed
Dream recallNone or vagueVivid dream recall correlating with behaviorUsually none
StereotypedNo (variable behaviors)No (dream-dependent)Yes (stereotyped movements)
Episodes/nightUsually 1Multiple (in REM periods)Multiple, clustered
DurationMinutesSeconds to minutesSeconds to <2 min (brief)
PSGArousal from N3REM without atoniaEpileptiform discharges (may be subtle/absent on scalp EEG)
TreatmentReassurance; clonazepam; safetyMelatonin; clonazepamCarbamazepine / lacosamide

9.4.2 NREM Parasomnia Treatment

ApproachDetails
Non-pharmacological (first-line)Safety measures (locks on doors/windows, alarms, remove hazards); adequate sleep duration (sleep deprivation is a major trigger); regular sleep schedule; avoid alcohol and sedative-hypnotics at bedtime; treat comorbid OSA (apnea-induced arousals trigger parasomnias)
Scheduled awakeningsFor children: wake child 15–30 min before typical parasomnia time for 4 weeks. Effective in ~80%
Pharmacotherapy (refractory/dangerous)Clonazepam 0.25–0.5 mg at bedtime (suppresses N3 arousals). Consider low-dose SSRI (controversial). Melatonin may help
Clinical Pearl — OSA as Parasomnia Trigger: Untreated OSA is one of the most common triggers for NREM parasomnias in adults. Apneic events cause forced arousals from SWS, precipitating sleepwalking/sleep terrors. Always screen for and treat OSA in any adult presenting with new-onset parasomnias. CPAP treatment alone may completely resolve the parasomnia without need for additional pharmacotherapy.
Q: Parents bring a 6-year-old boy who has had episodes of screaming, sitting up in bed with intense fear expression, tachycardia, and sweating approximately 1–2 hours after falling asleep. Episodes last 2–3 minutes, he is inconsolable during the event, and has no memory of it the next morning. He has had 3 episodes in the past month, all in the first half of the night. What is the diagnosis, and a 62-year-old man reports similar nocturnal episodes but occurring in the early morning hours with vivid recall of being chased. How does the differential differ?
6-year-old boy: Sleep terrors (NREM parasomnia)
• Typical features: first third of night (SWS-predominant), intense autonomic activation, inconsolable, complete amnesia, peak age 5–7 years
• Management: Reassurance (benign, self-limiting in most children); safety measures; ensure adequate sleep duration; scheduled awakenings if frequent. No PSG needed if classic presentation

62-year-old man: Very different differential — most likely RBD
• Early morning (last third) = REM-predominant timing
• Vivid dream recall (being chased) = dream enactment behavior (hallmark of RBD, absent in NREM parasomnias)
• Age >50 = high risk for synucleinopathy-associated RBD
• Must also consider: nocturnal seizures (SHE) — but stereotypy and brevity would be expected; nightmare disorder — but no motor enactment on PSG
• Required workup: PSG with video and expanded EMG montage to confirm RSWA; if RBD confirmed: screen for early synucleinopathy (olfaction, DaT-SPECT, αSyn-SAA, motor exam, MoCA); melatonin 6–12 mg for treatment; annual neurological follow-up for phenoconversion

9.5 Chronic Insomnia — CBT-I & Pharmacotherapy

9.5.1 Definition & Epidemiology

Chronic insomnia disorder (ICSD-3): difficulty initiating or maintaining sleep, or early morning awakening, with associated daytime impairment, occurring ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months, despite adequate opportunity to sleep. Prevalence: ~10–15% of adults for chronic insomnia; up to 30–50% report insomnia symptoms. Women > men (~1.5×). Prevalence increases with age.

Spielman 3-P Model of Insomnia

FactorDescriptionExamples
PredisposingBaseline vulnerability (trait)Genetic predisposition, hyperarousal phenotype, anxiety-prone personality, female sex, older age
PrecipitatingAcute trigger (usually identifiable)Stressful life event, illness, pain, medication change, grief, job loss, travel
PerpetuatingMaladaptive behaviors that maintain insomnia after precipitant resolvesExcessive TIB (spending too long in bed), napping, sleep effort/anxiety, caffeine, irregular schedule, bedtime phone use

Key insight: CBT-I targets the perpetuating factors — which is why it produces durable improvement even after the original precipitant resolves.

9.5.2 Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Insomnia (CBT-I)

CBT-I: First-Line Treatment for Chronic Insomnia (ALL Guidelines)

Every major guideline (ACP 2016, AASM 2021, NICE 2021, European Sleep Research Society 2023) recommends CBT-I as first-line treatment for chronic insomnia, superior to pharmacotherapy for long-term outcomes.

CBT-I ComponentTechniqueMechanism
Sleep Restriction Therapy (SRT)Limit time in bed (TIB) to match actual sleep time (e.g., if sleeping 5h, TIB = 5.5h). Gradually increase by 15–30 min/week as sleep efficiency (SE) improves. Goal: SE ≥85%Most powerful component. Builds homeostatic sleep drive; consolidates sleep. Temporarily increases sleepiness (warn patients)
Stimulus Control(1) Bed for sleep and sex only; (2) Go to bed only when sleepy; (3) If awake >20 min, get up and return when sleepy; (4) Fixed wake time regardless of sleep; (5) No nappingRe-associates bed with sleep; breaks conditioned arousal. Addresses conditioned hyperarousal in bed
Cognitive RestructuringChallenge dysfunctional beliefs: “I must get 8 hours,” “I won’t function tomorrow,” catastrophizing about insomnia consequencesReduces sleep-related anxiety and performance effort
Sleep HygieneRegular schedule, avoid caffeine after noon, limit alcohol, cool/dark/quiet bedroom, avoid screens 1h before bed, exercise (but not late evening)Supportive; insufficient alone as monotherapy. Must be combined with SRT and stimulus control
Relaxation TrainingProgressive muscle relaxation, diaphragmatic breathing, mindfulness meditation, body scanReduces somatic and cognitive hyperarousal

Delivery formats: Individual therapist (gold standard, 4–8 sessions); group CBT-I (effective, cost-efficient); digital CBT-I (dCBT-I) — validated apps (Sleepstation, Pear Somryst [FDA-cleared], Sleepio): effect sizes similar to in-person. Accessible in settings with limited sleep psychologist availability (relevant for Thailand).

Clinical Pearl — CBT-I vs. Medications: The ACP 2016 Clinical Guideline (Qaseem et al. Ann Intern Med) concluded: CBT-I should be first-line for ALL adult patients with chronic insomnia. Medications should be considered only when CBT-I alone is insufficient. Key advantage: CBT-I effects are maintained for ≥2 years post-treatment, whereas medication benefits cease upon discontinuation and carry risks of dependence, tolerance, falls, and cognitive impairment in elderly.

9.5.3 Insomnia Pharmacotherapy

Insomnia Pharmacotherapy — Evidence-Based Guide
Drug ClassDrugDoseTargetKey Features
Orexin Receptor Antagonists (DORAs)Suvorexant (Belsomra)10–20 mgSleep onset + maintenanceBlocks orexin OX1R/OX2R → permits sleep. Preferred for chronic insomnia in elderly (no respiratory depression, no dependence, no falls). Avoid in narcolepsy. Morning grogginess at higher doses
Lemborexant (Dayvigo)5–10 mgSleep onset + maintenanceMore balanced OX1R/OX2R antagonism. SUNRISE-2 trial: effective for 12 months, well-tolerated in elderly
Z-drugs (BzRA)ZolpidemIR: 5–10 mg (5 mg women); ER: 6.25–12.5 mg; SL: 1.75–3.5 mgSleep onset (IR); maintenance (ER)GABAA α1 selective. Short-term only (≤4 weeks). Risks: complex sleep behaviors (sleep-driving, sleep-eating — FDA black box 2019), tolerance, dependence, falls, next-morning impairment (especially ER). Women metabolize slower → lower dose
Eszopiclone (Lunesta)1–3 mg (1 mg in elderly)Sleep onset + maintenanceLongest-studied BzRA; efficacy maintained at 6 months in clinical trials. Metallic taste. Less risk of complex sleep behaviors than zolpidem
Melatonin Receptor AgonistRamelteon (Rozerem)8 mgSleep onset (circadian)MT1/MT2 agonist. No abuse potential (non-scheduled). Modest efficacy. Best for circadian-related insomnia. Safe in elderly. Avoid with fluvoxamine (CYP1A2)
Low-dose AntidepressantDoxepin (Silenor)3–6 mgSleep maintenanceAt ultra-low dose: selective H1 antagonist (no anticholinergic effects at this dose). FDA-approved for sleep maintenance insomnia. Excellent for elderly patients with early morning awakening. No tolerance in trials up to 3 months
Off-labelTrazodone25–100 mgSleep onset + maintenance5-HT2A antagonist + weak H1. Most prescribed off-label insomnia drug worldwide. Limited RCT evidence for insomnia specifically. Risks: orthostatic hypotension, priapism (rare). Commonly used in dementia patients for insomnia
AntihistamineDiphenhydramine / Doxylamine25–50 mgSleep onsetOTC. NOT recommended for chronic insomnia: rapid tolerance (3–5 days), anticholinergic burden in elderly (Beers list), cognitive impairment, urinary retention
Clinical Pearl — Benzodiazepines for Insomnia: Long-acting benzodiazepines (diazepam, clonazepam, flurazepam) should be AVOIDED for chronic insomnia: prolonged sedation, accumulation in elderly, falls, cognitive impairment, tolerance, dependence. Even short-acting BZDs (temazepam, triazolam) should be reserved for short-term use only. The Beers Criteria (AGS 2023) lists all benzodiazepines and Z-drugs as potentially inappropriate in adults ≥65 years for insomnia treatment.

9.5.4 Circadian Rhythm Sleep-Wake Disorders (CRSWDs)

Circadian Rhythm Disorders
DisorderFeaturesTreatment
Delayed Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder (DSWPD)Sleep onset typically 2–6 AM, wake noon–2 PM. Common in adolescents/young adults (~7–16%). Normal sleep quality/duration when on preferred schedule (weekends). Genetic: PER3, CRY1 variantsMorning bright light therapy (10,000 lux, 30 min upon waking) + low-dose melatonin (0.5–3 mg) 4–6 hours before desired bedtime + chronotherapy (progressive advancement or delay). Strategic light avoidance in evening (blue-light filters)
Advanced Sleep-Wake Phase Disorder (ASWPD)Sleep onset 6–9 PM, wake 2–5 AM. Common in elderly. Familial: CK1δ, PER2 mutations (FASPS)Evening bright light therapy (2–3 hours before habitual sleep onset). Avoid morning bright light. Chronotherapy less effective than in DSWPD
Shift Work DisorderInsomnia and/or EDS temporally associated with recurring work schedule that overlaps habitual sleep period. Affects ~10–40% of shift workersStrategic napping before/during shift; bright light during shift; melatonin before daytime sleep; modafinil/armodafinil for shift-time sleepiness (FDA-approved). Minimize consecutive night shifts. Fixed schedule better than rotating
Jet Lag DisorderInsomnia/EDS after ≥2 time zone crossing. Eastward travel worse (requires phase advance). ~1 day recovery per time zone crossedPre-trip light/melatonin timing. Melatonin 0.5–5 mg at destination bedtime. Light exposure per direction: eastward = morning light, avoid evening; westward = evening light, avoid morning
Non-24-Hour Sleep-Wake Disorder (Non-24)Free-running circadian rhythm (>24h period). Most common in totally blind individuals (50–75%) due to absent photic entrainment. Rare in sightedTasimelteon (Hetlioz): MT1/MT2 agonist; FDA-approved for Non-24 in blind. Melatonin 0.5–3 mg at fixed time
Irregular Sleep-Wake Rhythm DisorderFragmented sleep with no clear circadian pattern; multiple naps throughout 24h. Associated with dementia (AD, DLB), brain injury, developmental disordersStructured light exposure, melatonin, activity scheduling, treat underlying cause. Frequently encountered in your dementia patients (Section 8)
Clinical Pearl — Melatonin Timing: The timing of melatonin is more important than the dose. For DSWPD: give melatonin 4–6 hours before desired sleep onset (not at bedtime — too late for phase-advancing effect). Example: if desired sleep = midnight, give melatonin at 6–8 PM. For jet lag (eastward): melatonin at destination bedtime starting 1–2 days before travel. Low doses (0.5–1 mg) are as effective as high doses for circadian phase shifting, with fewer side effects.

9.6 Restless Legs Syndrome (RLS) & Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD)

9.6.1 Restless Legs Syndrome / Willis-Ekbom Disease

RLS is a sensorimotor disorder characterized by an irresistible urge to move the legs, usually accompanied by uncomfortable sensations, worsening at rest and in the evening/night, and relieved by movement. Prevalence: ~5–15% in Western populations; ~1–3% in Asia (including Thailand).

IRLSSG Diagnostic Criteria (2014 Revised)

ALL 5 criteria required:

#CriterionKey Points
1Urge to move the legs, usually accompanied by uncomfortable/unpleasant sensationsDescribed as: creeping, crawling, pulling, itching, tingling, aching, “electricity,” “worms crawling.” May affect arms
2Urge begins or worsens during rest or inactivitySitting, lying down. Worse in sedentary situations (meetings, flights, cinema)
3Urge is partially or totally relieved by movementWalking, stretching, rubbing. Relief persists as long as movement continues
4Urge occurs exclusively or predominantly in the evening or nightCircadian variation (dopaminergic nadir in evening). Critical for distinguishing from positional discomfort, neuropathy
5Not solely accounted for by another medical/behavioral conditionMust exclude: leg cramps, positional discomfort, peripheral neuropathy, venous stasis, habitual foot tapping, akathisia (neuroleptic-induced)

Supportive features: Family history (>50% in primary RLS); positive response to dopaminergic agents; PLMS on PSG (>80% of RLS patients).

Pathophysiology

MechanismEvidence
Brain iron deficiencyMRI shows ↓iron in substantia nigra even with normal serum ferritin; CSF ferritin ↓. Iron is cofactor for tyrosine hydroxylase (rate-limiting step in DA synthesis). Serum ferritin <75 μg/L = iron repletion indicated regardless of Hb
Dopaminergic dysfunctionA3 hypothalamic dopaminergic neurons (diencephalospinal pathway); circadian dopamine variation correlates with symptom timing; DA agonists relieve symptoms but long-term use causes augmentation
GeneticsBTBD9, MEIS1, MAP2K5 (GWAS loci); heritability ~60% in primary RLS. MEIS1 linked to iron regulation
Glutamate hyperexcitabilityThalamic glutamate ↑ in RLS (MRS studies); may explain why α2δ ligands (gabapentin, pregabalin) are effective

Secondary Causes of RLS

CauseMechanismManagement
Iron deficiencyMost important secondary cause; ↓brain iron → ↓DA synthesisOral iron (ferrous sulfate 325 mg + vitamin C on empty stomach) if ferritin <75. IV ferric carboxymaltose 500–1000 mg if oral fails or ferritin <30
End-stage renal diseasePresent in ~25–50% of dialysis patients; iron deficiency + uremic toxinsIron repletion; DA agonists; gabapentin (adjust dose); kidney transplantation may cure
Pregnancy~25% prevalence; iron/folate depletion; hormonalIron supplementation; resolves post-partum in most
Peripheral neuropathySensory nerve dysfunction mimics/triggers RLSTreat underlying neuropathy; α2δ ligands dual benefit
Medications that worsen RLSDopamine antagonists, antihistamines, SSRIs/SNRIs, lithiumSubstitute: bupropion (least likely antidepressant to worsen RLS); avoid metoclopramide (use domperidone)

9.6.2 RLS Treatment Algorithm

RLS Pharmacotherapy (IRLSSG/AASM 2024 Updated)
LineDrugDoseKey Notes
Step 0Iron repletionTarget ferritin >75 μg/L (some experts target >100)ALWAYS check and correct iron first, regardless of other therapy. May be sufficient monotherapy in mild-moderate RLS with low ferritin
First-lineGabapentin300–900 mg, 2h before bedtimePreferred first-line (IRLSSG 2024): no augmentation risk, helps sleep, helps pain/neuropathy. Dose adjust in renal impairment. Dizziness, sedation
Pregabalin75–300 mg, 2h before bedtimeSimilar efficacy to gabapentin; more predictable absorption. Allen et al. NEJM 2014: pregabalin superior to pramipexole at 52 weeks (no augmentation). Schedule V in US
Second-linePramipexole0.125–0.5 mg, 2h before bedtimeDA agonist; highly effective short-term. AUGMENTATION is the major limitation (see below). Use lowest effective dose for shortest time
Rotigotine (patch)1–3 mg/24h patchTransdermal DA agonist; less augmentation than oral DA agonists (~8% vs 30–70% at 10y). Skin irritation. Preferred DA agonist if one must be used
Third-line / RefractoryLow-dose opioidsOxycodone/naloxone PR 5/2.5–40/20 mg; tramadol 50–100 mg; methadone 5–15 mgFor severe refractory RLS; RELOXYN trial (Trenkwalder et al. Lancet Neurol 2013): oxycodone/naloxone effective. Requires careful monitoring (opioid risks). Methadone for the most refractory cases (specialist only)

Augmentation: The Critical Complication

Augmentation of RLS by Dopaminergic Agents

Definition (Max Planck Institute criteria): Paradoxical worsening of RLS symptoms during long-term dopaminergic therapy:

  • Symptoms start earlier in the day (≥4h earlier than pre-treatment)
  • Symptoms spread to previously unaffected body parts (arms, trunk)
  • Symptom intensity increases despite stable or increased dose
  • Shorter latency to symptom onset at rest
  • Paradoxical brief relief with dose increase (then worsens — chasing)

Prevalence: 30–70% of patients on pramipexole at 10 years; 8% on rotigotine patch; 0% on α2δ ligands.

Management of augmentation:

  1. Check and correct iron (ferritin >75 μg/L)
  2. Gradually taper and discontinue dopamine agonist (do NOT stop abruptly — DA agonist withdrawal syndrome: anxiety, panic, nausea, pain, depression, suicidality)
  3. Switch to α2δ ligand (gabapentin/pregabalin) as new maintenance
  4. Bridge with low-dose opioid during transition if severe (tramadol 50 mg or oxycodone 5 mg at bedtime for 2–4 weeks)
  5. If refractory after switch: combination therapy (α2δ + low-dose DA agonist at minimum dose) or long-term opioid

9.6.3 Periodic Limb Movement Disorder (PLMD)

PLMS (Periodic Limb Movements of Sleep): repetitive, stereotyped limb movements (typically triple flexion of leg: dorsiflexion of ankle + flexion of knee + flexion of hip) occurring in NREM sleep, every 20–40 seconds. PLMS index >15/h on PSG is abnormal.

PLMD is diagnosed only when PLMS causes clinically significant sleep disturbance or daytime symptoms AND no other cause (RLS, narcolepsy, RBD, OSA, medication effect). Isolated PLMS without symptoms is a PSG finding, NOT a disorder requiring treatment.

Treatment (if PLMD confirmed): Same as RLS (α2δ ligands preferred; DA agonists with augmentation risk).

9.7 Sleep Disorders in Neurological Disease

9.7.1 Sleep and Neurodegeneration

Bidirectional relationship: sleep disorders are both consequences and accelerators of neurodegeneration.

Neurological DiseaseSleep Disorder(s)Clinical Significance
Parkinson’s DiseaseRBD (40–50%), insomnia (60%), EDS (50%), OSA, RLS (15–20%), nocturiaRBD may precede PD by 10+ years; EDS from disease + medications (DA agonists); dopaminergic drugs cause sleep attacks
Alzheimer’s DiseaseSleep fragmentation, circadian disruption (sundowning), OSA (50–75%), irregular sleep-wake rhythm disorderOSA ↑amyloid deposition via impaired glymphatic clearance; sleep disruption worsens cognitive decline; melatonin may help circadian disruption but minimal cognitive benefit
StrokeOSA (50–75%), central sleep apnea (5–10%), insomnia (30–60%), circadian disruption, post-stroke hypersomniaOSA worsens stroke outcomes and recurrence; screen ALL stroke patients; CPAP improves recovery. Post-stroke insomnia associated with worse functional outcome and depression
EpilepsyInsomnia, OSA, NREM parasomnias (differential for nocturnal seizures), medication effectsSleep deprivation is a potent seizure trigger; OSA lowers seizure threshold; some ASMs worsen sleepiness (phenobarbital, carbamazepine, valproate); some improve sleep (gabapentin, pregabalin)
Multiple SclerosisInsomnia (40%), OSA, RLS (20%), fatigue (MS fatigue ≠ sleepiness), central hypersomnia (hypothalamic plaques)Fatigue is #1 complaint in MS; distinguish from sleepiness (ESS) and depression. Sleep-disordered breathing undertreated. Modafinil for MS-related fatigue (off-label)
Neuromuscular Disease (ALS, DMD, myasthenia gravis)Sleep-related hypoventilation, OSA/central apnea, insomniaFVC <50% predicted or supine ↓>20% → nocturnal NIV (BiPAP). In ALS: BiPAP prolongs survival by ~7–13 months. Monitor respiratory function regularly
Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI)Insomnia (30–70%), hypersomnia, circadian disruption, sleep apnea, narcolepsy (rare; hypothalamic injury)Post-TBI sleep disorders are common and underdiagnosed; contribute to poor rehabilitation outcomes. Screen and treat actively

9.7.2 Sleep-Related Epilepsy (SHE / NFLE)

Sleep-Related Hypermotor Epilepsy (SHE), formerly Nocturnal Frontal Lobe Epilepsy (NFLE), is the most important epilepsy differential for parasomnias:

FeatureDetails
SemiologySudden, brief (<2 min) hypermotor events: bicycling, rocking, thrashing, vocalizations. Highly stereotyped (same pattern every episode). Often multiple per night, clustered
EEGInterictal: often normal or frontal sharp waves. Ictal: may be obscured by movement artifact; deep frontal foci missed by scalp EEG. Normal EEG does NOT exclude SHE. Video-EEG with extended montage, or stereo-EEG in surgical cases
GeneticsAutosomal dominant SHE (ADSHE): CHRNA4, CHRNB2, CHRNA2 (nicotinic ACh receptor subunits); DEPDC5, NPRL2/3 (mTOR pathway)
TreatmentCarbamazepine low-dose (200–400 mg) — first-line, excellent response (~70%). Alternatives: oxcarbazepine, lacosamide, perampanel
Clinical Pearl — When to Suspect SHE over Parasomnia: Key red flags for SHE: (1) Events are stereotyped (identical every time — NREM parasomnias are variable); (2) Multiple events per night (>3 episodes in one night is unusual for parasomnias); (3) Brief (<2 min each); (4) Onset at any age (parasomnias usually childhood); (5) Family history of epilepsy; (6) No improvement with parasomnia treatment. When in doubt: prolonged video-EEG monitoring with EMG and expanded frontal electrodes. A trial of low-dose carbamazepine can be both diagnostic and therapeutic.

9.8 References & Key Guidelines

Key References & Guidelines

Diagnostic Criteria & Guidelines

  • American Academy of Sleep Medicine. International Classification of Sleep Disorders, 3rd edition (ICSD-3). 2014; Text Revision 2023.
  • Qaseem A et al. Management of chronic insomnia disorder in adults: a clinical practice guideline from the American College of Physicians. Ann Intern Med 2016;165(2):125–133. [ACP Insomnia Guideline — CBT-I as first-line]
  • Sateia MJ et al. Clinical practice guideline for the pharmacologic treatment of chronic insomnia in adults: AASM clinical practice guideline. J Clin Sleep Med 2017;13(2):307–349.
  • Allen RP et al. Restless legs syndrome/Willis-Ekbom disease diagnostic criteria: updated IRLSSG consensus criteria. Sleep Med 2014;15(8):860–873.
  • Berry RB et al. AASM Scoring Manual for the Scoring of Sleep and Associated Events. Version 3.0, 2023 (updated 2024).

Landmark Trials & Key Studies

  • Strollo PJ et al. Upper-airway stimulation for obstructive sleep apnea. N Engl J Med 2014;370:139–149. [STAR Trial — Inspire device]
  • McEvoy RD et al. CPAP for prevention of cardiovascular events in obstructive sleep apnea. N Engl J Med 2016;375:919–931. [SAVE Trial]
  • Malhotra A et al. Tirzepatide for the treatment of obstructive sleep apnea and obesity. N Engl J Med 2024;391:1193–1205. [SURMOUNT-OSA]
  • Allen RP et al. Comparison of pregabalin with pramipexole for restless legs syndrome. N Engl J Med 2014;370:621–631. [Pregabalin superiority at 52 weeks]
  • Trenkwalder C et al. Prolonged release oxycodone-naloxone for treatment of severe restless legs syndrome. Lancet Neurol 2013;12(12):1141–1150. [RELOXYN]
  • Postuma RB et al. Risk and predictors of dementia and parkinsonism in idiopathic REM sleep behaviour disorder: a multicentre study. Brain 2019;142(3):744–759. [NAPS cohort]
  • Iranzo A et al. Neurodegenerative disease status and post-mortem pathology in idiopathic rapid-eye-movement sleep behaviour disorder. Lancet Neurol 2013;12(5):443–453.
  • Wellman A et al. A method for measuring and modeling the physiological traits causing obstructive sleep apnea. J Appl Physiol 2011;110(6):1627–1637. [PALM endotype framework]

Thai Context

  • Lohsoonthorn V et al. Prevalence of sleep problems in the Thai population. Sleep (Bangkok Metropolitan Sleep Study).
  • Thai Society of Sleep Medicine — Practice Guidelines for OSA Diagnosis and Treatment (2022 update).
  • Drug availability: Modafinil (special authorization); methylphenidate (narcotics license); gabapentin/pregabalin (NLEM); clonazepam (NLEM); melatonin (available OTC in Thailand). Sodium oxybate, pitolisant, solriamfetol, suvorexant, lemborexant, tasimelteon: NOT available in Thailand as of 2025.

10. Neuro-Oncology

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of neuro-oncology. Coverage includes the WHO 2021 integrated histomolecular classification of CNS tumors (IDH, 1p/19q, ATRX, TERT, EGFR, MGMT, H3 K27M/G34, BRAF), adult diffuse gliomas (astrocytoma, oligodendroglioma, GBM), pediatric gliomas (DIPG, pilocytic astrocytoma), GBM treatment (Stupp protocol, TTFields EF-14, MGMT-guided elderly management, recurrent GBM), targeted therapies (vorasidenib INDIGO, dabrafenib+trametinib, ONC201, checkpoint inhibitors, CAR-T, tumor vaccines), PCNSL (HD-MTX, MATRix, ASCT), meningioma (WHO 2021 molecular grading, Simpson), schwannoma, medulloblastoma, pituitary tumors, brain metastases (SRS, WBRT, driver mutation-targeted systemic therapy, leptomeningeal disease), paraneoplastic syndromes (PNS-Care 2021 criteria, antibody-syndrome-cancer associations), and neurological complications of cancer therapy (radiation necrosis, CIPN, immune checkpoint irAE, ICANS, PRES).

10.1 WHO 2021 CNS Tumor Classification — Integrated Histomolecular Approach

10.1.1 Paradigm Shift: From Histology to Molecular

The WHO 2021 Classification of CNS Tumors (5th edition) represents a fundamental paradigm shift: tumors are now classified using integrated histomolecular diagnosis rather than histology alone. Molecular markers can override histological grading (e.g., an IDH-wildtype diffuse astrocytoma with TERT promoter mutation is now classified as GBM, Grade 4, regardless of histological appearance).

Key Molecular Markers in WHO 2021
MarkerMethodSignificance
IDH1/2 mutationIHC (IDH1 R132H — covers ~90%); sequencing for non-canonicalMost important prognostic marker. IDH-mutant = better prognosis. Defines astrocytoma vs GBM (IDH-wt). All diffuse gliomas require IDH testing
1p/19q codeletionFISH, CGH array, or methylation profilingDefines oligodendroglioma (requires IDH-mut + 1p/19q codel). Predicts chemosensitivity (PCV). Best prognosis among diffuse gliomas
ATRX lossIHC (loss of nuclear expression)Characteristic of astrocytoma, IDH-mutant. Mutually exclusive with 1p/19q codeletion. Associated with ALT (alternative lengthening of telomeres)
TERT promoter mutationPCR/sequencing (C228T, C250T)Present in oligodendroglioma (with IDH-mut) and GBM (IDH-wt). In IDH-wt diffuse glioma: upgrades to GBM Grade 4
EGFR amplificationFISH, CGH, methylationIn IDH-wt diffuse glioma: upgrades to GBM Grade 4. EGFRvIII variant targetable
+7/−10CGH, methylation profilingWhole chromosome 7 gain + chromosome 10 loss. In IDH-wt: upgrades to GBM Grade 4
CDKN2A/B homozygous deletionFISH, CGHIn IDH-mutant astrocytoma: upgrades to Grade 4 (regardless of histology). Worst prognosis among IDH-mutant tumors
MGMT promoter methylationMSP, pyrosequencingPredictive for temozolomide benefit in GBM. Methylated (~35–45%): better TMZ response, median OS 21.7 mo. Unmethylated: TMZ benefit uncertain
H3 K27MIHC, sequencingDefines diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered (thalamus, brainstem, spinal cord). Grade 4. Children & young adults. Poor prognosis
H3 G34R/VSequencingDefines diffuse hemispheric glioma, H3 G34-mutant. Grade 4. Hemispheric. Adolescents/young adults
BRAF V600EIHC, PCRPleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma, ganglioglioma, pilocytic astrocytoma (rare). Targetable with dabrafenib + trametinib
KIAA1549-BRAF fusionFISH, RNA sequencingPathognomonic for pilocytic astrocytoma (WHO Grade 1). Posterior fossa in children. Excellent prognosis

10.1.2 Adult-Type Diffuse Gliomas (WHO 2021)

WHO 2021 Classification of Adult-Type Diffuse Gliomas
Tumor TypeWHO GradeMolecular SignatureMedian SurvivalKey Features
Astrocytoma, IDH-mutant2, 3, or 4IDH1/2 mutation + ATRX loss + NO 1p/19q codeletion. Grade 4 if CDKN2A/B homozygous deletionGrade 2: 10–15 yr
Grade 3: 5–8 yr
Grade 4: 3–5 yr		Frontal/temporal lobes. T2/FLAIR hyperintense, non-enhancing (low grade) or enhancing (high grade). Can transform to higher grade over time
Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant & 1p/19q-codeleted2 or 3IDH1/2 mutation + 1p/19q codeletion + TERT promoter mutation (triple-positive)Grade 2: 12–20 yr
Grade 3: 7–12 yr		Best prognosis among diffuse gliomas. Cortical location, calcifications common. Exquisitely chemosensitive (PCV)
Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype4 (always)IDH-wildtype + ≥1 of: EGFR amp, +7/−10, TERT promoter mutation. MGMT methylation = predictive15–18 mo (MGMT+)
12–14 mo (MGMT−)		Most common malignant primary brain tumor. Ring-enhancing with central necrosis. Butterfly lesion (crosses corpus callosum). Stupp protocol standard
Clinical Pearl — The “Not Otherwise Specified” (NOS) vs. “Not Elsewhere Classified” (NEC) System: WHO 2021 introduces NOS (testing not performed/unavailable) and NEC (testing performed but results don’t match any defined entity). If IDH testing is unavailable: “Astrocytoma, NOS”. If tested but results are conflicting: “Astrocytoma, NEC”. This system ensures transparency about diagnostic certainty and is particularly relevant in resource-limited settings (e.g., Thailand where methylation profiling may not be readily available).

10.1.3 Pediatric-Type Diffuse Gliomas

TumorGradeMolecularKey Features
Diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered4H3K27M mutation (H3.3 or H3.1) OR EZHIP overexpressionThalamus, brainstem (DIPG), spinal cord. Children/young adults. Not resectable (midline location). RT is mainstay. Median survival ~11 months. ONC201 (HDAC/DRD2 agonist) showing promise
Diffuse hemispheric glioma, H3 G34-mutant4H3 G34R/V mutationCerebral hemispheres, adolescents/young adults. Aggressive. MGMT often methylated → TMZ may help
Diffuse pediatric-type high-grade glioma, PDGFRA-mutant4PDGFRA mutation/amplificationNew WHO 2021 entity. Targetable with PDGFRA inhibitors (avapritinib, trial)

10.1.4 Circumscribed Astrocytic Gliomas

TumorGradeMolecularKey Features
Pilocytic astrocytoma1KIAA1549-BRAF fusion (~70%); BRAF V600E (~10%)Most common pediatric brain tumor. Posterior fossa (cerebellum). Cystic with enhancing mural nodule. Excellent prognosis (>95% 10-yr survival). GTR curative in most
Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma (PXA)2 (3 if ≥5 mitoses)BRAF V600E (~70%)Temporal lobe, cortical-based, young adults. Seizures. BRAF V600E targetable (dabrafenib + trametinib)
Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma (SEGA)1TSC1/TSC2 (tuberous sclerosis)Near foramen of Monro; can cause hydrocephalus. Everolimus (mTOR inhibitor) shrinks SEGA without surgery (EXIST-1 trial)
Q: A 38-year-old man presents with new-onset seizures. MRI shows a non-enhancing T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion in the left frontal lobe. Biopsy reveals a diffuse glioma. IHC shows IDH1 R132H positive, ATRX retained, and FISH confirms 1p/19q codeletion. TERT promoter mutation is detected. What is the integrated diagnosis, WHO grade, and prognosis?
Integrated diagnosis: Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted, WHO Grade 2 (assuming no anaplastic features on histology; Grade 3 if increased mitotic activity, microvascular proliferation, or necrosis).
Molecular profile: Triple-positive (IDH mutation + 1p/19q codeletion + TERT promoter mutation) = pathognomonic for oligodendroglioma in WHO 2021. ATRX is typically retained (loss would favor astrocytoma, but 1p/19q codeletion overrides).
Prognosis: Best among diffuse gliomas. Grade 2: median survival 12–20 years. Grade 3: 7–12 years. Exquisitely chemosensitive.
Treatment:
• Maximal safe resection (GTR if feasible without eloquent cortex damage; awake craniotomy with cortical mapping if near language/motor areas)
• If low-risk Grade 2 (age <40, GTR achieved): observation with serial MRI (q3–6 months year 1, then q6–12 months). Consider vorasidenib (IDH inhibitor)
• If high-risk Grade 2 (age ≥40, subtotal resection, >6 cm, crosses midline) or Grade 3: RT (54 Gy/30 fr) + PCV chemotherapy (RTOG 9802: RT + PCV vs RT alone: median OS 13.3 vs 7.8 years in high-risk low-grade glioma)

10.2 GBM Treatment — Standard of Care & Novel Therapies

10.2.1 Glioblastoma Standard of Care: The Stupp Protocol

Stupp Protocol (Stupp et al. NEJM 2005; Lancet Oncol 2009)
PhaseTreatmentDetails
SurgeryMaximal safe resectionGoal: ≥98% extent of resection (EOR). 5-ALA fluorescence-guided surgery improves GTR rates (from ~35% to ~65%). Awake craniotomy for eloquent areas. Biopsy if unresectable
Concurrent chemoRTRT 60 Gy / 30 fractions + TMZ 75 mg/m2/dayRT over 6 weeks. TMZ daily including weekends. PCP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) during concurrent phase (lymphopenia risk). Weekly CBC
Adjuvant TMZTMZ 150–200 mg/m2 days 1–5 / 28-day cycle6 cycles (some extend to 12 if tolerated). Start cycle 1 at 150 mg/m2; escalate to 200 if tolerated. Monitor CBC
TTFields (Optune)Alternating electric fields 200 kHz via scalp arraysEF-14 trial (Stupp et al. JAMA 2015; JAMA 2017): TTFields + maintenance TMZ vs TMZ alone: median OS 20.9 vs 16.0 months; 5-year OS 13% vs 5%. Must be worn ≥18 h/day for benefit. Scalp irritation is main side effect. No systemic toxicity

MGMT Methylation: The Critical Biomarker

MGMT StatusSignificanceTreatment Implications
Methylated (~35–45%)MGMT gene silenced → reduced DNA repair → TMZ more effectiveFull Stupp protocol. Median OS 21.7 months (vs 12.7 unmethylated). Strongest predictor of TMZ benefit. Consider MGMT-enriched clinical trials
Unmethylated (~55–65%)MGMT active → repairs TMZ-induced DNA damage → TMZ less effectiveStill receives Stupp (modest benefit even in unmethylated). Consider clinical trials as first-line. RT alone reasonable in elderly with poor KPS. Lomustine (CCNU) alternative

Elderly & Frail GBM Patients

Age/KPSMGMT StatusRecommended TreatmentEvidence
≥70 yr, KPS ≥60MethylatedHypofractionated RT (40 Gy/15 fr) + TMZPerry et al. NEJM 2017 (CE.6/EORTC 26062): short-course RT + TMZ vs RT alone in elderly: OS 9.3 vs 7.6 mo
≥70 yr, KPS ≥60UnmethylatedHypofractionated RT (40 Gy/15 fr) aloneTMZ adds toxicity without clear benefit if MGMT unmethylated in elderly
Any age, KPS <60MethylatedTMZ alone (if MGMT+)NOA-08 (Wick et al. Lancet Oncol 2012): TMZ non-inferior to RT in elderly MGMT+ GBM
Any age, KPS <60UnmethylatedRT alone (25 Gy/5 fr ultra-short) or best supportive carePalliative approach; ultra-short RT for symptom palliation

10.2.2 Recurrent GBM

TreatmentDetailsEvidence
BevacizumabAnti-VEGF monoclonal antibody; 10 mg/kg q2wFDA-approved for recurrent GBM (2009). Improves PFS (4.2 vs 1.5 mo) but NOT overall survival. Reduces steroid requirements, improves quality of life. Main toxicities: hypertension, proteinuria, wound healing, hemorrhage, VTE. AVAglio (Chinot et al. NEJM 2014) and RTOG 0825: no OS benefit as first-line addition to Stupp
Lomustine (CCNU)Alkylating agent; 110–130 mg/m2 q6wStandard comparator in recurrent GBM trials. May combine with bevacizumab. CeTeG/NOA-09 (Herrlinger et al. Lancet 2019): CCNU + TMZ vs TMZ alone in MGMT+ GBM: OS 48.1 vs 31.4 mo (provocative but small study)
Re-resectionIf recurrence is resectable and KPS goodRetrospective data supports survival benefit if ≥80% EOR at second surgery. Consider LITT (laser interstitial thermal therapy) for deep lesions
Re-irradiationSRS or hypofractionated re-RT for focal recurrenceCase-by-case; cumulative brain dose constraints. SRS 15–18 Gy for small recurrences

10.2.3 Targeted & Novel Therapies in Gliomas

Emerging Targeted Therapies in Neuro-Oncology
TherapyTargetTumor TypeEvidence
Vorasidenib (Voranigo)IDH1/2 dual inhibitor (brain-penetrant)IDH-mutant Grade 2 glioma (astrocytoma or oligodendroglioma)INDIGO trial (Mellinghoff et al. NEJM 2023): vorasidenib vs placebo in residual/recurrent Grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma: PFS 27.7 vs 11.1 months (HR 0.39); 2-year PFS 63% vs 29%. FDA approved Aug 2024. First targeted therapy for low-grade glioma. Well-tolerated (ALT elevation main AE, monitor LFTs)
Dabrafenib + TrametinibBRAF V600E + MEKBRAF V600E-mutant glioma (PXA, ganglioglioma, pilocytic, rare GBM)ROAR basket trial (Wen et al. Lancet Oncol 2022): ORR 33% in high-grade glioma, 69% in low-grade. FDA approved for BRAF V600E solid tumors including glioma
ONC201 (dordaviprone)DRD2 agonist / ClpP activatorH3 K27M-altered diffuse midline glioma (DIPG)Breakthrough therapy designation. Showed radiographic responses and survival benefit in H3K27M gliomas. Oral, well-tolerated. Phase 3 ongoing
Checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1/PD-L1)PD-1/PD-L1GBMCheckMate 143 (Reardon et al. JAMA Oncol 2020): nivolumab vs bevacizumab in recurrent GBM: NO OS benefit. GBM is immunologically “cold” (low TMB, immunosuppressive microenvironment). Hypermutated (MMR-deficient) GBM may respond. Neoadjuvant anti-PD-1 before resection showing promise (Cloughesy et al. Nat Med 2019)
CAR-T cell therapyEGFRvIII, IL-13Rα2, GD2, HER2Recurrent GBM, DIPGPhase 1/2 trials. GD2-CAR-T for H3K27M DIPG: dramatic radiographic responses (Majzner et al. Nature 2022). CRS monitoring required. Intraventricular/intratumoral delivery being explored
Tumor vaccinesDCVax-L (dendritic cell), EGFRvIII peptide, survivinGBMDCVax-L (Liau et al. JAMA Oncol 2023): autologous dendritic cell vaccine + Stupp: median OS 19.3 mo (entire cohort), 30.2 mo in MGMT-methylated. Phase 3 completed but mixed interpretation
Clinical Pearl — Pseudoprogression vs. True Progression: After chemoRT, up to 20–30% of GBM patients show transient increase in enhancement on MRI within the first 3 months (pseudoprogression — actually treatment effect from TMZ-induced DNA damage + inflammation). More common in MGMT-methylated tumors. RANO criteria (2010): do not declare progression within 12 weeks of completing RT unless new lesion outside radiation field or pathological confirmation. Advanced MRI techniques to differentiate: MR perfusion (rCBV ↓ in pseudoprogression, ↑ in true progression), MR spectroscopy (choline/NAA ratio), amino acid PET (18F-FET PET superior to MRI for distinguishing).
Q: A 62-year-old man undergoes subtotal resection of a right temporal GBM (IDH-wildtype, MGMT promoter methylated, EGFR amplified). KPS is 80. How should he be treated, and what is his expected prognosis?
Treatment: Full Stupp protocol + TTFields:
1. Concurrent phase: RT 60 Gy/30 fractions + daily TMZ 75 mg/m2 × 6 weeks. PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX. Weekly CBC
2. Adjuvant phase: TMZ 150–200 mg/m2 days 1–5 q28 days × 6–12 cycles + TTFields (Optune) ≥18 h/day
3. Supportive: Dexamethasone for edema (taper as tolerated); levetiracetam if seizures; VTE prophylaxis
Prognosis: MGMT methylated = favorable subgroup. Expected median OS ~21–23 months with full Stupp + TTFields. EGFR amplification confirms GBM Grade 4 molecular diagnosis but doesn’t currently change standard treatment (no effective anti-EGFR therapy for GBM yet, though EGFRvIII-targeted approaches in trials).
At recurrence: Consider bevacizumab + lomustine, re-resection if feasible, re-irradiation (SRS), or clinical trial enrollment.

10.3 Primary CNS Lymphoma (PCNSL)

10.3.1 Overview

PCNSL is a rare aggressive non-Hodgkin lymphoma confined to the CNS (brain, leptomeninges, eyes, spinal cord) without systemic involvement at diagnosis. Histology: >95% Diffuse Large B-Cell Lymphoma (DLBCL). Incidence: ~0.5/100,000 (increasing in immunocompetent elderly). Two distinct populations: (1) immunocompromised (HIV/AIDS, post-transplant — decreasing with ART) and (2) immunocompetent elderly (>60y — increasing).

10.3.2 Clinical Features & Diagnosis

FeatureDetails
PresentationFocal neurological deficits (70%), cognitive/behavioral change (43%), raised ICP (33%), seizures (14%), ocular symptoms (10–20% — vitreous/retinal infiltration)
MRI patternImmunocompetent: homogeneously enhancing, periventricular/callosal, often multifocal (40%). Immunocompromised: ring-enhancing (mimics toxoplasmosis). Restricted diffusion (high cellularity). Low rCBV on perfusion (unlike GBM which has high rCBV)
Key diagnostic ruleDEFER STEROIDS until biopsy — corticosteroids cause rapid tumor lysis (“ghost tumor”) making histological diagnosis impossible. If steroids given and tumor shrinks, must wait for regrowth before biopsy
WorkupMRI brain + spine with Gd; stereotactic biopsy (NOT resection); slit-lamp examination (ocular involvement); CSF: cytology, flow cytometry, IL-10 ↑ / IL-6 ↓ (IL-10:IL-6 ratio >1); CT chest/abdomen/pelvis + PET to exclude systemic lymphoma; HIV testing; testicular US in elderly men
Clinical Pearl — PCNSL vs. GBM on MRI: Both can be periventricular and enhance. Key differentiators: (1) PCNSL: homogeneous enhancement (immunocompetent), restricted diffusion (DWI bright/ADC dark = high cellularity), low perfusion (rCBV ratio ~1–2). (2) GBM: ring enhancement with central necrosis, high perfusion (rCBV ratio >2–4), spectroscopy: high choline, lipid peak. (3) Response to steroids: PCNSL dramatically shrinks; GBM minimally responds.

10.3.3 Treatment

PCNSL Treatment Algorithm
PhaseTreatmentDetails
InductionHigh-dose methotrexate (HD-MTX)-basedHD-MTX 3.5 g/m2 (minimum ≥3 g/m2) q2w × 4–8 cycles. Leucovorin rescue 24h after MTX. Aggressive hydration + urine alkalinization (pH >7). Monitor creatinine (MTX nephrotoxicity). MATRix regimen: MTX + cytarabine + thiotepa + rituximab (Ferreri et al. Lancet Haematol 2016) — highest CR rate (~49%)
Consolidation (fit patients)HD-ASCT (thiotepa-based)Thiotepa + BCNU conditioning → autologous stem cell transplant. Best long-term outcomes: 5-year PFS ~75%. IELSG32 (Ferreri et al. Lancet Haematol 2017): ASCT superior to WBRT consolidation
Consolidation (unfit/elderly)Reduced-dose WBRT (23.4 Gy) or maintenance lenalidomide/ibrutinibWBRT causes severe neurotoxicity (leukoencephalopathy, cognitive decline) especially in elderly — avoid full-dose (45 Gy) WBRT if possible
Relapsed/RefractoryIbrutinib (BTK inhibitor), lenalidomide, HD-MTX re-challenge, temozolomide ± rituximabIbrutinib: CNS penetrant, ~50–70% ORR in relapsed PCNSL. LOC trial: lenalidomide + rituximab maintenance

CRITICAL: NO surgical resection for PCNSL (infiltrative, often multifocal; resection does not improve outcomes and delays chemotherapy). Stereotactic biopsy only.

10.4 Meningioma & Other Extra-Axial/Primary Tumors

10.4.1 Meningioma

Most common primary intracranial tumor (~38% of all primary CNS tumors). Arises from meningothelial cells of the arachnoid. WHO 2021 grading incorporates molecular markers alongside histology.

Meningioma WHO 2021 Grading
GradeFrequencyHistology/Molecular5-Year RecurrenceManagement
WHO 1~80%Benign histology (9 subtypes including meningothelial, fibrous, transitional, psammomatous). NF2 mutation most common (~60%)5–10%Observation (small, asymptomatic); Surgery (Simpson Grade I–III); SRS for residual or unresectable (≤3 cm)
WHO 2~15–18%Atypical (4–19 mitoses/10 HPF or ≥3 of 5 criteria: sheeting, spontaneous necrosis, prominent nucleoli, high cellularity, small cells). Brain invasion alone upgrades to Grade 2. Molecular: TERT promoter mutation, homozygous CDKN2A/B deletion30–40%Surgery + adjuvant RT (50–54 Gy; especially if STR or brain invasion)
WHO 3~2–5%Anaplastic/malignant (≥20 mitoses, frank malignant cytology). Any grade with TERT promoter mutation AND/OR CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion → Grade 3 regardless of histology50–80%Surgery + adjuvant RT (59.4–60 Gy). No proven systemic therapy; clinical trials (pembrolizumab, FAK inhibitors, sunitinib)

Simpson Grading of Meningioma Resection

GradeDefinition10-Year Recurrence
IGTR including dural attachment and involved bone9%
IIGTR + coagulation of dural attachment19%
IIIGTR without dural resection/coagulation29%
IVSubtotal resection39%
VBiopsy only / decompression>50%

10.4.2 Other Primary CNS Tumors

TumorKey FeaturesTreatment
Schwannoma (Vestibular)CN VIII at cerebellopontine angle. Unilateral hearing loss + tinnitus + imbalance. T1 Gd+ enhancing. Bilateral = NF2. WHO Grade 1Observation (small, stable); SRS (Gamma Knife ≤3 cm: 95% tumor control); Surgery (translabyrinthine/retrosigmoid if large/symptomatic)
EpendymomaPosterior fossa (children) or spinal cord (adults — myxopapillary at conus/filum). Molecular: ZFTA-RELA fusion (supratentorial, Grade 3); PFA/PFB (posterior fossa). WHO 2021: Grade 2–3Maximal safe resection + RT (local, 54–59.4 Gy). Chemotherapy limited role (except in very young children to defer RT)
MedulloblastomaMost common malignant pediatric brain tumor. Posterior fossa (4th ventricle). WHO 2021 molecular groups: WNT (best prognosis, >95% survival), SHH (intermediate), Group 3 (worst, MYC amplified), Group 4 (intermediate, most common)Surgery + craniospinal irradiation (CSI 23.4–36 Gy) + boost + chemotherapy (cisplatin, vincristine, CCNU). WNT-type: de-escalation trials ongoing. SHH: vismodegib/sonidegib (Hedgehog inhibitors)
Pituitary adenoma / PitNETMost common sellar tumor. Functional (prolactinoma 40%, GH-secreting 20%, ACTH 10%, non-functioning 30%). WHO 2022: “pituitary neuroendocrine tumor (PitNET)” replaces “adenoma.” MRI: sellar mass ± suprasellar extension (visual field defect — bitemporal hemianopia)Prolactinoma: cabergoline (DA agonist; first-line, even for macroadenomas). Non-functioning/GH/ACTH: transsphenoidal surgery. Radiotherapy for residual/recurrent. Temozolomide for aggressive PitNET (ESE guideline 2018)
CraniopharyngiomaSuprasellar, bimodal (children 5–14y + adults 50–75y). Adamantinomatous (calcified, cystic; CTNNB1/β-catenin) vs papillary (BRAF V600E). Visual loss, hypopituitarism, DISurgery (transcranial or endoscopic endonasal). Papillary with BRAF V600E: dabrafenib + trametinib (dramatic responses). Proton therapy for residual
Clinical Pearl — The NF Syndromes in Neuro-Oncology: NF1 (chr 17, neurofibromin): optic pathway gliomas, neurofibromas, plexiform neurofibromas (→ MPNST risk), FASI (focal areas of signal intensity). NF2 (chr 22, merlin/schwannomin): bilateral vestibular schwannomas (diagnostic), meningiomas, ependymomas. Schwannomatosis (SMARCB1/LZTR1): multiple schwannomas WITHOUT vestibular schwannomas; chronic pain prominent. For NF2: bevacizumab can stabilize/shrink vestibular schwannomas (hearing improvement in ~40%).

10.5 Brain Metastases & Leptomeningeal Disease

10.5.1 Brain Metastases

Brain metastases are the most common intracranial tumors in adults (×10 more common than primary brain tumors). Prevalence increasing due to improved systemic cancer control with longer survival and better neuroimaging detection. Occur in 20–40% of all cancer patients.

Primary CancerFrequency of Brain MetsKey Features
Lung (NSCLC, SCLC)40–50% (most common)NSCLC: driver mutations (EGFR, ALK, ROS1) have high brain tropism; SCLC: prophylactic cranial irradiation (PCI) reduces incidence
Breast15–25%HER2+ and triple-negative have highest brain metastasis rates. HER2+: T-DXd (trastuzumab deruxtecan) CNS activity
Melanoma40–60% (at autopsy)Often hemorrhagic. Highly responsive to immunotherapy (anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4). BRAF V600E: dabrafenib + trametinib CNS-penetrant
Renal cell carcinoma5–10%Often hemorrhagic. SRS preferred. Checkpoint inhibitors active
Colorectal3–5%Less common but increasing. Usually late in disease course

Imaging Characteristics

Typically at gray-white matter junction (hematogenous spread trapped at terminal arterioles). Multiple in 70–80%. Ring or solid enhancement. Surrounding vasogenic edema. Distribution: cerebral hemispheres (80%) > cerebellum (15%) > brainstem (5%).

Hemorrhagic brain mets: melanoma, RCC, choriocarcinoma, thyroid. Mnemonic: “MR CT” (Melanoma, Renal, Choriocarcinoma, Thyroid).

10.5.2 Treatment of Brain Metastases

Brain Metastases Treatment Algorithm
ScenarioTreatmentEvidence
1–4 brain mets, good KPSSRS (Stereotactic Radiosurgery) ± surgery (if large/symptomatic/diagnostic uncertainty)SRS: single fraction 15–24 Gy (Gamma Knife, CyberKnife, LINAC). Brown et al. JAMA 2016: SRS alone vs SRS + WBRT: no OS difference, better cognition with SRS alone. Post-op SRS to cavity preferred over WBRT
Single large met (>3–4 cm)Surgery + post-op SRS to cavityMahajan et al. Lancet Oncol 2017: post-op SRS reduces local recurrence from 43% to 28%. Avoids WBRT cognitive toxicity
Multiple (>4) metsWBRT (30 Gy/10 fr or 37.5 Gy/15 fr) or SRS if ≤10–15 mets (evolving)QUARTZ (Mulvenna et al. Lancet 2016): WBRT vs BSC in NSCLC brain mets with poor prognosis: NO survival benefit from WBRT. Hippocampal-sparing WBRT + memantine reduces cognitive toxicity (NRG CC001 (Brown et al. JCO 2020))
Driver mutation-positive NSCLCTargeted therapy with CNS activityOsimertinib (EGFR): IC ORR ~70–90% (FLAURA); Lorlatinib (ALK): IC ORR ~80%; Selpercatinib (RET); Capmatinib (MET). Deferral of RT reasonable in asymptomatic mets responding to systemic therapy
Melanoma brain metsImmunotherapy (anti-PD-1 + anti-CTLA-4)ABC trial (Long et al. Lancet Oncol 2018): nivolumab + ipilimumab: IC ORR 46–57% in treatment-naive melanoma brain mets. BRAF V600E: dabrafenib + trametinib: IC ORR ~55%

10.5.3 Leptomeningeal Carcinomatosis (LMC)

FeatureDetails
DefinitionNeoplastic infiltration of leptomeninges/CSF. Occurs in ~5–8% of solid tumors and ~5–15% of hematologic malignancies
Common primariesBreast (most common), lung (NSCLC), melanoma, hematologic (ALL, lymphoma)
PresentationMultifocal neurological deficits: cranial neuropathies (CN III, VI, VII, VIII common), radiculopathy, headache, nausea/vomiting, cognitive decline, hydrocephalus
DiagnosisCSF cytology (sensitivity ~50% first LP; ≥70% with repeat LP). CSF: ↑protein, ↓glucose, ↑cell count. CSF flow cytometry (lymphoma). Cell-free tumor DNA (cfDNA) in CSF (emerging). MRI with Gd: leptomeningeal enhancement, nodular deposits, cranial nerve enhancement, “sugar coating” of brain surface
TreatmentPrognosis is poor (median OS 2–4 months). Intrathecal chemotherapy: methotrexate 12 mg or cytarabine (liposomal: DepoCyt) via Ommaya reservoir. Systemic therapy: high-dose MTX, targeted agents with CNS penetration. Focal RT for bulky disease or symptomatic sites. VP shunt for symptomatic hydrocephalus

10.5.4 Paraneoplastic Neurological Syndromes (PNS)

Immune-mediated neurological disorders triggered by systemic cancer. Antibody classification divides into intracellular (onconeural, T-cell mediated, often irreversible) vs. cell-surface/synaptic (antibody-mediated, potentially reversible with immunotherapy).

Major Paraneoplastic Syndromes & Antibodies
SyndromeAntibodyCancerKey FeaturesResponse to Immunotherapy
Limbic encephalitisAnti-Hu (ANNA-1); anti-CV2/CRMP5; anti-Ma2; anti-LGI1*; anti-CASPR2*; anti-AMPAR*SCLC (Hu); testicular germ cell (Ma2); thymoma (LGI1, CASPR2)Subacute memory loss, seizures, psychiatric symptoms. MRI: medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensityIntracellular Ab: poor. Cell-surface Ab*: good
Anti-NMDAR encephalitisAnti-NMDAR (GluN1)Ovarian teratoma (40–60% in young women); may be non-paraneoplasticYoung women; psychiatric → seizures → movement disorder (orofacial dyskinesias) → autonomic instability → coma. See Section 5Excellent (80% with tumor removal + immunotherapy)
Subacute cerebellar degenerationAnti-Yo (PCA-1); anti-Tr (DNER); anti-Hu; anti-CV2; anti-mGluR1Ovarian/breast (Yo); Hodgkin lymphoma (Tr); SCLC (Hu)Rapidly progressive pancerebellar ataxia (weeks). Purkinje cell loss. Often irreversibleIntracellular Ab (Yo, Hu): poor. Anti-Tr: moderate. Tumor treatment may stabilize
Sensory neuronopathyAnti-Hu (ANNA-1)SCLCProgressive, asymmetric sensory loss (all modalities); sensory ataxia; areflexia. DRG neurons destroyed. PainfulPoor (intracellular target)
Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome (LEMS)Anti-VGCC (P/Q type)SCLC (50–60%); 40% autoimmune without cancerProximal weakness (legs > arms), hyporeflexia that IMPROVES with activity, autonomic dysfunction (dry mouth). EMG: low CMAP, ↑>100% post-exercise facilitationGood (3,4-DAP + treat cancer; IVIg for autoimmune)
Opsoclonus-myoclonus syndromeAnti-Ri (ANNA-2); often no detectable Ab in childrenNeuroblastoma (children); breast, SCLC (adults)“Dancing eyes, dancing feet”: chaotic saccadic eye movements + myoclonus + ataxiaChildren: moderate (rituximab). Adults: variable
Stiff Person SyndromeAnti-amphiphysin (paraneoplastic); anti-GAD65 (usually autoimmune)Breast (amphiphysin); rarely thymomaProgressive axial rigidity + painful spasms. Hyperexcitable motor neuronsAmphiphysin: poor. GAD65: moderate (benzodiazepines, IVIg, rituximab)

*Cell-surface antibodies (LGI1, CASPR2, AMPAR, NMDAR) are often autoimmune rather than strictly paraneoplastic but cancer screening is essential.

Clinical Pearl — PNS Diagnosis: The 2021 Updated Criteria: The Graus et al. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2021 updated PNS diagnostic criteria introduce a PNS-Care Score (0–14 points) incorporating: clinical phenotype (high/intermediate/low risk), antibody type (high/intermediate-risk), and cancer presence. Score ≥8 = definite PNS; 6–7 = probable; <6 = possible. Key principle: treat the cancer AND the immune response simultaneously. For intracellular antibodies: cancer treatment is the primary intervention (immunotherapy often ineffective). For cell-surface antibodies: aggressive immunotherapy (steroids, IVIg, PLEX, rituximab) + tumor removal.
Q: A 58-year-old female smoker presents with subacute onset of confusion, memory loss, and new-onset seizures over 3 weeks. MRI shows bilateral medial temporal T2/FLAIR hyperintensity without enhancement. CSF shows mild lymphocytic pleocytosis (15 cells) and normal glucose. EEG shows temporal slowing. What is the differential, workup, and management approach?
Differential: (1) Autoimmune/paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis (most likely given subacute onset, medial temporal MRI signal, CSF pleocytosis); (2) Viral encephalitis (HSV — but typically unilateral, fever, hemorrhagic); (3) GBM (unlikely without enhancement); (4) Neurosyphilis; (5) Status epilepticus with hippocampal edema.
Workup:
1. Serum + CSF autoimmune encephalitis panel: anti-LGI1, anti-CASPR2, anti-AMPAR, anti-Hu, anti-CV2, anti-Ma2, anti-NMDAR, anti-GABA-B
2. CSF HSV PCR (must exclude HSV encephalitis — empiric acyclovir while pending)
3. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (cancer screening — SCLC most common cause of paraneoplastic limbic encephalitis in smokers)
4. Whole-body PET-CT (if initial CT negative; detects occult cancer in 30–40%)
5. CSF cytology, RPR, additional standard CSF studies
Management (presumptive autoimmune/paraneoplastic LE):
1. Empiric acyclovir until HSV PCR negative
2. Start immunotherapy empirically (don’t wait for antibody results): IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 5 days + IVIg 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days
3. ASM for seizures: levetiracetam 1000–1500 mg BID (avoid sodium-channel blockers if anti-LGI1 suspected — these patients often have faciobrachial dystonic seizures)
4. Aggressive cancer search and treatment (if anti-Hu positive: search for SCLC even if CT negative; if anti-Ma2: testicular exam + US)

10.6 Neuro-Oncologic Emergencies & Seizure Management

10.6.1 Raised Intracranial Pressure & Herniation

EmergencyManagement
Vasogenic edema (peri-tumoral)Dexamethasone: 10 mg IV bolus → 4–8 mg q6h. Mechanism: reduces BBB permeability, decreases edema. Onset: 24–72 hours. Taper as soon as possible (steroid myopathy, immunosuppression, hyperglycemia, psychiatric effects). Bevacizumab for steroid-refractory edema (radiation necrosis, recurrent GBM)
Obstructive hydrocephalusEVD (external ventricular drain) for acute. VP shunt or ETV for subacute/chronic. Dexamethasone as bridge. Common with posterior fossa tumors, colloid cyst, pineal tumors
Impending herniationHead of bed 30°; IV mannitol 1–1.5 g/kg OR hypertonic saline 23.4% 30 mL bolus; dexamethasone 10 mg IV; hyperventilation (target pCO2 30–35 mmHg, temporizing); urgent neurosurgical consultation for decompression/EVD

10.6.2 Seizure Management in Brain Tumors

Seizures in Brain Tumors
AspectDetails
PrevalenceLow-grade glioma: 60–90% (seizure often the presenting symptom); GBM: 30–50%; Meningioma: 20–40%; Brain metastases: 20–35%
ASM choiceLevetiracetam (first-line): no enzyme induction, no drug interactions with chemotherapy, IV formulation. 500–1500 mg BID. Lacosamide: good alternative (no interactions, IV available). Avoid enzyme-inducing ASMs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital) — reduce efficacy of chemotherapy (TMZ, irinotecan) and dexamethasone metabolism. Valproate: consider in IDH-mutant glioma (possible HDAC inhibitor synergy with TMZ — retrospective data, not proven)
ProphylaxisNOT recommended for routine seizure prophylaxis in brain tumor patients who have not had seizures (AAN Practice Parameter 2000; reaffirmed 2019). Exception: perioperative (1–2 weeks post-craniotomy), then taper if no seizures. Prophylaxis reasonable in melanoma mets (high hemorrhage risk)
Status epilepticusStandard SE protocol (benzodiazepines → LEV/VPA/fosphenytoin). Address underlying cause: tumor progression? hemorrhage into tumor? metabolic (hyponatremia from TMZ, SIADH)? infection (immunosuppressed)?
Clinical Pearl — Drug Interactions in Neuro-Oncology: This is a critical area where neurologists must coordinate with oncologists. Key interactions: (1) Enzyme-inducing ASMs (phenytoin, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine) reduce levels of: TMZ, irinotecan, vincristine, dexamethasone, targeted agents (osimertinib, lorlatinib). Always use non-enzyme-inducing ASMs (levetiracetam, lacosamide, brivaracetam). (2) Dexamethasone is a CYP3A4 inducer: affects some targeted therapies. (3) TMZ + valproate: VPA may increase TMZ-related myelosuppression (monitor CBC closely).

10.6.3 Neurological Complications of Cancer Treatment

Neurological Toxicity of Cancer Therapies
TherapyNeurological ComplicationManagement
Radiation (cranial)Acute (days): cerebral edema, worsening symptoms → dexamethasone.
Early-delayed (1–6 mo): somnolence syndrome (children), pseudoprogression → observe, steroids.
Late (>6 mo): radiation necrosis (focal, enhancing — mimics recurrence; diagnose with perfusion MRI + amino acid PET; treat with bevacizumab), leukoencephalopathy, cognitive decline, endocrinopathy, radiation-induced tumors (meningioma, GBM), cavernomas		Radiation necrosis: bevacizumab 7.5 mg/kg q3w (most effective); dexamethasone; surgery if refractory. Cognitive decline: cognitive rehabilitation; memantine (with hippocampal-sparing WBRT)
Immune checkpoint inhibitors (anti-PD-1, anti-CTLA-4)Immune-related neurological adverse events (irAE): myasthenia gravis (most dangerous), Guillain-Barré, encephalitis, meningitis, hypophysitis, myelitis, peripheral neuropathy. Overlap syndromes (MG + myositis + myocarditis = “triple M”)Grade 1: observe. Grade 2: hold ICI + prednisone 0.5–1 mg/kg. Grade 3–4: permanently discontinue ICI + high-dose methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg/day. IVIg/PLEX for MG, GBS. Myocarditis: urgent cardiology consult
Chemotherapy-induced peripheral neuropathy (CIPN)Platinum (cisplatin, oxaliplatin): sensory > motor, DRG neurotoxicity. Taxanes: predominantly sensory. Vincristine: mixed sensorimotor, autonomic (constipation). Bortezomib: painful, small fiberPrevention: limited evidence (only duloxetine for treatment). Treatment: duloxetine 60 mg/day (Smith et al. JAMA 2013: modest benefit for CIPN pain). Gabapentin/pregabalin for neuropathic pain. Dose reduction/modification of causative agent
CAR-T cell therapyICANS (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome): confusion, aphasia, seizures, cerebral edema. Often follows CRS. ICE score for grading (Immune Effector Cell-Associated Encephalopathy)Grade 1–2: supportive, dexamethasone 10 mg q6h. Grade 3: dexamethasone 10 mg q6h + consider tocilizumab if concurrent CRS. Grade 4: methylprednisolone 1 g/day, ICU, seizure prophylaxis (levetiracetam)
Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)Associated with: cyclosporine, tacrolimus, bevacizumab, cisplatin, checkpoint inhibitors, hypertension. Headache, seizures, visual disturbance, confusion. MRI: posterior white matter vasogenic edema (parieto-occipital)Discontinue/reduce causative agent. Aggressive BP control. ASMs for seizures. Usually reversible if treated promptly
Q: A 45-year-old woman with known Grade 2 oligodendroglioma (IDH-mutant, 1p/19q-codeleted) on surveillance imaging shows a new 2 cm enhancing nodule within the known T2 lesion. She has been seizure-free on levetiracetam 1000 mg BID. What does the new enhancement suggest, and what is the next step?
Concern: New enhancement within a previously non-enhancing low-grade glioma strongly suggests malignant transformation (anaplastic progression to Grade 3). Oligodendrogliomas can transform over time, though their transformation rate is lower than IDH-mutant astrocytomas.
Differential of new enhancement: (1) Anaplastic transformation (most likely); (2) Treatment-related change (if recent RT/chemo — not applicable here on surveillance); (3) Seizure-related transient enhancement (unlikely if seizure-free); (4) Radiation necrosis (if prior RT)
Next steps:
1. Advanced MRI: perfusion (rCBV ↑ in high-grade), spectroscopy (choline/NAA ↑), DWI
2. Consider amino acid PET (18F-FET PET) if available — more specific than MRI for distinguishing high-grade from low-grade regions
3. Neurosurgical consultation for resection/biopsy of the enhancing component to confirm grade
4. If confirmed Grade 3: RT (59.4 Gy) + PCV chemotherapy (CODEL trial (ongoing): RT + PCV vs RT + TMZ in 1p/19q-codeleted anaplastic oligodendroglioma)
5. Continue levetiracetam (non-enzyme-inducing; compatible with PCV chemotherapy)
6. Consider vorasidenib if the patient is not a candidate for immediate aggressive treatment (IDH-mutant low-grade residual component)

10.7 References & Key Guidelines

Key References & Guidelines

Classification & Molecular

  • Louis DN et al. The 2021 WHO Classification of Tumors of the Central Nervous System: a summary. Neuro Oncol 2021;23(8):1231–1251.
  • Brat DJ et al. cIMPACT-NOW Update 3: recommended diagnostic criteria for “diffuse astrocytic glioma, IDH-wildtype, with molecular features of glioblastoma, WHO grade IV.” Acta Neuropathol 2018;136(5):805–810.

Landmark Trials

  • Stupp R et al. Radiotherapy plus concomitant and adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2005;352:987–996. [Stupp Protocol]
  • Stupp R et al. Effect of tumor-treating fields plus maintenance temozolomide vs maintenance temozolomide alone on survival in patients with glioblastoma: a randomized clinical trial. JAMA 2017;318(23):2306–2316. [EF-14 — TTFields]
  • Mellinghoff IK et al. Vorasidenib in IDH1- or IDH2-mutant low-grade glioma. N Engl J Med 2023;389(7):589–601. [INDIGO]
  • Perry JR et al. Short-course radiation plus temozolomide in elderly patients with glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2017;376:1027–1037. [CE.6/EORTC 26062-22061]
  • Buckner JC et al. Radiation plus procarbazine, CCNU, and vincristine in low-grade glioma. N Engl J Med 2016;374:1344–1355. [RTOG 9802]
  • Herrlinger U et al. Lomustine-temozolomide combination therapy versus standard temozolomide therapy in patients with newly diagnosed glioblastoma with methylated MGMT promoter (CeTeG/NOA-09). Lancet 2019;393:678–688.
  • Chinot OL et al. Bevacizumab plus radiotherapy-temozolomide for newly diagnosed glioblastoma. N Engl J Med 2014;370:709–722. [AVAglio]
  • Ferreri AJM et al. MATRix regimen for primary CNS lymphoma. Lancet Haematol 2016;3(5):e217–e227.
  • Brown PD et al. Postoperative stereotactic radiosurgery compared with whole brain radiotherapy for resected metastatic brain disease (NCCTG N107C/CEC-3). Lancet Oncol 2017;18(8):1049–1060.
  • Brown PD et al. Hippocampal avoidance during whole-brain radiotherapy plus memantine for patients with brain metastases: NRG CC001. J Clin Oncol 2020;38(10):1019–1029.
  • Mulvenna P et al. Dexamethasone and supportive care with or without whole brain radiotherapy in treating patients with non-small cell lung cancer with brain metastases unsuitable for resection or stereotactic radiosurgery (QUARTZ). Lancet 2016;388(10055):2004–2014.

Paraneoplastic & Complications

  • Graus F et al. Updated diagnostic criteria for paraneoplastic neurologic syndromes. Neurol Neuroimmunol Neuroinflamm 2021;8(4):e1014. [PNS-Care Score]
  • Glantz MJ et al. Practice parameter: anticonvulsant prophylaxis in patients with newly diagnosed brain tumors. Neurology 2000;54(10):1886–1893. [AAN — no routine prophylaxis]
  • Smith EML et al. Effect of duloxetine on pain, function, and quality of life among patients with chemotherapy-induced painful peripheral neuropathy. JAMA 2013;309(13):1359–1367.
  • Reardon DA et al. Effect of nivolumab vs bevacizumab in patients with recurrent glioblastoma: the CheckMate 143 Phase 3 Randomized Clinical Trial. JAMA Oncol 2020;6(7):1003–1010.
  • Majzner RG et al. GD2-CAR T cell therapy for H3K27M-mutated diffuse midline gliomas. Nature 2022;603:934–941.

11. Spinal Cord Disorders

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of spinal cord disorders. Coverage includes traumatic SCI (ASIA/ISNCSCI 2019 classification, incomplete cord syndromes, acute management with MAP targets, early decompression evidence from STASCIS/SCI-POEM, autonomic dysreflexia), cervical spondylotic myelopathy (most common non-traumatic myelopathy, mJOA grading, surgical approaches, CSM vs ALS differentiation), OPLL, spinal tumors by compartment (extradural metastases/MESCC emergency, intradural-extramedullary meningioma/schwannoma, intramedullary ependymoma/astrocytoma), inflammatory myelopathies (transverse myelitis differential, MS vs NMOSD vs MOGAD myelitis patterns, sarcoid myelopathy, infectious myelitis including HTLV-1/VZV/schistosomiasis), vascular myelopathies (spinal cord infarction, spinal dural AVF), metabolic myelopathies (B12/SCD, copper deficiency, nitrous oxide, radiation myelopathy), hereditary spastic paraplegia, cauda equina vs conus medullaris syndromes, syringomyelia, Chiari malformations, and tethered cord syndrome.

11.1 Spinal Cord Injury — ASIA Classification & Acute Management

11.1.1 Neuroanatomical Review

The spinal cord extends from the foramen magnum to the conus medullaris (typically L1–L2 vertebral level in adults). Understanding the cross-sectional anatomy of the cord is essential for localizing myelopathy:

TractLocationFunctionDeficit When Damaged
Corticospinal (lateral)Lateral column (decussates at medullary pyramids)Voluntary motor (UMN)Ipsilateral UMN weakness, spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski
Dorsal columns (fasciculus gracilis/cuneatus)Posterior column (decussates at medulla)Fine touch, vibration, proprioceptionIpsilateral loss of vibration, proprioception, sensory ataxia
Spinothalamic (lateral)Anterolateral (decussates 1–2 levels above entry in anterior white commissure)Pain, temperature, crude touchContralateral loss of pain/temperature (1–2 levels below lesion)
Anterior horn cellsVentral gray matterLMN to skeletal musclesIpsilateral LMN signs at lesion level: weakness, atrophy, fasciculations, hyporeflexia
Intermediolateral cell columnLateral horn (T1–L2)Sympathetic preganglionic neuronsAutonomic dysfunction (orthostatic hypotension, bladder/bowel, anhidrosis below level)
Sacral parasympatheticsS2–S4 (Onuf’s nucleus)Bladder detrusor, rectal, sexual functionNeurogenic bladder/bowel, erectile dysfunction

11.1.2 ASIA/ISNCSCI Classification

ASIA Impairment Scale (AIS) — International Standards 2019
AIS GradeDescriptionKey Definition
A — CompleteNo motor or sensory function in sacral segments S4–S5No voluntary anal contraction (VAC), no deep anal pressure (DAP), no light touch/pin prick at S4–S5
B — Sensory IncompleteSensory but no motor function preserved below NLI including S4–S5DAP or S4–S5 sensation present, NO motor below NLI (or motor present only in zone of partial preservation)
C — Motor IncompleteMotor function preserved below NLI; >50% of key muscles below NLI are < Grade 3Motor incomplete but mostly weak (cannot overcome gravity)
D — Motor IncompleteMotor function preserved below NLI; ≥50% of key muscles below NLI are ≥ Grade 3Motor incomplete with functional strength (overcomes gravity)
E — NormalMotor and sensory normal in all segmentsMay have abnormal reflexes (hyperreflexia); recovered SCI

Key terminology: NLI (Neurological Level of Injury) = most caudal segment with normal motor (Grade 5) AND sensory function bilaterally. ZPP (Zone of Partial Preservation) = applies only to AIS A; segments below NLI with some preserved function.

11.1.3 Spinal Cord Syndromes

Incomplete Spinal Cord Syndromes
SyndromeLesion LocationEtiologyClinical FeaturesPrognosis
Central Cord SyndromeCentral gray matter + medial portions of lateral columnsMost common incomplete SCI. Hyperextension injury in elderly with pre-existing cervical spondylosis. Syringomyelia. Intramedullary tumorArms >> legs weakness (lamination: cervical fibers medial in corticospinal tract). “Cape-like” dissociated sensory loss (pain/temp) if syrinx. Bladder dysfunctionBest recovery among incomplete syndromes. Legs recover first, then bladder, then arms/hands (hands recover last and least)
Brown-Séquard (Hemicord)Lateral half of cord (unilateral)Penetrating trauma (knife, gunshot), lateral disc herniation, lateral tumor, MS plaqueIpsilateral: UMN weakness, dorsal column loss (vibration, proprioception). Contralateral: spinothalamic loss (pain/temp, 1–2 levels below)Best functional prognosis. >90% regain ambulatory capacity
Anterior Cord SyndromeAnterior 2/3 of cord (anterior spinal artery territory)Anterior spinal artery occlusion (aortic surgery, dissection, hypotension), disc herniation, flexion-compression fractureMotor paralysis (corticospinal) + spinothalamic loss (pain/temp) below level. PRESERVED dorsal columns (vibration, proprioception, light touch)Worst prognosis among incomplete syndromes. Only 10–20% recover functional motor
Posterior Cord SyndromePosterior columns (rare in isolation)B12 deficiency (SCD), tabes dorsalis, Friedreich ataxia, MS, compressiveLoss of proprioception, vibration, fine touch. Sensory ataxia. Romberg positive. Preserved motor, pain/tempDepends on etiology; B12 replacement may improve if caught early

11.1.4 Acute SCI Management

Acute Traumatic SCI Management Protocol
DomainInterventionDetails & Evidence
Spinal immobilizationCervical collar + log-roll; spinal boardUntil cleared by imaging. CT cervical spine (not X-ray) is standard. MRI for suspected cord/ligamentous injury
Hemodynamic targetMAP 85–90 mmHg × 7 daysPrevents secondary cord ischemia. Vasopressors (norepinephrine preferred) for neurogenic shock. AO Spine Guidelines 2017: strong recommendation for MAP augmentation
Neurogenic shockHypotension + bradycardia (distinguishes from hemorrhagic shock which has tachycardia)Loss of sympathetic tone below injury (especially T6 and above). Warm/dry skin (vasodilation). Treat: IV fluids + vasopressors (norepinephrine, phenylephrine). Atropine for symptomatic bradycardia
RespiratoryC3–C5 injury: phrenic nerve at riskMonitor FVC q4h (intubate if FVC <15 mL/kg or declining). High cervical (C1–C3): usually requires ventilator. C3–C5: variable; may decompensate days later as edema ascends. Diaphragmatic pacing for chronic high cervical SCI
Surgical decompression<24 hours preferredSTASCIS (Fehlings et al. PLoS One 2012): early decompression (<24h) vs late (>24h): ≥2 AIS grade improvement in 19.8% vs 8.8% (p=0.01). SCI-POEM (Fehlings et al. Lancet Neurol 2021): ultra-early (<12h) may be even better. AO Spine 2017: strong recommendation for early decompression in incomplete SCI
MethylprednisoloneNOT standard of careNASCIS-2/3 protocol (30 mg/kg bolus → 5.4 mg/kg/h × 23–48h within 8h of injury): AAN/CNS/AO Spine recommend AGAINST routine use. Marginal benefit does not outweigh risk (infection, GI bleed, hyperglycemia, AVN). May be considered in shared decision-making within 8h for acute SCI. Never give >8h post-injury
VTE prophylaxisLMWH within 72h (once hemostasis ensured)SCI has highest DVT/PE risk of any medical condition (~40–80% without prophylaxis). LMWH + mechanical prophylaxis. Duration: 8–12 weeks. IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated

11.1.5 Autonomic Dysreflexia

Autonomic Dysreflexia (AD) — Medical Emergency in Chronic SCI
FeatureDetails
WhoSCI at T6 or above (loss of splanchnic sympathetic outflow regulation). Onset usually ≥3–6 weeks post-injury (once spinal shock resolves). Lifetime risk: up to 90% in high cervical SCI
MechanismNoxious stimulus below injury → massive uninhibited sympathetic discharge below lesion → severe vasoconstriction → hypertension. Baroreceptor-mediated parasympathetic response ABOVE lesion (bradycardia, flushing, sweating above level) but cannot descend past injury to counteract
TriggersBladder distension (most common: 75–85%), bowel impaction (second most common), pressure ulcers, ingrown toenail, tight clothing, UTI, fracture, DVT, sexual stimulation, labor in pregnant SCI patients
SymptomsSevere headache (pounding), hypertension (>20–40 mmHg above baseline; SBP can exceed 250–300), bradycardia (reflex), sweating/flushing above level, piloerection, nasal congestion, blurred vision, anxiety
Management1. Sit patient upright (orthostatic drop). 2. Identify and remove trigger: straight catheterize bladder (most common cause); check for fecal impaction (use lidocaine jelly). 3. Loosen clothing. 4. If SBP remains >150 after trigger removal: nifedipine 10 mg bite-and-swallow or nitroglycerine paste 2% 1 inch topically. 5. IV nitroprusside or labetalol for refractory cases
PreventionScheduled CIC (clean intermittent catheterization), regular bowel program, skin checks, patient/caregiver education. Medical alert card
Clinical Pearl — Spinal Shock vs. Neurogenic Shock: These are commonly confused. Spinal shock = transient loss of ALL spinal cord function (including reflexes) below the level of injury. NOT a hemodynamic state. Duration: days to weeks. Resolution marked by return of bulbocavernosus reflex (first reflex to return). Neurogenic shock = hemodynamic state: hypotension + bradycardia from loss of sympathetic tone. A cardiovascular emergency requiring vasopressors. Both can coexist in acute SCI but are distinct entities.
Q: A 72-year-old man with known cervical spondylosis falls, hitting his forehead. He presents with weakness more severe in the upper extremities than lower, bilateral hand weakness, and urinary retention. Lower extremity strength is 4/5 throughout. MRI shows C3–C6 spondylosis with central cord T2 hyperintensity. What is the syndrome, AIS grade, and management?
Syndrome: Central cord syndrome — the most common incomplete SCI. Classic mechanism: hyperextension injury in elderly with pre-existing cervical spondylosis.
AIS Grade: AIS D (motor incomplete; ≥50% of key muscles below NLI have grade ≥3, as LE strength is 4/5).
Key features: Upper extremity weakness > lower (arms affected more than legs), hand weakness (fine motor), bladder dysfunction (urinary retention).
Management:
1. Immobilization (cervical collar)
2. MAP target 85–90 mmHg × 7 days (prevent secondary ischemia)
3. MRI confirms cord edema/signal change + multi-level spondylotic compression
4. Surgical decompression within 24 hours (STASCIS evidence for early decompression benefit, especially in incomplete SCI). Approach: posterior laminoplasty or laminectomy for multi-level compression; anterior approach if single-level prominent disc
5. VTE prophylaxis: LMWH + SCDs once hemostasis stable
6. Bladder management: Foley catheter acutely; transition to CIC
7. Methylprednisolone: NOT recommended (AAN/AO Spine guidelines)
8. Prognosis: Central cord has the best recovery among incomplete syndromes. Legs recover first, then bladder, then proximal arms; hands recover last and least completely. Most AIS D patients regain ambulatory function

11.2 Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy & Compressive Spinal Cord Disorders

11.2.1 Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy (CSM)

CSM is the most common cause of non-traumatic myelopathy in adults over age 55 in developed countries. It results from degenerative changes causing progressive spinal cord compression. Understanding its pathophysiology, natural history, and surgical indications is essential for neurologists.

Cervical Spondylotic Myelopathy — Comprehensive Overview
FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyDegenerative cascade: disc desiccation → disc bulging/herniation → osteophyte formation (uncovertebral, facet joints) → ligamentum flavum hypertrophy/buckling → canal narrowing. Static factors (structural narrowing) + dynamic factors (repetitive flexion/extension microtrauma). Congenital canal stenosis (AP diameter <13 mm) predisposes.
