Ischemia from anterior spinal artery compression contributes. Pathology: demyelination of lateral columns > posterior columns; anterior horn cell loss at compression level
Most common levelsC5–C6 > C6–C7 > C4–C5 (greatest mobility = greatest degeneration). Multi-level disease common in elderly
Clinical featuresInsidious onset, stepwise progression. (1) Gait difficulty (spastic ataxic gait) — often earliest symptom. (2) Hand clumsiness, loss of fine motor (myelopathy hand: finger escape sign, grip-release <20 cycles in 10 sec). (3) Neck stiffness/pain (not always present). (4) UMN signs in legs: spasticity, hyperreflexia, Babinski. (5) LMN signs at level of compression (C5–C6: biceps/brachioradialis weakness, absent reflex). (6) Mixed UMN/LMN in arms. (7) Bladder dysfunction (late). (8) Lhermitte sign (electric shock with neck flexion — not specific to MS)
Natural historyTypically progressive. 75% stepwise deterioration, 20% slow steady decline, 5% rapid. Spontaneous improvement rare. Worse prognosis: older age, longer symptom duration, greater cord compression, T2 signal change (myelomalacia)

Diagnosis & Imaging

InvestigationFindingsClinical Significance
MRI cervical spine (gold standard)Disc bulging/herniation, osteophytes, cord compression. T2 hyperintensity in cord (myelopathy signal). T1 hypointensity (myelomalacia — worse prognosis). Cord cross-sectional area <60 mm² correlates with myelopathyT2 signal = edema/gliosis (may be reversible). T1 hypointensity = irreversible necrosis/cavitation. Snake-eye appearance on axial T2 (bilateral anterior horn signal) = poor surgical prognosis
CT myelographySuperior bony detail. Gold standard if MRI contraindicated. Better for OPLL characterizationShows extent of cord compression, canal diameter
X-ray (flexion/extension)Spondylosis, disc space narrowing, osteophytes, alignment. Dynamic instability (>3.5 mm translation)Screening; insufficient alone for myelopathy assessment
ElectrophysiologySSEPs: prolonged central conduction time. EMG: may show C5/C6 radiculopathy (denervation at compression level). MEPs: corticospinal tract dysfunctionHelps differentiate from peripheral neuropathy, ALS. Intraoperative monitoring during surgery

CSM Severity Grading

Modified Japanese Orthopaedic Association (mJOA) Score
SeveritymJOA ScoreClinical Implication
Mild15–17Can be observed with serial exams q3–6 months + MRI. Surgery if deterioration
Moderate12–14Surgery recommended (AO Spine CSM guidelines 2017)
Severe≤11Urgent surgery recommended. Worse baseline = less recovery potential

Maximum score = 18 (normal). Assesses motor function upper/lower extremities, sensory, and bladder. Most widely used outcome measure in CSM trials.

Surgical Management of CSM

ApproachIndicationProcedureKey Considerations
Anterior1–2 level disease, anterior compression (disc/osteophyte), kyphotic alignmentACDF (anterior cervical discectomy & fusion): 1–2 levels. ACCF (anterior cervical corpectomy & fusion): 1–3 levels, for retrovertebral disease/OPLLDirect decompression. Risk: dysphagia (most common), recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy, esophageal injury, adjacent segment disease (2.9%/year). C5 palsy (4–8%): new deltoid/biceps weakness post-op, usually recovers
PosteriorMulti-level (≥3) disease, posterior compression (ligamentum flavum), preserved/lordotic alignmentLaminoplasty (open-door or French-door): preserves motion, best for 3–4 levels. Laminectomy + fusion: for ≥3 levels, instability, kyphosis correction neededIndirect decompression (cord drifts posteriorly). Requires lordotic alignment (kyphosis = anterior compression persists). C5 palsy risk similar. Laminoplasty: preserves motion but axial neck pain common
Combined (360°)Severe multi-level compression with kyphosis, instability, OPLLAnterior decompression + posterior fixation/fusionHigher morbidity but best for complex deformity
Clinical Pearl — CSM vs. ALS: This is one of the most important differential diagnoses in neurology. Both present with upper AND lower motor neuron signs. Key differentiators: (1) CSM: sensory findings (absent in ALS); (2) CSM: bowel/bladder involvement (rare in ALS until late); (3) ALS: widespread fasciculations beyond one myotomal distribution, bulbar signs (tongue fasciculations, dysarthria), split-hand sign (APB > ADM weakness), EMG showing widespread active denervation across multiple levels. (4) CSM: MRI shows cord compression with signal change; (5) CSM: UMN/LMN signs correspond to structural compression level. Trap: Patients can have both (CSM + ALS). If a patient with confirmed CSM continues to deteriorate after adequate decompression, reconsider ALS.

11.2.2 Ossification of the Posterior Longitudinal Ligament (OPLL)

FeatureDetails
EpidemiologyPrevalence: East Asian populations (Japan 1.9–4.3%, Korea, China) >> Caucasian (0.1–1.7%). Male > female (2:1). Age 50–70. Associated with DM, metabolic syndrome, DISH (diffuse idiopathic skeletal hyperostosis)
Classification (Tsuyama)Continuous (most common), segmental, mixed, circumscribed (localized). CT classification: flat, mushroom-shaped (hill type), nodular
GeneticsPolygenic: BMP signaling pathway genes (BMP2, BMP4), COL6A1, COL11A2, TGF-β pathway. Genome-wide association studies identify multiple loci
ClinicalProgressive myelopathy identical to CSM. Higher risk of SCI from minor trauma (already stenotic canal). Occupying ratio >60% = high myelopathy risk. Dynamic factors contribute less (often fused segments)
ImagingCT: gold standard for OPLL morphology and occupying ratio. MRI: cord compression, myelopathy signal. Plain film: dense ossification posterior to vertebral bodies
TreatmentPosterior approach (laminoplasty) preferred for multi-level, occupying ratio <60%. Anterior approach (corpectomy) for 1–2 level, high occupying ratio, kyphosis. Anterior floating method: OPLL left in situ, dura separates. Risk of dural ossification (OPLL adherent to dura): CSF leak 10–30% with anterior approach. K-line concept: if OPLL does not cross line from C2 to C7 on lateral view, posterior approach effective

11.2.3 Other Compressive Myelopathies

ConditionKey FeaturesManagement
Thoracic disc herniationRare (1% of all disc herniations). Insidious myelopathy. T8–T12 most common. Often calcified. Diagnosis frequently delayed. MRI thoracic spine essential when cervical MRI doesn’t explain myelopathyAnterior or lateral approach (transthoracic, costotransversectomy). Avoid posterior laminectomy (cord cannot tolerate posterior retraction at thoracic level)
Thoracic spinal stenosisLess common than cervical. OLF (ossification of ligamentum flavum) more common in East Asian populations. Often combined with thoracic OPLLPosterior decompression (laminectomy + fusion). OLF: en bloc resection avoiding dural tear
Lumbar spinal stenosisNeurogenic (pseudo) claudication: bilateral leg pain/weakness with walking, relieved by sitting/bending forward (increases canal diameter). Distinguished from vascular claudication (which improves with standing still). Central stenosis: cauda equina compression. Lateral recess/foraminal: radiculopathyConservative: physical therapy, epidural steroid injections. Surgical: decompressive laminectomy ± fusion (SPORT trial: surgery superior to non-operative treatment at 2 and 4 years for moderate-severe stenosis)
Atlanto-axial subluxationRheumatoid arthritis (most common cause): pannus erodes transverse ligament → anterior subluxation of C1 on C2 (ADI >3 mm in adults). Also: Down syndrome, os odontoideum, Morquio syndrome. Myelopathy from cervicomedullary compressionC1–C2 posterior fusion (Magerl transarticular screws, Harms C1 lateral mass/C2 pedicle screws). Screen RA patients before intubation (flexion/extension views or MRI)
Paget disease of boneVertebral enlargement, stenosis, basilar invagination. Pagetic steal (hypervascularity diverts blood from cord). Rarely sarcomatous degenerationBisphosphonates (zoledronic acid). Decompressive surgery for myelopathy

11.2.4 Spinal Tumors

Spinal tumors are classified by anatomical compartment, which determines the differential diagnosis, clinical presentation, and surgical approach:

Spinal Tumor Classification by Compartment
CompartmentFrequencyCommon TumorsClinical FeaturesMRI Characteristics
Extradural55–60% (most common)Metastases (most common overall spinal tumor): lung, breast, prostate, kidney, myeloma. Primary: chordoma (sacrum/clivus), osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, giant cell tumorPain (often nocturnal, progressive, worse supine). Rapid progression. Pathological fracture. Epidural spinal cord compression (ESCC) = oncologic emergencyVertebral body destruction, epidural soft tissue mass, cord compression. T1 low signal replacing marrow fat. Enhancement heterogeneous
Intradural-Extramedullary30–35%Meningioma (F > M, thoracic predilection, en plaque or globular, dural tail sign). Schwannoma/Neurofibroma (nerve sheath tumors; schwannoma: S100+, encapsulated, Antoni A/B; dumbbell shape through foramen). Myxopapillary ependymoma (filum terminale). Drop metastases (medulloblastoma, ependymoma, PCNSL, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis)Slowly progressive myelopathy + radiculopathy. Well-circumscribed, displaces cord. Nerve sheath tumors: radicular pain often first. Meningioma: middle-aged women, thoracic levelWell-defined, homogeneous enhancement. Cord displaced but not invaded. CSF cap sign (meniscus of CSF around tumor). Dumbbell shape (schwannoma extending through foramen)
Intramedullary5–10%Ependymoma (most common IM tumor in adults; central canal origin; well-circumscribed; GTR often possible). Astrocytoma (most common IM tumor in children; infiltrative; GTR often not possible). Hemangioblastoma (VHL association; enhancing nodule + cyst + syrinx). Metastases (rare: lung, breast)Central cord syndrome pattern. Diffuse poorly localizable pain. Suspended dissociated sensory loss (cape-like). Late: UMN signs below, LMN at level. Syringomyelia frequently associated (tumor-related syrinx)Cord expansion + T2 hyperintensity. Ependymoma: central, well-marginated, “cap sign” (hemosiderin rim from prior hemorrhage). Astrocytoma: eccentric, ill-defined, patchy enhancement. Hemangioblastoma: brightly enhancing nodule

Metastatic Epidural Spinal Cord Compression (MESCC)

MESCC — Oncologic Emergency
AspectDetails
Epidemiology5–10% of cancer patients. Most common: lung > breast > prostate > renal > myeloma. Thoracic spine most affected (60–70%) > lumbar (20–25%) > cervical (10–15%)
PresentationBack pain (first symptom in >95%): progressive, worse at night, worse supine, may be radicular (thoracic band-like pain). Motor weakness (60–85%): rapidly progressive. Sensory level (50–70%). Autonomic/bladder dysfunction (40–60%): late, poor prognostic sign. Pre-ambulatory status at treatment = strongest predictor of outcome
ESCC Scale (Bilsky)MRI-based grading of epidural disease: 0 (bone only) → 1a (epidural fat impingement) → 1b (thecal sac abutment) → 1c (cord abutment, no compression) → 2 (cord compression, CSF visible) → 3 (cord compression, no CSF visible). Grade 2–3: high-grade ESCC requiring urgent intervention
Acute management1. Dexamethasone: high-dose 10–16 mg IV bolus then 4–8 mg q6h (NICE guidelines). Some use 96 mg bolus for motor deficits (Sorensen et al. 1994). 2. MRI whole spine with Gd (10–15% have additional asymptomatic levels). 3. Emergency referral: neurosurgery/spine surgery + radiation oncology
Definitive treatmentSurgery + post-op RT vs. RT alone: Patchell et al. Lancet 2005: direct decompressive surgery + RT significantly superior to RT alone for maintaining/regaining ambulation (84% vs 57%, p=0.001), continent (1), reduced opioid use. Inclusion: single site, no radiosensitive histology, reasonable prognosis. RT alone: multiple levels, radiosensitive tumors (lymphoma, myeloma, SCLC, seminoma), non-surgical candidates, poor prognosis (<3 months). SBRT/SRS: increasingly used for re-irradiation and oligometastatic disease (16–24 Gy single fraction or 27 Gy/3 fractions)
Prognostic factors (Tokuhashi, Tomita scores)Consider: primary tumor type, visceral/bone metastases, performance status, neurological deficit, life expectancy. Radiosensitive histologies (lymphoma, myeloma, breast, prostate) have better outcomes. Ambulatory status at treatment initiation is the single most important predictor of post-treatment ambulation
Clinical Pearl — Urgency in MESCC: This is truly a neurological emergency where hours matter. A patient who is ambulatory at the time of treatment has ~80% chance of remaining ambulatory. A patient who is paraplegic at treatment has only ~10–20% chance of regaining ambulation. The window of opportunity is narrow: motor deficits progressing over hours to days. Key principle: MRI the ENTIRE spine urgently (multi-level disease in 10–38%), start high-dose dexamethasone immediately, and arrange surgery + RT within 24–48 hours for high-grade ESCC in appropriate candidates. Do not wait for a biopsy if the diagnosis of metastatic disease is established. Delays for “further workup” are the most common preventable cause of poor outcome.
Q: A 62-year-old man presents with 3 months of progressive difficulty walking, hand clumsiness (cannot button his shirt), and neck stiffness. Examination shows spastic gait, hyperreflexia in all four limbs, Babinski bilateral, inverted radial reflex (finger flexion when brachioradialis tested), and reduced vibration in the feet. No fasciculations. MRI shows multi-level (C3–C7) spondylotic stenosis with T2 cord signal at C4–C5. What is the diagnosis, mJOA severity, and surgical approach?
Diagnosis: Cervical spondylotic myelopathy (CSM) — the most common non-traumatic myelopathy. The clinical triad of gait difficulty + hand clumsiness + UMN signs with structural cord compression on MRI is classic.
Key examination findings: (1) Spastic gait (corticospinal tract involvement). (2) Hand clumsiness (myelopathy hand — corticospinal + dorsal column). (3) Inverted radial reflex (pathognomonic for C5–C6 myelopathy): LMN at C5–C6 abolishes brachioradialis reflex, but UMN below allows spread to C8 finger flexors. (4) Hyperreflexia + Babinski (UMN). (5) Vibration loss (dorsal column involvement). (6) Absence of fasciculations helps against ALS.
mJOA Severity: Likely moderate (12–14) based on impaired hand function, gait difficulty, but still ambulatory and independent.
Surgical approach: Multi-level (C3–C7) = posterior approach preferred. Options: (1) Laminoplasty (if lordotic alignment, no instability — preserves motion); (2) Laminectomy + lateral mass fusion (if kyphosis or instability). Anterior approach (ACCF or multi-level ACDF) less favorable for 5-level disease (higher complication rate). Intraoperative neuromonitoring (SSEPs + MEPs) essential. Post-op: collar for 6–12 weeks if fusion, immediate mobilization. Expected improvement: 50–70% show mJOA improvement with surgery, though complete recovery is uncommon.

11.3 Inflammatory & Infectious Myelopathies

11.3.1 Transverse Myelitis (TM) — Overview & Differential Diagnosis

Transverse myelitis is an inflammatory disorder of the spinal cord characterized by acute/subacute bilateral (though often asymmetric) motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction. The Proposed Diagnostic Criteria for TM (Transverse Myelitis Consortium Working Group 2002) require: (1) bilateral sensory/motor/autonomic dysfunction attributable to spinal cord; (2) clear sensory level; (3) evidence of inflammation (CSF pleocytosis, elevated IgG index, or Gd enhancement on MRI); (4) progression to nadir between 4 hours and 21 days; (5) exclusion of compressive etiology.

Transverse Myelitis — Etiologic Classification
CategorySpecific DiagnosesKey Distinguishing Features
Demyelinating / AutoimmuneMS, NMOSD, MOGAD, Sarcoidosis, SLE, Sjögren, Behçet, ADEMMRI pattern, antibodies, systemic features differentiate
Idiopathic TMDiagnosis of exclusion after comprehensive workup negative~15–30% of acute TM cases. Monophasic course expected. Partial or complete myelitis. May represent forme fruste of antibody-negative autoimmune myelitis
InfectiousViral (VZV, HSV-2, EBV, CMV, HTLV-1, HIV, enterovirus, WNV), bacterial (TB, syphilis, Lyme, Brucella), parasitic (schistosomiasis, Toxoplasma, cysticercosis), fungal (Aspergillus)Immunosuppressed patients, endemic exposure, CSF cultures/PCR. Acute flaccid myelitis (enterovirus D68, polio): anterior horn
Para-/Post-infectiousPost-viral TM (1–4 weeks after infection), post-vaccination (rare)Molecular mimicry mechanism. Often monophasic. ADEM if multifocal brain + cord involvement
ParaneoplasticAnti-CRMP5/CV2, anti-amphiphysin, anti-GFAP (meningoencephalomyelitis)Cancer screening essential. Longitudinally extensive. Often poor prognosis

Initial Workup for Acute Myelopathy

InvestigationPurpose
MRI spine with Gd (entire spine if possible)Exclude compression (urgent). Characterize lesion: length (<3 vs ≥3 vertebral segments), location (central, dorsal, anterior, peripheral), enhancement pattern, expansion
MRI brain with GdIf brain lesions present: MS (periventricular, Dawson fingers), NMOSD (area postrema, periaqueductal, diencephalic), MOGAD (fluffy, cortical), ADEM (large poorly demarcated), sarcoid (leptomeningeal, nodular)
CSF analysisCell count (pleocytosis: lymphocytic = autoimmune; neutrophilic early = NMOSD, infection; eosinophilic = parasitic, sarcoid). Protein, glucose. Oligoclonal bands (MS). IgG index. Cytology. Cultures, PCR panel (HSV, VZV, EBV, CMV, enterovirus). VDRL. AFB/TB culture
Serum antibodiesAQP4-IgG (NMOSD — cell-based assay preferred, sensitivity ~77%, specificity >99%). MOG-IgG (MOGAD — cell-based assay only; live CBA preferred). ANA, dsDNA, SSA/SSB, ACE, ANCA
AdditionalB12, copper, zinc, HIV, HTLV-1/2, RPR, Lyme serologies, chest CT (sarcoid), CT C/A/P (malignancy if paraneoplastic suspected). Visual evoked potentials (subclinical optic neuritis = MS/NMOSD)

11.3.2 MS-Related Myelitis

FeatureDetails
MRI characteristicsShort-segment TM (<3 vertebral segments), typically cervical. Peripheral/dorsolateral location (posterior columns, lateral corticospinal tract). Partial cross-section (usually <50% cord area). No or mild cord swelling. Multiple lesions over time. T1 hypointensity (chronic “black holes”). May show ring enhancement in active lesions
ClinicalAsymmetric, partial myelitis. Lhermitte sign (40–50% of MS myelitis). Sensory > motor at presentation. Uhthoff phenomenon (worsening with heat). Typically: unilateral or asymmetric leg weakness, hemisensory level, paresthesias. Brown-Séquard-like presentation more common than complete TM
CSFOligoclonal bands positive in >95% of MS. Mild pleocytosis (usually <50 cells). Normal or mildly elevated protein. IgG index elevated
PrognosisGenerally good recovery from individual attacks. Risk of second attack (and MS diagnosis) depends on brain MRI lesions and OCBs
TreatmentAcute: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days. PLEX for steroid-refractory attacks. DMT initiation for MS prevention of further attacks (see Section 4)

11.3.3 NMOSD Myelitis

Neuromyelitis Optica Spectrum Disorder (NMOSD) — Myelitis Features
FeatureDetails
PathogenesisAQP4-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibody) binds astrocyte foot processes → complement-dependent cytotoxicity → astrocytopathy → secondary demyelination + necrosis. AQP4 concentrated at perivascular end-feet, subpial, subependymal regions
MRI characteristicsLongitudinally extensive TM (LETM): ≥3 contiguous vertebral segments (hallmark, sensitivity ~75%). Centrally located (gray matter > white matter). Bright spotty lesions on T2 (cavitation/necrosis). Cord swelling (often marked). Sagittal: “pencil-thin” or “rail-track” enhancement. Ring enhancement may mimic tumor. In chronic phase: cord atrophy (“pencil-thin cord”). Conus involvement more common than MS
Clinical severitySevere attacks: complete or near-complete TM with dense paraplegia, sensory level, urinary retention. Bilateral involvement typical. Painful spasms (tonic spasms — characteristic of NMOSD). Nausea/vomiting/hiccups (area postrema syndrome often accompanies or precedes myelitis). Each attack causes more disability than MS (but inter-attack period stable)
CSFPleocytosis often >50 cells (can be neutrophil-predominant early — distinguishes from MS). Elevated protein. OCBs absent in ~80% (vs >95% positive in MS). GFAP elevated (astrocyte damage marker)
Treatment — AcuteIV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days. Early PLEX (plasmapheresis) if no response in 3–5 days — or consider upfront in severe attacks (5–7 exchanges). PLEX effective because AQP4-IgG is directly pathogenic. Do NOT use fingolimod, natalizumab, or other MS DMTs (can worsen NMOSD)
Treatment — PreventionMonoclonal antibodies (FDA-approved): (1) Eculizumab (anti-C5 complement; PREVENT trial 2019: ARR 0.02 vs 0.35; NNT=3). (2) Inebilizumab (anti-CD19 B-cell depleting; N-MOmentum 2019: 73% reduction in attacks). (3) Satralizumab (anti-IL-6R; SAkuraSky 2019, SAkuraStar 2020: 55–74% reduction). Off-label: rituximab (anti-CD20), azathioprine, mycophenolate mofetil

11.3.4 MOGAD Myelitis

FeatureDetails
AntibodyMOG-IgG (myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein). Must use cell-based assay (CBA) — ELISA unreliable (high false positive). Live CBA > fixed CBA. Re-test if initially negative and clinical suspicion high
DemographicsBimodal: children (ADEM-like presentation) and young adults. Male = female (unlike NMOSD female predominance). Any ethnicity (NMOSD: non-white predominance)
Myelitis patternLETM (like NMOSD) but often involves conus/lower thoracic cord (distinctive). Gray matter-predominant (central/H-shaped T2 signal). Can be short-segment. Less cavitary necrosis than NMOSD (less destructive). Cord swelling present. Enhancement: patchy, sometimes leptomeningeal
Key clinical differences from NMOSD(1) Better recovery per attack (less necrotic). (2) Optic neuritis often bilateral with optic disc edema (papillitis). (3) ADEM phenotype in children. (4) Relapsing course in ~50% (especially if MOG-IgG persistently positive). (5) Conus/cauda equina involvement more common. (6) Seizures and cortical encephalitis phenotype
CSFPleocytosis (can be >100 cells, neutrophils possible). OCBs usually absent or transient. Elevated protein
TreatmentAcute: IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days + prolonged oral taper (6–12 months — MOGAD flares on rapid steroid taper). IVIg for steroid-refractory or as maintenance. PLEX for severe. Maintenance: IVIg (monthly), rituximab, azathioprine, mycophenolate. No FDA-approved therapy yet. Proposed MOGAD diagnostic criteria (Banwell et al. Lancet Neurol 2023): core clinical + MOG-IgG positive (CBA) + supportive features
Clinical Pearl — NMOSD vs MOGAD vs MS Myelitis at a Glance: (1) MS: short-segment (<3 VB), peripheral/dorsolateral, partial TM, OCB+, chronic lesions/atrophy. (2) NMOSD: LETM (≥3 VB), central, severe/necrotic, AQP4+, OCB−, bright spotty lesions, cord atrophy. (3) MOGAD: LETM or short, central/H-shaped, conus predilection, less necrotic, good recovery, MOG+, OCB−. A patient with LETM who is AQP4-negative should ALWAYS be tested for MOG-IgG. If both negative, consider sarcoid, paraneoplastic, and idiopathic TM.

11.3.5 Sarcoid Myelopathy

FeatureDetails
EpidemiologyNeurosarcoidosis in ~5–10% of sarcoidosis patients. Myelopathy in ~10% of neurosarcoidosis (overall rare but important). Young to middle-aged adults. African American predominance
MRI patternLongitudinally extensive (often >3 VB, can span entire cord). Dorsal subpial enhancement (“trident sign” on axial: dorsal column + lateral column enhancement). Central canal enhancement. Persistent enhancement despite treatment (unlike MS). Leptomeningeal enhancement
DiagnosisTissue biopsy gold standard (non-caseating granulomas). Serum ACE (sensitivity ~60%, specificity poor). Chest CT (bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy in 50–90%). CSF: pleocytosis, elevated protein, low glucose (25%), elevated ACE (sensitivity ~55%). FDG-PET: identifies occult systemic disease for biopsy targets. Conjunctival biopsy (positive in 30–40% even without visible nodules)
TreatmentFirst-line: IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days → high-dose oral prednisone (1 mg/kg) with very slow taper over 6–12+ months. Steroid-sparing: mycophenolate mofetil (preferred), methotrexate, azathioprine. Refractory: infliximab (anti-TNFα; strong evidence in neurosarcoidosis, especially meningeal and myelopathic disease). Duration: prolonged (years to indefinite)

11.3.6 Infectious Myelopathies

Infectious Myelitis — Key Pathogens
PathogenMyelopathy TypeKey FeaturesTreatment
HTLV-1 (HAM/TSP)Slowly progressive spastic paraparesisEndemic: Japan, Caribbean, sub-Saharan Africa, South America. Sexual/blood/breast milk transmission. Insidious onset over months to years. Thoracic cord predilection. UMN signs in legs, bladder dysfunction, mild sensory involvement. CSF: lymphocytes, HTLV-1 antibodies. MRI: thoracic cord atrophy, sometimes T2 signalNo curative treatment. Corticosteroids may slow progression. Antiretrovirals not effective (not actively replicating). Symptomatic: baclofen for spasticity, oxybutynin for bladder. Interferon-α (some studies)
HIV-associated vacuolar myelopathyProgressive spastic paraparesis (resembles SCD)AIDS-defining (CD4 <200). Posterior + lateral column involvement (vacuolar degeneration). Slowly progressive. Often coexists with HIV-associated peripheral neuropathy. MRI: may be normal or show subtle T2 changesART (antiretroviral therapy) may stabilize. B12 supplementation (HIV patients often B12 deficient). Symptomatic treatment
VZV myelitisAcute TM, may follow zoster by 1–3 weeksDermatomal zoster (may be absent in immunocompromised). Vasculitis component (small vessel). MRI: short or long segment. CSF: VZV PCR (sensitivity ~50–70%) + VZV IgG/IgM in CSF (intrathecal antibody production more sensitive than PCR)IV acyclovir 10–15 mg/kg q8h × 10–14 days. Consider adjunctive corticosteroids for vasculitic component. Prognosis: variable, often incomplete recovery
HSV-2 myelitis/radiculitisElsberg syndrome (sacral myeloradiculitis)Lumbosacral radiculitis, urinary retention, saddle anesthesia. Often recurrent (especially in immunosuppressed). Cauda equina/conus. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, HSV-2 PCR positiveIV acyclovir, then oral valacyclovir suppression for recurrence
SchistosomiasisConus/cauda equina myelitisMost common parasitic cause of TM worldwide. S. mansoni and S. haematobium. Endemic: sub-Saharan Africa, Southeast Asia, South America. Ova deposit in epidural/peri-medullary veins (Batson plexus). Conus medullaris predilection. MRI: lower cord/conus expansion with heterogeneous enhancement (“arrowhead sign”). Travel history criticalPraziquantel (kills adult worms) + corticosteroids (anti-inflammatory, reduces granulomatous reaction). Early treatment = better outcome
TuberculosisPott disease (vertebral), epidural abscess, arachnoiditis, intramedullary tuberculomaVertebral body collapse (gibbus deformity), epidural abscess with cord compression. Arachnoiditis: CSF block, clumped nerve roots. Intramedullary tuberculoma: ring-enhancing lesion. Endemic areas. CSF: lymphocytic, low glucose, high protein, AFB stain (low sensitivity), GeneXpertAnti-TB therapy (RIPE) + adjunctive dexamethasone (reduces arachnoiditis). Surgical decompression for abscess/instability. Duration: 12–18 months for CNS TB
Syphilis (tabes dorsalis)Posterior column degeneration (tertiary syphilis)Lightning pains (lancinating), sensory ataxia, Argyll Robertson pupils (accommodate but don’t react to light), Romberg positive, areflexia, Charcot joints. CSF: positive VDRL/FTA-ABSIV penicillin G 18–24 MU/day × 10–14 days (neurosyphilis protocol). Damage often irreversible at this stage
Clinical Pearl — Acute Flaccid Myelitis (AFM): Distinct from typical TM. Presents with acute LMN pattern (flaccid paralysis, areflexia) predominantly in children. Anterior horn cell involvement (poliomyelitis-like). Most commonly associated with enterovirus D68. MRI: longitudinal gray matter T2 lesion, predominantly anterior horns (“owl eyes” or “snake eyes” on axial). CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, EV-D68 PCR may be negative in CSF (test nasopharyngeal/stool). Treatment: supportive (IVIg and steroids of uncertain benefit). CDC reportable condition. Poor motor recovery in many cases.
Q: A 28-year-old African American woman presents with rapidly progressive bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and a sensory level at T6 over 5 days. MRI shows T2 hyperintensity spanning T2–T8 with patchy enhancement and mild cord swelling. Brain MRI shows subtle leptomeningeal enhancement. CSF: 80 WBC (85% lymphocytes), protein 120 mg/dL, glucose 35 mg/dL (serum 100). AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG are negative. Chest CT shows bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. What is the most likely diagnosis and management?
Diagnosis: Neurosarcoidosis with spinal cord involvement (sarcoid myelopathy).
Supporting evidence: (1) LETM (≥3 vertebral segments: T2–T8). (2) Leptomeningeal enhancement on brain MRI (classic for neurosarcoidosis). (3) CSF: marked pleocytosis with low glucose (25% of neurosarcoid has low CSF glucose — uncommon in MS/NMOSD/MOGAD). (4) Bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy on chest CT (cardinal feature of pulmonary sarcoidosis). (5) Young African American woman (demographic at risk). (6) AQP4 and MOG negative, ruling out NMOSD and MOGAD.
Additional workup: (1) Serum ACE (may be elevated, but sensitivity ~60%). (2) Serum calcium, 24-hour urine calcium. (3) Tissue biopsy for non-caseating granulomas (mediastinal lymph node biopsy via EBUS preferred — least invasive with hilar LAD present). (4) CSF ACE. (5) FDG-PET if no accessible biopsy target. (6) Rule out TB (AFB cultures, QuantiFERON — caseating vs non-caseating granulomas).
Management:
1. IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 5 days (acute attack)
2. Transition to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with very slow taper over 6–12 months (neurosarcoid relapses on rapid taper)
3. Start steroid-sparing agent early: mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg BID (preferred) or methotrexate
4. If refractory to steroids + MMF: infliximab (anti-TNF-α) — strong evidence for neurosarcoidosis, especially myelopathy
5. Monitor: serial MRI spine, clinical assessments, ACE levels (imperfect marker)
6. PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX if on high-dose steroids + immunosuppression

11.4 Vascular & Metabolic Myelopathies

11.4.1 Spinal Cord Infarction

Spinal cord infarction accounts for only ~1–2% of all strokes but has devastating consequences. The anterior spinal artery territory is preferentially affected due to watershed vulnerability.

Spinal Cord Vascular Anatomy & Infarction Syndromes
FeatureDetails
Arterial supplyAnterior spinal artery (ASA): single midline vessel, supplies anterior 2/3 of cord (corticospinal tracts, spinothalamic tracts, anterior horns). Posterior spinal arteries (PSA): paired, supply posterior 1/3 (dorsal columns). Fed by radiculomedullary arteries (6–8 anterior, 10–20 posterior). Artery of Adamkiewicz: largest anterior radiculomedullary artery, enters left side T9–T12 in 75% of cases, supplies lower 2/3 of cord. Watershed zones: T4–T8 (between superior and Adamkiewicz territories) = most vulnerable to ischemia
Anterior spinal artery syndromeMost common spinal cord infarction pattern. Acute onset (minutes to hours): bilateral motor paralysis (corticospinal) + bilateral spinothalamic loss (pain/temp) below level. PRESERVED dorsal columns (vibration, proprioception, light touch). Bladder dysfunction. Bilateral LMN signs at level of infarction (anterior horn involvement). Flaccid initially (spinal shock) → spastic later
Posterior spinal artery syndromeRare in isolation. Bilateral dorsal column loss (proprioception, vibration). Sensory ataxia. Preserved motor and pain/temp. Often partial/asymmetric
Central/sulcal artery infarctionUnilateral anterior cord infarction (Brown-Séquard pattern). Sulcal arteries arise from ASA and alternate left/right supply to each hemicord

Etiology & Risk Factors

CauseDetails
Aortic surgery/proceduresMost common identified cause (up to 30% of cases). Thoracoabdominal aortic aneurysm repair (open > endovascular). Cross-clamping interrupts intercostal/lumbar arteries feeding Adamkiewicz. Risk: 2–10% for open repair. Prevention: CSF drainage, evoked potential monitoring, sequential clamping, permissive hypertension
Aortic pathologyAortic dissection (especially type B involving descending aorta). Aortic atherosclerosis. Cholesterol embolization (fibrocartilaginous embolism)
Hypotension/systemicCardiac arrest, severe hypotension (watershed infarction at T4–T8). Sickle cell disease
Fibrocartilaginous embolism (FCE)Nucleus pulposus material embolizes to spinal vessels. Typically young patients after minor trauma/Valsalva. Acute myelopathy with no vascular risk factors. MRI: cord infarction adjacent to disc level. Pathological diagnosis (often presumptive clinically)
VasculitisSLE, PAN, sarcoid, syphilis, ANCA-associated
Hypercoagulable statesAntiphospholipid syndrome, protein C/S deficiency, polycythemia vera
Venous infarctionSpinal dural AVF (congestive myelopathy), epidural abscess with venous compromise

Diagnosis & Management

AspectDetails
MRI findingsT2 hyperintensity in ASA territory. DWI restriction (acute phase, like cerebral stroke). “Owl eyes” or “snake eyes” on axial T2 (bilateral anterior horn involvement). “Pencil-like” hyperintensity on sagittal (longitudinally extensive, anterior). Vertebral body infarction may coexist (shared blood supply). MRI may be negative first 24h; repeat if clinically suspicious
InvestigationsCTA/MRA aorta (dissection, aneurysm). Echocardiography (cardiac source). Hypercoagulable workup. ESR/CRP/ANA/ANCA (vasculitis). CSF: usually normal or mild protein elevation (no pleocytosis, unlike inflammatory myelopathy)
TreatmentNo proven specific treatment (unlike cerebral stroke, no thrombolysis evidence for spinal cord infarction). Supportive: MAP augmentation (avoid hypotension), lumbar CSF drainage (reduces pressure, may improve perfusion — especially post-aortic surgery), anticoagulation if embolic source or dissection. Treat underlying cause. Rehabilitation critical. DVT prophylaxis. Prognosis: ~50% remain non-ambulatory; 30% achieve independent ambulation

11.4.2 Spinal Dural Arteriovenous Fistula (dAVF)

Spinal Dural AVF — The “Great Mimicker” of Myelopathy
FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyAbnormal arteriovenous connection in dural sleeve of nerve root. Arterial pressure transmitted to coronal venous plexus → venous hypertension → reduced arteriolar perfusion pressure → chronic congestive myelopathy (NOT hemorrhagic). The cord is injured by chronic ischemia/edema, not hemorrhage
EpidemiologyMost common spinal vascular malformation (60–80%). Middle-aged to elderly men (5:1 M:F). Acquired lesion. Lower thoracolumbar location (T6–L2 most common)
Classification (Anson & Spetzler)Type I (dural AVF): most common, slow flow, ventral intradural vein. Type II (glomus AVM): intramedullary, nidus in cord, high-flow. Type III (juvenile/metameric AVM): extensive, intra- + extradural. Type IV (intradural perimedullary AVF): on cord surface
Clinical presentationProgressive myelopathy (stepwise or slowly progressive over months to years). Lower extremity weakness + numbness ascending. Foix-Alajouanine syndrome (subacute necrotizing myelopathy): rapidly progressive course with necrosis. Bladder/bowel dysfunction. Pain (radicular or diffuse). Symptoms may worsen with exercise (increased venous pressure). Diagnosis often delayed 1–3 years (misdiagnosed as inflammatory myelopathy, lumbar stenosis, peripheral neuropathy)
MRI findingsT2 hyperintensity (edema) in conus/lower thoracic cord, often extensive (≥3 segments). Flow voids on cord surface (dilated perimedullary veins — pathognomonic but absent in 30–50% on conventional MRI). Cord expansion (edema). Contrast enhancement (chronic breakdown of blood-spinal cord barrier). Key: T2 signal + flow voids in an older male = think dAVF
DiagnosisSpinal angiography (DSA) = gold standard. Must catheterize ALL feeding arteries (T4–S2). MRA (time-resolved contrast-enhanced) can screen and localize fistula level. Identifies the feeding artery and draining vein
TreatmentSurgical disconnection of the draining vein (intradural) — preferred, definitive, high success rate (>95% obliteration). Endovascular embolization with Onyx or NBCA: less invasive but higher recurrence rate (15–30%). Must occlude the proximal draining vein/fistula point. Outcomes: stabilization in most; improvement in ~50–70% (motor > bladder > sensory). Earlier treatment = better outcome (established cord necrosis is irreversible)
Clinical Pearl — When to Suspect Spinal dAVF: Consider this diagnosis in any patient with: (1) Progressive myelopathy in an older male without clear compressive/inflammatory cause. (2) Lower thoracic/conus T2 signal that seems “too extensive” for the degree of cord compression. (3) Myelopathy that worsens with exercise. (4) Flow voids on MRI surface of cord. (5) Elevated CSF protein without cells (congestive). (6) Failed treatment for presumed “inflammatory myelopathy.” Missing this diagnosis is devastating because it is surgically curable but causes irreversible cord damage if untreated. When in doubt, order a spinal MRA or proceed to spinal angiography.

11.4.3 Metabolic Myelopathies

Subacute Combined Degeneration (SCD) — Vitamin B12 Deficiency
FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyB12 (cobalamin) is essential for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. Deficiency → impaired myelin synthesis (SAM pathway) + toxic accumulation of methylmalonic acid and homocysteine. Affects posterior columns (fasciculus gracilis first, then cuneatus) and lateral corticospinal tracts. Can also cause peripheral neuropathy and optic neuropathy
Causes of B12 deficiency(1) Pernicious anemia (autoimmune gastritis, anti-intrinsic factor antibodies; most common cause in developed countries). (2) Dietary (strict vegans). (3) Malabsorption: celiac, Crohn’s, bariatric surgery, chronic PPI use. (4) Nitrous oxide abuse (“whippets” — rapidly precipitates SCD even with borderline B12 levels by oxidizing cobalt in B12). (5) Fish tapeworm (Diphyllobothrium latum)
Clinical features(1) Sensory ataxia (loss of proprioception/vibration — dorsal columns). (2) Spastic paraparesis (lateral corticospinal tracts). (3) Romberg positive. (4) Paradox: absent ankle jerks + upgoing plantar responses (combined peripheral neuropathy + myelopathy). (5) Peripheral neuropathy (stocking-glove numbness/paresthesias). (6) Cognitive impairment, dementia, psychiatric symptoms. (7) Megaloblastic anemia (but neurological damage can occur WITHOUT anemia)
MRIT2 hyperintensity in posterior columns (“inverted V sign” or “reversed rabbit ears” on axial). Cervical and upper thoracic cord. May extend to lateral columns. No enhancement. Cord may be normal-sized or mildly swollen acutely
DiagnosisSerum B12 (<200 pg/mL definite; 200–400 borderline). Methylmalonic acid (MMA): elevated (most specific — check when B12 borderline). Homocysteine: elevated (less specific). Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (specific for pernicious anemia). Anti-parietal cell antibodies. CBC: macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils
TreatmentIM cyanocobalamin 1000 μg daily × 7 days, then weekly × 4 weeks, then monthly for life (pernicious anemia). Oral high-dose B12 (1000–2000 μg/day) may be adequate for dietary deficiency. Neurological recovery depends on duration: <6 months of symptoms = good recovery; >12 months = often permanent deficits. CRITICAL: Do not give folate alone without B12 (corrects anemia but worsens/precipitates neurological damage)

Copper Deficiency Myelopathy

FeatureDetails
Clinical presentationClinically and radiologically identical to SCD from B12 deficiency (posterior + lateral column involvement). Progressive sensory ataxia + spastic paraparesis. Often coexists with peripheral neuropathy (axonal sensorimotor)
Causes(1) Excess zinc supplementation (most common; zinc induces metallothionein in enterocytes which binds copper preferentially, blocking absorption). (2) Post-gastric surgery. (3) Malabsorption (celiac). (4) Excessive denture cream use (contains zinc). (5) Idiopathic
DiagnosisLow serum copper (<75 μg/dL), low ceruloplasmin. Check zinc level (elevated zinc explains copper deficiency). CBC: anemia, neutropenia (cytopenia often precedes neurological symptoms). MRI: T2 hyperintensity in dorsal columns (indistinguishable from B12 deficiency)
TreatmentOral copper supplementation (copper gluconate 2–8 mg/day) or IV copper (2 mg/day × 5 days if severe). Stop zinc source. Neurological recovery variable (stabilization expected; full recovery uncommon if delayed diagnosis)

Other Metabolic/Toxic Myelopathies

ConditionKey Features
Nitrous oxide (N2O) myelopathyOxidizes cobalt in B12 → functional B12 deficiency → SCD. Can occur with normal B12 levels. Recreational use (“whippets”) increasingly common in young adults. Acute/subacute posterior column + corticospinal tract dysfunction. Treatment: stop N2O, high-dose B12, methionine supplementation. MMA/homocysteine elevated despite normal B12
Vitamin E deficiencyPosterior column + spinocerebellar tract degeneration. Mimics Friedreich ataxia. Causes: abetalipoproteinemia (Bassen-Kornzweig), fat malabsorption, cholestatic liver disease. Progressive ataxia, proprioceptive loss, areflexia. Treatment: high-dose vitamin E supplementation
Hepatic myelopathyRare complication of chronic liver disease/portosystemic shunting. Selective corticospinal tract degeneration. Progressive spastic paraparesis. MRI: T1 hyperintensity in globus pallidus (manganese deposition) + subtle cord T2 changes. Treatment: liver transplant (only definitive therapy); portosystemic shunt occlusion may help
LathyrismToxic myelopathy from Lathyrus sativus (grass pea) consumption. Contains BOAA (β-N-oxalylamino-L-alanine), an excitotoxic glutamate analogue. Pure UMN spastic paraparesis. Endemic in Ethiopia, India, Bangladesh during famine. Irreversible. No specific treatment
KonzoAcute spastic paraparesis from dietary cyanide exposure (insufficiently processed cassava) in sub-Saharan Africa. Affects women and children predominantly. Abrupt onset UMN paraparesis during famine conditions. Irreversible

11.4.4 Radiation Myelopathy

PhaseTimingFeaturesManagement
Early-delayed (transient)2–6 months post-RTLhermitte sign (most common presentation). Self-limiting (weeks to months). Due to transient demyelination. No cord signal change on MRI typicallyReassurance. No treatment needed. Resolves spontaneously
Late (progressive)6 months–several years (median 12–18 months)Progressive myelopathy — usually irreversible. Dose-dependent: risk increases significantly >50 Gy (2 Gy/fraction). T2 hyperintensity, enhancement, possible cord atrophy. Pathology: white matter necrosis, vascular damage, demyelinationNo proven treatment. Bevacizumab may help (anecdotal). Hyperbaric oxygen (weak evidence). Prevention: respect cord tolerance (45–50 Gy in conventional fractionation; BED/EQD2 calculations for hypofractionation)

11.4.5 Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia (HSP)

Hereditary Spastic Paraplegia
FeatureDetails
DefinitionGenetically heterogeneous group (>80 SPG loci identified) characterized by progressive spasticity and weakness of the lower extremities due to corticospinal tract degeneration (retrograde, dying-back axonopathy — longest axons affected first)
ClassificationPure (uncomplicated): spastic paraparesis + bladder urgency + mild dorsal column loss (vibration). Complicated: spasticity + additional features (cerebellar ataxia, cognitive impairment, thin corpus callosum, peripheral neuropathy, optic atrophy, retinitis pigmentosa, epilepsy)
Common genetic formsSPG4 (spastin): AD, most common adult pure HSP (~40% AD cases). Variable age of onset. SPG3A (atlastin): AD, most common childhood pure HSP. Early onset (<10 years). SPG7 (paraplegin): AR, complicated (cerebellar ataxia + spasticity). SPG11 (spatacsin): AR, most common complicated HSP (thin corpus callosum, cognitive decline). SPG5 (CYP7B1): AR, may respond to cholesterol pathway modulation
DiagnosisClinical + family history + genetic testing (gene panel or whole exome sequencing). MRI spine: may show thoracic cord atrophy (subtle). MRI brain: thin corpus callosum (SPG11, SPG15), white matter changes, cerebellar atrophy (SPG7). Exclude structural, metabolic, and acquired causes
TreatmentSymptomatic: Baclofen (oral or intrathecal pump for severe spasticity), tizanidine, botulinum toxin (focal spasticity). Physical therapy and stretching (essential). Ankle-foot orthoses. Bladder management (anticholinergics, CIC). No disease-modifying therapy available
Q: A 35-year-old man presents to the ER with acute-onset severe back pain followed within minutes by bilateral leg weakness and inability to void. He had an aortic stent-graft placed 6 hours ago for a type B aortic dissection. Examination shows flaccid paraplegia, absent bilateral lower extremity reflexes, loss of pain/temperature bilaterally below T10, but PRESERVED vibration and proprioception in both feet. What is the diagnosis, expected MRI findings, and immediate management?
Diagnosis: Anterior spinal artery syndrome (spinal cord infarction) — complicating aortic stent-graft procedure.
Mechanism: The stent-graft likely occluded intercostal/lumbar arteries feeding the artery of Adamkiewicz (the major radiculomedullary artery supplying the lower 2/3 of the cord, typically arising T9–T12 on the left). This results in ischemia of the anterior 2/3 of the cord (anterior spinal artery territory).
Clinical localization: (1) Bilateral motor paralysis (corticospinal tracts — flaccid acutely due to spinal shock). (2) Bilateral loss of pain/temperature below T10 (spinothalamic tracts). (3) PRESERVED dorsal columns (vibration, proprioception) — posterior spinal arteries have separate blood supply and are paired with better collateral flow. (4) Urinary retention (autonomic/sacral involvement). This pattern is pathognomonic for anterior spinal artery syndrome.
Expected MRI findings: (1) T2 hyperintensity in anterior cord (sagittal: “pencil-like” longitudinal signal). (2) DWI restriction (confirms acute ischemia). (3) “Owl eyes” or “snake eyes” on axial T2 (bilateral anterior horn involvement). (4) Possible vertebral body infarction at same level (shared blood supply). Note: MRI may be negative in first 12–24 hours.
Immediate management:
1. MAP augmentation: maintain MAP ≥85–90 mmHg (vasopressors if needed) to maximize spinal cord perfusion
2. Lumbar CSF drainage: place lumbar drain, target CSF pressure <10 cmH2O (reduces intraspinal pressure, improves perfusion gradient — established protocol in post-aortic surgery cord ischemia)
3. Avoid hypotension at all costs
4. Urgent MRI spine + MRA aorta (confirm diagnosis, assess aortic repair)
5. DVT prophylaxis, bladder catheterization, pressure ulcer prevention
6. No role for systemic thrombolysis in spinal cord infarction (no evidence, bleeding risk post-procedure)
7. Prognosis: guarded; ~50% remain non-ambulatory. CSF drainage within first 6–12 hours post-onset has best chance of benefit

11.5 Cauda Equina & Conus Medullaris Syndromes

11.5.1 Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES) vs. Conus Medullaris Syndrome

Distinguishing these two syndromes is critical for localization and urgency of intervention. They result from pathology at different levels but can overlap (epiconus/conus-cauda lesions).

CES vs. Conus Medullaris Syndrome — Key Distinctions
FeatureCauda Equina SyndromeConus Medullaris Syndrome
AnatomyNerve roots below conus (L2–S5 roots in lumbar cistern)Conus medullaris (terminal spinal cord, typically at L1–L2)
Lesion typeLMN only (peripheral nerve roots)UMN + LMN mixed (cord terminates here; sacral segments S2–S5 in conus)
OnsetOften gradual (unless acute disc herniation)Often sudden
PainSevere radicular pain (prominent, often bilateral sciatic)Less prominent pain; more perineal/bilateral
MotorAsymmetric LMN weakness in L2–S2 distribution. Areflexia. AtrophySymmetric mild weakness. UMN signs may be present (if epiconus involved: L4–S1). Babinski may be present
SensorySaddle anesthesia (S2–S5 dermatomal). Asymmetric. Radicular patternSaddle anesthesia (perianal, perineal). More symmetric. Dissociated sensory loss possible (central cord pattern at conus)
Bladder/BowelUrinary retention (atonic bladder — LMN, overflow incontinence). Constipation. Reduced anal tone. Often lateEarly bladder/bowel dysfunction (UMN or LMN pattern depending on level). Urinary retention or incontinence. Sexual dysfunction prominent. Reduced anal tone
ReflexesAbsent knee/ankle jerks (depending on root level). Bulbocavernosus reflex absentAnkle jerks absent (S1–S2). Knee jerks may be preserved. Bulbocavernosus reflex absent. Babinski may be present (epiconus)
SymmetryAsymmetric (individual roots affected variably)Symmetric (bilateral cord structure)

Cauda Equina Syndrome — Etiology & Emergency Management

AspectDetails
Common causes(1) Large central disc herniation L4–L5 or L5–S1 (most common surgical cause). (2) Spinal stenosis (degenerative or congenital). (3) Tumors: ependymoma (myxopapillary, filum terminale), schwannoma, metastases, lymphoma. (4) Epidural abscess/hematoma. (5) Trauma (burst fracture). (6) Arachnoiditis (post-surgical, post-infection, post-intrathecal injection)
CES classificationCES-Incomplete (CES-I): urinary difficulties (altered sensation, poor stream, need to strain) but still has some bladder control. CES-Retention (CES-R): painless urinary retention with overflow incontinence. CES-R has worse prognosis and requires more urgent decompression
Red flags (when to suspect)Bilateral sciatica + saddle numbness + urinary difficulty/retention + reduced anal tone. Any patient with acute LBP + new urinary retention = MRI urgently to rule out CES
ImagingUrgent MRI lumbosacral spine (gold standard). If MRI unavailable: CT myelography. Shows compression, disc sequestration, tumor, abscess
Surgical timingSurgical emergency. Decompressive laminectomy/discectomy. Traditional teaching: within 48 hours. Meta-analysis (Ahn et al. J Neurosurg Spine 2010): surgery within 48 hours showed better bladder outcomes than delayed. Within 24 hours preferred, and emerging evidence suggests even earlier (<24h) may be better for CES-R. Key principle: earlier = better; don’t wait for a “complete” syndrome to develop before operating
PrognosisCES-I (incomplete): good recovery expected with early surgery (~70–90% significant improvement). CES-R (retention): recovery less predictable; 30–50% have residual bladder dysfunction. Sexual dysfunction often persists. Duration of symptoms before surgery is strongest predictor of outcome

11.5.2 Syringomyelia

Syringomyelia — Comprehensive Review
FeatureDetails
DefinitionFluid-filled cavity (syrinx) within the spinal cord parenchyma. Syringomyelia = syrinx in cord; Syringobulbia = extends into brainstem (medulla)
PathophysiologyDisruption of normal CSF dynamics at the craniocervical junction or spinal canal → fluid pulsation drives CSF into cord parenchyma. Theories: (1) Williams’ suck/slosh theory (pressure dissociation across obstruction); (2) Oldfield’s piston theory (tonsillar pulsation drives CSF into cord). Result: progressive central cord cavitation and expansion
EtiologyChiari I malformation (most common association; tonsillar ectopia ≥5 mm below foramen magnum). Post-traumatic (20–30% of SCI patients develop syrinx, months to years later). Tethered cord. Arachnoiditis (post-meningitis, post-surgery). Intramedullary tumor (ependymoma, hemangioblastoma — tumor-associated syrinx). Idiopathic
Clinical featuresCervical/cervicothoracic predilection. (1) “Cape-like” dissociated sensory loss: loss of pain/temperature over shoulders, arms, upper trunk (spinothalamic fibers crossing in anterior commissure are disrupted). Preserved light touch/proprioception initially. (2) LMN signs at level: hand weakness and atrophy (intrinsic hand muscles), areflexia. (3) UMN signs below (legs). (4) Horner syndrome (if syrinx involves C8–T2 intermediolateral column). (5) Neuropathic pain. (6) Scoliosis (especially in pediatric Chiari + syrinx). Progression typically slow (years)
MRI findingsCSF-isointense tubular cavity within cord. T1 low signal, T2 high signal (same as CSF). Cord expansion at level of syrinx. Look for associated pathology: Chiari malformation (tonsillar ectopia), tethered cord, tumor (enhancing component within or at margin of syrinx = tumor). Post-contrast MRI essential to exclude intramedullary tumor
TreatmentTreat the underlying cause: (1) Chiari I + syrinx: posterior fossa decompression (suboccipital craniectomy + C1 laminectomy ± duraplasty). Syrinx resolves/stabilizes in 80%. (2) Post-traumatic: syrinx shunting (syringosubarachnoid or syringoperitoneal shunt) if progressive; arachnolysis. (3) Tumor-associated: tumor resection usually resolves syrinx. (4) Tethered cord: untethering. Observation appropriate for stable, asymptomatic small syrinx

11.5.3 Chiari Malformations

TypeDefinitionClinical FeaturesManagement
Chiari ICerebellar tonsil herniation ≥5 mm below foramen magnum. Congenital/developmental (small posterior fossa). Most common type encountered clinicallyOften discovered incidentally. Symptomatic: suboccipital headache (exacerbated by Valsalva, coughing), neck pain, syringomyelia symptoms (60–70% have associated syrinx), lower cranial neuropathies (dysphagia, sleep apnea), cerebellar signs, downbeat nystagmus (localizing sign)Observation if asymptomatic + no syrinx. Posterior fossa decompression (PFD) for symptomatic Chiari I or progressive syrinx. PFD: suboccipital craniectomy + C1 (sometimes C2) laminectomy ± duraplasty. Headache improves in 80–90%. Syrinx resolves in 60–80%
Chiari II (Arnold-Chiari)Tonsils + vermis + brainstem herniation through foramen magnum. Almost always associated with myelomeningoceleDiagnosed prenatally/neonatally with spina bifida. Hydrocephalus (nearly universal — VP shunt). Brainstem compression. Cranial nerve dysfunction. ApneaVP shunt for hydrocephalus. PFD if brainstem symptomatic. Myelomeningocele repair (prenatal: MOMS trial showed benefit of prenatal vs postnatal repair)
Chiari IIIOccipital or cervical encephalocele containing cerebellar/brainstem tissueRare. Usually fatal or severely disabling. High morbidity from brainstem involvementSurgical repair of encephalocele. Poor prognosis

11.5.4 Tethered Cord Syndrome

FeatureDetails
DefinitionAbnormal fixation of the spinal cord limiting its normal ascent, resulting in traction on the conus. Normal conus position: at or above L1–L2 disc space in adults. Conus below L2–L3 = low-lying conus suggestive of tethering (though “occult tethered cord” may have normal conus position with an abnormally taut filum)
Causes(1) Fatty filum terminale (most common). (2) Prior myelomeningocele repair (scar tethering). (3) Lipomyelomeningocele. (4) Dermal sinus. (5) Split cord malformation (diastematomyelia). (6) Sacral dimple with tract
Clinical featuresPediatric: progressive foot deformity (pes cavus), gait abnormality, scoliosis, bladder dysfunction (enuresis persisting beyond expected age), cutaneous stigmata (hairy patch, dimple, lipoma, hemangioma overlying spine). Adult: back/leg pain (exacerbated by flexion/stretching), progressive neurological deficit, bladder dysfunction. Symptoms may worsen during growth spurts or physical activity
ImagingMRI lumbosacral: low-lying conus (<L2), thickened filum (>2 mm), fatty infiltration of filum (T1 hyperintense), associated anomalies (lipoma, split cord). Note: in “occult tethered cord” conus may be normally positioned but filum is taut and thickened
TreatmentSurgical untethering (section of filum terminale). Low morbidity for fatty filum release. More complex for re-tethering after myelomeningocele repair. Improvement/stabilization in 80–90% for pain and motor; bladder function improvement less reliable
Clinical Pearl — The Myelopathy DDx Approach: When evaluating a patient with progressive myelopathy, systematically consider: (1) Compressive (MRI for structural pathology — spondylosis, tumor, disc, abscess); (2) Inflammatory (MS, NMOSD, MOGAD, sarcoid, TM); (3) Vascular (spinal cord infarction, dAVF); (4) Infectious (HTLV-1, HIV, VZV, TB, syphilis); (5) Metabolic (B12, copper, vitamin E); (6) Genetic (HSP, adrenomyeloneuropathy, Friedreich ataxia); (7) Neoplastic (intramedullary tumor, paraneoplastic); (8) Radiation. A structured approach prevents missed diagnoses. The three most commonly missed myelopathies are: dAVF (surgically curable!), copper deficiency (often from zinc supplements), and HTLV-1 (in endemic populations).
Q: A 14-year-old girl presents with progressive right foot drop, increasing scoliosis, and recent-onset urinary incontinence. Examination shows a hairy patch over the lower lumbar spine, right foot weakness (tibialis anterior 3/5, peronei 3/5), absent right ankle jerk, and decreased perineal sensation. MRI lumbosacral spine shows the conus at L3–L4 level with a thickened, fatty filum terminale. What is the diagnosis and management?
Diagnosis: Tethered cord syndrome due to fatty filum terminale.
Evidence: (1) Low-lying conus at L3–L4 (normal ≤L1–L2 by 3 months of age). (2) Thickened fatty filum on MRI (T1 hyperintense signal in filum, >2 mm diameter). (3) Cutaneous stigmata (hairy patch over lumbosacral spine — a classic marker of occult spinal dysraphism). (4) Progressive neurological deficit: foot drop (L4–L5 radiculopathy from traction), scoliosis (asymmetric tethering), urinary incontinence (sacral nerve dysfunction from conus traction).
Management:
1. Surgical untethering: sectioning of the fatty filum terminale. This is a relatively straightforward procedure with low morbidity. Laminectomy at filum level, identification and division of the thickened filum under intraoperative neuromonitoring (EMG of anal sphincter, lower extremity muscles)
2. Pre-operative urodynamics (baseline bladder function for comparison post-op)
3. Expected outcomes: Pain and motor function stabilize/improve in ~80–90%. Scoliosis may stabilize (less likely to reverse if established). Bladder function improvement variable (less predictable than motor recovery; early intervention before irreversible sacral nerve damage is key)
4. Follow-up: Serial clinical exams + MRI to monitor for re-tethering (especially important in growing children). Scoliosis may need orthopedic co-management
5. Key principle: Operate early in symptomatic tethered cord — neurological deficits are easier to prevent than reverse

11.6 References & Key Guidelines

Key References & Guidelines

SCI & Acute Management

  • Fehlings MG et al. A clinical practice guideline for the management of acute spinal cord injury: introduction, rationale, and scope. Global Spine J 2017;7(3 Suppl):84S–94S. [AO Spine Guidelines]
  • Fehlings MG et al. Early versus delayed decompression for traumatic cervical spinal cord injury: results of the Surgical Timing in Acute Spinal Cord Injury Study (STASCIS). PLoS One 2012;7(2):e32037.
  • Fehlings MG et al. Safety and efficacy of early versus late decompression in traumatic acute cervical spinal cord injury (SCI-POEM): a multicentre prospective cohort study. Lancet Neurol 2021;20(6):449–458.
  • Bracken MB et al. A randomized, controlled trial of methylprednisolone or naloxone in the treatment of acute spinal-cord injury (NASCIS-2). N Engl J Med 1990;322(20):1405–1411.

CSM & Degenerative Spine

  • Fehlings MG et al. A clinical practice guideline for the management of degenerative cervical myelopathy: recommendations for patients with mild, moderate, and severe disease and nonmyelopathic patients with evidence of cord compression. Global Spine J 2017;7(3 Suppl):70S–83S. [AO Spine CSM Guidelines]
  • Weinstein JN et al. Surgical versus nonsurgical therapy for lumbar spinal stenosis. N Engl J Med 2008;358(8):794–810. [SPORT Trial]
  • Patchell RA et al. Direct decompressive surgical resection in the treatment of spinal cord compression caused by metastatic cancer: a randomised trial. Lancet 2005;366(9486):643–648.

Inflammatory Myelopathy

  • Transverse Myelitis Consortium Working Group. Proposed diagnostic criteria and nosology of acute transverse myelitis. Neurology 2002;59(4):499–505.
  • Wingerchuk DM et al. International consensus diagnostic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders. Neurology 2015;85(2):177–189. [IPND 2015 NMOSD Criteria]
  • Pittock SJ et al. Eculizumab in aquaporin-4-positive neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder. N Engl J Med 2019;381(7):614–625. [PREVENT]
  • Cree BAC et al. Inebilizumab for the treatment of neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder. Lancet 2019;394(10206):1352–1363. [N-MOmentum]
  • Yamamura T et al. Trial of satralizumab in neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder. N Engl J Med 2019;381(22):2114–2124. [SAkuraSky]
  • Banwell B et al. Diagnosis of myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody-associated disease: International MOGAD Panel proposed criteria. Lancet Neurol 2023;22(3):268–282.

Vascular Myelopathy

  • Defined by consensus statement. Spinal dural arteriovenous fistula: diagnosis and treatment. Review articles in Lancet Neurol and J Neurosurg provide current management algorithms.
  • Sorensen PS et al. Effect of high-dose dexamethasone in carcinomatous metastatic spinal cord compression treated with radiotherapy: a randomised trial. Eur J Cancer 1994;30A(1):22–27.

Metabolic & Other

  • Stabler SP. Clinical practice: Vitamin B12 deficiency. N Engl J Med 2013;368(2):149–160.
  • Fink JK. Hereditary spastic paraplegia: clinico-pathologic features and emerging molecular mechanisms. Acta Neuropathol 2013;126(3):307–328.
  • Adzick NS et al. A randomized trial of prenatal versus postnatal repair of myelomeningocele. N Engl J Med 2011;364(11):993–1004. [MOMS Trial]

12. Neurological Emergencies

This section provides a comprehensive subspecialist-depth review of neurological emergencies requiring immediate recognition and time-critical intervention. Coverage spans raised intracranial pressure (Monro-Kellie doctrine, cerebral edema types, tiered ICP management, decompressive craniectomy evidence from DESTINY/DECIMAL/HAMLET/RESCUEicp, herniation syndromes), brain death determination (AAN 2010/World Brain Death Project 2020 complete protocol, ancillary testing), coma evaluation (FOUR score, pupillary localisation, respiratory patterns), neuroprognostication after cardiac arrest (ERC/ESICM 2021 multimodal algorithm: SSEP, EEG, NSE, MRI), PRES (pathophysiology, hemorrhagic variants, treatment), hypertensive emergencies by syndrome, neuroleptic malignant syndrome vs serotonin syndrome (mechanism, Hunter criteria, treatment), malignant hyperthermia (dantrolene), acute neuromuscular respiratory failure (20-30-40 rule), myasthenic crisis, GBS, botulism, SAH management (ISAT coiling vs clipping, nimodipine, vasospasm DCI), acute bacterial meningitis (antibiotic timing, dexamethasone protocol, de Gans/van de Beek trial), neurotoxicology (opioid, anticholinergic, cholinergic, TCA, lithium, alcohol withdrawal/Wernicke), viral encephalitis (HSV empirical acyclovir, anti-NMDAR encephalitis), and a comprehensive time-critical emergencies rapid reference table.

12.1 Raised Intracranial Pressure & Herniation Syndromes

12.1.1 Physiology of ICP & Cerebral Perfusion

ICP Physiology — Core Concepts for the Neurointensivist
ConceptDetails
Normal ICP5–15 mmHg in adults (supine). <6 in children. Measured via EVD (gold standard), intraparenchymal monitor (Codman, Camino), or lumbar puncture (if no obstruction). Opening pressure on LP: normal <20 cmH2O (<25 in obese patients)
Monro-Kellie doctrineFixed cranial vault volume = brain parenchyma (~80%) + blood (~10%) + CSF (~10%). Increase in one compartment must be compensated by decrease in another, or ICP rises. Compliance: initially, small volume increases are tolerated (CSF/venous blood displacement). When compliance exhausted, small further volume increases cause dramatic ICP rise (exponential pressure-volume curve)
Cerebral perfusion pressure (CPP)CPP = MAP − ICP. Target: 60–70 mmHg (BTF Guidelines 2016). CPP <50 mmHg = cerebral ischemia. CPP >70 mmHg = risk of ARDS from vasopressor use. Optimal CPP varies by patient (individualized via autoregulation monitoring: PRx, ORx indices)
Cerebral autoregulationCBF remains constant over MAP 50–150 mmHg in healthy adults (autoregulatory plateau). Mediated by myogenic, metabolic, neurogenic mechanisms. Lost in TBI, SAH, large stroke: CBF becomes pressure-passive → both hypotension and hypertension directly cause ischemia or hyperemia. Chronic hypertension shifts curve rightward (higher MAP needed to maintain CBF)
ICP waveformsNormal: P1 (percussion) > P2 (tidal) > P3 (dicrotic). Reduced compliance: P2 > P1 (rounding of waveform). Lundberg waves: A waves (plateau waves, 50–100 mmHg lasting 5–20 min — PATHOLOGICAL, indicate critically reduced compliance), B waves (0.5–2/min oscillations — may indicate hydrocephalus), C waves (4–8/min, physiologic)

12.1.2 Types of Cerebral Edema

TypeMechanismLocationExamplesMRI/CT Features
VasogenicBBB breakdown → plasma proteins + fluid leak into extracellular spaceWhite matter predominantTumors (peritumoral edema), abscess, PRES, meningitis, contusionCT: hypodensity in white matter. MRI: T2/FLAIR high signal, follows white matter tracts. Responds to dexamethasone
CytotoxicCellular swelling from energy failure (Na+/K+ ATPase failure) → intracellular fluid accumulationGray + white matter (astrocytes, neurons)Ischemic stroke, hypoxic-ischemic injury, hepatic encephalopathyDWI restriction (bright on DWI, low ADC). CT: loss of gray-white differentiation. Does NOT respond to dexamethasone or osmotherapy long-term
Interstitial (transependymal)CSF forced across ependyma from obstructed ventriclesPeriventricular white matterObstructive hydrocephalusCT/FLAIR: periventricular hypodensity/hyperintensity. Responds to CSF drainage
OsmoticOsmolar gradient drives water into brainDiffuseAcute hyponatremia, rapid hemodialysis (dialysis disequilibrium syndrome), DKA (rapid glucose correction)Diffuse brain swelling. Treat underlying osmolar disturbance
HydrostaticIncreased transmural pressure gradientVariableAcute severe hypertension (related to PRES mechanism)May overlap with vasogenic pattern

12.1.3 Tiered Management of Raised ICP

Tiered ICP Management Protocol
TierInterventionsKey Details & Evidence
Tier 0: General Measures (All Patients)HOB 30°; midline head position (avoid jugular venous obstruction); adequate sedation/analgesia (propofol, fentanyl); normothermia (fever increases ICP by ~10% per °C); normoglycemia; normocarbia (PaCO2 35–40 mmHg); treat seizures; avoid hypotension (MAP ≥70 mmHg); DVT prophylaxis; avoid excessive PEEP (>15 cmH2O can impede venous return)Fever is one of the most common and underappreciated causes of ICP elevation. Even low-grade fever increases cerebral metabolic rate by 5–8% per °C. Aggressive fever control with acetaminophen + surface cooling is foundational
Tier 1: First-LineOsmotherapy: (1) Mannitol 20% 0.5–1 g/kg IV bolus over 15–20 min, repeat q4–6h. Hold if serum osmolality >320 mOsm/L or osmolar gap >20. (2) Hypertonic saline 23.4% 30 mL IV bolus via central line (or 3% NaCl 250 mL bolus), target Na 150–160 mEq/L. CSF drainage via EVD (if in place): intermittent drainage at 10–15 cmH2O; most rapid ICP reduction methodMannitol vs HTS: Burgess et al. Neurocrit Care 2016: meta-analysis suggests HTS may be more effective than mannitol for refractory ICP. Mannitol: osmotic diuresis → risk hypovolemia/renal injury if repeated; must replace fluid losses. HTS: no diuresis, expands intravascular volume; preferred in hypovolemic patients. Key: Either agent is effective; choose based on clinical context
Tier 2: Second-LineMild hyperventilation: PaCO2 30–35 mmHg (temporary measure; onset seconds; duration limited by pH adaptation). Neuromuscular blockade (if agitation/ventilator dyssynchrony causing ICP spikes): cisatracurium preferred (no histamine). Targeted temperature management: 35–36°C (mild hypothermia)Hyperventilation caution: CO2 is the most potent regulator of CBF. Each 1 mmHg decrease in PaCO2 reduces CBF by ~3%. PaCO2 <30 mmHg can cause dangerous cerebral vasoconstriction and ischemia. Monitor with brain tissue oxygenation (PbtO2) or jugular venous oximetry (SjvO2) if available. Never use prolonged hyperventilation as primary ICP strategy
Tier 3: RefractoryBarbiturate coma: Pentobarbital 5–10 mg/kg loading over 30 min → 1–4 mg/kg/h to achieve burst suppression on cEEG. Decompressive craniectomy (DC): removal of large bone flap (≥12 cm in adults) with duraplastyBarbiturate coma: Reduces CMRO2 and CBF. Major risks: hypotension (vasopressors usually required), immunosuppression, paralytic ileus. Monitor thiopental/pentobarbital levels. DC evidence (see below). Hypothermia 32–34°C: Eurotherm3235 (Andrews et al. NEJM 2015): therapeutic hypothermia for TBI with elevated ICP showed WORSE outcomes → not recommended as routine ICP management

Decompressive Craniectomy — Key Evidence

TrialPopulationKey Findings
DECIMAL 2007Malignant MCA infarction, age 18–55, within 48hDC reduced mortality (25% vs 78%), BUT increased survival with mRS 4 (severe disability). Individual trial pooled into analysis below
DESTINY 2007Malignant MCA infarction, age 18–60, within 36hSimilar: reduced mortality, increased survival with disability
HAMLET 2009Malignant MCA infarction, within 96hMortality benefit mainly in surgery within 48h subgroup
Pooled analysis (Vahedi et al. Lancet Neurol 2007)3 European trials above pooled (n=93)DC: mortality 22% vs 71% (NNT=2). mRS ≤3 at 1 year: 43% vs 21%. Conclusion: DC saves lives but many survivors have severe disability. Requires careful family discussion about goals of care
DESTINY II (Jüttler et al. NEJM 2014)Malignant MCA infarction, age >60DC reduced mortality (33% vs 70%). BUT: all survivors had mRS 4–5 at 12 months. In elderly: DC prevents death but does not result in functional independence
RESCUEicp (Hutchinson et al. NEJM 2016)TBI with refractory ICP >25 mmHgDC vs medical: lower mortality (26% vs 49%) at 6 months. But higher rate of vegetative state in DC group (8% vs 2%). At 12 months: favorable outcome (GOS-E ≥4) similar. DC is last resort for TBI; consider patient age and bilateral fixed dilated pupils (poor prognosis regardless)

12.1.4 Herniation Syndromes

Cerebral Herniation Syndromes
TypeAnatomyClinical FeaturesKey Findings
Uncal (transtentorial, lateral)Medial temporal lobe (uncus) herniates over tentorial edge → compresses CN III, PCA, cerebral peduncleIpsilateral CN III palsy (dilated pupil, ptosis) → contralateral hemiplegia (cerebral peduncle compression) → progressive coma (brainstem compression). Kernohan notch: contralateral peduncle compressed against opposite tentorial edge → ipsilateral hemiplegia (false localizing sign)Most important to recognize clinically. Fixed dilated pupil = emergency. Hutchinson pupil: initial brief miosis then progressive mydriasis as CN III compressed. PCA compression → occipital infarction
Central (downward transtentorial)Bilateral or diffuse supratentorial mass pushes diencephalon + midbrain downwardStages: Diencephalic (small reactive pupils, Cheyne-Stokes, decorticate) → Midbrain (mid-fixed pupils, central hyperventilation, decerebrate) → Pontine (pinpoint pupils, apneustic breathing) → Medullary (dilated fixed pupils, ataxic breathing → arrest)Progressive rostral-to-caudal deterioration. Both pupils affected simultaneously (vs uncal: unilateral first)
Subfalcine (cingulate)Cingulate gyrus herniates under falx cerebriContralateral leg weakness (ACA compression). Relatively mild clinically but heralds progression to uncal herniationCT: midline shift. ACA compression → ACA territory infarction
Tonsillar (foramen magnum)Cerebellar tonsils herniate through foramen magnum → compress medullaSudden respiratory arrest + cardiovascular collapse. Neck stiffness, lower cranial nerve palsy, Cushing triad (hypertension + bradycardia + irregular respiration)Most immediately life-threatening. Posterior fossa mass (hemorrhage, tumor, abscess). LP is contraindicated if suspected (can precipitate herniation)
Upward (ascending transtentorial)Posterior fossa mass pushes cerebellum upward through tentorial notchMidbrain compression: bilateral CN III palsy, altered consciousness. Upward gaze palsy. Hydrocephalus from aqueduct compressionCT: effacement of quadrigeminal cistern. Requires EVD for hydrocephalus + posterior fossa surgery
Transcalvarial (external)Brain herniates through skull defect (post-craniotomy, fracture)Fungus cerebri. Bulging through defectPost-surgical complication. Requires surgical management
Clinical Pearl — Emergency Herniation Protocol (ENLS 2020): When a patient shows signs of acute herniation (new pupil asymmetry, posturing, rapid GCS decline), act within MINUTES: (1) Elevate HOB 30°; (2) Osmotherapy STAT: HTS 23.4% 30 mL IV push (or mannitol 1 g/kg if no central access); (3) Brief hyperventilation to PaCO2 30–35 (temporary bridge, NOT sustained); (4) Intubate if not already (RSI avoiding ketamine controversy — actually ketamine is safe and does NOT raise ICP per modern evidence); (5) Urgent CT; (6) Neurosurgical consultation for EVD or surgical decompression. Do NOT wait for imaging before giving osmotherapy if clinical signs of herniation are present.

12.1.5 ICP Monitoring Indications & Multimodal Neuromonitoring

ModalityWhat It MeasuresKey ThresholdsClinical Use
EVD (external ventricular drain)ICP (gold standard) + allows CSF drainageICP >22 mmHg = treatment threshold (BTF 4th Ed 2016)Therapeutic + diagnostic. Infection risk ~5–10%. Placed in Kocher’s point (non-dominant hemisphere). Can recalibrate
Intraparenchymal monitorICP (Codman, Camino, Raumedic)Same thresholds as EVDLower infection risk. Cannot drain CSF. Cannot recalibrate (drift over days). Measures focal pressure (may differ from global ICP)
Brain tissue oxygenation (PbtO2)Local brain oxygen tensionPbtO2 <20 mmHg = brain hypoxia. <15 = critical. Goal ≥20 mmHgBOOST-3 (in progress), BOOST-II (2017): PbtO2-guided therapy reduced brain tissue hypoxia. Placed in vulnerable tissue. Complements ICP monitoring. Treat low PbtO2: increase FiO2, optimize CPP, transfuse if Hb low, treat ICP
Cerebral microdialysisBrain extracellular glucose, lactate, pyruvate, glutamate, glycerolLactate/pyruvate ratio (LPR) >40 = metabolic crisis. Low glucose <0.7 mmol/L = energy failureResearch/specialized centers. Detects secondary injury before ICP rises. Guides metabolic optimization
Jugular venous oximetry (SjvO2)Global cerebral oxygen extractionSjvO2 <55% = ischemia (high extraction). >75% = hyperemia or infarction (low extraction)Guides hyperventilation (safe if SjvO2 normal during hyperventilation)
Continuous EEG (cEEG)Cortical electrical activityDetect non-convulsive seizures (NCSE), burst suppression during barbiturate coma, assess depth of sedationEssential in neurocritical care. NCSE prevalence 10–35% in comatose ICU patients. Seizure burden correlates with worse outcome
Q: A 48-year-old woman with a large right MCA infarction (CT shows >50% MCA territory involvement at 18 hours) develops left-sided hemiplegia, right pupil dilation (6 mm, fixed), and GCS drops from 11 to 7 over 30 minutes. CT shows 12 mm midline shift. What is the herniation syndrome, and what is the evidence-based management?
Herniation syndrome: Uncal (transtentorial) herniation — right temporal lobe (swollen from malignant MCA infarction) herniating over right tentorial edge, compressing the right CN III (ipsilateral fixed dilated pupil) and the left cerebral peduncle (contralateral hemiplegia). The progressive GCS decline reflects ongoing brainstem compression.

Immediate emergency management:
1. HOB 30°, midline head position
2. Osmotherapy STAT: HTS 23.4% 30 mL IV bolus (if central line) or mannitol 20% 1 g/kg IV over 15–20 min
3. Brief hyperventilation: target PaCO2 30–35 mmHg (temporary bridge to surgery)
4. Intubation if not already secured (GCS 7 = cannot protect airway)
5. Urgent neurosurgical consultation for decompressive craniectomy (DC)

Evidence for DC: This patient is an ideal candidate based on the European pooled analysis (DECIMAL/DESTINY/HAMLET): age <60, malignant MCA infarction >50% territory, clinical deterioration within 48 hours. DC reduces mortality from ~71% to ~22% (NNT=2). However, 43% achieve mRS ≤3 at 1 year vs 21% medical. Critical family discussion: DC saves life but ~50% of survivors will have mRS 4 (severe disability, dependent for basic activities) — specifically for dominant hemisphere infarction, aphasia will be a major factor.

Surgery: Large fronto-temporo-parietal DC (≥12 cm bone flap, including temporal bone to skull base) with duraplasty. Should be performed as early as possible (within 48 hours of stroke onset, before irreversible brainstem injury). Cranioplasty (bone flap replacement) typically at 6–12 weeks.

12.2 Brain Death, Coma Evaluation & Neuroprognostication

12.2.1 Brain Death / Death by Neurologic Criteria (DNC)

Brain death is the irreversible cessation of all functions of the entire brain, including the brainstem. This is legal death in virtually all US states (Uniform Determination of Death Act 1981) and most countries. The AAN Practice Parameter 2010 (Wijdicks et al.) and the World Brain Death Project (Greer et al. JAMA 2020) provide current standards.

Brain Death Determination — Complete Protocol
StepRequirementCritical Details
Prerequisites (ALL must be met)(1) Established etiology capable of causing brain death. (2) Neuroimaging consistent with devastating brain injury. (3) Exclusion of confounders:No CNS depressants: drug levels below therapeutic (barbiturate <10 μg/mL; wait 5 half-lives or measure levels). Core temperature ≥36°C (hypothermia mimics brain death). SBP ≥100 mmHg (hypotension reduces brainstem reflexes). No neuromuscular blockade (confirm with train-of-four ≥2/4). No severe metabolic derangement (correct Na, glucose, pH, endocrine). No residual effects of therapeutic hypothermia (wait ≥24h after rewarming)
1. ComaNo evidence of arousal or awareness. No eye opening or purposeful response to noxious stimulation (supraorbital pressure, nail bed, TMJ)Spinal reflexes may be present and do NOT preclude brain death: triple flexion, Lazarus sign (arm raising), finger movements, undulating toe. These are spinal cord-mediated. Must inform family that these may occur to avoid misunderstanding
2. Absent brainstem reflexes (ALL)(a) Pupillary: fixed pupils (4–9 mm), no response to bright light bilaterally. (b) Corneal: no blink to cotton wisp or saline drop. (c) Oculocephalic (Doll’s eyes): no eye movement with rapid head turning (only if C-spine cleared). (d) Vestibulo-ocular (cold calorics): 50 mL ice water instilled in each ear; observe 1 min, 5 min between ears; no eye deviation. (e) Gag: no response to posterior pharynx stimulation. (f) Cough: no response to deep tracheal suctioningAtropine in prior code may cause fixed dilated pupils — wait for clearance. CN VII palsy can mimic absent corneal — ensure afferent (CN V) is being tested. Cannot assess oculocephalic if C-spine not cleared — rely on cold calorics. Bilateral tympanic membrane rupture may invalidate cold calorics
3. Apnea test(a) Preoxygenate 100% FiO2 × 10 min. (b) Baseline ABG (PaCO2 35–45 mmHg). (c) Disconnect ventilator. (d) Deliver O2 via catheter at carina (6 L/min) or CPAP with O2. (e) Observe for respiratory effort × 8–10 min. (f) Repeat ABG at endPositive (= brain death): NO respiratory effort AND PaCO2 ≥60 mmHg (or ≥20 mmHg rise above baseline).
