Aborted test: if SpO2 <85%, SBP <90, or cardiac arrhythmia — reconnect ventilator, obtain ABG, proceed to ancillary testing. PaCO2 rises ~3 mmHg/min when disconnected. CAUTION: High PEEP patients may desaturate rapidly; consider modified technique (T-piece with CPAP)
Number of examsAAN 2010: 1 exam in adults (some states/institutions require 2). World Brain Death Project 2020: recommends single comprehensive exam by attending physician experienced in DNC. Observation period between exams if 2 required: 6–24h (varies by jurisdiction)Pediatric (AAP 2011): <1 month of age: 2 exams 24h apart. 1 month–18 years: 2 exams 12h apart. Ancillary test can shorten observation period
DocumentationDate and time of death = time brain death criteria fulfilled (NOT time of cardiac arrest or ventilator withdrawal). Examiner qualifications. All findings documentedLegal time of death is the completion of the brain death exam (even if heart is still beating on ventilator)

Ancillary Tests for Brain Death

TestFindingSensitivity/SpecificityWhen Required
Tc-99m HMPAO SPECT“Hollow skull” — no intracranial radiotracer uptakeHigh sensitivity (~95%) and specificity (~100%)Gold standard ancillary. Cannot complete clinical exam (facial trauma, cervical instability, drug confounders, COPD high baseline PaCO2)
4-vessel cerebral angiographyNo intracranial filling above carotid siphon / vertebral entry to skullTraditional gold standard. High sensitivityInvasive. Rarely used now; replaced by CTA in many centers
CT angiography (CTA)Absent opacification of cortical MCA and internal cerebral veins (4-point or 7-point scoring systems)Sensitivity ~85–95%, varies by methodologyRapidly available. AAN does not formally endorse but widely accepted. World Brain Death Project: acceptable ancillary
EEGElectrocerebral inactivity (ECI): no electrical activity >2 μV for ≥30 min, using standard 16+ channelsSensitivity ~90%. Can be confounded by sedatives, hypothermia, ICU artifactWhen drug confounders or apnea test cannot be completed. Must meet technical standards (interelectrode distance, impedance, sensitivity). Artifact is a major limitation in ICU
Transcranial Doppler (TCD)Reverberating (oscillating) flow pattern or systolic spikes bilaterally in MCAs and basilar arteryOperator-dependent. ~90% sensitivity. 10% no temporal windowsBedside, non-invasive, repeatable. Best as complement, not standalone
Clinical Pearl — Common Pitfalls in Brain Death Determination: (1) Therapeutic hypothermia (TTM): After cardiac arrest with TTM, must wait ≥24h after rewarming to normothermia AND ensure drug clearance before brain death exam. Hypothermia prolongs drug metabolism dramatically. (2) High-dose barbiturates: Must measure pentobarbital level (<10 μg/mL) or wait 5 half-lives. (3) Spinal reflexes: Lazarus sign (bilateral arm flexion/raising) occurs in up to 75% of brain-dead patients during apnea test. Warn the family and exam team. (4) Posterior fossa lesions: Devastating brainstem lesion may obliterate all brainstem function while cerebral cortex is relatively preserved initially — still qualifies as brain death if ALL criteria met. (5) Chronic CO2 retainers (COPD): baseline PaCO2 may be >60 mmHg — use 20 mmHg rise above baseline AND absolute ≥60 as dual threshold, or proceed to ancillary testing.

12.2.2 Coma Evaluation — Structured Approach

Systematic Coma Evaluation
StepAssessmentKey Findings & Implications
1. Level of consciousnessGCS (Glasgow Coma Scale): E (1–4) + V (1–5) + M (1–6) = 3–15. FOUR Score (Full Outline of UnResponsiveness): Eye (0–4) + Motor (0–4) + Brainstem (0–4) + Respiration (0–4) = 0–16FOUR Score advantages over GCS: assesses brainstem reflexes, detects locked-in syndrome (eye tracking preserved), evaluates respiratory drive, not confounded by intubation. GCS ≤8 = coma = intubate for airway protection
2. Pupillary examSize, reactivity, symmetryBilateral fixed dilated: bilateral uncal herniation, anticholinergics, anoxia. Unilateral fixed dilated: ipsilateral CN III (uncal herniation). Bilateral pinpoint: pontine lesion, opioids (naloxone-responsive). Bilateral mid-fixed (4–6 mm): midbrain lesion. Small reactive: metabolic/toxic coma (pupils usually preserved!)
3. Eye movementsSpontaneous, oculocephalic, vestibulo-ocular reflexesRoving conjugate: intact brainstem, cortical cause. Ocular bobbing: pontine lesion. Ping-pong gaze: bilateral cerebral lesion. Skew deviation: brainstem (posterior fossa). Absent VOR: severe brainstem dysfunction
4. Motor responseBest response to noxious stimuliLocalization: cortical function intact. Withdrawal: subcortical. Flexion (decorticate): above red nucleus. Extension (decerebrate): below red nucleus (midbrain/pons). No response: medulla/spinal or deep metabolic. Asymmetric response = structural lesion
5. Respiratory patternSpontaneous breathing patternCheyne-Stokes: bilateral cortical/diencephalic. Central neurogenic hyperventilation: midbrain/pons. Apneustic (inspiratory hold): pons. Cluster/Biot: lower pons/upper medulla. Ataxic (Gasping): medulla (impending arrest)
6. “Treatable” causesThe mnemonic AEIOU TIPSAlcohol/Acidosis. Epilepsy/Encephalopathy. Insulin (hypo/hyperglycemia). Opioids/Overdose. Uremia. Trauma/Temperature. Infection. Psychiatric/Porphyria/Poison. Stroke/SAH/Shock. Always check glucose, toxicology screen, basic metabolic panel, ammonia, blood gas

12.2.3 Neuroprognostication After Cardiac Arrest

Neuroprognostication After Cardiac Arrest — ERC/ESICM 2021 Algorithm
ModalityPoor Prognosis Indicator (FPR 0–5%)TimingCaveats
Clinical examAbsent pupillary light reflex bilaterally at ≥72h. GCS Motor 1–2 at ≥72h (high sensitivity, moderate specificity alone). Bilateral absent corneal reflex at ≥72h. Status myoclonus within 72h (<24h: Lance-Adams syndrome possible if early myoclonus with EEG-reactive background — NOT always poor)≥72h post-ROSC (or ≥72h after rewarming if TTM used). Ensure no residual sedation/paralysisClinical exam ALONE is insufficient for prognostication. Multimodal approach required. Confounders: sedatives, paralytics, hypothermia. Quantitative pupillometry (NPi) adds objectivity
EEGBurst suppression without reactivity at ≥72h. Suppressed background without reactivity (<10 μV). Status epilepticus on unreactive backgroundContinuous monitoring from admission. Prognostic assessment at ≥72hReactive EEG background = favorable sign regardless of pattern. Some burst suppression patterns early (<24h) can evolve favorably. Continuous EEG patterns are important for treatment of non-convulsive status
SSEP (somatosensory evoked potentials)Bilateral absent N20 cortical responses (with preserved N13/N14 peripheral responses to confirm technical adequacy)≥24h post-ROSC (can be done early; robust even during TTM)Most reliable single predictor (FPR <1% for bilateral absent N20). Technical pitfalls: electrode issues, ICU electrical noise. Must confirm peripheral N13/N14 present. Unilateral absent N20 is NOT reliable
Serum biomarkersNSE (neuron-specific enolase) >60 μg/L at 48–72h (highly suggestive of poor outcome). Rising NSE trend (48h > 24h) worse than falling. No absolute cutoff with 0% FPR exists24h and 48–72h post-ROSC. Serial measurements more valuable than singleHemolysis falsely elevates NSE (RBCs contain NSE). Neuroendocrine tumors. Use trending, not single values. Should NOT be used as sole basis for WLST
MRI brainExtensive DWI restriction in cortex/deep gray matter at 2–7 days. Quantitative: whole-brain ADC <650–700 × 10−6 mm2/sOptimal timing: day 2–5 post-ROSCSensitive for global anoxic injury. Pattern: cortex, basal ganglia, thalami affected. DWI restriction in isolated regions = less predictive. Motion artifact in ICU patients
CT brainGeneralized cerebral edema with loss of gray-white differentiation. GWR (gray-white ratio) <1.10–1.20 at ≥24h≥24h post-ROSCLess sensitive than MRI. GWR cutoffs vary by study. Severe generalized edema at 24h = very poor sign
Clinical Pearl — The Multimodal Prognostication Approach: ERC/ESICM 2021 Guidelines emphasize: NEVER use a single modality to determine poor prognosis and withdraw care. The recommended approach: (1) Wait ≥72h post-ROSC (or ≥72h after rewarming) with sedation off. (2) If GCS-M ≤3 at ≥72h: enter prognostication algorithm. (3) Look for ≥2 of the following concordant poor predictors: bilateral absent N20, bilateral absent pupillary/corneal reflex, highly malignant EEG (≥72h), markedly elevated NSE, extensive DWI changes on MRI, status myoclonus <72h. (4) If ≥2 concordant: poor outcome very likely (consider WLST after family discussion). (5) If discordant or uncertain: continue observation, repeat assessments, consider further testing. Self-fulfilling prophecy is the greatest risk in neuroprognostication: if care is withdrawn based on a pessimistic prediction, we cannot know if the patient might have recovered.
Q: A 55-year-old man is admitted after out-of-hospital cardiac arrest with ROSC after 25 minutes of CPR. He underwent TTM at 33°C for 24 hours, rewarmed 36 hours ago. Sedation has been off for 48 hours. At 72h post-rewarming: GCS is E1V1M2 (extension to pain), bilateral pupils 3 mm fixed, corneal reflex absent bilaterally. cEEG shows burst suppression without reactivity. SSEP shows bilateral absent N20 cortical responses with preserved N13 peripheral responses. NSE is 112 μg/L at 72h (24h value was 85). How do you interpret these findings?
Interpretation: Multiple concordant predictors of poor neurological outcome are present.

Prerequisites confirmed: (1) ≥72h after rewarming (≥108h post-ROSC). (2) Sedation discontinued ≥48h. (3) No confounders (drug levels, temperature, metabolic derangement should be verified). (4) No neuromuscular blockade (train-of-four must be confirmed).

Poor prognostic indicators present:
1. Clinical: GCS-M 2 (extension) at ≥72h + bilateral absent pupillary and corneal reflexes = 2 clinical markers of poor prognosis (each with FPR <5%)
2. SSEP: bilateral absent N20 with preserved N13 (technically adequate) = most reliable single predictor of poor outcome (FPR <1%). This essentially confirms that cortical somatosensory processing is absent
3. EEG: burst suppression without reactivity at ≥72h = highly malignant pattern (unreactive background is the key negative feature)
4. NSE: 112 μg/L at 72h, rising from 85 at 24h = markedly elevated with uptrending (both the absolute value >60 and the rising trend support poor prognosis). Ensure hemolysis excluded

Concordance: 4/4 modalities concordantly poor. Per ERC/ESICM 2021 algorithm, ≥2 concordant poor predictors after meeting prerequisites = poor outcome very likely (specificity for poor outcome approaches 100%).

Recommended next steps:
1. MRI brain (if not yet done): expected to show extensive cortical + deep gray matter DWI restriction, confirming global anoxic injury
2. Family meeting: transparent discussion of prognosis using multimodal data. Explain that with 4 concordant negative predictors, chance of meaningful recovery is extremely low (<1%)
3. Goals of care discussion: explore family values, prior patient wishes, advance directives. Discuss options including withdrawal of life-sustaining treatment vs continued observation
4. Organ donation consideration: early involvement of organ procurement organization if family considering this
5. Document thoroughly: all prognostic assessments, confounders excluded, timing, family discussions

12.3 PRES, Hypertensive Emergencies & Acute Drug-Related Encephalopathies

12.3.1 Posterior Reversible Encephalopathy Syndrome (PRES)

PRES — Comprehensive Review
FeatureDetails
PathophysiologyTwo proposed mechanisms: (1) Hypertensive theory: BP exceeds autoregulatory limit → breakthrough hyperperfusion → vasogenic edema. Posterior circulation preferentially affected because vertebrobasilar territory has less sympathetic innervation (→ less autoregulatory protection). (2) Endothelial toxicity theory: direct endothelial injury (calcineurin inhibitors, chemotherapy, eclampsia) → capillary leak → vasogenic edema (explains PRES in normotensive patients)
EtiologyHypertensive emergencies (most common overall). Eclampsia/preeclampsia (most common in obstetric setting). Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus — dose-related endothelial toxicity). Chemotherapy: bevacizumab (anti-VEGF), cisplatin, gemcitabine, VEGF-TKIs. Autoimmune: SLE, scleroderma renal crisis, TTP/HUS. Sepsis/MAHA. Transplant (solid organ or HSCT). Renal failure
Clinical presentation(1) Headache (most common, ~50%). (2) Visual disturbances (cortical blindness, homonymous hemianopia, blurred vision, visual neglect — ~60%). (3) Seizures (~60–75%, often the presenting feature; can be status epilepticus). (4) Altered consciousness (confusion to coma). (5) Focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia). (6) Hypertension (present in ~75%; but 25% are normotensive!)
MRI findingsClassic: bilateral symmetric T2/FLAIR hyperintensity in posterior parietal-occipital regions (vasogenic edema). Atypical variants (30–40%): frontal involvement, deep gray matter (basal ganglia, thalami, brainstem), unilateral, hemorrhagic. DWI/ADC: high ADC (vasogenic = extracellular water, reversible). If low ADC (cytotoxic) = ischemic conversion = worse prognosis, may be irreversible. Enhancement in ~30% (BBB breakdown)
Hemorrhagic PRES10–15% of cases. Petechial, focal hematoma, or SAH. Associated with anticoagulation, thrombocytopenia, extreme hypertension. Hemorrhagic PRES = worse outcome, higher mortality
Treatment1. BP reduction: lower by 15–25% in first hour. IV nicardipine (5–15 mg/h, easily titratable) or labetalol (20 mg IV bolus q10 min or 1–2 mg/min infusion). 2. Eclampsia: IV magnesium sulfate 4–6 g loading → 1–2 g/h + delivery of fetus (definitive treatment). 3. Remove offending agent (reduce calcineurin inhibitor dose, stop bevacizumab). 4. Seizures: levetiracetam or IV lorazepam acutely; avoid phenytoin in eclampsia (Mg is first-line). 5. Monitor in ICU
Prognosis~90% full neurological recovery within days to weeks if treated promptly. MRI normalizes. Poor prognostic factors: cytotoxic edema on DWI, hemorrhage, delayed treatment, persistent uncontrolled BP, underlying TTP/HUS. Recurrence possible if re-exposed to trigger

12.3.2 Hypertensive Emergency — Neurological Manifestations

PresentationBP TargetPreferred AgentKey Points
Hypertensive encephalopathyLower MAP 20–25% in first hourNicardipine, labetalol, clevidipineHeadache, confusion, visual changes, papilledema, seizures. PRES is imaging correlate. If focal deficit: must exclude stroke before aggressive lowering
Acute ischemic stroke + hypertensionIf thrombolysis candidate: <185/110 before tPA, then <180/105 × 24h. If NOT tPA candidate: treat only if >220/120 (permissive hypertension)Labetalol, nicardipine. Avoid nitroprusside (raised ICP risk)Do NOT aggressively lower BP in acute ischemic stroke (penumbra perfusion is pressure-dependent). Exception: aortic dissection, acute heart failure
Intracerebral hemorrhageSBP target <140 mmHg (INTERACT2: safe; ATACH-2: intensive SBP <120 showed harm, increased renal adverse events)Nicardipine (most studied in ICH trials), labetalolRapid lowering to <140 within 1h is safe. Avoid SBP <120 (ATACH-2 showed increased adverse events without benefit). BP variability also harmful
Subarachnoid hemorrhageSBP <160 mmHg (pre-aneurysm securing)Nicardipine, labetalol. Avoid nitroprusside (raises ICP)Balance rebleeding risk (high BP) vs cerebral ischemia (low BP). After aneurysm secured: BP management liberalized. Vasospasm window (day 3–14): avoid hypotension, consider induced hypertension
EclampsiaSBP <160, DBP <110IV magnesium sulfate (primary for seizure prevention/treatment) + IV labetalol or IV hydralazineDelivery of fetus is definitive treatment. Avoid ACE inhibitors (teratogenic), nitroprusside (cyanide toxicity to fetus). Can occur up to 6 weeks postpartum

12.3.3 Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)

NMS vs. Serotonin Syndrome — Critical Comparison
FeatureNeuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)Serotonin Syndrome (SS)
MechanismCentral dopamine (D2) blockade in hypothalamus (thermoregulation) + basal ganglia (rigidity)Excess serotonergic activity (5-HT1A and 5-HT2A receptors)
Causative agentsDopamine antagonists: typical antipsychotics (haloperidol most common), atypical antipsychotics, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine. Dopamine withdrawal: sudden levodopa/dopamine agonist discontinuation in PD (PD hyperpyrexia syndrome)Serotonergic drugs: SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol, meperidine, linezolid (MAOI activity), triptans, MDMA/ecstasy, St John’s wort, dextromethorphan, lithium. Usually from drug interaction (SSRI + MAOI most dangerous)
OnsetGradual (days to weeks after drug initiation or dose increase)Rapid (within 24 hours of drug change, often within 6 hours)
Classic tetrad(1) Hyperthermia (>40°C). (2) Lead-pipe rigidity (generalized, severe). (3) Altered mental status (confusion to coma). (4) Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis)(1) Hyperthermia. (2) Clonus (especially lower extremity > upper, ocular clonus). (3) Altered mental status (agitation, confusion). (4) Autonomic instability (diaphoresis, tachycardia, diarrhea)
Key distinguishing featureRIGIDITY (lead-pipe, not clonus). Bradykinesia. Slow onset. CK markedly elevated (>1000, often >10,000 U/L)CLONUS + HYPERREFLEXIA (not rigidity). Myoclonus. Rapid onset. Dilated pupils. Diarrhea (bowel hyperactivity). CK may be mildly elevated
LabsCK massively elevated. WBC elevated. Metabolic acidosis. Acute kidney injury (rhabdomyolysis). LFTs elevatedCK mildly elevated (unless severe). Metabolic acidosis in severe cases. No specific lab test
DiagnosisClinical diagnosis. Levenson criteria or DSM-5: exposure to dopamine antagonist + hyperthermia + rigidity + 2 of: diaphoresis, dysphagia, tremor, incontinence, altered consciousness, tachycardia, labile BP, elevated CK, leukocytosisClinical diagnosis. Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria: serotonergic agent + any of: spontaneous clonus, inducible clonus + (agitation or diaphoresis), ocular clonus + (agitation or diaphoresis), tremor + hyperreflexia, hypertonia + temperature >38°C + (ocular or inducible clonus)
Treatment1. Stop offending agent. 2. Aggressive cooling (target <39°C). 3. IV fluids (rhabdomyolysis prevention). 4. Dantrolene 1–2.5 mg/kg IV (muscle relaxant, reduces rigidity/thermogenesis). 5. Bromocriptine 2.5–10 mg PO/NG TID (D2 agonist). 6. Benzodiazepines for agitation. 7. ICU monitoring. Recovery: days to weeks. Mortality: 5–10%1. Stop ALL serotonergic agents. 2. Cyproheptadine 12 mg PO/NG loading then 2 mg q2h (5-HT2A antagonist). 3. Aggressive cooling. 4. Benzodiazepines (for agitation, myoclonus, seizures). 5. IV fluids. 6. Avoid: dantrolene (not effective), antipyretics (not central fever). Typically resolves within 24–72h after stopping drug. Mortality: 2–12%
Clinical Pearl — NMS vs SS Quick Mnemonic: “NMS = Slow and Stiff” (gradual onset, lead-pipe rigidity, bradykinesia). “SS = Fast and Twitchy” (rapid onset, clonus, hyperreflexia, myoclonus). If you see clonus, think serotonin syndrome. If you see lead-pipe rigidity without clonus, think NMS. Another key: NMS patients look parkinsonian; SS patients look hyperkinetic. Also remember: PD patients who abruptly stop levodopa can develop a syndrome clinically identical to NMS (“PD hyperpyrexia syndrome” or “neuroleptic malignant-like syndrome”) — treat with restarting dopaminergic medications + dantrolene.

12.3.4 Malignant Hyperthermia

FeatureDetails
TriggerVolatile anesthetic agents (sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, halothane) and/or succinylcholine. Genetic susceptibility (RYR1 mutation, autosomal dominant; DHP receptor mutations)
PathophysiologyUncontrolled calcium release from sarcoplasmic reticulum in skeletal muscle → sustained muscle contraction → hypermetabolism, rhabdomyolysis, hyperthermia
ClinicalIntraoperative or early post-op: masseter rigidity after succinylcholine (earliest sign), rising end-tidal CO2 (hypermetabolism), tachycardia, mixed respiratory/metabolic acidosis, rising temperature (can exceed 43°C), generalized rigidity, hyperkalemia, myoglobinuria
Treatment1. Stop triggering agent immediately. 2. Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus, repeat q5 min up to 10 mg/kg (muscle relaxant that blocks RYR1 calcium release — SPECIFIC antidote). 3. Hyperventilate with 100% O2. 4. Active cooling. 5. Treat hyperkalemia (insulin/glucose, calcium gluconate, bicarbonate). 6. Hydration + mannitol for rhabdomyolysis. Without dantrolene, mortality >70%. With dantrolene: <5%
Q: A 32-year-old woman on sertraline (SSRI) and tramadol presents with agitation, confusion, temperature 39.5°C, tachycardia (HR 130), diaphoresis, bilateral lower extremity clonus, hyperreflexia, and dilated pupils. CK is mildly elevated at 450 U/L. What is the diagnosis, key diagnostic criteria, and treatment?
Diagnosis: Serotonin syndrome (serotonin toxicity).

Explanation: This patient is on two serotonergic agents: sertraline (SSRI, inhibits serotonin reuptake) + tramadol (also inhibits serotonin reuptake + weak opioid). The combination creates excess serotonergic activity.

Hunter Serotonin Toxicity Criteria (validated, preferred over Sternbach):
In the presence of a serotonergic agent, requires ONE of: (1) Spontaneous clonus (present — bilateral LE clonus). This single finding with a serotonergic agent satisfies the criteria. Additionally: inducible clonus + diaphoresis (present), hyperreflexia + tremor (hyperreflexia present). Criteria clearly met.

Why NOT NMS: (1) Rapid onset (SS develops within hours). (2) Clonus + hyperreflexia (NMS has rigidity WITHOUT clonus). (3) No lead-pipe rigidity. (4) Serotonergic agents, not dopamine antagonists. (5) CK only mildly elevated (NMS typically >1000–10,000). (6) Dilated pupils (serotonergic effect). (7) No exposure to antipsychotics.

Treatment:
1. Stop ALL serotonergic agents (sertraline AND tramadol)
2. Cyproheptadine 12 mg PO/NG loading, then 2–4 mg q2h until improvement (5-HT2A antagonist — specific treatment for SS). Maximum 32 mg/day
3. IV benzodiazepines (lorazepam 1–2 mg) for agitation and myoclonus
4. Active cooling if temp >41°C (cooling blankets, ice packs). Antipyretics (acetaminophen) are NOT effective because this is not a centrally-mediated fever
5. IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis prevention and tachycardia
6. Avoid: physical restraints (increase muscle activity → worsen hyperthermia), dantrolene (not effective in SS), additional serotonergic drugs
7. Expected recovery: within 24–72 hours after stopping serotonergic agents. Most resolve within 24h with treatment

12.4 Acute Neuromuscular Emergencies & Time-Critical Syndromes

12.4.1 Acute Respiratory Failure in Neuromuscular Disease

Respiratory failure is the leading cause of death in neuromuscular diseases. Anticipating and monitoring respiratory compromise is a fundamental neurological skill, especially for GBS, MG crisis, ALS, and DMD.

Neuromuscular Respiratory Failure — The “20-30-40 Rule”
ParameterCritical ThresholdAction
FVC (Forced Vital Capacity)<20 mL/kg (or <1 L absolute)Consider elective intubation. Do NOT wait for respiratory distress — intubate before emergency
MIP/NIF (Maximum Inspiratory Pressure)<−30 cmH2O (less negative = weaker)Borderline; watch closely. <−20 cmH2O = very high risk
MEP (Maximum Expiratory Pressure)<40 cmH2OPoor cough = secretion retention risk = pneumonia. <40 = airway clearance device or intubation
TrendSerial measurements q4–8h; downward trendRate of decline matters more than absolute value. Halving of FVC in 24h = urgent intubation

Danger signs: Paradoxical breathing (abdominal paradox — in-drawing on inspiration = diaphragm failure), inability to count to 20 in one breath, orthopnea (worse supine = diaphragm weakness), SaO2 desaturation (late sign), rising PaCO2 (very late, impending arrest). Do NOT rely on SpO2 alone — O2 saturation can remain normal until moments before arrest in pure hypoventilation.

Specific Neuromuscular Emergencies

ConditionEmergency FeaturesManagement
Myasthenic CrisisRapid worsening of bulbar/respiratory weakness requiring ventilatory support. Precipitants: infection (most common), medications (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers, Mg), surgery, pregnancy, tapering immunosuppression. FVC <1 L = intubateHold cholinesterase inhibitors (pyridostigmine) during crisis (worsens secretions). PLEX ×5 exchanges over 10–14 days (faster onset, preferred) OR IVIg 2 g/kg over 2–5 days (equivalent efficacy). High-dose IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 3–5 days as bridge. Treat precipitant. (See Section 5 for full MG management)
GBS with respiratory failure25–30% of GBS require mechanical ventilation. Brighton criteria for diagnosis. Autonomic instability: labile BP, bradycardia (may need pacemaker), ileus, urinary retention. Bilateral facial palsy + bulbar weakness = high intubation riskSerial FVC (20-30-40 rule). Elective intubation when FVC trending toward <20 mL/kg. IVIG 0.4 g/kg × 5 days OR PLEX (5 exchanges over 2 weeks) — equivalent, but IVIG more convenient. Avoid succinylcholine for RSI (hyperkalemia from upregulated nicotinic receptors). DVT prophylaxis, pain management (often severe neuropathic pain), autonomic monitoring
BotulismDescending paralysis starting with bulbar signs (ptosis, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia). Dilated FIXED pupils (distinguishes from MG). Dry mouth, constipation (anticholinergic autonomic). Respiratory failure. Foodborne, wound, infant, iatrogenicEquine heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (CDC release) — give ASAP (halts disease progression; doesn’t reverse paralysis). Wound debridement + antibiotics (wound botulism). Supportive: mechanical ventilation (weeks to months). Avoid aminoglycosides (worsen NMJ blockade). Infant botulism: BabyBIG (human-derived BIG-IV)
Acute flaccid paralysis differentialGBS, botulism, polio/AFM, transverse myelitis, severe hypokalemia, hypermagnesemia, organophosphate poisoningEMG/NCS for GBS vs NMJ vs anterior horn. Lumbar puncture (albumino-cytological dissociation in GBS). Stool culture/PCR for botulism/enterovirus. Spinal MRI for myelopathy. Electrolytes (K, Mg)

12.4.2 Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH) — Neurocritical Care

SAH Management — Time-Critical Interventions
PhaseComplicationManagement
Immediate (0–72h)Rebleeding (highest risk first 24h; 4–14% within 24h untreated). Risk: SBP >160, large aneurysm, poor Hunt-Hess gradeBP control SBP <160 until aneurysm secured. Secure aneurysm ASAP: endovascular coiling (preferred, ISAT trial: coiling superior to clipping for suitable aneurysms, lower morbidity/mortality at 1 year) vs neurosurgical clipping. IV tranexamic acid (if >3h delay to aneurysm treatment, reduces early rebleeding risk — not recommended routinely). Avoid anticoagulants/antiplatelets until secured
HydrocephalusAcute obstructive hydrocephalus (blood in ventricles; 15–20% of SAH) or communicating hydrocephalusExternal ventricular drain (EVD) if hydrocephalus + impaired consciousness. Weaning EVD: gradual (over days) vs immediate clamping. Shunt insertion for persistent communicating hydrocephalus (10–15%)
Vasospasm & DCI (Days 3–14)Delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI): most feared complication, peak day 5–10. Angiographic vasospasm in 70%; symptomatic DCI in 30%. Worse with poor-grade SAH, large subarachnoid blood, cigarette smoking1. Nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h × 21 days (α-1 and L-type calcium channel blocker; prevents DCI though not angiographic vasospasm; level A evidence; ONLY calcium channel blocker proven to improve neurological outcome in SAH). 2. Euvolemia (avoid hypovolemia; "Triple H" therapy abandoned for euvolemia + induced hypertension only if DCI). 3. TCD monitoring (daily/q12h): MCA velocities >120 cm/s = vasospasm; >200 cm/s = severe. 4. Induced hypertension (SBP 160–200) for symptomatic DCI. 5. Endovascular rescue (intra-arterial verapamil, balloon angioplasty) for refractory DCI
Electrolyte disturbancesHyponatremia (most common electrolyte complication): SIADH (euvolemic) vs cerebral salt wasting (hypovolemic — distinguish by volume status!). Hyponatremia worsens cerebral edema and seizure thresholdSIADH: fluid restriction. Cerebral salt wasting (CSW): volume replacement + salt supplementation. CSW is more common than SIADH in SAH. Hypertonic saline may be needed. Avoid aggressive fluid restriction in SAH (risk of vasospasm/DCI)
Cardiac complicationsECG changes (T-wave inversions, QTc prolongation, ST changes — 50–100% of SAH). Neurogenic stunned myocardium (Takotsubo-like, apical ballooning pattern). Troponin elevation in 30%Cardiac monitoring in ICU. Echo (TTE/TEE) for cardiac function. Arrhythmias: treat if hemodynamically significant. Avoid QTc-prolonging drugs. Neurogenic cardiomyopathy usually reversible over days to weeks
Grading scalesClinical severity: Hunt-Hess scale (I–V; grade I: asymptomatic or minimal headache; grade V: deep coma, decerebrate posturing). CT blood burden: Fisher grade (modified Fisher: grade I–IV based on subarachnoid blood thickness and IVH) — predicts vasospasm riskHigher Hunt-Hess and Fisher grade = higher vasospasm/DCI risk = more intensive monitoring

12.4.3 Acute Bacterial Meningitis — Emergency Protocol

StepActionDetails
1. RecognitionClassic triad: fever + headache + neck stiffness (present in <50%!). Kernig + Brudzinski signs (low sensitivity but specific). Altered consciousness. Petechial/purpuric rash = N. meningitidis — EMERGENCYThe classic triad is ABSENT in up to 50% of cases. Any 2 of 4 features (headache, fever, neck stiffness, altered consciousness) is sufficient to act. Do NOT wait for all three
2. CT before LP?CT head first if: papilledema, focal neurological deficit, immunocompromised, new seizure, GCS <10, known CNS disease. Otherwise: LP can be done without CT if none of these risk factorsCRITICAL: Do NOT delay antibiotics for CT or LP. Draw blood cultures FIRST, then immediately start antibiotics, THEN do CT/LP. Antibiotics within 60 min of recognition. Every hour of delay increases mortality by ~5–10%
3. AntibioticsEmpiric: Ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h (all ages; S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis). Add ampicillin 2 g IV q4h if age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant (Listeria coverage). Add vancomycin 15–20 mg/kg IV q8–12h (cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus)After CSF sent: narrow if organism identified. Penicillin allergy: meropenem + vancomycin. Duration: S. pneumoniae 10–14 days; N. meningitidis 7 days; Listeria 21 days; gram-negative (E. coli, Klebsiella) 21 days
4. DexamethasoneDexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV q6h × 4 days. Give BEFORE or WITH first antibiotic dose (no benefit if given after)de Gans & van de Beek NEJM 2002: dexamethasone reduced unfavorable outcomes (34% vs 25%) and mortality (15% vs 7%) specifically in S. pneumoniae meningitis. Mechanism: reduces cytokine-mediated inflammation, decreases brain edema, maintains BBB. May reduce CSF antibiotic penetration — controversial for N. meningitidis but give empirically, discontinue if not S. pneumoniae
5. CSF interpretationBacterial: WBC >1000 (PMN predominant), protein >100, glucose <0.4 serum ratio, positive culture/Gram stain. Viral: WBC 100–1000 (lymphocytic), protein mildly elevated, glucose normalPartially treated meningitis: culture may be negative; antigen tests (pneumococcal, meningococcal) + FilmArray meningitis/encephalitis panel can help

12.4.4 Time-Critical Neurological Emergencies Summary Table

Time-Critical Neurological Emergencies — Rapid Reference
EmergencyTime WindowKey InterventionOutcome Impact
LVO ischemic stroke — tPA<4.5h from onsetIV alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg); door-to-needle <60 minNNT ~10 for independence (mRS 0–2)
LVO ischemic stroke — EVT<24h (selected patients); ideally <6hMechanical thrombectomy; door-to-groin <90 minNNT 2.6 for independence; 6% absolute mortality benefit
SAH — aneurysm securingWithin 24h (ideally same-day)Endovascular coiling or surgical clipping; nimodipine 60 mg q4hRebleeding risk: 4–14% untreated first 24h
Status epilepticus — benzodiazepine<5 min of seizure; phase 1 by 20 minLorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV or IM midazolam 10 mg; second agent by 30 minNNT 5 for terminating SE; each 10-min delay increases refractoriness by 30%
MESCC — dexamethasoneIMMEDIATE on recognitionDexamethasone 10 mg IV bolus then 4 mg q6h; surgery within 24–48hAmbulatory at treatment: 80% stay ambulatory vs 10% paraplegic
Bacterial meningitis — antibiotics<60 min of recognitionCeftriaxone + vancomycin ± ampicillin; dexamethasone BEFORE antibioticsEach hour delay increases mortality 5–10%
Cauda equina syndrome<24–48h (earlier = better)Decompressive laminectomy/discectomyCES-I early surgery: 70–90% improvement; CES-R: 30–50% bladder recovery
Herniation — osmotherapyIMMEDIATEHTS 23.4% 30 mL IV push; mannitol 1 g/kg IV; HOB 30°; hyperventilation bridgeDelays herniation by minutes to hours; bridge to definitive surgical decompression
Malignant hyperthermiaIMMEDIATE (intraoperative)Stop triggering agent; dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus; 100% O2Mortality: >70% without dantrolene vs <5% with
Botulism — antitoxinASAP (before paralysis progresses)Heptavalent equine antitoxin (HBAT) via CDC emergency operationsHalts progression; doesn’t reverse existing paralysis
Acute SCI — MAP targetWithin first 7 daysMAP ≥85–90 mmHg (vasopressors); surgical decompression <24hEvery AIS grade improvement = major functional benefit; STASCIS: 19.8% vs 8.8%
Stroke — ICH blood pressureWithin 1h of recognitionNicardipine to SBP <140; avoid <120 (ATACH-2)Reduces hematoma expansion; does not improve functional outcome (INTERACT2)
Clinical Pearl — The Neurological Emergency Mindset: In neurological emergencies, the key is recognizing that time = neurons. Every minute of ischemia destroys ~1.9 million neurons in stroke. Every hour of untreated meningitis deepens the inflammatory cascade. Every day of untreated SAH risks rebleeding. The most dangerous cognitive error is anchoring to a diagnosis and delaying treatment. The second most dangerous is waiting for complete evaluation before starting empirical therapy. In bacterial meningitis: start antibiotics before the LP result. In suspected herniation: give osmotherapy before CT confirms it. In status epilepticus: give benzodiazepine at the 5-minute mark, not after 10 minutes of observation. Train yourself to identify the “do-not-miss” emergency in every altered patient and act on it first.

12.5 Neurotoxicology & Acute CNS Infections in Emergency

12.5.1 Drug-Induced Encephalopathy — Key Toxidromes

Neurotoxicology Syndromes — Diagnosis & Antidotes
ToxidromeAgentsClinical FeaturesAntidote/Treatment
Opioid toxidromeMorphine, heroin, oxycodone, fentanyl, methadone, tramadolMiosis + respiratory depression + altered consciousness (triad). Bradycardia, hypotension. Pulmonary edema (heroin). Seizures (tramadol, meperidine — normeperidine accumulation)Naloxone 0.4–2 mg IV/IM/IN (repeat q2–3 min; titrate to respiratory drive NOT full reversal). Infusion for long-acting opioids. Caution: precipitation of acute withdrawal (seizures, pulmonary edema). Fentanyl analogs (carfentanil): may require >10 mg total naloxone
Anticholinergic toxidromeAtropine, scopolamine, diphenhydramine (Benadryl), TCAs, antipsychotics, jimsonweed, deadly nightshade“Mad as a hatter, blind as a bat, hot as a hare, red as a beet, dry as a bone”: confusion/psychosis, mydriasis (fixed dilated pupils), hyperthermia, flushing, anhidrosis. Tachycardia, urinary retention, ileus. Seizures, coma in severe casesPhysostigmine 1–2 mg IV slow push (AChE inhibitor; crosses BBB). For agitation/delirium when benzodiazepines inadequate. Contraindicated if TCA overdose (cardiac toxicity: physostigmine can cause bradycardia/asystole). ECG first! Benzodiazepines for agitation. Cooling for hyperthermia. Foley for urinary retention
Cholinergic toxidrome (SLUDGE/DUMBBELS)Organophosphates (insecticides, nerve agents), carbamates, pilocarpine, physostigmine, neostigmineSLUDGE: Salivation, Lacrimation, Urination, Defecation, GI distress, Emesis. PLUS: bronchospasm/bronchorrhea (most deadly), miosis, bradycardia. Nicotinic effects: muscle fasciculations → weakness → flaccid paralysis, tachycardia, hypertension. KILLER-Bs: Bronchospasm, Bronchorrhea, BradycardiaAtropine (antimuscarinic): large doses needed (2 mg IV q5–10 min until secretions dry; may need >100 mg). Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1–2 g IV over 15–30 min (reactivates AChE before “aging” — must give within 24–48h, especially for nerve agents). Benzodiazepines for organophosphate-induced seizures (not phenytoin — ineffective for cholinergic seizures). Decontamination critical (remove clothing, wash skin)
Sympathomimetic toxidromeCocaine, amphetamines, MDMA, methamphetamine, PCP, ephedrineTachycardia, hypertension, hyperthermia, diaphoresis, mydriasis. Agitation, psychosis, seizures. Stroke (ischemic or hemorrhagic from vasospasm/hypertension). Rhabdomyolysis. MDMA: hyponatremia (SIADH + water intake)Benzodiazepines (first-line for agitation, hypertension, seizures). Avoid beta-blockers (unopposed alpha → severe hypertension). Phentolamine or nitroprusside for severe hypertension. Cooling for hyperthermia. IV fluids. MDMA-induced hyponatremia: hypertonic saline if severe/symptomatic
Tricyclic antidepressant (TCA) overdoseAmitriptyline, imipramine, clomipramine, nortriptylineAnticholinergic features + QRS widening (>100 ms = cardiac toxicity risk) + hypotension + seizures. Prolonged QTc → TdP. Altered consciousness. Anticholinergic delirium. QRS >160 ms = ventricular arrhythmia riskSodium bicarbonate 1–2 mEq/kg IV bolus (alkalinizes serum: reduces TCa binding to Na channel; targets serum pH 7.45–7.55). Indicated: QRS >100 ms, ventricular arrhythmia, hypotension. Avoid physostigmine (cardiac risk). Avoid flumazenil. Lipid emulsion therapy for refractory cases. Intubate early (rapid deterioration)
Lithium toxicityAcute overdose vs chronic toxicity (different clinical pictures)Acute: GI dominant (N/V/D), then delayed neurological. Chronic: neurological dominant (tremor → ataxia → confusion → coma → seizures). SILENT syndrome: Syndrome of Irreversible Lithium-Effectuated NeuroToxicity. EEG: diffuse slowing, triphasic wavesStop lithium. IV normal saline (maintain adequate renal clearance). Hemodialysis for: Li >4 mEq/L any context, or >2.5 mEq/L with symptoms, or renal failure, or persistent/deteriorating neurotoxicity (Li distributes into CNS, rebounds after HD — may need multiple sessions). Whole-bowel irrigation for acute ingestion
Alcohol withdrawal & WernickeAlcohol withdrawal: CNS disinhibition after ethanol cessation. Wernicke: thiamine (B1) deficiencyAlcohol withdrawal: tremor (6–8h) → seizures (12–24h, usually brief tonic-clonic) → delirium tremens (48–72h): confusion + tremor + autonomic instability (fever, diaphoresis, tachycardia). Wernicke encephalopathy (classic triad): oculomotor abnormalities (horizontal nystagmus, CN VI palsy, conjugate gaze palsy) + ataxia + confusion/altered consciousness. MRI: bilateral symmetric FLAIR/DWI signal in mammillary bodies, periaqueductal gray, medial thalamiAlcohol withdrawal: Benzodiazepines (CIWA-Ar protocol; lorazepam preferred in liver disease). Phenobarbital for refractory DT or benzo-resistant withdrawal. Propofol or dexmedetomidine adjuncts for severe DTs. Wernicke: IV thiamine 500 mg TID × 2–3 days (NEVER give dextrose before thiamine — can precipitate acute Wernicke by depleting remaining thiamine). Glucose and thiamine together is acceptable. Treat Wernicke aggressively to prevent permanent Korsakoff syndrome (anterograde amnesia)

12.5.2 Viral Encephalitis — Emergency Approach

PathogenKey FeaturesDiagnosisTreatment
HSV encephalitisMost common sporadic fatal encephalitis. Temporal lobe predilection (fever + personality/behavioral change + focal seizures). MRI: unilateral or bilateral temporal/insular T2 hyperintensity. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, HSV PCR (sensitivity 96%, specificity 99%)CSF HSV-1/2 PCR. MRI brain (temporal + insular changes). EEG: temporal periodic lateralized epileptiform discharges (PLEDs/LPDs)IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h × 14–21 days. Start EMPIRICALLY if clinical + imaging suspicion (do not wait for PCR). Steroid adjunct controversial (consider if vasculitis or severe edema). Without treatment: mortality 70%; with acyclovir: mortality ~15%
Anti-NMDA receptor encephalitisMost common autoimmune encephalitis. Young women, ovarian teratoma (50% of cases in young women). Stages: prodrome (fever, headache) → psychiatric phase (psychosis, behavioral change — often initially admitted to psychiatry) → unresponsive/catatonic phase → hyperkinetic phase (orofacial dyskinesias, abnormal movements) → autonomic instability. CSF: lymphocytic pleocytosis. Anti-NMDAR IgG in serum/CSFSerum AND CSF anti-NMDAR antibodies (CSF more sensitive). MRI often normal (FLAIR changes in 30–50%). EEG: “extreme delta brush” (pathognomonic). Screen for teratoma (pelvic MRI/US in women, CT chest for others)1st-line: IV methylprednisolone 1 g × 5 days + IVIg 2 g/kg. Teratoma removal (if found — dramatically improves outcomes). 2nd-line (refractory): rituximab (anti-CD20) ± cyclophosphamide. Up to 80% improve with aggressive immunotherapy. Relapses in 12–25%
Arboviral encephalitisWest Nile virus (WNV): flavivirus; Culex mosquito; poliomyelitis-like acute flaccid paralysis (anterior horn); DWI lesions in deep gray matter (substantia nigra, basal ganglia, thalami). Eastern equine encephalitis (EEE): severe (mortality 30–75%); periventricular T2 changes. Japanese encephalitis (JE): most common cause of viral encephalitis worldwide; thalamic T2 signal; vaccine-preventableSerum and CSF IgM antibodies (primary diagnostic). PCR less sensitive in CSFNo specific antiviral; supportive care. IVIg (WNV: possible benefit). JE vaccine (prevention)
Clinical Pearl — The “Treat First, Diagnose Later” Principle in Encephalitis: When a patient presents with acute encephalitis (fever + confusion + seizures), immediately start empirical IV acyclovir (covers HSV and VZV), empirical ceftriaxone + vancomycin + dexamethasone (covers bacterial meningitis), and consider empirical ampicillin (covers Listeria in elderly/immunocompromised). Then obtain LP, MRI, and serological workup. The risk of treating empirically and it not being needed is vastly outweighed by the risk of delaying treatment. HSV encephalitis without acyclovir has 70% mortality; started within 48h it drops to 15%. This is one situation in neurology where the diagnostic workup must run parallel to, not precede, treatment.

12.6 References & Key Guidelines

Key References & Guidelines

ICP & Herniation

  • Carney N et al. Guidelines for the management of severe traumatic brain injury, 4th edition. Neurosurgery 2017;80(1):6–15. [BTF 4th Ed 2016]
  • Andrews PJ et al. Hypothermia for intracranial hypertension after traumatic brain injury (Eurotherm3235). N Engl J Med 2015;373(25):2403–2412.
  • Hutchinson PJ et al. Trial of decompressive craniectomy for traumatic intracranial hypertension (RESCUEicp). N Engl J Med 2016;375(12):1119–1130.
  • Jüttler E et al. Hemicraniectomy in older patients with extensive middle-cerebral-artery stroke (DESTINY II). N Engl J Med 2014;370(12):1091–1100.
  • Vahedi K et al. Early decompressive surgery in malignant infarction of the middle cerebral artery: a pooled analysis of three randomised controlled trials. Lancet Neurol 2007;6(3):215–222.

Brain Death & Prognostication

  • Wijdicks EF et al. Evidence-based guideline update: determining brain death in adults. Neurology 2010;74(23):1911–1918. [AAN 2010 Brain Death]
  • Greer DM et al. Determination of brain death/death by neurologic criteria: the World Brain Death Project. JAMA 2020;324(11):1078–1097.
  • Sandroni C et al. Neuroprognostication after cardiac arrest. Intensive Care Med 2022;48(10):1513–1523. [ERC/ESICM 2021 Guidelines Update]

SAH & Hypertensive Emergencies

  • Molyneux AJ et al. International subarachnoid aneurysm trial (ISAT) of neurosurgical clipping versus endovascular coiling in 2143 patients. Lancet 2002;360(9342):1267–1274.
  • Anderson CS et al. Rapid blood-pressure lowering in patients with acute intracerebral hemorrhage (INTERACT2). N Engl J Med 2013;368(25):2355–2365.
  • Qureshi AI et al. Intensive blood-pressure lowering in patients with acute cerebral hemorrhage (ATACH-2). N Engl J Med 2016;375(11):1033–1043.

Meningitis & Encephalitis

  • de Gans J, van de Beek D. Dexamethasone in adults with bacterial meningitis. N Engl J Med 2002;347(20):1549–1556.
  • Tunkel AR et al. Practice guidelines for the management of bacterial meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004;39(9):1267–1284. [IDSA Guidelines]
  • Dalmau J et al. Anti-NMDA-receptor encephalitis: case series and analysis of the effects of antibodies. Lancet Neurol 2008;7(12):1091–1098.

NMS & Serotonin Syndrome

  • Boyer EW, Shannon M. The serotonin syndrome. N Engl J Med 2005;352(11):1112–1120.
  • Strawn JR et al. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome. Am J Psychiatry 2007;164(6):870–876.
